items.title: Registration & Coffee
School Lecture
items.abstract: The handling of noise in networks requires care. "Noise" can mean many things, and come from various sources, and can be viewed alternately as something to be eliminated, adjusted for, or \u2013 in fact \u2013 leveraged, depending on context. In this talk we will look at networks and noise (in particular, edge noise), and how that relates to uncertainty quantification. We will look at noise in the sense not only of "measurement error" but also bias (i.e., structured noise), and hence the emerging literature on fairness in networks. Similarly, we will look at formal privacy in networks, where noise is injected intentionally, with a nod as well to the related emerging literature on machine unlearning in graphs. The overall goal is to provide an accessible introduction to how we formalize "noise" in these settings and a sense of the types of principled statistical solutions available for uncertainty quantification.

items.abstract: Network Science studies relational structure: how entities interact, how information propagates, and how global patterns emerge from local connectivity. This lecture introduces the foundations of deep learning on networks from a geometric perspective, with a focus on graph neural networks as the prevalent model class. We begin by introducing the message-passing paradigm and survey major graph neural network architectures that build on this design principle. We will then examine key theoretical and practical challenges, including over-smoothing, over-squashing, and the representational limits of message-passing models, using network-theoretic tools, and present remedies motivated by geometric insights.

items.abstract: Network visualizations are often treated as neutral tools for making relational data visible. This workshop challenges that assumption by approaching visualization as a methodological and cultural practice, one that shapes how we interpret, analyze, and communicate networks. Drawing on examples from art, design, and the history of visualization, the session introduces practical heuristics for working with network data while critically examining their underlying assumptions. Through a series of short, hands-on exercises, participants will explore four core operations: constructing visualizations from minimal information, reducing representations to their essential components, reframing network data through alternative layouts beyond force-directed graphs, and reflecting on what is left out when we imagine the world as a network. Each task foregrounds decisions about what to show, what to omit, and how representation structures interpretation. Participants will leave with a set of conceptual tools and concrete strategies for developing more deliberate, reflective, and effective network visualizations in their own research.

items.title: Invited Talk
items.abstract: Community-engaged or participatory data science research has been showed to produce actionable outcomes representing the aspirations and goals of the community. When adapted purposefully, such approaches can have significant and long-term impacts. Using examples from the Data Science Africa community and beyond, this talk will show how researchers are engaging with community networks to define and implement projects that address important questions and challenges affecting their local communities.

items.title: Invited Talk
items.abstract: Brain networks exhibit striking organizational principles across scales, from cellular connectomes to large-scale human brain networks. Over the past two decades, network neuroscience has shown that these architectures are linked to cognition, development, and disease. Yet much of this work has remained descriptive, leaving open fundamental questions about how network structure gives rise to neural computation. In this talk, I will present recent work using reservoir computing (RC) as a framework for probing structure-function relationships in neural systems across scales. I will describe applications in large-scale human connectomes reconstructed from diffusion MRI, in biologically grounded spiking neural network models, and in in-vitro neuronal cultures and cerebral organoids. Together, these studies illustrate how RC can bridge connectomics, dynamics, and computation, offering a more mechanistic approach to network neuroscience and new insights into how neural architecture supports cognition and dysfunction.

Keynote
items.abstract: Your friends tend to have more friends than you do because following a random edge—taking a random walk—samples nodes with probability roughly proportional to their degree. This insight seems almost too elementary to lead anywhere new. Yet we keep bumping into this same principle everywhere: epidemic spreading, contact tracing, link prediction, graph embedding, and so on. Because degree and random walks are so central to networks, this property is often the signal we most want to leverage, or the biggest bias we need to correct for. This talk is about my own random walk, where I have been stumbling onto the same fundamental principle over and over, maybe a reminder that the most basic idea often reaches farthest.

items.title: Keynote
items.abstract: Over the past decade, epidemic modelling has progressively evolved from compartmental representations to increasingly complex frameworks that attempt to capture the social and behavioral processes underlying disease transmission. The COVID-19 pandemic has accelerated this shift, highlighting that epidemic curves are not solely biological phenomena, but emergent outcomes of interacting demographic structures, social mixing patterns, behavioral responses, and policy interventions. In this keynote, I will argue that epidemics should be seen as dynamical processes spreading on heterogeneous social structures, where population subgroups differ in susceptibility, contact patterns, and behavioral adaptation. I will revisit key steps in the integration of demographic structure, empirical contact data, socio-economic stratification, and behavioral feedback into infectious disease models. I will discuss recent advances in modelling behavioral adaptation, including vaccination decisions and peer influence mechanisms, and show how these processes require moving beyond a single contact structure towards coupled epidemic-behavioral network representations. By connecting empirical data collection with modelling innovation, I will outline an integrated research agenda for epidemic network science to produce policy-relevant insights in both endemic and emergency settings.

items.abstract: Recent years have seen remarkable progress in AI and its applications in learning problems involving images, text, proteins, and myriad data modalities. The next frontier in AI revolution will move beyond single agent learning to systems with multiple human or AI agents interacting with each other in dynamic settings. Many such interactions take place in local environments where agents have payoff or dynamic state dependencies only with their neighborhoods. In this talk, we will study multi-agent reinforcement learning with state dynamics and agent payoffs governed by an underlying graph. Structured dynamics and payoffs of this type emerge in a number of applications, including epidemic control, communication and information networks, and smart building systems. Since the global state-action space scales exponentially with the number of agents (and is not available at the individual agent level), we seek local policies where each agent makes decisions based on information that depends on her local neighborhood. We provide conditions under which m-hop local policies approximate the global optimal policy, where the suboptimality gap is controlled by a Dobrushin-type stability matrix and decays exponentially in the neighborhood diameter. I will also talk about a new class of networked Markov games, where payoffs decompose according to an interaction network.

items.abstract: Phase transitions continues to attract wide interest within the physics community. Furthermore, the Kuramoto model has been widely employed to study the emergence of synchronization across diverse domains, including neuroscience, circadian biology, power grid operation, and collective social behaviors. The continuous, second-order phase transition to synchronization in Kuramoto networks has been thoroughly investigated, revealing universal scaling behavior. An analogous theoretical framework for abrupt first-order transitions is still missing. We show that the abrupt desynchronization transition from synchronous to asynchronous state yields the signs of critical slowing down, marked by diverging correlation length and time scale. Few previous studies have reported divergent time scales at a first order transition in spatially interacting systems and termed mixed-order transition. On the contrary, here we observe such divergences due to interactions in a number space which does not hold any concept of locality. Further, although scaling relations at saddle node bifurcations have been analyzed within mathematical nonlinear dynamics, we connect mathematical formulations with first-order phase transitions in physical systems (modelled by Kuramoto oscillators). The results fill an essential gap in the study of the celebrated Kuramoto oscillator on networks and address an important aspect of physics: the first order phase transition.

items.title: Invited Talk
items.abstract: I will briefly review some of our recent work on dynamic player positioning in association football (a.k.a. soccer), before moving to a slightly more speculative analysis of who is going to win the FIFA World Cup 2026. While the first part features spatio-temporal networks in an analysis of collective behavior, the second is based on competition networks and a novel method for ranking. The overarching goal is to pitch team sports as a model system for network scientists and offer an excuse to get excited about the impending tournament.

Hypergraph reconstruction from dynamics
items.abstract: A plethora of methods have been developed in the past two decades to infer the underlying network structure of an interconnected system from its collective dynamics. However, methods capable of inferring nonpairwise interactions are only starting to appear. Here, we develop an inference algorithm based on sparse identification of nonlinear dynamics (SINDy) to reconstruct hypergraphs and simplicial complexes from time-series data. Our model-free method does not require information about node dynamics or coupling functions, making it applicable to complex systems that do not have a reliable mathematical description. We first benchmark the new method on synthetic data generated from Kuramoto and Lorenz dynamics. We then use it to infer the effective connectivity in the brain from resting-state EEG data, which reveals significant contributions from non-pairwise interactions in shaping the macroscopic brain dynamics.
items.authors: Zhang, Yuanzhao*

items.title: Philanthropic Influence of Art Collectors on U.S. Art Museums
items.abstract: Art museum collections evolve through complex social relationships [1]. In particular, art collectors play a key role: their philanthropic donations sustain many museum holdings [2], yet their influence on artists’ institutional careers remains poorly quantified. Here, we compile a large-scale dataset of historical museum acquisitions from 15 major publicly funded art museums in the United States, selected to balance institutional prestige and geographic diversity. We extract artists and collectors names from unstructured text through an AI-human name disambiguation pipeline. The final data comprise 1,341,762 artworks linked to 100,686 artists, 47,123 collectors, and 3,640 organizations. Each artwork in the data includes its acquisition year, title of the artwork, artist name, and name of the collector, who donated or provided resources for the respective museum acquisition. From these data, we map the network of collectors linking artists with museums over time through philanthropic donations. We characterize connectivity in the museum network along two general dimensions: scope and frequency. Scope captures whether connections remain local, concentrated within a single museum, or global, spanning multiple institutions. Frequency captures whether these connections occur one-time, or repeat over time. Together, these dimensions define four connectivity types: local one-time, local repeat, global one-time, and global repeat. Applied jointly to collectors and artists, these dimensions describe how relationships between artists and museums are established and sustained. We find that 20% are local repeat collectors, who act as institutional anchors, supporting 56% of the long-term growth of museum collections. A 69% are one-time donors (local or global) who contribute occasionally with a 12% of total donations. In contrast, 11% are global repeat collectors responsible for 32% of total donations, who emerge as cultural connectors by linking museums and introducing artists into multiple institutional contexts. On the artist side, 75% of artists are distributed in only one museum, whereas 25% of artists achieve global institutional presence. We observe that wider artists’ visibility is closely associated with the giving patterns by global repeat collectors, who enable the circulation of artworks across institutions. Interestingly, global repeat collectors form connections with artists and institutions through mixed selective patterns (Fig. 1a), demonstrating that collectors’ donation choices can significantly influence artists’ institutional career success (Fig. 1b). Collectors’ selective connectivity evidences that they function as bridges, or gatekeepers, for artists’ visibility across institutions, forming or reinforcing the artists-museum connection. Overall, our network-based framework captures fundamental dimensions of collector-mediated connectivity, offering a quantitative lens on how artistic visibility is gained and why artists’ careers diverge within museum systems shaped by philanthropic giving. More broadly, this work points to new ways of turning cultural data into structured knowledge, creating building blocks that can feed into future AI systems, data science tools, and new forms of computational art.
items.authors: Herrera Guzman, Yessica*; Barabási, Albert-László

items.title: Bridge Criticality in a Spatial Road Network
items.abstract: Transportation network vulnerability has become increasingly important given the critical role infrastructure plays in national mobility and economic resilience and remains an important problem to understand how these systems can withstand and recover from failures and disruptions. While extensive research has examined the robustness of road networks through various frameworks, the critical role bridges play in road networks and the risks posed by bridge infrastructure to transportation network connectivity is understudied. As structural bottlenecks in highway systems, bridge failures can disproportionately impact network connectivity, making their assessment vital for transportation planning and risk management. Here, using National Bridge Inventory data from the Long Term Bridge Performance project of the Federal Highway Administration of the US, we study the National Highway System and develop a percolation analysis framework to study the impact of removal of bridges on the network. Different removal scenarios are compared, and we describe a combined risk score accounting for increases in distance from detours caused by removal of bridges, which can be used to identify critical bridges based on their position in their network as well as features of their infrastructure such as condition and age. Accounting for bridge feature conditions and daily traffic volume highlights the bridges that are critical in disconnecting the network and delays in travel time caused by their disruption can be identified. The figure shows the case for a single county in the state of Iowa, where a small subset of bridges removed lead to non-trivial delays and disconnection of the network. This framework when extended to the entire system can be used to provide actionable insights for infrastructure resilience and planning in the event of disruptions caused by systemic failures or natural disasters.
items.authors: Parameswaran, Aarathi*

items.title: Unifying Information-Theoretic and Pair-Counting Community Similarity
items.abstract: Comparing community structures is fundamental to evaluating unsupervised methods in network science, yet existing similarity measures often yield inconsistent or even contradictory assessments[1]. Community similarity measures are typically divided into two broad families: pair-counting and information-theoretic, depending on whether agreement is quantified through element pairs or through aggregate information over community contingency tables. While prior work has noted empirical relationships between these families and proposed normalization or chance-correction schemes, their underlying analytical connection remains incomplete[2]. Here, we introduce a unifying framework that links pair-counting and information-theoretic community similarity measures through two complementary perspectives. First, we show that both families can be written as weighted expansions of observed versus expected co-occurrences: pair-counting measures arise as low-order approximations, whereas information-theoretic measures correspond to higher-order, frequency-weighted expansions of community residuals (Fig 1C,D). Second, we generalize pair-counting to $k$-tuples and demonstrate that information-theoretic measures can be interpreted as systematically accumulating higher-order co-assignment structure beyond the pairwise level (Fig 1A,B). We illustrate these connections analytically for the Rand index and Mutual Information, and show how other commonly used measures in each family emerge as natural extensions of this framework. Our key insight is that reported agreement between community detection algorithms can be fundamentally scale-biased. In networks with heterogeneous community sizes, high similarity scores may indicate consensus on a few large communities while concealing substantial and systematic disagreement in mesoscale and peripheral organization. Our framework clarifies when and why pair-counting and information-theoretic measures diverge, links their behavior directly to weighting and approximation order, and establishes a principled foundation for evaluating and designing community similarity measures that faithfully reflect network structure across scales.
items.authors: Gates, Alexander*

items.title: Early Rules Cast Long Shadows: Dependency Dynamics in Wikipedia’s Regulatory Network
items.abstract: Do rules have their own dynamics? Administrative codes in universities grow slowly as problems get absorbed, shift with leadership, and respond to external shocks [1]. But rules do not exist in isolation. In legal codes, manuals, and online platforms, rules reference each other for justification or exception. These cross-references create dependencies: a rule’s meaning is incomplete without the rules it cites. Prior work showed that Wikipedia’s regulatory pages form exactly such a network, with early rules occupying central positions [2]. Why do some rules become foundational while others remain peripheral? Here we trace the temporal evolution of Wikipedia’s regulatory dependency network to understand how this hierarchy forms and stabilizes. We construct a directed network where nodes are Wikipedia regulatory pages (policies, guidelines, etc.) and edges are cross-references in the text. Figure 1a illustrates this: the Neutral Point of View (NPOV) policy contains links like “please read [[WP:NOT]]” and “content should be [[WP:V]] verifiable.” Each link creates a directed edge. We reconstruct it yearly from 2001 to 2016. Figure 1b shows that the network grows rapidly until 2008, then saturates, consistent with problem absorption [1,2]. The inset shows that in-degree inequality (Gini) rises from 0.59 to 0.81 by 2008, then stabilizes [2]. This reflects cumulative advantage: early rules gain visibility, attract citations, and become difficult to displace. Once formed, the hierarchy persists. Figure 1c traces how this hierarchy forms. In 2001, the emerging core was dominated by NPOV and What Wikipedia Is Not (NOT). By 2003, Verifiability (V) and No Original Research (NOR) emerged, operationalizing neutrality by specifying how to achieve it. By 2008, NPOV was cited by 309 pages, and this core remained stable even as the network grew from 30 to 1,294 pages. Figure 1d reveals the dependency structure. There are asymmetric connections: some rules have far more in-degree than out-degree. This asymmetry indicates hierarchy among rules [3]. Rules with high in-degree but low out-degree are foundational: others depend on them, but they depend on few. NPOV has 452 incoming and 34 outgoing citations (13:1). All four fall below the diagonal, anchoring the network while other rules elaborate on them. Together, foundational rules established in the first two years remain central, making them costly to change. New rules do not displace existing foundations; they attach to them. Wikipedia offers a lens to trace how regulatory hierarchy emerges and persists: what drives this lock-in, is it universal, and what happens when foundations must change?
items.authors: Bhattacharya, Shambhobi*; Youn, Hyejin ; Yoon , Jisung

items.title: All contacts are not equal: Spatiotemporal constraints shape network structure and transmission dynamics
items.abstract: Contact network structure is widely studied because of its influential role in infectious disease transmission. Since the POLYMOD study [1], routine contact surveys have consistently used similar distance (e.g., 2 meters) and duration (e.g., 5 minutes) constraints to define close contacts relevant to respiratory transmission. This standardization facilitates comparison between studies but critically overlooks the unique transmission profiles of respiratory viruses [2]. We hypothesize that pathogens may be limited to different contact network structures based on their spatiotemporal transmission constraints and thus demonstrate distinct transmission dynamics. Using a dataset with fine-scale interaction data, we investigate how changes in contact definition impact contact network structure and respiratory disease dynamics. To characterize how network structure varies with different contact distance and duration thresholds, we use app-based GPS location data from anonymized and opted-in mobile devices aggregated to protect individual privacy. We focus our analysis on locations relevant to public health (e.g., restaurants, grocery stores), transmission constraints that are realistic for respiratory pathogens (e.g., influenza, measles, coronaviruses), and network features known to predict epidemic outcomes (e.g., mean degree, degree heterogeneity, transitivity). After adjusting for network sampling biases [3], we find that contact network structure is sensitive to contact definition (Figure 1). Pathogens capable of transmission over large distances or through brief exposures have access to denser and more heterogeneously connected contact networks, whereas pathogens requiring close proximity or longer interactions are restricted to sparser networks with lower degree heterogeneity. Through network disease modeling, we show that this structural variation leads to distinct transmission dynamics, which can inform the inference of transmission modes for emerging pathogens from epidemiological data. These findings shed light on the evolutionary pressures faced by different respiratory pathogens and help characterize which pathogens are more likely to exhibit superspreading. [1] Mossong J, et al. Social contacts and mixing patterns relevant to the spread of infectious diseases. PLOS Medicine. 2008 Mar;5(3):e74. [2] Leung NHL. Transmissibility and transmission of respiratory viruses. Nat Rev Microbiol. 2021 Aug;19(8):528–45. [3] Kolaczyk ED. Statistical Analysis of Network Data: Methods and Models. New York, NY: Springer; 2009.
items.authors: Taube, Juliana*; Tiu, Andrew; Bansal, Shweta

items.title: From Ultra-Processed Foods to Processing Pathways: A Network-Aware Inference Framework
items.abstract: Ultra-processed foods (UPFs) are consistently associated with an increased risk of metabolic, cardiovascular, hepatic, and inflammatory diseases. However, dominant frameworks for characterizing food processing rely on qualitative and descriptive criteria that collapse highly heterogeneous industrial practices into coarse categorical labels. Foods that differ markedly in ingredients, nutrient profiles, and industrial processing steps and sequences are therefore treated as equivalent, obscuring meaningful variation and potentially masking differential disease risk. Moreover, large-scale product-level analyzes, including our study of more than 70,000 U.S. grocery products, show that food processing information is often incomplete or inconsistently annotated, underscoring the need for systematic methods that can infer fine-grained processing steps. To address these limitations, we constructed a harmonized food-processing database comprising more than 62,000 foods annotated with LanguaL-derived processing descriptors, nutrient profiles, and food-group information aggregated from heterogeneous international sources. Processing annotations are inherently relational and sparse: each food is associated with multiple semantically related descriptors reflecting combinations and sequences of operations, while many plausible food–process associations remain unobserved. This structure naturally induces a bipartite network linking foods and processing descriptors, where observed edges encode partial processing pathways and missing edges represent absent or latent operations. Inferring food processing, therefore, becomes a network learning problem under extreme label sparsity, with the central challenge of distinguishing true absences from missing annotations—a limitation that fundamentally constrains feature-based classifiers lacking access to relational structure. Here, we present a unified inference pipeline that integrates network-aware models with top-k multilabel classification baselines trained on BERT-derived embeddings from curated food metadata. By leveraging network topology, the proposed framework substantially improves recovery of known food–process associations by reducing confusion between absent and unobserved processing operations. The best-performing model achieves Top-5 and Top-10 accuracies of 88.3% and 94.4%, respectively, and a Top-10 recall of 70.9%, despite foods being annotated with only ~5 labels on average out of 269 possible descriptors—corresponding to an 8–15X improvement over random ranking. By reframing food processing as a problem of missing-edge inference in hierarchical bipartite networks, this framework moves beyond coarse UPF categories and enables systematic identification of fine-grained processing steps within and across food groups, informing targeted interventions in food formulation and public health policy. More broadly, it illustrates how network topology can be leveraged to recover latent relational structure from sparse, incomplete annotations, a setting common to many large-scale data integration problems in network science.
items.authors: Ispirova, Gordana*; Aldana, Andres; Menichetti, Giulia

items.title: Belief Coevolution in a Social Network of Generalist and Specialist Large Language Models
items.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) now generate a significant portion of online content. AI-generated text appears in official press releases, scientific articles, and social knowledge-sharing platforms. As LLMs become more accessible, they are increasingly used in multi-agent settings alongside humans, where collaboration occurs through sustained multi-turn interactions. This environment creates an ongoing feedback loop in which LLMs shape users’ beliefs while simultaneously adapting to user-generated content. However, we still lack a systematic understanding of how LLMs form and propagate beliefs. Moreover, existing research on interactions between LLMs focuses primarily on behavioral proxies (e.g., decisions LLMs make in collaborative tasks) rather than the underlying belief states that drive these behaviors. Additionally, experiments often examine settings where LLM agents interact in pairs or share information globally, overlooking network-mediated scenarios in which agents interact only with immediate neighbors. Belief dynamics are further complicated by the heterogeneity of LLMs’ expertise. Some LLMs are finetuned on domain-specific corpora (e.g., biomedical literature, cybersecurity reports), while others are optimized for particular capabilities (e.g., storytelling or coding). When such diverse agents interact within a social network, their expertise and social roles (how LLMs present themselves to others) create a complex interactive environment. We propose CoevolveSim, an agent-based framework for simulating the coevolution of beliefs in a social network composed of LLMs. At each iteration, an LLM agent receives a summary of its neighbors’ beliefs along with their social roles. The agent then updates its belief based on local information and broadcasts the updated belief to its neighbors in the next round. CoevolveSim disentangles the effects of three factors: (1) expertise: domain knowledge embedded through finetuning, (2) social roles: identity labels that signal credibility to neighbors, and (3) network structure: who interacts with whom. Using both global and local measures of influence, we identify conditions under which belief diffusion leads to (1) stable convergence, (2) sharp phase-transition-like cascades, or (3) fragmented disagreement. Our preliminary results on 16 generalist and specialist LLMs connected by Erdős–Rényi graphs show that generalist LLMs without finetuning but with social roles exhibit rapid belief convergence toward consensus, while specialist LLMs with finetuning and corresponding social roles resist updating beliefs, allowing errors to persist throughout interactions.
items.authors: Savcisens, Germans*; Dies, Samantha; Maynard, Courtney; Eliassi-Rad, Tina

items.title: The U.S. Code as a Growing Networked System (1926–2023)
items.abstract: Legal systems are among the most consequential—and structurally complex—human-made information networks. Yet, network science has only begun to engage with statutory law at scale, largely due to historical barriers in accessing machine-readable texts and consistent structural representations. Here we present an open dataset (to be publicly released via Scientific Data) that captures the century-scale evolution of the United States Code (U.S. Code), the primary codification of federal statutory law. The dataset provides two network-native layers central to network science research: (i) hierarchical links among legal components within each title, and (ii) directed, weighted interdependencies among titles via explicit cross-references (Fig. 1a). We formalize statutory law as a time-resolved, multi-layer networked system. Each title is represented as a hierarchical tree spanning structural units (e.g., Title to Chapter to Section to Subsection to Item), enabling quantitative analysis of depth, breadth, branching, and organizational change over time. We also extract inter-title cross-references by sentence-level parsing to construct yearly directed, weighted networks, where an edge from title i to title j counts explicit references to j within i. We additionally provide statistically filtered backbones (e.g., disparity-filter-based structures) for mesoscale analysis. A key methodological contribution is the reconstruction of early, pre-digital editions of the U.S. Code that exist only as scanned volumes. We combine OCR outputs with a constrained generative-AI normalization pipeline to correct typographical noise while preserving original content, and validate the reconstructed corpus and derived networks against born-digital records and prior reference corpora. This dataset lowers the barrier for network scientists to study law as (1) a growing hierarchical infrastructure, (2) an interdependent citation network, and (3) a dynamic institutional memory shaped by reorganization and domain coupling. It enables investigations of densification, shifting centrality, backbone stability, modularity, motifs, and robustness of statutory interdependencies. Finally, we outline ongoing work extending this approach from statutes to the coupled system of statutory law (U.S. Code) and administrative law (the Code of Federal Regulations, CFR). By constructing authority–implementation links between statutes and the regulations that implement them, we observe that the 1996 coupling network exhibits multiple clusters, whereas the 2023 network shows a more pronounced core–periphery structure (Fig.1b).
items.authors: Jeong, Dawoon*; Holehouse, James; Yoon, Jisung; Youn, Hyejin

items.title: Markovian ε-SIR on the complete graph
items.abstract: We investigate the continuous-time Markovian SIR epidemic with self-infection on the complete graph. We introduce a labelling of the state space based on Cantor’s zig-zag enumeration and show that, under this ordering, the infinitesimal generator of the process is an upper triangular matrix with a bidiagonal block structure. This structure allows us to derive an exact analytical solution for the time evolution of the state probabilities. However, given the complexity of the explicit solution, we derive an asymptotic expansion valid in the large-system limit, thus allowing a clearer understanding of the model’s limiting behavior as the system size becomes arbitrarily large. We then compare the exact dynamics of the Markovian SIR on the complete graph with the classical Kermack McKendrick (KM) approximation and also derive an analytical criterion to evaluate the accuracy of the approximation. Our analysis indicates that the KM model overestimates the number of infected individuals, in agreement with mean-field approximations. The discrepancy between Markovian SIR and KM is particularly significant near the epidemic threshold, consistent with theoretical predictions.
items.authors: Giovannelli, Benedetta*; Van Mieghem, Piet

items.title: Are scale-free networks also timescale-free?
items.abstract: Our understanding of a physical system's dynamic behavior is often unraveled through its intrinsic timescales, as it responds to external excitation. The classic example is the harmonic oscillator, which is characterized by two timescales: its decay rate (damping) and its oscillation period. But what happens when the system is complex and heterogeneous, such as a scale-free network. Such systems lack a characteristic structural scale, but do they also lack a dynamic timescale? In simple terms, are scale-free networks also timescale-free? To address this, we investigate the network’s response to arbitrary forms of excitation. Our analysis shows that complex systems fall into distinct dynamic classes: Homogeneous dynamics, where, despite the scale-free network structure, the system's actual dynamics exhibit uniform timescales. Timescale-free dynamics, where the systems, indeed, lacks a typical timescale and, instead, features a coexistence of vastly diverse excitation lifetimes. Most excitations are extremely short-lived, but some live on for extended periods, sustained for orders of magnitude above the system's average timescale. These newly discovered timescale-free networks feature special modes, that the network is dynamically wired to preserve for extended periods. These modes, capturing timescale-hubs, decay extremely slowly, and are thus preserved for orders of magnitude longer than all other dynamic states. More broadly, our analysis offers a systematic translation of topology (degree distribution) into dynamics (timescale distribution), that helps us predict the system’s uniquely long-lasting vs. short-lived dynamic states.
items.authors: Barzel, Baruch*; Berger, Guy

items.title: Real-Space Renormalization and Sparsification of Complex Networks
items.abstract: Graph sparsification and renormalization are fundamental tools for reducing the complexity of networks and network models, both to make them more amenable to numerical simulation and to reveal how macroscopic behavior scales with system size [1]. Existing renormalization schemes rely either on heuristic blocking, which yields poorly controlled approximations, or on spectral constructions, which can show poor performance on heterogeneous networks. We introduce a general real-space sparsification and renormalization framework based on message passing, which provides a recipe for performing local surgery on networks---removal of nodes or edges---while preserving the values of physically meaningful quantities. The method can be applied to any network process that can be represented using message passing. As examples, we apply the approach to edge percolation and to the solution of the Ising model at finite temperature. We also show that when our method is applied to the calculation of effective conductance in networks, the well-known Spielman–Teng spectral sparsifier [2] emerges as a limiting case. [1] Andrea Gabrielli et al. Network renormalization. Preprint arxiv:2412.12988. 2024. [2] Daniel A. Spielman and Shang-Hua Teng. “Spectral sparsification of graphs”. In: SIAM J. Comput. 40 (2011), pp. 981–1025
items.authors: Sheeran, Cornelia*; Newman, Mark

items.title: Graph-aware proper scoring rules for node selection tasks
items.abstract: Let $G=(V,E)$ be a graph with $N=|V|$ nodes. A \emph{node selection task} on $G$ consists of inferring a single node $i\in V$. This subsumes important graph learning problems such as epidemic source detection, influence maximisation, or fault detection. Any edge selection task can also be recast as a node selection task on the line graph. Many approaches to such problems are probabilistic (e.g., Monte Carlo methods or graph neural networks), which means they estimate $i$ by providing a probability distribution $\vec{p}\in\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^N$, $\|\vec{p}\|_1=1$, over $V$. Standard evaluation metrics in this setting such as top-$K$ error rate or the rank of $i$ are unsatisfactory for at least two reasons: (a)~these metrics do not take into account the graph structure when assessing how closely a prediction matches the observed true node, and (b)~they do not penalise unjustified overconfidence appropriately. We address this gap by introducing two graph-aware scores for node selection tasks: firstly, the \emph{resistance score} $S(\vec{p},i)=-\frac{N}{2}(\vec{p}-\vec{e}_i)^\top\Omega(\vec{p}-\vec{e}_i)$ where $\vec{e}_i$ is the $i$-th unit vector and $\Omega$ is the matrix of resistance distances between all pairs of nodes~[1]; secondly, the \emph{diffusion score} $S_\tau(\vec{p}, i)=\frac{1}{4}(\vec{p}-\vec{e}_i)^\top e^{-\frac{\tau}{2}(L-\mathbb{I})}(\vec{p}-\vec{e}_i)$ with scaling parameter $\tau\in(0,\infty)$ where $L$ denotes the Laplacian of $G$. We show that these scores are \emph{proper scoring rules} [2] in the statistical sense, i.e., they are optimised when $\vec{p}$ coincides with the true distribution of $i$, discouraging unjustified overconfidence. On the complete graph both scores reduce to the well-known multi-class Brier score for $N$ independent classes. The resistance score admits an intuitive interpretation in terms of the recently introduced variance of Devriendt et al. [1] and reduces on the path graph $P_N$ to the standard ranked probability score for ordinal data [3]. The diffusion score, meanwhile, behaves like the Brier score at different scales: it coincides with the Brier score for $\tau\to 0$, while for $\tau\to\infty$ it only assesses whether $\vec{p}$ is concentrated on the correct global cluster. In this sense, it connects to recent work on Laplacian coarse-graining [4]. Figure~\ref{fig:fig1} illustrates the improved discriminative ability of our scores in a toy example. Further simulation experiments for source detection on empirical networks demonstrate that our scores successfully distinguish between models that traditional metrics fail to separate. Moreover, we demonstrate that both scores can be efficiently approximated even for very large networks using techniques based on Krylov subspaces and Laplacian solvers as explored in [5]. Their use as loss functions in graph neural networks is left for future work.
items.authors: Hilfiker, Lorenz*; Brack, Nathan; Sterchi, Martin

items.title: Construction of Hidden Metric Space with Community Structure
items.abstract: Recent research has demonstrated that many empirical networks possess a hidden metric space (HMS)—a metric space that underlies and shapes the observable network topology. Distances within the HMS generally reflect the similarity between nodes, making it particularly well-suited for network routing via the greedy routing strategy (GRS). When the HMS of a given network is known, the GRS can establish routes between any pair of nodes without relying on global information, a key advantage over alternative routing methods. Various approaches have been proposed for constructing the HMS, yielding different metric spaces such as planar, hyperbolic, and high-dimensional spaces. However, no principled method exists for selecting the appropriate metric space, and embedding a network into an unsuitable metric space can substantially degrade the GRS routing performance. This work introduces a hierarchical approach for computing the HMS of a given network. Unlike traditional methods that embed networks into a single metric space, our approach relies on the network's community structure to construct a hierarchical HMS, thereby avoiding the performance limitations imposed by a predetermined metric space. Fig. 1(a) illustrates the application of our algorithm to a US population model network, where the result is a two-layer HMS. We demonstrate that this approach can be applied to an arbitrary network regardless of its physical embedding, as shown in Fig. 1(b). Compared to existing embedding algorithms, the hierarchical HMS approach consistently achieves superior routing performance while significantly reducing computational time, particularly for large-scale networks. Moreover, for certain networks, routing performance with the hierarchical HMS surpasses that achieved using real-space coordinates, suggesting that the constructed HMS captures network structure more effectively than physical embedding.
items.authors: Xie, Zhuoxuan*; Motter, Adilson

items.title: Graph-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning for Resilience-Oriented Decision-Making in Critical Infrastructure Systems
items.abstract: Critical infrastructure systems (CIS), such as water distribution networks, road networks, and power networks, are often referred to as lifelines because they are essential for supporting the daily functioning of communities. The rapid expansion of CIS driven by urbanization has increased their vulnerability to high-consequence hazards, including earthquakes, flooding, and wildfires, thereby highlighting the urgent need for effective resilience-enhancement strategies. The resilience of a CIS is commonly defined as its ability to recover functionality following disruptive events, and sound repair-sequence decisions play a crucial role in achieving rapid and efficient recovery. This study presents the development of a Graph Convolutional Neural Network--integrated Deep Reinforcement Learning (GCN--DRL) framework to support optimal repair decisions aimed at improving CIS resilience under multiple hazard scenarios. To demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed GCN--DRL model, a water distribution network (WDN) and a road network subjected to earthquake and flooding hazards are used as case studies. Resilience evaluation frameworks for both systems are first developed, explicitly capturing the dynamic evolution of system performance during post-event recovery. For the WDN, the performance indicator accounts for the relative importance of service nodes and the extent to which post-earthquake water demands are satisfied. For the road network, the performance indicator reflects network connectivity under road segment closures. Within the GCN--DRL framework, the GCN encodes the structural and operational information of both the WDN and the road network. Network topology and node-level performance states (i.e., water demand satisfaction levels and road segment availability) are provided as inputs to the GCN, which outputs action-specific reward values. These values are then used by the DRL agent to identify optimal repair sequences from a large action space, with the objective of maximizing overall system resilience. The repair strategies generated by the proposed GCN--DRL model are compared with those obtained using four conventional decision-making approaches. The results demonstrate that the GCN--DRL model consistently achieves the highest system resilience index (SRI) values and enables the fastest recovery of system performance. Furthermore, by leveraging transfer learning from pre-trained models, the proposed framework exhibits high computational efficiency when determining optimal repair sequences under new damage scenarios. Overall, the GCN--DRL model demonstrates strong robustness and generalizability, providing an effective decision-support tool for enhancing the resilience of interdependent infrastructure systems under multiple hazard conditions.
items.authors: Fan, Xudong*; Hackl, Jürgen; Yu, Xiong (Bill)

items.title: Evolution and determinants of firm-level systemic risk in local production networks
items.abstract: Recent crises like the COVID-19 pandemic and geopolitical tensions have exposed vulnerabilities and caused disruptions of supply chains, leading to product shortages, increased costs, and economic instability. This has prompted increasing efforts to assess systemic risk, namely the effects of firm disruptions on entire economies. However, the ability of firms to react to crises by rewiring their supply links has been largely overlooked, limiting our understanding of production networks resilience. Here, we study dynamics and determinants of firm-level systemic risk in the Hungarian production network from 2015 to 2022. We use as benchmark the stripe-corrected gravity model [1], a heuristic maximum entropy null model that generates an ensemble of production networks at equilibrium, by preserving the total input (demand) and output (supply) of each firm at the sector level. By computing the Economic Systemic Risk Index (ESRI) [2] we can see how the empirical values align well with the null model until the onset of the pandemic, as shown in Figure 1 A. It becomes significantly smaller afterwards, as the adaptive behavior of firms leads to a more resilient economy – both in absolute terms and with respect to equilibrium values. Furthermore we show that the fairly stable set of firms with highest systemic risk undergoes a structural change during COVID-19, as those enabling economic exchanges become key players in the economy – a result which can be seen in Figure 1 B and that is not reproduced by the null model. Moreover, regressions highlight how firms’ international trade volume (being a subject of disruption) becomes a significant predictor of their systemic risk. However, international links cannot provide an unequivocal explanation for the observed trends, as imports and exports have opposing effects on local systemic risk through the supply and demand channels.
items.authors: Mancini, Anna*

items.title: Integrating comparative protein biology with network medicine to elucidate the therapeutic role of plant chemicals for cardiovascular disease
items.abstract: Cardiovascular diseases remain the leading cause of death globally. Epidemiological studies demonstrate that plant-based diets provide cardioprotective benefits, yet our inability to predict precise molecular mechanisms limit precise dietary interventions. While network medicine offers a powerful framework for predicting how compounds modulate disease, the poor protein target characterization of plant chemicals creates severe data sparsity that prevents the systematic analysis of potential cardiovascular effects. We developed a computational framework that addresses this critical data limitation by integrating comparative protein biology with network medicine. This approach leverages the shared evolutionary origin and the co-evolutionary history of plants and humans, offering insight into how plant chemicals are recognized in human processes despite not being endogenously produced. Evolutionary conserved proteins and their associated functions between humans and plants account for 17{\%} of the human genome, allowing us to use the substantial body of plant biochemistry research to expand phytochemical-protein interaction data in humans. We identify a conserved homology module of 5,077 orthologous proteins within the human protein-protein interactome, creating a statistically significant largest connected component for use in network medicine analysis. For ten commonly consumed plants, we identify their plant chemicals and their human protein targets. We find that their targets are enriched within the homology module, indicating their importance to the modulation of the plant-human homology module. Additionally, the homology module is enriched with genes from cardiovascular disease modules. While the homology module contains many genes essential to eukaryotic life whose alternations typically associate with pathophenotypically severe Mendelian disorders or death rather than chronic, complex diseases, these orthologous proteins also include less connected proteins that contribute to CVD pathophysiology. By integrating plant-human homology with network medicine we create a systematic framework for predicting how plant chemicals affect cardiovascular health.
items.authors: Sebek, Michael*

items.title: The Extended Community of Diet Culture Around Eating Disorders: A Network Analysis of Reddit Forums
items.abstract: Clinical studies suggest a history of dieting is a known risk factor for eating disorders (ED), yet the relationship between diet culture and ED in online spaces remains underexplored. While ED online communities offer insight into psychological and behavioral experiences about the illness, broader dieting culture---often shaped by restrictive eating norms and body hierarchies---may inadvertently promote disordered behaviors. We investigate the structural and thematic connections between ED and diet-culture online communities by combining network analyses with large language model (LLM)-assisted topic modeling. We analyzed posts and comments from 12 ED and 105 dieting or body-image forums on Reddit. We constructed two forum-level networks: a semantic similarity network, where edge weights are defined as the average cosine similarity between Sentence-BERT embeddings of posts across forum pairs, and a co-engagement intensity network, where edge weights capture normalized cross-posting and co-commenting activity from shared users based on each forum’s newest posts and associated comments. To identify clusters of forums with dense semantic or behavioral connectivity, we detected communities by optimizing the modularity objective function using the Louvain method, selecting the resolution parameter that maximized modularity. We further quantified between-group connectivity using average edge weight and assessed statistical significance via a resampling-based null model. To interpret these clusters, we examined the prevalence of 13 topics using annotations by advanced LLMs, aggregated into forum-level prevalences. Results show significantly higher semantic similarity and co-engagement intensity between ED and dieting forums than random topics. The detected community structures on both networks revealed extended communities in which a subset of dieting forums consistently clustered with ED forums. Within these communities, ED and ED-adjacent dieting forums shared elevated discussions of 'nutritional restriction', 'food cravings', 'body dissatisfaction', and 'family or social relationships'. These findings highlight the entanglement of ED and diet culture in online discourse and underscore the need for nuanced public health approaches to monitoring not only ED but general dieting discussions in digital spaces.
items.authors: Qiu, Jiaxing*; Levinson, Cheri; Kropko, Jonathan; Henry, Teague; Gates, Alexander

items.title: Diverse Human Dilemmas, Uniform AI Preferences: Mapping Value Trade-Off Networks in Online Advice-Seeking Communities and Large Language Models
items.abstract: People increasingly seek advice online, not only from other users but also from large language model (LLM)–based chatbots [1], which now mediate how individuals navigate personal, relational, and professional dilemmas. Such advice rarely involves identifying a single correct answer; instead, it typically requires navigating trade-offs among competing values. We aim to characterize how LLMs navigate value trade-offs across different advice-seeking contexts. In this study, we examine the value trade-off structure underlying advice-seeking using a curated dataset [2] drawn from four advice-oriented subreddits. Each dilemma is represented in a structured format consisting of a real-life scenario and two alternative courses of action. Building on the bottom-up approach of prior work [3], we inductively construct a hierarchical value framework by aggregating thousands of fine-grained values extracted from individual advice options into progressively higher-level value categories. We find substantial heterogeneity in value trade-off structures across advice-seeking contexts: a women-focused subreddit exhibits the highest network density, indicating more complex value conflicts; women’s, men’s, and friendship-related subreddits show highly correlated patterns; and career advice forms a more distinct structure, with values such as security, respect, and commitment often in tension with autonomy, change, or opportunity. We then evaluate LLM value preferences against these human dilemmas and find that, across models and contexts, LLMs consistently prioritize values related to 'Exploration & Growth over' 'Benevolence & Connection'. This prioritization remains stable across dilemmas drawn from different subreddits, suggesting cross-model and cross-context consistency rather than domain-specific adaptation. Taken together, these findings highlight important societal implications as online communities and LLMs become increasingly embedded in advice-seeking ecosystems. This uniform value orientation highlights a potential risk of value homogenization in AI-mediated advice, raising concerns about how such systems may shape decision-making and normative outcomes at scale. [1] Kumar, H., Chahal, J., Zhao, Y., Zhang, Z., Wei, A., Tay, L., \& Anderson, A. (2025). When AI Gives Advice: Evaluating AI and Human Responses to Online Advice-Seeking for Well-Being. arXiv:2512.08937. [2] Bhatia, S., van Baal, S. T., Wang, F., and Walasek, L. (2025). Computational analysis of 100K choice dilemmas: Decision attributes, trade-off structures, and model-based prediction. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 122(17). [3] Huang, S., Durmus, E., Handa, K., McCain, M., Tamkin, A., Stern, M., Hong, J., \& Ganguli, D. (2025). Values in the wild: Discovering and mapping values in real-world language model interactions. Conference on Language Modeling.
items.authors: Chen, Zhiyi; Choi, Eun Cheol*; Luo, Yingjia; Wang, Xinyi; Xiao, Yulei; Yang, Aizi; Luceri, Luca

items.title: Graph evolution rules meet communities: assessing global and local patterns in the evolution of dynamic networks
items.abstract: Dynamic networks often exhibit modular structure, yet the mechanisms driving the evolution of their communities remain poorly understood. Graph evolution rules (GERs) capture recurrent local transformations in evolving networks in a data-driven and interpretable way. In this work, we combine GER mining with community detection to analyze network evolution at both global and mesoscopic scales. Using EvoMine and the Leiden algorithm, we derive evolutionary profiles that characterize networks and their communities. Our results show that communities may follow heterogeneous or uniform evolutionary patterns depending on the system, and that structurally connected communities tend to evolve similarly. This approach provides a fine-grained perspective on how local temporal processes shape community evolution.
items.authors: Galdeman, Alessia*; Zignani, Matteo; Gaito, Sabrina

items.title: Evidence of Gender-based Network Biases in Respondent-driven Sampling Data
items.abstract: Respondent-driven sampling (RDS) is a network-based method for studying hidden populations, such as people who inject drugs (PWID) (Heckathorn, 1997; Gile et al., 2010). Statistical inference using RDS relies on modeling recruitment as a random walk on the network, assuming participants accurately report their network composition and recruit peers approximately at random (Heckathorn, 1997; Volz and Heckathorn, 2008). In practice, reported networks may not fully reflect the true network structure, and some subgroups may face barriers to participation, potentially biasing statistical inference. Using a uniquely rich RDS dataset (Akiyama et al., 2025) spanning multiple sites and several types of network data, we investigate gender differences in network structure, reporting, and participation, and assess potential biases impacting inference. We analyze RDS data from 3,152 PWID across 17 sites in Kenya, collected as an extension of the TLC-IDU study (NCT01557998), focusing on the reported gender composition of participants’ social networks. Our analysis evaluates whether the observed proportion of women in the sample is consistent with expectations of random recruitment and the observed gender–degree structure of the sample. We further compare gender differences in reported 30-day networks, close contact data, and observed network ties. Population estimates were calculated using standard RDS methods (Volz and Heckathorn, 2008), and network simulation tests were used to assess discrepancies in participation and reporting. Results indicate that participants report more women in their networks than expected, suggesting potential over-reporting or under-participation of women. Across multiple sites, results were more nuanced, but indicate that women report more women in their 30-day networks, close contacts, and observed ties compared to men. These discrepancies highlight challenges in creating valid inferences from reported social network data and provide insight into the complexity of designing targeted interventions for PWID.
items.authors: Costa, Anabel*

items.title: Higher-order information-theoretic organization of motor modularity in health and stroke
items.abstract: Motor coordination subserving even simple activities of daily living requires navigating a high-dimensional space of muscle activations through nonlinear and task-dependent transformations. Despite this apparent complexity, the human motor system achieves robust, flexible, and transferable behavior across a wide range of tasks and contexts. A leading theoretical explanation for this efficiency is the existence of muscle modules: low-dimensional coordination patterns that constrain and organize muscle activity (d'Avella, A., & Bizzi, E.; 2005). Traditionally, muscle modules have been extracted using linear dimensionality-reduction techniques, which provide compact representations but are limited in their ability to characterize multivariate and higher-order dependencies among muscles. Here, we adopt a higher-order network perspective to investigate muscle coordination, leveraging information-theoretic tools that explicitly disentangle redundancy and synergy in multivariate interactions. We analyze publicly available data comprising electromyography (EMG) recordings from 12 upper-limb muscles during 30 distinct motor tasks, performed by 23 healthy controls and 21 individuals with mild stroke (Wrist/Hand Fugl-Meyer: 14-20) (Averta, et al., 2021). For each participant, we computed the O-information, a multivariate measure quantifying the balance between redundant and synergistic interactions across muscle subsets (Rosas et al.,2019). From the top-k multiplets, we constructed participant-specific hypergraphs capturing dominant redundancy- and synergy-based interactions (Figure 1A). Interpreting redundancy as reflecting stable muscle modules and synergy as a marker of inter-module coordination, we observed marked reorganization in stroke participants. At the group level, stroke patients exhibited increased redundancy alongside significantly reduced synergistic interactions (Figure 1B), consistent with more rigid or stereotyped motor control. Crucially, at the participant level, this imbalance was highly heterogeneous (Figure 1C), revealing substantial inter-individual variability in higher-order coordination patterns following stroke—variability that may inform individualized rehabilitation strategies. Together, these results demonstrate that higher-order information-theoretic networks provide a principled framework for characterizing motor coordination beyond pairwise interactions, revealing clinically relevant alterations in the balance between stability and flexibility of muscle control after stroke.
items.authors: Deshpande, Raj*; Huber, Maximilian; Yarossi, Matthew; Petri, Giovanni

items.title: The Emergence of Causal Curiosity from Prior Causal Belief Networks
items.abstract: Causal curiosity is foundational to human cognition. It is the desire to understand why events happen, what mechanisms underlie them, and how outcomes can be explained or anticipated. It motivates exploration, sustains attention, and fuels the search for new knowledge. However, despite consensus on the importance of causal curiosity, little is known about how causal curiosity arises from existing causal belief systems. In our work, we examine how causal curiosity emerges from prior causal belief systems. With Reddit data from 2020 to 2023, and leveraging language models to extract cause-and-effect relationship pairs and identify causal curiosity-driven questions, our findings reveal that concepts that serve more as causes are more likely to appear in causal curiosity than those that serve more as effects. Moreover, our findings indicate that positive nouns are more likely to be the object of causal curiosity. Lastly, our results also suggest that causal curiosity emerges from the central of the prior causal belief network. These novel insights reveal that causal curiosity is not random but systematically grounded in prior knowledge structures, and suggest their implication to facilitate the human learning process by motivating people to actively explore and construct deeper understandings rather than passively receiving information.
items.authors: Shi, Zhuoyu*; Jiang, Xintong; Jiang, Bohan; Morstatter, Fred

items.title: Intersectional inequalities in social ties
items.abstract: Social categories, such as race, gender, or socioeconomic status, are core driving forces of social tie formation. They shape our identities, determining our social behavior and our connection preferences. Homophily, the preference for connecting with similar others, is one of the most common and widely studied interaction patterns; however, the dynamics driving multidimensional connection preferences remain largely unexplored. Furthermore, while homophily in social networks leads to smoother coordination and enhanced trust between individuals, it also reduces diversity in knowledge, exacerbates segregation, and breeds inequality. As complex social beings, we belong to multiple groups, so individuals can experience marginalization in several dimensions simultaneously, suffering intersectional inequalities. In this work, we develop a network model of homo / heterophilic interactions with multidimensional attribute vectors. We use the model to tackle two crucial questions: How do we integrate information from our multidimensional identities to form connections with each other, and how do multidimensional connection preferences impact intersectional inequalities of social capital? To answer the first question, we systematically model group-preference aggregation mechanisms and compare them using Bayesian model selection [1]. We test which aggregation mechanisms best describe empirical data from high school friendship networks [3] and marriages [4], finding that a simple aggregation mechanism consistently outperforms more complex alternatives. By controlling for relative group sizes in the population distribution and for attribute correlation, our model also enables us to measure multidimensional group connection preferences (e.g., of Hispanic girls towards Asian boys). We use the model to operationalize intersectional inequalities of social capital and derive analytical closed-form expressions for the predicted inter-group degree inequalities [2]. We rigorously demonstrate that when attributes are uncorrelated, multidimensional degree disparities mirror one-dimensional systems: majorities gain the advantage in homophilic regimes while minorities benefit in heterophilic ones. Attribute correlation induces counterintuitive patterns of \emph{emergent intersectionality}. For example, individuals belonging to one minority group in a two-dimensional system may be privileged, but those belonging to both minorities simultaneously can be severely disadvantaged. We verify the model's predictions with real-world network data, finding a remarkable alignment.
items.authors: Cartier van Dissel, Mauritz*; Martin-Gutierrez, Samuel; Karimi, Fariba

items.title: Cyberbullying Interaction Networks on Instagram
items.abstract: Cyberbullying continues to grow in prevalence and its impact is felt by thousands worldwide. This study seeks a Network Science perspective on cyberbullying interaction patterns on the popular social media platform, Instagram. Using a human-annotated cyberbullying dataset of 411 Instagram posts and 35,364 comments, we outline a set of heuristics for building Session Graphs, where nodes represent users and their cyberbullying roles, and edges represent their exchanged communications via comments. Over these graphs, we compute the Bully Score, a measure of the net malice introduced by Bullies as they attack Victims (attacks minus pushback), and the Victim Score, a measure of the net support Victims receive from their Defenders (support minus attacks). In conjunction, these scores quantify the balance of power within each post. We find that a majority of posts have negative Victim Scores (attacks outweighing support), while the Bully Score distribution has a slight positive skew (attacks outweighing pushback). Utilizing small subgraph (motif) enumeration, our analysis uncovers the most common interaction patterns over all cyberbullying posts. The most prevalent structure that emerges has Bullies repeatedly attacking the Victims, with some variation introduced through the intensity of the edge weights. The presence of Defenders in high-ranking motifs with light edges reflects quick attempts to counteract bullying. We believe this is the first study to explore granular cyberbullying network interactions using human annotations on Instagram at the post and comment levels. Our findings can help develop proactive moderation techniques to better tackle cyberbullying on social media.
items.authors: Sikdar, Satyaki*; Sandoval, Manuel; Hales, Taylor; Kilroy, Chloe; Juarez, Maddie; Rosario, Tyler; Rosendo, Juan J.; Hall, Deborah L.; Silva, Yasin N.

items.title: Cognitive Expectations of Homophily in Village Social Networks
items.abstract: Homophily, the tendency for individuals to associate with similar others, is a central principle of social organization. Here, we investigate individuals’ expectations of homophily and compare these expectations to actual homophily among 10,072 adults in 82 isolated Honduras villages. We elicited subjects’ beliefs about the existence of social ties between pairs of people within their village-wide social networks. We show that people deploy cognitive heuristics that substantially overestimate the level of homophily, including based on wealth, ethnicity, gender, and religion. We also find that people exploit network structure when predicting ties between others. Understanding cognitive homophily enhances theoretical models of network formation; has implications for interventions aiming to influence social behavior and information diffusion; and has relevance for the maintenance of social inequality.
items.authors: Feltham, Eric*

items.title: The structure and function of antagonistic ties in village social networks
items.abstract: Negative or antagonistic relationships are common in human social networks, but they are less often studied than positive or friendly relationships. The existence of a capacity to have and to track antagonistic ties raises the possibility that they may serve a useful function in human groups. Here, we analyze empirical data gathered from 24,770 and 22,513 individuals in 176 rural villages in Honduras in two survey waves 2.5 y apart in order to evaluate the possible relevance of antagonistic relationships for broader network phenomena. We find that the small-world effect is more significant in a positive world with negative ties compared to an otherwise similar hypothetical positive world without them. Additionally, we observe that nodes with more negative ties tend to be located near network bridges, with lower clustering coefficients, higher betweenness centralities, and shorter average distances to other nodes in the network. Positive connections tend to have a more localized distribution, while negative connections are more globally dispersed within the networks. Analysis of the possible impact of such negative ties on dynamic processes reveals that, remarkably, negative connections can facilitate the dissemination of information (including novel information experimentally introduced into these villages) to the same degree as positive connections, and that they can also play a role in mitigating idea polarization within village networks. Antagonistic ties hold considerable importance in shaping the structure and function of social networks.
items.authors: Ghasemian, Amir*; Christakis, Nicholas

items.title: Simple contagion drives population-scale platform migration
items.abstract: Social media platforms exhibit strong network effects that raise switching costs and entrench incumbents [2]. Theory predicts that costly, identity-laden behaviors like platform migration require complex contagion, meaning social reinforcement from multiple peers before adoption [3,4]. Because network effects stabilize incumbents, mass exits are rarely observed, and the mechanisms driving them remain poorly understood. We study this using the Twitter/X to Bluesky migration, linking 276,431 academics to their profiles among 16.7M Bluesky accounts through a two-stage matching pipeline [1]. Overall, 18% of academics transitioned, ranging from 13% in Medicine to 31% in Arts \& Humanities. Contrary to lock-in predictions, users deeply embedded in Twitter's social graph are more likely to transition, while academic metrics add little predictive power. Political expression also predicts migration. Early reconstruction of one's network on Bluesky predicts sustained engagement, with day-one reconnection associated with over 10% increases in active days, and posting. We establish causal peer influence through two strategies. First, we exploit Brazil's court-ordered suspension of Twitter/X (August 2024) as a natural experiment. Non-Brazilian academics with Brazilian followees experienced sharp increases in transition probability post-ban. Second, we implement dynamic propensity-score matching on 225 pre-treatment covariates [5]. Transition risk doubles within one day of a peer's departure and returns toward baseline within a week. Naive comparisons inflate effects dramatically, with 62 to 93% of apparent clustering reflecting homophily. Among exposed users, 56% of adoptions reflect peer influence and 44% homophily. Training a classifier on synthetic cascades [6], we find simple contagion dominates (64%) while complex contagion is rare (4%). This contradicts theoretical expectations but admits a resolution through multi-homing (being present on multiple platforms). Our results suggest that because users multi-home the trial is nearly costless. Mechanism heterogeneity supports this interpretation. Heavily invested users transition via simple contagion, while peripheral users require reinforcement or external shocks. Our work reveals how multi-homing transforms a collective action problem into a set of independent decisions, suggesting that platform competition is more contestable than theory implies.
items.authors: Quelle, Dorian*; Denker, Frederic; Garg, Prashant; Bovet, Alexandre

items.title: Hypergraph inference from network data via the minimum description length principle
items.abstract: Reconstructing higher-order interactions from network data is important for understanding and predicting the behavior of complex systems, yet in most applications only a static pairwise projection of the underlying structure is available. Currently, many approaches either rely on supervision or side information, or adopt fully Bayesian formulations with Markov chain Monte Carlo sampling, which limits scalability and makes it difficult to deploy such methods on large real-world systems. Here, we present an unsupervised recovery scheme that combines a Minimum Description Length objective with a greedy merge-split algorithm to infer latent hyperedges solely from static pairwise edge data, making the trade-off between model complexity and explanatory power explicit while avoiding MCMC. On synthetic benchmarks that are globally sparse but locally dense, our method achieves high accuracy in recovering higher-order structure.
items.authors: Zang, Yingbang*; Zhang, Yanting; Kirkley, Alec

items.title: Modeling Homophilic Hypergraph Growth Using Edge Copying
items.abstract: In many real-world networked systems, growth depends on both multi-way relationships and homophily (the tendency of individuals to connect with others similar to them). Hypergraphs, and the growth models associated with them, allow for explicit representation of multi-way connections in the evolution of a complex system. We propose a hyperedge-copying growth model for hypergraphs with node labels that parametrizes the level of homophily, extending the hyperedge-copy model for unlabeled networks presented in [1]. The model proceeds by iteratively sampling an edge from the hypergraph, selecting a node within the edge, and making a noisy copy of the sampled edge with some amount of preference (set by the parameters) for nodes with the same label as the selected node. We analyze the degree and edge size distributions of hypergraphs grown using our model, perform dynamical analysis on the mean number of nodes of each label within an edge, and derive and implement a Stochastic Expectation Maximization (SEM) algorithm to infer the model's parameters. We perform experiments on synthetic data to test the ability of the algorithm to recover true parameters and, finally, apply the SEM algorithm to the hypergraph of United States Senate bills. The SEM algorithm performs well on synthetic data, estimating each of the parameters of a 2000-edge hypergraph within 7\% of their true value when the algorithm is run for 2000 iterations. On the Senate data, SEM-estimated parameter value describes patterns in homophily according to political party, in the level of retention of sponsors between bills, and in the presence of brand new sponsors on bills in the senate. Our approach accounts for the influence of two key mechanisms (multi-way relationships and homophily) in the generation of new edges. In doing so, the model allows for more accurate and explainable modeling, prediction, and inference of network growth and homophily. [1] He, Xie, Philip S. Chodrow, and Peter J. Mucha. "Edge correlations and link prediction in growing hypergraphs." Physical Review E 112.2 (2025): 024305.
items.authors: Ross, Violet*; Chodrow, Phil

items.title: Creating multilayer networks with controlled overlap and degree distributions
items.abstract: Prior research has found that non-negligible edge-overlap between the layers of a multiplex can impact the propagation of dynamical processes [1,2]. Yet, the generation of overlapping multiplexes from empirical data is still an open problem. Available methods often involve random edge rewiring [2], without control over the degree distribution, or ensemble generation [1], constraining the overlap only on average. However, non of these strategies are applicable when both layers are required to reproduce degree distributions inferred from empirical data. To this end, we propose a method that, starting from two known degree distributions ($P(k_1)$, $P(k_2)$) and a target level of overlap $\alpha$, generates a biplex satisfying simultaneously all constraints. This is achieved through the combination of a classical configurational model, for layer generation, and a simulated annealing routine that performs random degree-preserving edge-swaps, until $\alpha$ is reached. In contrast with prior approaches, our method ensures that the original degree-sequence of each layer is preserved, making it highly suitable for the creation of realistic multilayer network models. We demonstrated the performance of our algorithm by generating biplexes with different degree distributions in both layers, exploring how their heterogeneity conditions the maximum overlap achievable ($\alpha_{\text{max}}$). We further support this analytically, showing its dependence on each layer's degree distribution and inter-layer degree correlations. Finally, as a validation, we applied our algorithm to generate overlapping networks for epidemic modeling. Based on empirically collected mobility data reflecting in-person and non-physical interactions [3], we built multiple biplexes with different levels of overlap and explored the impact of $\alpha$ on different dynamical processes, like percolation and Behaviour and Disease models (BaD).
items.authors: Fosch, Ariadna*; Traversa, Pietro; Aleta, Alberto; Moreno, Yamir

items.title: A Guide to Higher-Order Homophily
items.abstract: Homophily—the tendency for similar agents to interact—is a foundational mechanism shaping network structure and formation. Rooted in the social sciences [1], homophily has shaped network science broadly, motivating measures like assortativity and modularity, informing stochastic block and latent-space models, and more recently influencing graph machine learning. However, as network data increasingly record higher-order interactions—teams, group chats, collaborations, and collective problem solving—our understanding of homophily beyond pairwise ties remains underdeveloped.
