In higher-order representations (e.g., hypergraphs and simplicial complexes), dyadic homophily neither determines whether groups are attribute-homogeneous nor captures changes in homophily with group size. We organize this review around three questions. First, how should higher-order homophily be measured (Fig. 1(a))? We survey approaches that generalize homophily and related metrics (e.g., modularity) from pairwise networks to the case of hypergraphs and/or simplicial complexes (e.g., [2,3]), as well as novel metrics unique to the higher-order setting (e.g., [4,5]). We clarify what each measure captures, when different measures agree, and when they diverge. Second, how can one generate higher-order networks with homophily (Fig. 1(b))? We review generative models of higher-order networks that incorporate different notions of homophily, including higher-order stochastic block models, generalized configuration models with node attributes, and growth mechanisms with attribute-dependent attachment. Third, how does higher-order homophily affect dynamical processes (Fig. 1(c))? Focusing on spreading and social contagion (e.g., [6,7]), but including other dynamics such as message passing [5], we synthesize evidence that higher-order mixing can shift thresholds and change which interventions are effective. Taken together, we hope this guide provides a common vocabulary, a map of existing methods, and practical recommendations for measuring and modeling homophily in higher-order networks. [1] McPherson et al. Birds of a feather: Homophily in social networks Ann. Rev. Soc. 27:415-444 (2001) [2] Sarker et al. Higher-order homophily on simplicial complexes PNAS 121 (12) e2315931121 (2024) [3] Veldt et al. Combinatorial characterizations and impossibilities for higher-order homophily Sci. Adv. 9 eabq3200 (2023) [4] Telyatnikov et al. Hypergraph neural networks through the lens of message passing TMLR (2025) [5] Kumar et al. Perplexity-homophily index: Homophily through diversity in hypergraphs arXiv:2511.19170 (2025) [6] Laber et al. Effects of higher-order interactions and homophily on information access inequality Comm. Phys. (2025) [7] Rizi et al. Homophily within and across groups Nat. Comm. 16 11351 (2025)
items.authors: Laber, Moritz*; Klein, Brennan

items.title: The Role of Interlayer Directionality in Diffusion and Jamming on Multiplex Networks
items.abstract: Diffusion on multiplex networks exhibits a rich phenomenology arising from the interplay between network topology and interlayer coupling. While previous studies have shown that directionality within layers can induce superdiffusion and the prime regime (optimal coupling), the role of directionality in the interlayer connections themselves has remained largely unexplored. In this work [1], we investigate diffusion dynamics in multiplex networks with directed interlayer couplings. We demonstrate, both numerically and analytically, that interlayer directionality alone, even when all individual layers are undirected, can reproduce a rich diffusion phenomenology including both superdiffusion and the prime regime. We also provide an analytical formulation for the convergence of eigenvalue families at different interlayer diffusivity intensities (e.g. see Fig. 1a). More importantly, we uncover a previously unreported dynamical phase, which we term the jamming regime. In this regime, strong asymmetric interlayer couplings hinder diffusion, dynamically fragmenting the system into effectively disconnected components and preventing convergence to the steady state. We characterize this transition through the spectral properties of the supra-Laplacian, demonstrating that the smallest nonzero eigenvalue $\Lambda_2$ vanishes asymptotically as the interlayer coupling increases ($\Lambda_2 \sim D_X^{-1}). As shown in Figure 1, this phenomenon is attainable in both synthetic toy models (panel b) and real-world systems, such as the London transport network (Panel b). Our results identify interlayer directionality as a fundamental control parameter governing transport, relaxation, and connectivity in multiplex systems, with significant implications for the design and optimization of interconnected infrastructures. [1] Bouchet, Mateo, Alejandro Tejedor, Xiangrong Wang, and Yamir Moreno. Directionality-Induced Jamming in Multiplex Networks. arXiv:2510.22634, 2025. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2510.22634
items.authors: Bouchet, Mateo; Tejedor Cubero, Alejandro*; Wang, Xiangrong; Moreno, Yamir

items.title: Semi-supervised Classification in an Undirected Multilayer Networks with Groupwise Subspace Structure
items.abstract: We study inference in multilayer undirected networks where layers share common latent structure only within unknown groups. Each layer connects the same set of nodes to possibly different sets of target nodes and follows a generalized random dot product graph model. While layers within the same group share a common left latent subspace, all other structural components may vary, and individual layers can be too sparse or heterogeneous for reliable standalone analysis. To address these challenges, we propose a two-stage semi-supervised classification framework. In the first stage, the method automatically identifies a subset of informative layers and clusters them using spectral methods applied to projection-based embeddings. In the second stage, group-level subspace estimates are constructed by aggregating information across clustered layers and are subsequently used to classify the remaining low-signal layers through projection-based similarity scores. We establish non-asymptotic theoretical guarantees showing that, under mild balance and sparsity conditions, the proposed procedure achieves perfect layer classification with high probability and consis- tent recovery of shared latent subspaces. Simulation results demonstrate strong empirical performance and robustness to heterogeneous layer sizes and noise levels. The proposed semi-supervised approach provides a computationally efficient and statistically reliable alternative to fully unsupervised clustering methods and enables principled analysis of multilayer networks in regimes where many layers are individually uninformative.,
items.authors: Toleugazin, Abdulla*

items.title: Disentangling Communities and Factions in Signed Networks through Gremban Expansions
items.abstract: Signed networks capture both cooperative and antagonistic interactions in complex systems: friendships and rivalries in social networks, activations and inhibitions in biology, or positive and negative correlations in finance. A central challenge is to identify and classify mesoscale structures in these networks. In particular, communities are groups of nodes with dense internal connectivity, while factions are characterized by predominantly positive ties within and negative ties across groups. Standard tools from network science rarely separate these two forms of organization, leading to ambiguous results. We introduce a new approach based on the Gremban expansion, a transformation that maps a signed network into a larger unsigned one without losing information. This expanded network possesses a natural symmetry that makes it possible to apply well-established spectral clustering techniques while still accounting for the role of negative ties. Our main result is a rigorous way to distinguish between communities and factions: symmetric eigenvectors of the expanded Laplacian correspond to community partitions, while antisymmetric ones reveal factional divisions. This provides the first method that jointly captures and separates both structures in a principled way. We test our framework on synthetic networks generated with stochastic block models, showing that the Gremban approach successfully identifies the different mesoscale structures across regimes where communities, factions, or mixtures of both prevail. The method also generalizes naturally to multi-group settings and connects to dynamical processes on networks, highlighting its potential for broad applications. Beyond offering a mathematically robust foundation, the framework is simple to implement, interpretable, and applicable to diverse empirical systems where cooperation and conflict coexist.
items.authors: Diaz-Diaz, Fernando*; Devriendt, Karel; Lambiotte, Renaud

items.title: Community Detection in Psychometric Subject Networks
items.abstract: Psychometrics studies psychological constructs through questionnaire data. Traditional factor analysis describes responses via latent variables representing common causes (e.g., personality traits or pathological symptoms). Network psychometrics offers an alternative paradigm, modeling items or symptoms as interacting nodes in a network. Within this paradigm, questionnaire datasets can be viewed as bipartite networks, projectable onto either item or subject space. While item-space networks have been extensively studied [1], the subject space remains largely unexplored, particularly regarding community structure that could reveal clinically or theoretically meaningful subgroups. We here propose and compare a set of modularity-based approaches for community detection in subject networks, differing in their choice of null model and adjacency matrix type. We construct subject similarity networks using two complementary measures: (i) a positive weighted similarity metric based on item-wise distance, ranging from 0 to 1, and (ii) cosine similarity of response vectors, yielding signed weights in [-1,1]. For the positive similarity matrix, we maximise Newman modularity on a binarized network, hence using the configuration model as a null model. For the cosine similarity, we consider various possible null models that, based on random matrix theory, filter noise-consistent eigenvalues, extending the Garlaschelli-MacMahon framework[2] on correlation networks. In both cases, maximising the modularity amounts to finding the ground state of a Potts model with disordered couplings, that we sample via simulated annealing. The whole procedure eventually provides us with a significance assessment of the obtained communities. We test these methods on psychometric datasets including the Big Five personality inventory, DASS-42 depression-anxiety-stress scale, and Empathy Quotient. Preliminary results show that all the approaches consistently detect similar well-defined community structures, identifying a characteristic number of communities for each dataset. This work provides methodological tools for exploring population heterogeneity in psychometric data through subject-space network analysis.
items.authors: Armanetti, Arianna*; Ibáñez-Berganza, Miguel; Garlaschelli, Diego

items.title: Community Detection for Directed Graphs Reveals Interdisciplinarity in Computer Science
items.abstract: Network modeling can be applied to many real-world data and phenomena. One important application is the discovery of community structure: sets of nodes that are more densely connected to one another than to the rest of the graph [1]. However, conventional community detection does not fully fully account for the information contained in directed graphs. To address this shortcoming, we recently introduced bicommunities, which define a community in terms of its sending and receiving parts [2]. We developed a new spectral framework tailored to directed graphs, which reverts to edge clustering in an embedding space. Here, we apply the new framework to the Wiki-cs dataset [3], which is often used as a benchmark for machine learning architectures. The Wiki-cs network captures directed links between Wikipedia pages in computer science (CS) that have been classified into 10 subcategories. We first investigate the directed communities and assess how the ground truth categories are represented. Second, we evaluate the specificity of bicommunities for these classes and compare the retrieved patterns with those from randomized edge clusters. Bicommunities of Wiki-cs exploit directed edge information within the network, hinting at a hierarchical and interdisciplinary organization of the CS subfields (Fig. 1.B). The unsupervised detection of bicommunity captures a meaningful representation with respect to the labeling of the nodes (Fig. 1.C). Using Wiki-cs as a case study, we demonstrate that bicommunity detection effectively highlights the directed organization of networks. The perspective of bimodularity extends the analytical toolkit of network science. [1] Newman, Mark EJ. ”Modularity and community structure in networks.” Proceedings of the national academy of sciences 103, no. 23 (2006). [2] Cionca, Alexandre, Chun Hei Michael Chan, and Dimitri Van De Ville. ”Community detection for directed networks revisited using bimodularity.” Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 122, no. 35 (2025). [3] Mernyei, P´eter, and C˘at˘alina Cangea. ”Wiki-cs: A wikipedia-based benchmark for graph neural networks.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2007.02901 (2020).
items.authors: Cionca, Alexandre*; Chan, Chun Hei Michael; Van De Ville, Dimitri

items.title: Community Detection Beyond Network Structure
items.abstract: Community detection is a central problem in network science and is traditionally approached through explicit connectivity patterns. However, coordination does not always manifest as dense structure, and groups may act in a coordinated manner even when observable networks remain sparse. In such regimes, topology alone provides limited information for identifying communities. Here, we show that latent community structure can be inferred from bursty dynamics, without relying on observed network connections. In particular, we demonstrate that members of the same latent community exhibit similar inter-event time distributions, extending earlier results on the informativeness of bursty temporal fingerprints [1]. This alignment persists even when explicit connectivity is weak or absent. Building on this observation, we introduce a community-detection framework based on bursty inter-event time dynamics. We evaluate the framework both within single platforms, where explicit structure may be available, and across platforms, where explicit links are absent by definition. In both settings, over four distinct datasets, our bursty framework outperforms structure-based community detection and temporal embedding baselines, including time-series foundation models. To explain the origin of similar bursty patterns arising among community members, we introduce a generative model. The latent community structure is represented by a weighted stochastic block model $A_w \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$. At each iteration, a source node $v_{source}$ is chosen with probability proportional to $A_w \cdot z$, capturing how a member's recent activity stimulates other community members into action. $z[v_{source}]$ holds the time since $v_{source}$ last activity. A target node $ v_{target} $ is chosen according to: \[ P(v_{target}) \propto \lambda A_w \cdot d + (1 - \lambda) A_{rand} \cdot d \] following [2]. $\lambda \in [0,1]$ controls the strength of community-based ($A_w$) vs. random selection ($A_{rand}$). Preferential attachment is applied to favor nodes with higher existing degree ($d$). The model reproduces the observed alignment of inter-event time distributions within communities, an effect that is not captured by former individual-level queuing-based models [3]. The model also predicts a detectability transition. Namely, unless latent communities induce highly dense observable sub-networks ($~70\%$ intra-community connectivity), bursty inter-event time statistics enable more accurate community detection compared to structure-based methods and temporal embedding models. Simulations reproduce this transition and are consistent with the empirical results. Together, these findings identify bursty dynamics as a signature of community structure in regimes where topology alone is insufficient.
items.authors: Somin, Shahar*; Pentland, Alex ; Cohen, Tom; Kepner, Jeremy

items.title: Detectability threshold in weighted modular networks
items.abstract: We study the necessary condition to detect, by means of spectral modularity optimization, the ground-truth partition in networks generated according to the weighted planted-partition model with two equally sized communities. We analytically derive a general expression for the maximum level of mixing tolerated by the algorithm to retrieve community structure, showing that the value of this detectability threshold depends on the first two moments of the distributions of node degree and edge weight. We focus on the standard case of Poisson-distributed node degrees and compare the detectability thresholds of five edge-weight distributions: Dirac, Poisson, exponential, geometric, and signed Bernoulli. We show that Dirac distributed weights yield the smallest detectability threshold, while exponentially distributed weights increase the threshold by a factor 2‾√, with other distributions exhibiting distinct behaviors that depend, either or both, on the average values of the degree and weight distributions. Our results indicate that larger variability in edge weights can make communities less detectable. In cases where edge weights carry no information about community structure, incorporating weights in community detection is detrimental.
items.authors: Radicchi, Filippo; N. Silva, Filipi ; Flammini, Alessandro ; Fortunato, Santo; Kojaku, Sadamori*

items.title: NetPair: Structural motifs for physical network distance
items.abstract: Network motifs are commonly used to find repeating patterns within a network; however, in some fields, topology alone provides inadequate granularity to distinguish patterns. One of these such fields is Population-based Structural Health Monitoring (PBSHM) [1,2]. The aspiration of PBSHM is to acquire greater knowledge on the health of an individual structure by accumulating data across a range of similar structures. The belief is that by taking learned knowledge from one structure, this can be transferred [3] to another similar structure and improve the overall knowledge. To establish which structures are similar, a unified language - an Irreducible Element model - is first used to describe the geometrical, material, contextual, and interactional properties of the structure in graph form. Comparisons are then drawn between these representations in a network space where the links between the structures are a weight of how similar the associated structures are. Despite there being numerous network based similarity measures, they often view attributes as second-class data. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes a new algorithm which promotes attributes to first-class data and facilitates partial attributing matching in motifs; NetPair. The NetPair algorithm is evaluated against the Population-based SHM Engineered Asset Resource (PEAR) dataset.
items.authors: Brennan, Daniel*; Rogers, Timothy; Worden, Keith; Klein, Brennan

items.title: Network Resilience Theory of Aging
items.abstract: Two major theories compete to explain the origin of aging. The first, proposed in 1959 by Leo Szilard, attributes aging to the accumulation of DNA damage. The second, articulated by Robin Holliday in the 1980s, emphasizes the role of epigenetic alterations. While they reveal potential molecular origins of aging, both theories leave important puzzles unresolved. First, mutation and epimutation burdens increase linearly with age. In contrast, aging phenotypes follow markedly nonlinear trajectories: mortality risk rises exponentially with age (Gompertz law), and the onset of age-related diseases increases nonlinearly with age. Second, key aging phenotypes such as cell-to-cell variability, inflammaging, and immunosenescence cannot be linked to specific genetic or epigenetic alterations. Instead, they appear as collective phenomena emerging from the cumulative impact of these alterations on cellular function. Here, we present a network resilience theory of aging to resolve these puzzles. Network resilience is formalized as the ability of a network to sustain its basic functions under changes in its topology (mutations) and dynamical variables (epigenetics). Our theory conceptualizes aging as the progressive loss of network resilience as cells approach a novel critical mutation-epigenetic line. We characterize the changes in cellular susceptibility over time, identifying two fundamental regimes of cellular stability. Away from the critical line (young age), cells remain stable despite the accumulation of mutations and epimutations. In contrast, cells approaching the critical line (old age) exhibit a marked nonlinear increase in cellular susceptibility. Using both GTEx data and numerical simulations, we formalize a fluctuation–dissipation relation linking increased transcriptional noise to elevated cellular susceptibility with age. We also predict a nonlinear temporal delay in gene regulatory network (GRN) dynamics as the cell approaches the critical line, analogous to critical slowing down in statistical physics. Simulations of the mouse lung GRN reproduce this behavior and match experimental observations of delayed immune activation in aged mice. Our framework also predicts cellular lifespan and rejuvenation potential across different tissues and links the accumulation of mutations and epimutations to aging phenotypes through resilience and critical phenomena, offering a unified perspective on the aging process.
items.authors: Gross, Bnaya*; Barabási, Albert-László

items.title: Asynchronous Boolean Network Modeling of the Neuro–Immune System to uncover Autoimmune Disease Emergence
items.abstract: Autoimmune diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis (RA), systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), multiple sclerosis (MS), and type-1 diabetes (T1D) arise from dysregulation of neuro–immune–endocrine (NEI) homeostasis. We model this system using a curated Boolean network comprising immune cells, cytokines, hormones, tissues, and glands, and study how perturbations drive transitions between healthy and disease states. While prior work has examined autoimmune processes at the genomic level or within isolated immune subsystems, our approach integrates heterogeneous biological components into a unified dynamical framework. Pairwise regulatory interactions were extracted through extensive literature mining using LLM-assisted prompting and validated against established biological interaction databases. We develop an asynchronous Boolean network framework to explore disease-reachable dynamical regimes in a NEI regulatory network consisting of 47 nodes and 214 directed edges encoding activating and inhibitory interactions [1,3]. Nodes integrate upstream signals using biologically motivated aggregation rules, including AND, OR, activation- dominant, inhibition-dominant, and majority-vote logic, enabling the representation of diverse regulatory mechanisms observed across immune and endocrine pathways [2, 4]. Network dynamics are simulated using asynchronous updates, which better capture stochasticity and heterogeneous signaling timescales in biological systems. Disease phenotypes are defined by canonical cytokine activation patterns associated with RA, SLE, MS, and T1D. Through systematic perturbation of healthy initial conditions, we identify minimal sets of node activations that repro- ducibly drive the system into disease-specific dynamical states, demonstrating that autoimmune phenotypes are emergent and mechanistically grounded in network structure. Re-simulating full asynchronous trajectories reveals intermediate components that mediate the propagation and stabilization of disease signals. These intermediates, often distinct from disease markers, represent key control points for intervention. Together, this perturbation-to-disease mapping uncov- ers causal pathways underlying autoimmune emergence and provides a principled foundation for identifying therapeutic strategies that disrupt disease trajectories upstream of irreversible pathological states.
items.authors: Urmi, Tamanna*

items.title: Assessment of methods for network analysis of single-time-point microbial samples
items.abstract: Harnessing information from a single-time-point microbial sample holds transformative potential for advancing personalized medicine. While the microbiome ecosystem is shaped by inter-species interactions, traditional methods for single-time-point analysis mainly focus on the abundance of the individual species rather than their interrelationships. Recently, two novel approaches have been proposed, that, instead of assessing abundance of the individual species, focus on the inter-species relationships in the test sample, the `network-impact' (NI) approach and the individualized dissimilarity-overlap analysis (IDOA). The NI is a measure of the contribution of the test sample to the calculated inter-species correlations in a `bottom-up' approach, while the IDOA analyzes the relationships between species assemblages and their abundances in a `top-down' approach. In this research, we comprehensively evaluate and compare the traditional and the new approaches. Employing the Generalized Lotka-Volterra (GLV) model, we create synthetic samples to rigorously test the classification capabilities of each measure in both supervised and semi-supervised setups. Our findings reveal that when the species of the test sample share similar self-dynamics as the reference cohort but distinct inter-species interactions, they typically can not be classified by conventional dissimilarity-based measures. In contrast, IDOA and NI parameters emerge as successful tools in assessing microbial relationships within a single-time-point sample, while a neural network is highly dependent on the training set size. This study makes two key contributions: First, it provides a thorough performance comparison of methods aimed at analyzing microbial interactions from a single sample. Second, it contrasts two approaches for analyzing complex microbial systems: `bottom-up' approach that starts with the detailed structure of the interaction network and `top-down' approach that avoids the details and analyzes the system as a whole. It underscores the potential of extracting information from the intricate inter-species relationships based on individual microbial snapshots, paving the way for improving microbiome-based personalized medicine.
items.authors: Calinsky, Yehonatan; Goldental, Meital Mili; Bashan, Amir*

items.title: Challenges and Opportunities in Single-Sample Network Modeling
items.abstract: Analysis of biological networks can provide unprecedented insight into the mechanisms underlying disease. Although many methods have been developed to estimate biological networks, these approaches typically use multiple experimental samples to estimate a single overall network which does not capture population-level heterogeneity. To overcome this limitation, several methods have been proposed to infer single-sample networks, i.e. one network for each sample in an input data set. However, despite their common goal, the mathematics of these methods has been formulated differently, with different variable names used to represent similar concepts. To address this issue, we re-cast the mathematics of several common single-sample network methods, including LIONESS, SSN, SWEET, and BONOBO, using common variables. We then systematically explore the parameters, caveats, and underlying assumptions made by each method and examine how these differences impact single-sample network prediction. Our results demonstrate the importance of developing an intuition for these methods grounded in their mathematical formulation. For example, both SWEET and BONOBO include "other" scale factors that strongly impact edge weights in the predicted single-sample networks, driving them toward the "background" network. Our analysis also points to a critical trade-off when trying to simultaneously predict network edges that are both shared across samples as well as those that are specific to a given sample. In particular, we found that BONOBO and SWEET consistently estimate the most accurate but least specific networks, while SSN estimates the most specific but least accurate networks. LIONESS straddles these domains, with an accuracy almost as high as SWEET and BONOBO and a specificity almost as high as SSN. In other words, although methods for which the predicted single-sample networks are highly similar to the "background" network tend to have the highest accuracy, this often comes at the cost of significantly lower specificity. This is important since the primary goal of sample-specific network modeling is to obtain networks that are specific to each input sample. Overall, our analyses highlight some of the broader challenges in single-sample network inference, but also point to important methodological synergies. Code and resources supporting this work can be found at: https://github.com/kimberlyglass/single-sample-networks.
items.authors: Glass, Kimberly*

items.title: Minimal model of spatial growth approaches biological multi-objective optimality
items.abstract: Many biological networks grow by elongation of filaments that can branch and fuse---typical examples include fungal mycelium or slime mold. These networks must simultaneously perform multiple tasks such as transport, exploration, and robustness under finite resources. Yet, how such multi-task architectures emerge from local growth processes remains poorly understood. Here, we introduce a minimal model of spatial network morphogenesis based solely on stochastic branching, fusion, and stopping, during elongation. Despite the absence of global optimization or feedback, the model generates a broad morphospace from tree-like, to loopy, as well as hybrid architectures. By quantifying multiple functional objectives, we show that (i) these synthetic structures occupy similar regions of performance space than evolved empirical fungal networks, and (ii) that their Pareto front of optimal trade-offs lies close to that of these same fungal networks. Our results show that biological architectures approaching multi-objective optimality can arise from simple local growth rules, and identify branching and fusion as fundamental ingredients shaping the architecture of living transport networks.
items.authors: Lucas, Maxime*; Bisot, Corentin; Petri, Giovanni; Declerck, Declerck; Carletti, Timoteo

items.title: Subject-specific networks reveal disease-specific regulatory changes
items.abstract: Most biological network research efforts in humans focus on generating and analyzing population-level network models. However, these models struggle to capture the intrinsic variability between individual humans. In this project, we capture subject-specific contributions to network structures using LIONESS[1], and combine with the message-passing algorithm PUMA[2] to estimate subject-specific miRNA-mRNA regulatory networks (Figure 1A). Using a similar pipeline, Gentili, 2025[3] previously identified differential regulatory network patterns between chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and control subjects based on data collected from whole blood. We performed a complementary analysis using a lung tissue data set containing individuals with COPD and idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF), another lung disease which shares many common clinical features and causes with COPD. In this study, we further expand the analysis to explore differences in the community structure between COPD and IPF subject networks. Linear regression on the edge-weights in subject-specific networks as a function of subject diagnoses identifies edges strongly associated with either COPD or IPF. These identified edges connected to several well known COPD and IPF associated regulators and genes and formed core networks that vary greatly between healthy, COPD, and IPF subjects. We additionally used a bipartite network community detection algorithm (CONDOR[4]) to identify communities in each subject-specific network and used these communities to identify structural commonalities across the subjects. We found that certain structural motifs are more commonly shared among diseased subjects compared to control subjects (Figure 1B). These findings show the importance of generating subject-specific network models and of systematically evaluating the structure of these networks.
items.authors: Dalldorf, Christopher*

items.title: Money does not buy time: Time-richness predicts ability to access and allocate time across urban life
items.abstract: Urban residents inhabit the same physical city yet experience profoundly different opportunity structures. Traditional social science theory attributes these disparities to income-based access to resources and amenities [1]. However, we uncover an important empirical puzzle: individuals with comparable incomes utilize the same amenities at systematically different times of day. These temporal divergences are poorly explained by socioeconomic status alone. We argue that this reflects a deeper, unmeasured dimension of inequality, time-richness, capturing individuals’ capacity to flexibly structure and allocate their time across the day. While social scientists have long recognized that the unequal distribution of time generates downstream forms of inequality [2], prevailing approaches operationalize time through coarse aggregates such as discretionary hours after work. Such measures cannot explain why some individuals can reconfigure their daily rhythms (e.g., visiting parks in the early afternoon) while others cannot. To address this gap, we introduce a mobility-derived measure of time-richness based on two orthogonal components extracted from large-scale smartphone geolocation data: self-similarity, capturing how predictable an individual’s daily movement patterns are, and peer-similarity, capturing how synchronized those patterns are with one’s socioeconomic peers. Using smartphone geolocation data across 11 U.S. metropolitan areas, we show that these dimensions of time-richness are statistically orthogonal to income. Further, incorporating time-richness into behavioral models substantially improves our ability to predict when and where individuals engage in discretionary activities. Most critically, we demonstrate that time-richness explains variation in experienced segregation far better than income or traditional measures of discretionary time. Our findings reveal that time constitutes an independent axis of inequality, shaping how individuals access the social and spatial dimensions of the city. [1] Chetty, R., Hendren, N., Kline, P., & Saez, E. (2014). Where is the land of Opportunity? The Geography of Intergenerational Mobility in the United States. The Quarterly Journal of Economics, 129(4), 1553–1623. https://doi.org/10.1093/qje/qju022 [2] Vickery, C. (1977). The Time-Poor: A New Look at Poverty. The Journal of Human Resources, 12(1), 27–48. https://doi.org/10.2307/145597
items.authors: Rosen, Joshua*; Weis, Erik; Gibbs, Hamish; Sanchez, Sarah; Yabe, Takahiro; Klein, Brennan; O'Brien, Daniel; Moro, Esteban

items.title: Daily mobility contributes to urban heat exposure inequalities across income and age groups
items.abstract: Segregation has long been recognized as a driver of environmental inequalities, concentrating disadvantaged groups in hotter neighborhoods. Yet the interplay between daily mobility patterns and intra-urban temperature variability remains poorly understood. Using anonymized mobile phone records and high-resolution temperature fields for 23 major Spanish cities, we quantify how residents from different income and age groups experience heat through their daily movements. We adopt a network-based framework where nodes represent districts and weighted directed edges represent trips between them. Each node is classified by its average temperature using deciles, allowing us to study how groups distribute their trips across the urban temperature field. We find robust and systematic disparities: income-related exposure inequalities are substantially larger in magnitude, while age-related inequalities are smaller but highly consistent in direction across cities. To assess whether widely used population-based mobility models can capture these inequalities, we evaluate the gravity and radiation models. The radiation model reproduces most of the observed income-exposure gap, whereas the gravity model systematically underestimates it, showing that a large share of mobility-driven inequality can be explained by the spatial distribution of people and opportunities alone. Although more complex models could improve predictive accuracy, they would lose the generality and interpretability — key advantages of parsimonious mobility models. Overall, our results reveal systematic mobility-driven exposure inequalities within cities and highlight both the potential and the limitations of parsimonious mobility models for characterizing socio-environmental risk.
items.authors: Duran Sala, Marc*

items.title: Beyond Proximity: Rethinking Accessibility Through Human Mobility Patterns in US Cities
items.abstract: Traditional measures of urban accessibility rely on proximity to home, either in distance or travel time. Planning paradigms such as the "15-minute city" assume that nearby access ensures use. However, empirical evidence suggests otherwise: only 14% of daily consumption trips in the U.S. occur within a 15-minute walk from home, and residents of large U.S. cities travel a median of 6.7 km to consume fast food. These patterns indicate that people organize daily necessities across multiple urban locations, not just around their homes. We analyze individual-level mobility data from 1.86 million anonymized smartphones across 11 U.S. metropolitan areas to examine how people access daily necessities and which locations anchor their routines. We identify visits to daily-necessity points of interest (POIs)—Banks, Coffee Shops, Grocery Stores, Gyms, Laundries, Pharmacies, Post Offices, and Salons—using Foursquare data. For each individual, we define "offers" as the k-nearest POIs (k=10) around specific frequently visited locations, called containers. Containers are ranked by the total time spent there, with home always defined as the primary container. We introduce the offer-meets-demand ratio, phi^n, which measures the fraction of an individual’s visits to POIs that fall within the combined offers of their first n containers. Although the median distance from home to the nearest POI is only 700 meters, the median distance traveled to visit a POI is about 7 km, showing that people often bypass nearby options. Consistent with this, phi^1 (offers around home only) reveals three groups: individuals who exclusively use nearby POIs, those who never do, and many in between. Adding the second container—the most time-consuming location outside home—substantially increases phi^n for many individuals, indicating that daily necessities are often accessed near other routine locations. As more containers are included, the share of individuals with phi^n = 1 increases, though at different rates across POI categories. Importantly, locations serving as second containers tend to cluster in areas with high job density, highlighting workplaces as key anchors for accessing daily necessities beyond home. Overall, our findings reveal a dual nature of accessibility shaped by both residential and routine activity locations. Accessibility measures based solely on home location miss this behavioral dimension. Identifying where routine containers cluster can inform more effective and equitable placement of services and policy interventions, moving toward accessibility metrics grounded in how people actually use cities.
items.authors: Kulkarni, Saumitra*; Moro, Esteban

items.title: The Parenthood Effect in Urban Mobility
items.abstract: We investigate how parenthood and marriage (two major life events) reshape urban mobility patterns, an aspect overlooked in traditional `average citizen' mobility models. Leveraging US census data, we analyse whether these life transitions create distinct urban experiences. Parenthood introduces new priorities including caregiving responsibilities, work-life balance adjustments, and access to family-friendly environments. Similarly, marriage introduces new dynamics including shared household decision-making, potential dual-income benefits, combined residential preferences, and shifts in social networks and lifestyle patterns. Our analysis demonstrates that cities vary significantly in how mobility can be accommodated by different household arrangements: some better accommodate either single individuals (Houston, Virginia Beach) or married people (Atlanta, Baltimore), whereas others favour parents (Cincinnati, Chicago). This classification becomes increasingly relevant for individuals and families as remote work expands relocation possibilities. We find that parents and married individuals face different mobility costs and amenity access patterns compared to their counterparts, with variations consistent across multiple null model tests. This research advances urban planning discourse by advocating for tailored design strategies addressing diverse demographic needs rather than one-size-fits-all approaches.
items.authors: Macedo, Mariana*; Menezes, Ronaldo; Cardillo, Alessio

items.title: Quantifying the Human Mobility Shift with Remote Work
items.abstract: The widespread adoption of remote work has profoundly reshaped mobility patterns following the COVID-19 pandemic, yet there existed limited quantitative results for these mobility changes. In this study, we analyze large-scale mobility trajectories from over seven million individuals across the United States and develop an activity-based–inspired model to provide a comprehensive understanding of mobility changes induced by remote work [1]. Based on large-scale mobility data in 2019 (pre-remote work) and 2022 (post-remote work), we find people have reduced traveling distance, extended stay duration at home, and higher flexibility regarding home departure time. The average radius of gyration was approximately 5.8 km in 2019 and decreased to about 5.5 km in 2022, reflecting the overall reduced need for daily commuting. Besides, we noticed an increase in the duration of time people spent in their home census block groups (see Figure 1b). The overall mobility shift comes from hybrid and remote workers in 2022. Based on weekly office visits, we classified workers into three categories: remote, hybrid and on-site. As shown in Fig. 1a, weekly travel distances for hybrid and remote workers are reduced by 22.3% and 59.6%, respectively, compared to on-site workers. Remote workers also exhibit greater temporal flexibility, with home departure times more evenly distributed throughout the day, in contrast to the pronounced peak hours observed for on-site workers (Fig. 1c). Motivated by these observations, we introduce a statistical-physics framework for daily human mobility in which complete daily activity sequences are treated as microstates weighted by a day-level energy (see Fig. 1d). The energy rewards activity durations (Home, Work, Other) and penalizes travel costs between successive activities, while the configuration space is restricted by simple behavioral constraints such as a minimum sleep duration. Remote workers work from home with zero commuting cost, resulting in feasible sets of daily sequences that differ fundamentally from those of on-site workers. This framework accounts for key mobility changes associated with remote work through feasibility constraints rather than explicit behavioral optimization. [1] J.L Bowman, M.E Ben-Akiva, Transportation Research Part A: Policy and Practice, Volume 35, Issue 1, 2001.
items.authors: Gu, Francois*; Zhao, Pei

items.title: Exploring the Relationship Between Human Mobility and Inter-Firm Economic Networks
items.abstract: Recent urban planning policies in Japan stress the need to create people-centered public spaces that foster social interaction among diverse agents and support productive economic activities. However, it remains unclear how everyday encounters in cities translate into formation of local economic networks. Previous studies have revealed how urban environments shape opportunities for physical encounters and generate patterns of segregation by using large-scale mobility data. In parallel, recent research has highlighted the critical role of face-to-face interactions in driving knowledge spillovers and economic productivity. Together, these findings suggest physical encounters may reflect the underlying social interactions patterns through which cities generate economic value. Focusing on the Tokyo metropolitan area, we leverage mobility data and inter-firm transaction records to examine how mobility-driven social interaction relates to economic link formation. Using an anonymized mobility data set of 2.68 million mobile phone users and geolocated information for 196,317 firms, we construct meeting probabilities between firms by aggregating the physical encounters of their workers across space and time. We then empirically test the relationship between the worker mobility and inter-firm transaction network, which records annual buyer–supplier relationships among firms in Tokyo. Cross-firm meeting probabilities exhibit only a weak distance-decay pattern in Tokyo, suggesting that physical distance imposes limited constraints on workers’ encounters in dense metropolitan areas. We model inter-firm transactions with a linear regression model with meeting probability, physical distance, firm size, and transaction volume as covariates. The results show that higher meeting probabilities are associated with a greater likelihood of inter-firm economic ties. These findings underscore the role of everyday social interactions in shaping urban economic connectivity and provide insights for planning cities that facilitate productive economic linkages.
items.authors: Chen, Ketian; Yabe, Takahiro*

items.title: Enhancing structural resilience in healthcare through patient flow network
items.abstract: Large-scale disasters, such as pandemics and climate-related events, test the resilience of healthcare systems, that is, their ability to ensure patient care under extreme strain. Most existing studies measure resilience using broad, system-wide outcomes, but often overlook how coordination between regions helps manage stress and keep care accessible. In this study, we analyzed billions of electronic medical records to construct cross-regional patient flow networks in the U.S., mapping how patients moved across healthcare facilities. During the COVID-19 pandemic, we observed that cross-regional flow rose to 4.59\%, compared to pre-pandemic level of 3.53\%. This redistribution absorbed, on average, 63\% of the excess stress on facilities, meaning nearly two-thirds of surging demand was handled by shifting patients to less burdened regions, an absolute 32 percentage point improvement from the pre-pandemic baseline of 31\%. Further analysis suggests that strengthening cross-regional coordination could allow the healthcare system to absorb even more stress, reduce clinicians' demand, and reduce excess deaths. These findings show that structural strategies, like reinforcing patient flow between regions, can substantially enhance resilience, providing critical insights for future pandemic preparedness and disaster response planning, and ultimately improving patient care.
items.authors: Zhong, Lu*

items.title: Inferring Transient Behavior Changes During the 2022 Mpox Outbreak Using Adaptive Stochastic Temporal Networks
items.abstract: Mathematical modeling of the spread of infectious diseases on temporal networks has gained popularity in complex systems science to understand the intricate interplay between social dynamics and epidemics. In these models, the spread of the pathogen and the dynamics of the contact networks are described by stochastic processes. The spreading process depends on the state of the network, since transmission is only possible between connected agents. Importantly, the evolution of the network also depends on the state of the infection process. For example, diagnosed agents may isolate themselves and meet fewer people until they recover. Similarly, a critical number of infected agents may induce risk-averse behavior in susceptible agents. It was hypothesized that exactly this mechanism shaped the epidemic curve of mpox clade IIb in non-endemic regions during the summer of 2022. Mpox denotes a viral zoonosis caused by the Orthopoxvirus monkeypox (MPXV), which is endemic in West and Central Africa. In spring 2022, notable outbreaks of MPXV clade IIb were recorded around the world, predominantly affecting men who have sex with men (MSM). Within the same year, the outbreak significantly declined, and it is unclear which factors contributed to this rapid decrease [1,2]. To investigate the concomitant effects of sexual contact networks, transient contact reductions and the effect of infection- vs. vaccine-derived immunity on the 2022 mpox outbreak in Berlin, we calibrated an adaptive temporal network model with epidemic, vaccination, contact- and behavioral data [3]. The simulation of the model was done using high acceptance sampling [4], a technique especially tailored to efficiently sample pathogen spread on stochastic temporal network that adaptively evolve with the pathogen. The method is multiple orders of magnitude faster than the Gillespie algorithm, while it maintains to be exact. Our results indicate that transient behavior changes played a major role in the decline of mpox cases in Berlin (see Figure 1). Mpox shredding kinetics are relatively fast compared to the speed of the contact network (or other sexually transmissible diseases) and hence, people with many contacts are most at risk of mpox infection. This group at risk either got vaccinated, infected or transiently changed their behavior and hence were not at highest risk for infection. Our findings, supported by serological studies conducted in Berlin, highlight that, in addition to vaccination, timely and clear communication of transmission routes may trigger spontaneous protective behavior within key populations; underscoring the importance of targeted sexual health education as a core component of outbreak response.
items.authors: Gubela, Nils*

items.title: Sic Transit Imperium: A Metapopulation-Network Reconstruction of the Antonine Plague
items.abstract: In the winter of 165 AD, during the Parthian Wars, a group of Roman legionaries stormed the sacred temple of Seleucia. Their impiety did not go unanswered: they carried away not treasure, but a curse of a deadly disease. From that moment, according to ancient Roman lore, began the chain of contagion we now call the Antonine Plague, an epidemic that swept across the Roman Empire for more than fifteen years, claiming numerous lives, including the co-emperor Lucius Verus [1]. Despite its vast impact, the plague remains shrouded in mystery. We still lack molecular evidence of its causative agent, and much of its natural history continues to elude us. Previous studies have used mathematical epidemic models to reconstruct the plague’s dynamics and to infer likely causative agents, comparing alternative model hypotheses against the limited evidence available on its impact [2]. However, these studies have typically relied on single-population approaches, overlooking the spatial spread of infection along the Empire’s travel routes. As a result, comparisons are often limited to totals of cases and deaths, rather than leveraging the spatio-temporal timeline of the epidemic across the Empire as an additional clue to its origin and underlying characteristics. In this work, we aim to close this gap by using a metapopulation network model to reconstruct the spread of the Antonine Plague. We draw on ORBIS data, which provide detailed, multimodal travel routes between major urban centres of the Roman Empire in the 2nd century [3]. After data cleaning, our mobility network comprises 677 nodes and 1104 edges. We simulate mobility flows using a gravity model that incorporates travel costs between nodes, calibrated to historical sources. We then couple this mobility layer to a compartmental epidemic model to simulate disease spread. Preliminary results suggest that a model parameterised on modern smallpox, when run on the reconstructed network, generates dynamics compatible with reported historical timelines—both the estimated time to reach Rome from the spillover site and the subsequent epidemic waves described in contemporary accounts. A representative simulation is shown in Fig. 1, illustrating the spatio-temporal diffusion of the epidemic across the ORBIS mobility network, with early transmission along major travel corridors and a progression consistent with historically attested spread patterns. Additionally, we extend the modeling framework to evaluate alternative transmission models, test competing hypotheses on the causative agent (e.g., measles and bubonic plague), and conduct extensive sensitivity analyses to assess the robustness of our findings. In conclusion, our work shows how computational, network-based epidemic models can support the exploration ancient pandemics by unifying fragmentary historical evidence into quantitative, testable hypotheses about their spread and underlying drivers. [1] Duncan-Jones, Richard P. "The impact of the Antonine plague." Journal of Roman Archaeology 9 (1996) [2] Karasaridis, Anestis, and Aleš Chalupa. "Comparative SIR/SEIR modeling of the Antonine Plague in Rome." PloS one 20.2 (2025) [3] Scheidel, Walter. "ORBIS: The Stanford geospatial network model of the Roman world." (2015)
items.authors: Riso, Biagio; Chinazzi, Matteo; Davis, Jessica; Urbinati, Alessandra; Gozzi, Nicolò*; Vespignani, Alessandro

items.title: Inferring spatial transmission matrices from Austrian wastewater sequence data
items.abstract: Comprehensive wastewater surveillance programs enable continuous monitoring of the geographic distribution of infectious diseases. Beyond tracking prevalence, sequencing approaches quantify circulating variants and provide early warning signals for emerging ones [1]. However, current analyses largely ignore most mutations in wastewater sequence datasets, which are typically rare, often biologically neutral, yet still encode information about transmission dynamics. Here, we introduce a method that leverages this overlooked signal to infer Estimated Transmission Matrices (ETMs) between wastewater catchments, which yield symmetrized transmission weights expected to correlate with true transmission counts. Using network-based epidemic simulations, we evaluate multiple statistical methods and identify a robust approach based on weekly counts of shared mutations, inversely weighted by their spatial ubiquity. Applied to the Austrian SARS-CoV-2 wastewater dataset [1], time-aggregated ETMs reveal transmission network structures consistent with expected transmission patterns (Fig. 1(a)). Seasonal analyses show longer-range connections between major cities in summer and more localized transmission in winter (Fig. 1(b,c)). This seasonal pattern is further quantified by fitting a gravity model of the form $\log (ETM_{ij})= \log((P_iP_j)^\alpha) - \log(d_{ij}^\beta) + \gamma$, with population counts $P_i$ and geographic distances $d_{ij}$. The model fit shows higher spatial exponents $\beta$ during winter and higher population exponents $\alpha$ during summer (Fig. 1(d)). Finally, we compare time-varying ETMs to Austrian census commuting data [2] and to a gravity model, with $\alpha$ and $\beta$ fitted to the entire dataset. We find that commuting data correlate more strongly with ETMs until early 2022, around the time when most Austrian leisure time restrictions were lifted, while the gravity model exhibits higher correlation thereafter (Fig. 1(e)). These results provide a new framework for benchmarking mobility datasets used in operational epidemic models, and for extracting transmission patterns from large-scale genetic sequence data [3,4]. Amman, F., et al. \textit{Nature Biotechnology} 40, 1814--1822 (2022). Statistik Austria. \textit{Abgestimmte Erwerbsstatistik} (2025). Ódor, G., et al. \textit{Nature Communications} 16, 4758 (2025). Tran-Kiem, C., et al. \textit{Nature} 640, 176--185 (2025).
items.authors: Odor, Gergely*; Amman, Fabian; Bergthaler, Andreas

items.title: Demographic Dependence of Vaccine Adoption under Opinion Persuasion
items.abstract: Inspired by contagion models of social belief formation, we develop an \emph{epistemically-informed} modeling framework, SIS-Vo [1], in which vaccine-related information propagates on a signed opinion network. Our model allows for heterogeneous treatment effects of policy messages across subpopulations through demographic-specific responses. We derive fixed-point characterizations of the healthy (disease-free) and endemic equilibria of this model, and obtain conditions for local stability of the healthy state in terms of the contact network and opinion-dependent vaccination capacities. We derive fixed-point characterizations of the healthy (disease-free) and endemic equilibria of the SIS-Vo model and obtain a sufficient condition for local stability of the healthy state in terms of the contact network and the vaccination carrying capacities. Using numerical simulations, we illustrate how suitably targeted policy interventions, acting through opinion dynamics, can stabilize the epidemic process by moving the system towards the healthy regime. The SIS-Vo framework thus provides a natural basis for \emph{control-theoretic} analysis of vaccination policies that remain robust even when misinformation targets specific subgroups.
items.authors: Mitchell, Lewis*

items.title: Optimal prevention strategies for chronic diseases in a compartmental disease trajectory model
items.abstract: In countries with growing elderly populations, multimorbidity poses a significant healthcare challenge. Despite this pressing development, important questions remain on how to model the development of multimorbidity. Leveraging a comprehensive dataset from approximately 45 million hospital stays spanning 17 years in Austria, we propose a compartmental model, traditionally used in infectious diseases, describing chronic disease trajectories across 132 distinct multimorbidity patterns (compartments). Our compartmental disease trajectory model (CDTM) forecasts changes in the incidence of 131 diagnostic groups and their combinations until 2030, highlighting patterns involving hypertensive diseases with cardiovascular diseases and metabolic disorders. We additionally use the model to pinpoint specific diagnoses with the greatest potential for preventive interventions to promote healthy aging. According to our model, a 5% reduction in new cases of hypertensive disease (I10--I15) leads to a 0.57 (SD 0.06)% reduction in all-cause mortality over a 15-year period, and a 0.57 (SD 0.07)% reduction in mortality for malignant neoplasms (C00--C97). Furthermore, we use the model to assess the long-term consequences of the Covid-19 pandemic on hospitalizations, revealing earlier and more frequent hospitalizations across multiple diagnoses. Our fully data-driven approach identifies leverage points for proactive preparation by physicians and policymakers to reduce the overall disease burden in the population, emphasizing a shift toward patient-centered care.
items.authors: Ledebur, Katharina*

items.title: Beyond positive and negative: enriching balance theory with neutral interactions
items.abstract: Network theory offers a powerful framework for modelling interacting systems, representing units as nodes and their interactions as links. Signed networks, capturing positive and negative social interactions, have recently gained a renewed attention, but little attention has been paid to neutral relationships. In many real-world settings we encounter a broader spectrum of relational attitudes: individuals may hold conflicting attitudes towards another person or simply feel neutral. Crucially, when representing this diversity, \textit{neutrality is not the mere absence of a relationship} as it represents a distinct relational state with its own structural and cognitive implications. Reducing interactions to a positive/negative dichotomy is overly limiting and fails to reflect the inherent complexity of human attitudes and decision-making, thus resulting in an oversimplified representation~[1,2]. Since neutral ties arise from relational mechanisms differing from the ones driving positive or negative connections, their presence leads to the definition of patterns falling outside what classical conceptual frameworks were intended to describe, such as the one of Balance Theory~[3]. When considering triads composed entirely of neutral ties there is no cognitive tension, exerting no pressure for relational change. By contrast, triads containing a mix of both neutral and signed edges may create instabilities, arising from asymmetric engagements or ambiguous expectations; moreover, one may argue that neutral ties may evolve, `absorbing' the sign of adjacent relationships, thus pushing the system towards more familiar configurations. These considerations call for a novel formulation of (im)balance, capable of capturing structural and dynamical nuances that are absent in the traditional Balance Theory framework. To this aim, we extend state-of-the-art probabilistic models to binary, undirected, neutral-enriched signed networks, imposing both global constraints (total number of positive, negative and neutral ties) and local ones (node-specific degree sequences)~[4]: from a modelling perspective, our goal is to develop the most appropriate tools to study these structures within a statistical framework. We, then, employ these neutral-inclusive models to study the emergence of microscopic and mesoscopic patterns (triads and communities), evaluate their stability under alternative theoretical assumptions, and test whether neutral-aware formulations provide more expressive, cognitively-grounded instruments for analysing and predicting social dynamics. In Figure~1 we exemplify synthetic configurations with a variety of modular structures. To retrieve the planted partitions, we optimize the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC), instantiated with the Neutral-Enriched Signed Stochastic Block Model (NE-SSBM): as we can see, blocks are correctly recovered, independently from the kind of links constituting the majority of connections. Application to both synthetic and empirical networks demonstrates that neutral-inclusive models reveal mesoscale structures invisible to binary frameworks.
items.authors: Gallo, Anna*; Squartini, Tiziano; Teixeira, Andreia Sofia

items.title: Choosing the right stochastic block model
items.abstract: Many types of stochastic block models (SBMs) have been proposed and used to model community structure in networks. Canonical and microcanonical, degree-corrected and non-degree corrected, assortative and non-assortative. When applied to the same network, variants of the model often yield markedly different groupings of nodes and so produce competing interpretations and predictions. We introduce a parametric model that directly generalizes many of these forms, allowing us to for instance interpolate between a non degree-corrected and a degree-corrected SBM. We discuss how the posterior distribution of the parameter that bridges these models not only reveals which endpoint better represents the network, but also itself measures something meaningful about the network, in this case the inequality of degrees within communities. While individual SBMs can identify interpretable groups of nodes under restricted assumptions, we demonstrate that in an unsupervised, purely data-driven sense (model evidence and predictive power), our generalized model routinely adjudicates between and out-performs existing SBM variants on real-world networks.
items.authors: Jerdee, Maximilian*

items.title: Reproducible Assembly of Complex Networks with Designed Building Blocks
items.abstract: Many real systems assemble networks reproducibly from building blocks with local interaction rules. In contrast, traditional stochastic network models produce widely varying connectivity patterns. Here, we derive exact conditions on the local rules between node types that guarantee a unique target network. We demonstrate this framework across diverse systems, from protein complexes to electronic circuits, highlighting how specific design principles can ensure robust network formation.
items.authors: van der Kolk, Jasper*

items.title: Network theory and machine learning perspectives in the quest of new materials
items.abstract: Understanding and predicting the emergence of novel materials is a fundamental challenge in condensed matter physics, materials science, and technology. With the rapid growth of materials databases in both size and reliability, the challenge shifts from data collection to efficient exploration of this vast and complex space. A key strategy lies in the smart use of descriptors at multiple scales, ranging from atomic arrangements to macroscopic properties, to represent materials in high-dimensional abstract spaces. Network theory provides a powerful framework to structure and analyze these relationships, capturing hidden patterns and guiding discovery. Machine learning complements this approach by enabling predictive modeling, dimensionality reduction, and the identification of promising material candidates. By integrating network-based methods with machine learning (ML) techniques, researchers can construct, analyze, and efficiently navigate the material space, uncovering novel materials with tailored properties. This review explores the synergy between network theory and ML, highlighting their role in accelerating materials discovery through a systematic and interpretable approach.
items.authors: Caldarelli, Guido*

items.title: Network Field Theory
items.abstract: The multidisciplinary study of complex systems has been greatly facilitated by the simplicity and flexibility of networks as a modeling paradigm. However, traditional network models often focus on scalar interactions of similar types, limiting their ability to fully capture systems characterized by multifaceted, multidimensional, and/or complex interactions. Here, I will introduce Network Field Theory (NFT), a novel and general framework that extends field theory to systems structured as networks, incorporating higher-order fields to model interactions spanning multiple dimensions. Within this framework, I will present edge decomposition, edge expansion, and node fixation as fundamental operations in NFT for modifying topology, modeling adaptive systems, and coarse-graining across multiple scales (Fig. 1). By integrating network topology and geometry as central components of system behavior, NFT offers the power and versatility to enable a unified description of diverse interactions across a wide range of physical, biological, and social networks.
items.authors: Motter, Adilson*

items.title: Adaptive Cannistraci Hebb Network Automata Modelling of Complex Networks for Path based Link Prediction
items.abstract: Many complex networks have partially observed or evolving connectivity, making link prediction a fundamental task. Topological link prediction infers missing links using only network topology, with applications in social, biological, and technological systems. T he Cannistraci Hebb (CH) theory provides a topological formulation of Hebbian learning, grounded on two pillars: (1) the minimization of external links within local communities, and (2) the path based definition of local communities that capture homophilic (similarity driven) interactions via paths of length 2 and synergetic (diversity driven) interactions via paths of length 3. Building on this, we introduce the Cannistraci Hebb Adaptive (CHA) network automata, an adaptive learning machine that automatically selects the optimal CH rule and path length to model each network. CHA unifies theoretical interpretability and data driven adaptivity, bridging physics inspired network science and machine intelligence. Across 1,269 networks from 14 domains, CHA consistently surpasses state of the art methods including Structural Perturbation Method (SPM) SPM), Stochastic Block Models SBM )), graph embedding methods, and message passing graph neural networks while revealing the mechanistic principles governing link formation. Our code is available at https://github.com/biomedical cybernetics/Cannistraci_Hebb_network_automata
items.authors: Zhao, Jialin; Muscoloni, Alessandro; Michieli, Umberto; Zhang, Yingtao; Cannistraci, Carlo*

items.title: Dynamics of Gender Representation in Academic Mathematics
items.abstract: In 2024, women remain highly underrepresented in academic mathematics. We study the mechanisms that drive gender representation in academic mathematics, learn these mechanisms from individual-level data, and analyze networked mathematical genealogies to understand the roles of long-range inﬂuence in the diversiﬁcation of the ﬁeld. We study a data set derived from the Mathematics Genealogy Project1, which logs advisor-advisee relationships for 300,000 PhD mathematicians, as well as graduation years, institutions, and mathematical subﬁeld classiﬁcations. We used a name- based gender inference package to estimate binary (female/male) gender for 93% of records. We propose a stochastic model of the data that explicitly models several unobserved mechanisms in the data gener- ating process, including ﬁeld-speciﬁc productivity eﬀects, gender homophily by mathematical subﬁeld, and production of graduate students by advisors. This model supports a view of the data as a nonstationary multitype branching process with parameters to be inferred from incomplete data through the lens of the model’s unobserved mechanisms. We perform inference in this model using maximum-likelihood estimation. We ﬁnd gendered disparities in both hiring rates and career lengths, as consistent with recent studies. We also ﬁnd ﬁeld-speciﬁc eﬀects related to faculty production of graduate students and gender homophily. We simulate our model forward in time, ﬁnding that interventions focused towards equalizing faculty attrition and production power of PhDs can accelerate the pace of gender diversiﬁcation by 10-15 years. We also study the network structure of academic genealogy trees (Figure 1) with the goal of identifying inﬂuential individuals and groups that contribute to the diversiﬁcation of a subﬁeld, institution, or lineage. We develop a novel measure of centrality-like inﬂuence quantifying the impact of an individual node on downstream production of female PhD students, validating our measure via a link prediction task.
items.authors: Chodrow, Philip*; Brooks, Heather; Lee, Harlin; Porter, Mason; Restrepo, Juan Gabriel; Haensch, Anna

items.title: The Murmuration of Scientists
items.abstract: Scientists shift their research interest, or “move” through the space of knowledge, to explore new ideas and topics. Can this collective behavior of scientists resemble that of animals foraging in physical space? Here, we create a continuous mapping of authors' positions in a topic space to examine how scientists "move" through the knowledge space in relations with their peers (Fig. 1a). Drawing inspiration from the boids model of collective animal behaviors [1], we model research topic dynamics through a set of simple peer-interaction rules that shape how scientists move through the knowledge space (Fig. 1b). We then analyze how scientific prominence as measured by the h-index modulates these basic rules. Our empirical analysis shows that scientists are more likely to align with the movement of, or cohere around, more highly prominent scientists within their local peer neighborhood (Fig. 1c). In contrast, highly proficient scientists exhibit a lower propensity to follow their peers’ movements overall (Fig. 1d). Furthermore, we find that the propensity to align with peers exhibits an U-shaped relationship with subsequent h-index growth (Fig. 1e), suggesting that scientific impact may benefit either from pursuing a more independent research trajectory or from closely following prevailing trends within the local peer community. Taken together, our framework treats scientists’ research trajectories as a form of collective motion, offering a useful framework for quantifying how peer dynamics shape the evolution of research interests and impact.
items.authors: Kim, Munjung; Ahn, Yong-Yeol *

items.title: Faculty mid-career moves among US PhD granting universities
items.abstract: Despite numerous enlightening studies of the hiring and retention of academic faculty, relatively little is known about the rates, patterns, and causes of mid-career moves (MCMs). Here, we analyze 10 years (2011-2020) of an annual U.S. faculty census to characterize MCMs. MCMs identified from this data induce flows between universities that naturally form a network such that there is a directed edge from university i to university j for every MCM from i to j. Certain analyses that depend strongly on the underlying network structure cannot be done with typical regression methods, and naturally require network null/reference models. In this study, we mainly investigate who moves and why, where faculty go when they move and the consequences of these moves for faculty careers and academia as a whole. Although MCMs are rare in academia (1.03% annually), we find a rich heterogeneity in the annual MCM rate by field and domain. Younger faculty, faculty at elite institutions and rural institutions are at a higher risk of moving, self-hires are at a much lower risk, while we do not find a significant gender effect on MCM risk. MCMs tend to move faculty to institutions closer in prestige to their origin institution more often than we expect, exhibit significant assortativity in public and private institutions and effectively increase the number of self-hires and faculty at private and urban institutions. Faculty experience prestige advantages when they move to their alma mater, to private and urban institutions, and women gain slightly more prestige than men over MCMs. Finally, we find that faculty tend to trade prestige for a promotion when they move, prompting us to develop a utility theory of faculty MCMs. These findings quantify large-scale patterns in MCMs in tenure-track/tenured faculty in PhD granting universities in the US at an unprecedented scale, enriching the grounds for further research to determine causal mechanisms that drive these patterns.
items.authors: Prasanna, Chethan*; Zhang, Sam; Clauset, Aaron; Larremore, Daniel B.

items.title: Tenure and Research Trajectories
items.abstract: Tenure is a cornerstone of the US academic system, yet its relationship to faculty research trajectories remains poorly understood. Conceptually, tenure systems may act as a selection mechanism, screening in high-output researchers; a dynamic incentive mechanism, encouraging high output prior to tenure but low output after tenure; and a creative search mechanism, encouraging tenured individuals to undertake high-risk work. Here, we integrate data from seven different sources to trace US tenure-line faculty and their research outputs at a remarkable scale and scope, covering over 12,000 researchers across 15 disciplines. Our analysis reveals that faculty publication rates typically increase sharply during the tenure track and peak just before obtaining tenure. Post-tenure trends, however, vary across disciplines: In lab-based fields, such as biology and chemistry, research output typically remains high post-tenure, whereas in non-lab-based fields, such as mathematics and sociology, research output typically declines substantially post-tenure (Figure 1). Turning to creative search, faculty increasingly produce novel, high-risk research after securing tenure. However, this shift toward novelty and risk-taking comes with a decline in impact, with post-tenure research yielding fewer highly cited papers. Comparing outcomes across common career ages but different tenure years or comparing research trajectories in tenure-based and non-tenure-based research settings underscores that breaks in the research trajectories are sharply tied to the individual’s tenure year. Overall, these findings provide an empirical basis for understanding the tenure system, individual research trajectories, and the shape of scientific output.
items.authors: Tripodi, Giorgio*

items.title: The Network of Interdisciplinary US Faculty Hiring
items.abstract: Hiring a faculty member is a high-stakes investment and represents an academic department’s belief about the future of innovation in their field. Faculty hired across disciplinary boundaries, with a PhD in one field and a professorship in another, provide a window into the fungibility of skills and credentials between fields. They also reveal how the labor market for academic faculty responds to the increasing emphasis on interdisciplinary research as a driver of innovation. However, a major hurdle to mapping interdisciplinary faculty hiring is that existing faculty hiring networks draw institution- to-institution edges [1], but interdisciplinary hiring is only detectable at the department-to-department level. This lack of data leaves numerous questions unanswerable, including: How does interdisciplinary faculty hiring shape the academic labor force? How does the system of interdisciplinary hiring affect the career outcomes of individual faculty members? We transformed existing institution-level faculty hiring networks to the department level by systematically annotating a large-scale faculty hiring census from the Academic Analytics Research Center with the PhD department and field for each faculty member (from OpenAlex, ProQuest, and ORCID). The result is a novel dataset of department-to-department faculty hiring in the US, which naturally forms a directed multigraph where each directed edge (i, j) represents a professor in department j who received their PhD in department i. This network tracks 107,812 hiring flows between 12,833 US academic departments and can be coarse-grained to represent flows between fields. Although interdisciplinary hiring is common, the field-to-field hiring network is sparse. 38% of faculty are hired into a different field than that of their PhD, but only 11% of field pairs exchange substantial flows of faculty (which account for 26% of interdisciplinary hires). Some fields such as Economics exhibit unreciprocated outflows of faculty, while others such as Computer Science and its computational neighbors are mutually connected by faculty hiring (Fig. 1a). Our results shed light not just the structure of the system, but the consequences for the people in it. Despite ubiquitous downward prestige mobility in faculty hiring [1], interdisciplinary hires experience smaller prestige drops from degree to hiring department—19% versus 23% for their peers hired from the same field. At the same time, interdisciplinary hires take about one year longer to secure faculty positions than disciplinary hires (Fig. 1b). Interdisciplinary hiring thus represents a trade-off between higher prestige but longer training times for individual faculty.
