The sparsity and asymmetry of the network suggest that interdisciplinary hiring, despite its prevalence, flows through narrow channels shaped by limited training fungibility between fields.
items.authors: Aoki-Sherwood, Ben*; Larremore, Daniel

items.title: Second Acts in Scientific and Creative Careers
items.abstract: Careers are often defined by periods of concentrated achievement, when high-impact work occurs in close succession. Yet behind every celebrated first act lies a deeper question: what happens next? Understanding how a second act unfolds is essential to explaining the long-term dynamics of success. Competing theories offer divergent predictions. The Matthew Effect emphasizes “success-breeds-success” dynamics. Yet the same success also reshapes identity, expectations, and networks in ways that narrow subsequent search. These competing forces may operate at different levels, but their net effect is unified: success re-parameterizes the explore–exploit problem. It tilts the payoff surface toward exploitation, raises the perceived and realized costs of pivoting, and constrains feasible exploration through reputational and structural path dependence. Here, we reconstruct the career histories of 21,470 individual scientists, 3,046 film directors, 2,243 artists, and 4,278 authors, including domain-specific impact measures. We assign topics to each work using methods from network science and machine learning. To robustly identify high-impact periods within individual careers while minimizing noise from single lucky hits, we use the hot-streak framework. This allows us to distinguish individuals who achieve repeated success from those who have a single high-impact period. We quantify topical dynamics using cosine similarity between topics in the first hot streak and the remainder of the career and exploration after the first hot streak using the entropy measure. Across all four domains, we find a consistent pattern: the probability of a second hot streak decreases as the impact of the first hot streak increases. This mechanism operates separately from the Matthew effect, which preserves long-standing status differences by amplifying the consequences of early achievements. Mechanistically, early success is associated with a lock-in effect: the larger the impact of the first hot streak, the more an individual will continue to exploit the same direction afterwards. This lock-in effect, in turn, limits exploration and decreases the chances of achieving another period of highly successful work. Crucially, lock-in is not inevitable. Mid-career exploration significantly increases the likelihood of experiencing a second act. This exploration, however, is costly. The larger the pivot, the longer the interval between the two streaks. Interestingly, the seeds of a second act, when different from the first, are planted in the early stages of the career. Even when pivoting, however, individuals do not abandon the first main topic of interest but rather embrace ambidexterity and a balanced portfolio approach. Taken together, our findings suggest that the traditional emphasis on early-career promise may be too narrow. In many domains, and particularly in science, the age of peak achievement is rising. This shift reflects a growing burden of knowledge and the lengthening learning curve required to reach the frontier of innovation. As a result, the widespread emphasis on early accomplishments may not reliably predict long-term impact. Our novel perspective is potentially informative for funding agencies, investors, and organizations that need to identify and support long and impactful careers, as well as support more forward-looking hiring strategies. The capacity to continuously recognize opportunities for exploitation, remain open to novelty, and adapt over time may be as important as early signals of potential.
items.authors: Erkol, Sirag*; Tripodi, Giorgio; Wang, Dashun

items.title: Asymmetric Feedback Loops in Cross-Partisan Interaction Networks
items.abstract: Cross-cutting interactions provide critical pathways for information flow across polarized social networks, yet how such interactions shape and are shaped by network dynamics remains poorly understood. In particular, little is known about how different \emph{types} of cross-boundary interactions generate asymmetric visibility responses and how these responses feed back into future network behavior. We address this question using a large-scale longitudinal interaction network of U.S.\ state legislators and their audiences on Twitter/X, comprising over 1.1M interactions from 2020-21. We model elite-audience communication as a temporal, directed, and \emph{typed} network, where nodes represent legislators and audience members, and edges represent cross-partisan interactions (retweets, replies, and mentions). Interaction edges are characterized by content attributes (posting styles), and audience engagement induces a node-level visibility signal measured relative to each legislator’s historical baseline. For legislator $u$ and post $i$, visibility is defined as $V_{ui} = E_{ui}/(\tilde{E}_u + b_0)$, % \[ % V_{ui} = \frac{E_{ui}}{\tilde{E}_u + b_0}, % \] where $E_{ui}$ is observed engagement and $\tilde{E}_u$ is the median engagement of $u$ over a sliding temporal window, and $b_0$ is a small stabilizing constant. This normalization isolates changes in prominence attributable to interaction structure rather than global activity fluctuations. Figure~1 shows the study design and observed feedback loop dynamics. Our analysis reveals pronounced \emph{asymmetries} in visibility dynamics across interaction layers. Cross-partisan retweets are associated with higher visibility for Republican legislators but lower visibility for Democrats, whereas cross-partisan replies and mentions exhibit the opposite pattern. These patterns indicate that distinct audience communities reward different network actions—amplification versus direct engagement—despite operating within the same platform. Crucially, we demonstrate that visibility serves as a feedback signal within the network. Let $\rho_{u,t}$ denote the rate of cross-partisan interactions by legislator $u$ at time $t$, and let $\delta_{u,t}$ denote the difference in visibility between cross-partisan and non-cross-partisan interactions. We model the feedback process as, \[ \rho_{u,t+1} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \delta_{u,t} + \beta_2 \rho_{u,t} + \mathbf{X}_u + \epsilon_{u,t}, \] where $\mathbf{X}_u$ captures node-level attributes and $\beta_1$ quantifies reinforcement from audience response. We find that increased visibility on cross-partisan retweets and mentions is positively associated with higher rates of future cross-partisan interactions, revealing a reinforcement loop between audience response and elite behavior. Together, our findings characterize cross-partisan engagement as a \emph{typed, asymmetric diffusion process} embedded in a polarized temporal network, where visibility mediates feedback between local interaction structure and future edge formation. More broadly, this work highlights how heterogeneous interaction modes shape information flow and behavioral adaptation in socio-technical networks.
items.authors: Biswas, Ahana*; Lin, Yu-Ru

items.title: Concentration in Digital Information Ecosystems: From Sharing to Exposure
items.abstract: The internet was expected to democratize information access by weakening traditional gatekeepers and expanding choice. Yet online attention remains highly concentrated, consistent with a network science account in which information visibility emerges from heavy-tailed, power law-like degree and popularity distributions [1–4]. We analyze concentration using two complementary datasets: Twitter/X URL-sharing by approximately 1.6 million U.S. registered voters (2017–2022) and Facebook URL-sharing data (2020–2022). We measure concentration across domains, URLs, users, and exposure, and we assess how these patterns vary by content type, demographics (age, gender, party), and time. Concentration is pervasive and multi-layered. On Twitter, the top 10 domains account for 24% of shares, and news concentrates about 10% more intensely than general content. Exposure is more concentrated than sharing: the top 0.1% of domains account for 50% of shares but 65% of exposure, indicating that network structure reconcentrates attention even when sharing is moderately diversified. User activity is even more skewed, with 1% of users generating 40% of shares and the top 10% producing 75–80% of content. These patterns are broadly similar across demographic groups, pointing to structural rather than population-specific drivers. Over time, concentration remains high but fluctuates with major events, including diverging trends during COVID-19, and Facebook shows similar though slightly lower concentration than Twitter. Together, the results indicate that extreme attention inequality reflects robust properties of digital information systems, with direct implications for diversity in online information exposure.
items.authors: Ozturan, Burak*

items.title: Reaching a Consensus in Predictive Loops
items.abstract: The study of opinion formation through peer interactions has a long history in the social networks community. With the rise of digital platforms, these peer interactions are increasingly mediated and influenced by algorithmic systems. In this work, we are interested in characterizing the complex dynamics that emerge between populations and platforms when algorithmic systems are repeatedly optimized based on observed data about expressed opinions. To this end, we combine concepts and insights from performative prediction, a growing line of work in the machine learning community, with established models and equilibrium concepts of opinions dynamics. In particular, we propose a model where a platform provides predictions to individuals based on observed data about their past expressed opinions and these predictions influence individuals' future opinions which then spread across the network through peer interactions. In our formulation, we build on the classical Friedkin-Johnsen model (FJ) to characterize how individuals update their beliefs through peer interactions, and we let predictions influence individuals' initial opinions. The equilibrium of interest is the state where peer dynamics are at equilibrium and the platform has no reason to change its predictions based on observed data. This long-term equilibrium state, termed performative stability, depends on the choice of learning policy and platform susceptibility of individuals, as well as network structure and peer susceptibility. We characterize performative stability under varying assumptions on the platform's learning policy. A key insight is that platforms that provide faithful predictions have a homogenizing effect on the equilibrium opinions. In particular, in the limit of high platform susceptibility, the dynamics lead to consensus under the minimal and standard assumption of connectivity. This stands in contrast with the heterogeneous equilibrium emerging from the FJ model in isolation. Another insight is that when a platform systematically deviates from perfect prediction, qualitatively different equilibria emerge. We analyze how the platform actions affect individuals even if those individuals are not at all susceptible to platform influence, and we show how this indirect influence through peer interactions can be exploited by platforms to steer collective opinions. We analyze the convergence rate and show that the most persistent individuals in the population dominate the convergence where the persistence is determined by the platform susceptibility and the strength of platform policies. In other words, the individuals who are least influenced by the platform bottleneck the convergence. Our theoretical results are supported by simulations and highlight the important role of the platform's learning policy when characterizing equilibria in social networks.
items.authors: Wu, Jiduan*; Abebe, Rediet; Mendler-Dünner, Celestine

items.title: Attraction to politically extreme users on social media
items.abstract: Political segregation can be seen in almost any facet of social interactions, from the choice of social partners (Nicholson et al., 2016) to the composition of neighborhoods and cities (Brown & Enos, 2021; Motyl et al., 2020). Political segregation is associated with attitudinal polarization, intergroup hostility, and an increased spread of misinformation (Iyengar et al., 2019; Stein et al., 2023). Prior work has proposed that a key driver of political segregation is political homophily, which is the tendency to affiliate with others who have similar political views (Halberstam & Knight, 2016; Kossinets & Watts, 2009; McPherson et al., 2001). Recently, however, it has been proposed that tie-selection decisions in the context of politics are also impacted by political acrophily, the tendency to prefer to affiliate with others who hold more extreme (as opposed to more moderate) political views in the direction of one’s political leaning (Goldenberg et al., 2022; Zimmerman et al., 2022). However, there is no direct evidence for the occurrence of acrophily in social media interactions. To address this issue, we conducted two studies. First, we performed an online preregistered experiment in which we created fictional social media users and modified their degree of political extremity. Participants (N=400; 200 Democrats, 200 Republicans) were presented with these profiles and were asked whether they would follow them. Participants were more likely to follow an extreme copartisan rather than a moderate one (Fig. 1A). We also explored individual-level predictors of this preference and found that it was associated with negative feelings toward the opposite party. In the second study, we analyzed real-world tie connections on social media using a dataset (Chen et al., 2022) related to the 2020 U.S. presidential election. This dataset comprises tweets from 1.9 million users. We evaluated users’ political affiliations based on their media consumption (Badawy et al., 2019; Bovet & Makse, 2019; Ferrara et al., 2020), and we examined retweet connections between users because they reflect how information spreads across the network. Notably, simply analyzing users’ retweets does not necessarily indicate a preference for more extreme political views, as user behavior might reflect the political distribution of their network rather than individual preferences. Accurate detection of acrophily, therefore, necessitates contrasting observed behaviors with those predicted by a hypothetical simulation to understand the influence of network characteristics on user interactions. We conducted two simulations. In the homophily simulation, we examined the case in which users were more likely to retweet users whose political views were closer to their own, regardless of whether they were more or less extreme (Fig. 1B). In the acrophily simulation, we considered users to be more likely to interact with others who were politically more extreme than themselves (Fig. 1B). Our results showed that Twitter/X users were more inclined to retweet individuals with more extreme views compared to the homophily simulation, suggesting that users' tie-selection decisions are shaped by both homophily and acrophily. Moreover, we analyzed the sentiment of users’ tweets using VADER (Hutto & Gilbert, 2014) and found that users who were more likely to retweet others with more extreme views also tended to write and retweet more negative tweets.
items.authors: Zimmerman, Federico*; Goldenberg, Amit

items.title: A Stochastic Model for Understanding the Volatility of the Infodemic Risk Index
items.abstract: Gallotti et al.[1] proposed three metrics to quantify this phenomenon of infodemic in social media, including the Infodemic Risk Index (IRI). This index measures the extent to which social media accounts are exposed to unreliable content, considering the number of followers as a key factor in amplifying such content. While the IRI is a practical tool, it exhibits significant volatility over time, especially in certain countries where it may spike one day and sharply decline the next. In this work, we aim to investigate the causes behind these rapid fluctuations, identifying key metrics that contribute to the instability of the index. We analyzed Twitter data collected by the COVID-19 Infodemic Platform from over 50 countries, covering the period from February 2020 to the end of May 2022. To evaluate the instability of the IRI (also referred to as \emph{volatility}), we computed its standard deviation over time for each country. As expected, we found a strong anti-correlation between the number of daily tweets and the volatility of the IRI: countries with a high volume of tweets tend to have a more stable Infodemic Risk Index over time. In principle, this finding should align with the expected scaling of standard deviation with the number of daily messages, which suggests a volatility scaling of \( \sigma \approx N^{0.5} \). However, our fitted model instead reveals a scaling exponent of -0.24, indicating an unexpected dependence of volatility on tweet volume. To understand this discrepancy, we developed a stochastic model that accounts for key factors influencing the IRI's fluctuations. Specifically, our model incorporates: \begin{itemize} \item The number of followers for users sharing both reliable and unreliable sources, particularly the slope \( \alpha \) of the follower distribution. \item The disinformation rate, defined as the fraction of news domains classified as unreliable over the total number of news domains shared per day. \end{itemize} We extracted the \( \alpha \) values of the follower distribution for a set of 51 countries, finding that users sharing reliable content had an \( \alpha \) value of 2.10, while users sharing unreliable content had an \( \alpha \) value of 2.12. These extracted values were then applied to our simulation, where we conducted simulations for different sets of users, with user groups of sizes 20, 40, 80, 160, 320, and 2000. In each case, we measured the standard deviation of the IRI and plotted the results in a log-log scale. The simulation revealed a scaling exponent of 0.11, which contrasts with the traditional \( N^{0.5} \) scaling. To further test that the long tail character of the distribution of followers was the source of this smaller scaling exponent, we examined the relationship between the number of messages and the standard deviation of the IRI with different follower distributions: exponential, uniform, and normal. We found that for these distributions, the scaling exponent \( \alpha \) was 0.5, as expected according to the Central Limit Theorem. Our goal is to compare nations of different sizes in terms of daily tweet volume while capturing the mechanisms driving IRI volatility. The model, despite its simplicity, already reproduces a power-law relationship with an exponent lower than 0.5, consistently closer to the empirical observations.
items.authors: Bertani, Anna*; Gallotti, Riccardo

items.title: How Out-Group Animosity Shapes Partisan Divisions: A Dynamical Model of Affective Polarization on Social Networks
items.abstract: Politically divided societies are also often divided emotionally: people like and trust those with similar political views (in-group favoritism) while disliking and distrusting those with different views (out-group animosity). This phenomenon, called affective polarization, influences individual decisions, including seemingly apolitical choices such as whether to wear a mask or what car to buy. We present a dynamical model of decision-making in an affectively polarized social network, identifying three potential global outcomes separated by a sharp boundary in the parameter space: consensus, partisan polarization, and nonpartisan polarization. Analysis reveals that larger out-group animosity compared to in-group favoritism, i.e., more hate than love, is sufficient for polarization, while larger in-group favoritism compared to out-group animosity, i.e. more love than hate, is necessary for consensus. We also show that, counterintuitively, increasing cross-party connections facilitates polarization, and that by emphasizing partisan differences, mass media creates self-fulfilling prophecies that lead to polarization. Affective polarization also creates tipping points in the opinion landscape where one group suddenly reverses their trends. Our findings aid in understanding and addressing the cascading effects of affective polarization, offering insights for strategies to mitigate polarization.
items.authors: Nettasinghe, Buddhika*

items.title: "Dark information" in complex networks and systems: revealing the synergistic shadow structure.
items.abstract: There has been an explosion of interest recently in the problem of ``higher-order" interactions in complex systems. In classic, network-based approaches, higher-order interactions between elements can only be inferred obliquely by observing patterns of low-order edges (as in community detection, or transitivity). In contrast, information-theoretic analyses can directly assess high-dimensional interactions of many types, including redundant information (information duplicated over many elements) and synergistic information (information only disclosed by the joint state of multiple elements when observed simultaneously). A natural question is how well the classic, oblique approaches represent the genuine higher-order structures: how much (if any) ``structure" have classical complex systems and network science been missing? This presentation shows that statistical (``functional") connectivity networks are systematically biased in favor of redundant information and insensitive to the higher-order synergistic information. Drawing on analogy with ``dark matter" in cosmology, we argue that the scaffold of synergistic dependencies forms a kind of ``dark information" - a potentially vast set of possible interactions that are largely inaccessible to tools commonly used in network science and complexity science. Exploring this structure will require the development of novel mathematical machinery in information theory, and has the potential to reveal new vistas of discovery. Finally, we will argue that synergistic dependencies should be of broad interest. Far from being a mere statistical curiosity, synergistic information is closely related to the phenomena of causal ``colliders" - when a variable's state is an integrative function of multiple colliding influences, the dependency structure is synergy-dominated. This suggests that the ``dark information" in complex systems is significant, as it reflects that ``integrated" information produced when multiple elements interact in non-trivial ways.
items.authors: Varley, Thomas*

items.title: When Can We Detect Higher-Order Contagion? A Likelihood-Based Approach
items.abstract: Contagion dynamics on higher-order networks are increasingly recognized as essential for modelling group interactions in social and biological systems. However, distinguishing these higher-order mechanisms from classical pairwise interactions using observational data remains a significant challenge. We present a rigorous likelihood-based framework for birth-death processes (BDPs) to investigate if and when the disentanglement of pairwise and higher-order rates from a single observed trajectory is possible. We formulate the inference problem for finite populations where extinction is inevitable by conditioning on non-extinction. This allows us to construct the Doob h-transformed Q-process and derive a consistent Quasi-Maximum Likelihood Estimator (QMLE) validated via martingale central limit theory. We focus on the realistic unlabelled scheme where only aggregate population counts are available. We treat the specific infection mechanism as missing data and derive a Conditional Expectation-Maximization (Q-EM) algorithm, adapting existing EM framework for BDPs to the survival-conditioned regime. Finally, we provide a boundary likelihood-ratio test to rigorously detect the presence of higher-order interactions accounting for non-standard asymptotics. With fully observed data, we recover rates with high precision. When the data is unlabelled, the pairwise and higher-order rate functions can become locally collinear near the quasi-steady state. In this "hard regime", parameters become practically exchangeable, confining most distinguishing information primarily to the transient growth phase. We quantify this information loss via the asymptotic covariance matrix. Crucially, we demonstrate that despite this challenge, the derived QMLE converges to the true parameters, validating that our robust Q-EM algorithm successfully disentangles the composite dynamics and recovers the underlying pairwise and higher-order rates as the observation window increases.
items.authors: Lalovic, Marko*; Kiss, Istvan; Georgiou, Nicos; Malizia, Federico

items.title: A comprehensive characterisation of the landscape of Partial Information Decomposition applied to synthetic and real-world networks
items.abstract: Information theory is a powerful framework for investigating higher-order interdependencies in complex networks. Recent advances have led to a variety of tools capable of detecting, characterising, and quantifying multivariate information relationships on graphs, with applications ranging from neural and brain systems to social and technological networks [1]. Among these frameworks, Partial Information Decomposition (PID) has emerged as a particularly influential approach [2]. PID seeks to explain statistical dependencies among multiple variables by decomposing the information that a set of sources provides about a target into redundant, unique, and synergistic components. However, while PID offers a conceptually powerful formalism that has been successfully applied in a wide range of settings in network science [3], it does not prescribe a unique functional form for its atomic components. This lack of a canonical definition has led to the proposal of an increasing number of desiderata that a redundancy function should satisfy, resulting in the introduction of many different PID measures. Moreover, several of the suggested properties were also shown to be mutually incompatible, implying that no single redundancy measure can satisfy all of them, and hindering the search of a universal PID definition. Hence, the field now comprises a vast collection of measures grounded in different, and often irreconcilable, axiomatic choices, challenging both the theoretical interpretation and empirical application of PID. Thus, our work provides a unified and comprehensive perspective on the PID landscape, both mathematically and empirically, clarifying the suitability of different PID measures in specific scenarios and offering practical guidance for their application. We first perform an exhaustive theoretical analysis of all PID measures existing in the literature, compiling the full set of axioms each measure satisfies and classifying them according to their similarity distance (Fig.~1a). We then study the logical dependencies and mutual (in)compatibility of the axioms themselves, establishing a series of theorems and no-go results that elucidate their interrelations (Fig.~1b). Finally, we conduct an large-scale empirical analysis by computing all PID measures on the pyspi dataset, a rich collection of synthetic and real-world networks [4]. A hierarchical clustering of the results shows that the measures group into a small number of large clusters (Fig.~1c), which can be explained by a limited set of mathematical properties. Interestingly, this structure cannot be predicted from standard time-series statistical estimators, thus hinting that it reflects genuinely different modes of information flow. Overall, this study reconciles the growing diversity of PID measures and axioms by organising them into coherent clusters. By highlighting a small number of measures representative of each cluster and proposing a unified open codebase, we explain the practical consequences of specific axiomatic choices and provide concrete guidance for the application of PID. This enables more interpretable and reproducible investigations of higher-order structures in complex networks. [1] Timme, Nicholas, et al. Journal of Computational Neuroscience 36.2 (2014): 119-140. [2] Williams, Paul L., and Randall D. Beer. arXiv preprint arXiv:1004.2515 (2010). [3] Milzman, Jesse, and Vince Lyzinski. Journal of Complex Networks 10.5 (2022): cnac026. [4] Cliff, Oliver M., et al. Nature Computational Science 3.10 (2023): 883-893.
items.authors: Liardi, Alberto*

items.title: Pairwise representation of higher-order interactions
items.abstract: Comparing higher-order and pairwise network dynamics is often ambiguous due to their use of fundamentally different building blocks: hyperedges versus edges. These building blocks lack natural correspondence for allocating coupling strengths and matching interaction structures. To enable fair comparison between these two frameworks, we introduce a transformation using auxiliary variables to construct equivalent pairwise systems that reproduce higher-order dynamics exactly (Fig. 1A and 1B). Our approach can be interpreted as a converse of dimensionality reduction: while eliminating degrees of freedom in pairwise networks generates higher-order interactions as a byproduct [Thibeault et al., Nat Phys 2024], introducing suitable auxiliary variables can lower the order of the interactions. We prove that for systems with nonlinear coupling, the auxiliary variables must be linear combinations of state variables. Otherwise, infinite recursion occurs because partial derivatives couple variables across interactions. This establishes a sharp boundary: higher-order systems violating this condition cannot be reduced to pairwise form. For transformable systems, we present an optimal construction minimizing auxiliary variable count and prove that alternative strategies (multilevel composition, symmetry reduction) offer no advantage. To understand the network size dependence of representation cost, we analyze uniform random hypergraphs. For Kuramoto-type interactions, the required auxiliary variable count grows linearly with system size $n$ (Fig 1C). For product-type interactions, it exhibits a sparse regime scaling $\sim (nz/d)(2^d-1-d)$, revealing exponential growth with the interaction order $d$. This framework enables principled comparison between higher-order and pairwise representations using identical metrics (node and edge count) and provides rigorous grounds for assessing when higher-order structure is genuinely irreducible versus reducible through dimensional expansion. Exact transformation also unlocks analytical tools developed for pairwise networks, including spectral methods and the master stability formalism, providing rigorous foundations for stability analysis of higher-order systems. Finally, our work raises an important question on whether observed phenomena attributed to higher-order structure reflect genuine higher-order effects or could emerge from pairwise dynamics.
items.authors: Lee, Jongshin*; Motter, Adilson

items.title: Towards GPU-accelerated Temporal Graph Learning with Higher-Order De Bruijn Graphs
items.abstract: The growing polularity of deep learning for graph-structured data generates a number of opportunities as well as interesting challenges for the network science community. The lack of tools that make it easy to bridge between network science methods and data structures and computational techniques used in deep learning hinder a much needed exchange that could benefit both communities. Addressing this gap, we introduce pathpyG, a python-based Open Source package for network analysis and learning that leverages data structures provided by the geometric deep learning framework pytorch-geometric. It particularly allows to model time-respecting paths in temporal networks, which are the basis to model temporal networks with k-th order De Bruijn graphs and to apply causality-aware GNNs. However, computing time-respecting paths in large temporal networks can be challenging. To address this, we represent (temporal) networks as multi-dimensional pytorch tensors. This facilitates massively parallel tensor operations to efficiently calculate time-respecting paths, which can be transparently moved to the GPU, Here, we study whether the modelling of time-respecting paths benefits from GPU operations for two exemplary data sets. Our preliminary results show a complex picture, where potential speed-ups depend on characteristics like size, density, number of unique timestamps and temporal distribution of edges. pathpyG also provides comprehensive methods for GPU-based operations on static networks and interfaces to established tools like networkx and graphtool. To facilitate approaches that bridge network science and deep graph learning, we implemented random graph models and (temporal) network randomization techniques, added methods to plot and analyse generating functions, and included an interface to netzschleuder, which makes it easy to apply GNNs in thousands of attributed and temporal networks.
items.authors: Scholtes, Ingo*; Hackl, Jürgen; Heeg, Franziska; Lampert, Moritz; Qarkaxhija, Lisi; Perri, Vincenzo

items.title: Information theory for hypergraph similarity
items.abstract: Comparing networks is essential for a number of downstream tasks, from clustering to anomaly detection. Despite higher-order interactions being critical for understanding the dynamics of complex systems, traditional approaches for network comparison are limited to pairwise interactions only. Here we construct a general information theoretic framework for hypergraph similarity, capturing meaningful correspondence among higher-order interactions while correcting for spurious correlations. Our method operationalizes any notion of structural overlap among hypergraphs as a principled normalized mutual information measure, allowing us to derive a hierarchy of increasingly granular formulations of similarity among hypergraphs within and across orders of interactions, and at multiple scales. We validate these measures through extensive experiments on synthetic hypergraphs and apply the framework to reveal meaningful patterns in a variety of empirical higher-order networks. Our work provides foundational tools for the principled comparison of higher-order networks, shedding light on the structural organization of networked systems with non-dyadic interactions.
items.authors: Felippe, Helcio*; Kirkley, Alec; Battiston, Federico

items.title: A Bayesian approach to out-of-sample network reconstruction
items.abstract: Networks underpin systems from finance to biology, yet their structure is often only partially observed. Exponential Random Graph Models (ERGMs)~\citep{fronczak2014,squartini2017} reconstruct network topologies from constrained statistics, still they are typically fitted independently to each snapshot. Designed to carry out network reconstruction in an \emph{in-sample} fashion, such an inference scheme cannot be employed to predict future configurations. To overcome such a limitation, here we introduce a Bayesian framework that integrates past information into an empirical, time-aware prior and uses it to predict subsequent snapshots, thus enabling genuine \emph{out-of-sample} reconstruction. More specifically, our approach employs the fitness-based ERGM named \emph{density-corrected Gravity Model} (dcGM)~\citep{cimini2015} to calculate the posterior predictive distribution $P(\mathbf{A}_{t+1}|\mathbf{A}_t)$, i.e. the probability of observing a given configuration at time $t+1$, on the basis of the previous snapshot. Our framework turns the dcGM into a Bayesian Fitness Model (BFM) where the link probability $p_{ij}=zs_is_j/(1+zs_is_j)$ depends on the strengths of nodes, $\{s_i\}$, and a prior, $\pi(z)$, calibrated over a rolling window gathering three years of historical data: this prior, compatible with a Gamma distribution, evolves dynamically, as the estimate of the link density at time $t$ enriches it to carry out the prediction exercise at time $t+1$. When applied to the Electronic Market for Interbank Deposit (eMID) at the weekly time scale~\citep{cimini2015}, the BFM accurately reproduces both global and local properties. More detailedly, the BFM achieves a predictive accuracy of $\simeq80\%$ and a link-level precision that is roughly twice the one achieved in~\citep{parisi2018} by the Directed Binary Configuration Model, implemented to carry out the in-sample version of the same exercise. Most strikingly, each predicted configuration serves as a reliable prior for the next one: in symbols, $P(\mathbf{A}_{t+1}|\langle\mathbf{A}_t\rangle)$ can be employed to carry out a fully `self-sustained', out-of-sample reconstruction of ten years of the eMID evolution - an exercise requiring the knowledge of the strength sequence, the prior at $t=0$ and just one, empirical snapshot (i.e. $A_0$).
items.authors: Marzi, Mattia*; Squartini, Tiziano

items.title: "Reducing" transfer entropy for nonparametric network reconstruction from dynamics
items.abstract: The task of network reconstruction from dynamics, in which edges indicating functional dependence are inferred from time series observed on a set of nodes, has wide reaching applications across various scientific fields. Bayesian network reconstruction provides a principled means to reconstruct networks from dynamics, allowing for model selection and quantification of uncertainties. However, applying the standard Bayesian paradigm is challenging when the observed process is highly nonlinear (e.g. chaotic) and the underlying dynamics are unknown. For such dynamics, it is helpful to incorporate autoregressive dependencies through time delay embeddings, which are difficult to reproduce in inference objectives through explicit Bayesian generative models. With its capacity for capturing flexible nonparametric dependencies among time series, transfer entropy has become widely used for network reconstruction when the underlying dynamics are highly nonlinear, gaining popularity in research focusing on neural, climate, and other complex systems. However, when applied to discretized data, transfer entropy in its standard form exhibits two major shortcomings for network reconstruction: (a) It provides substantially inflated values for completely uncorrelated time series when the data is sparsely sampled; (b) It has no clear means of assessing statistical significance, requiring expensive simulations and the choice of an arbitrary significance level (which should be corrected for multiple comparisons and large sample sizes). These issues are fundamentally a result of treating the time series as instances of random variables---Shannon entropy is non-increasing under additional conditioning, so it is always ``worth'' (in an entropy sense) including the past time series values at a node i to predict the future time series values at a node j, regardless of how correlated these series actually are and how much information is already accounted for in j's past time series. By considering a combinatorial formulation of transfer entropy in which information is shared only during a single finite transmission event rather than among underlying stochastic processes, here we utilize the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle to alleviate the above issues and automatically reconstruct networks from discrete dynamics. Our approach results in a ``reduced'' transfer entropy which is asymptotically equivalent to the standard maximum likelihood plug-in estimator but introduces a finite-size correction that allows for negative transfer entropy values when it is more compressive to transmit the future time series values at node j using only its own time delay embedding than to also include i's time delay embedding. Our measure also allows for automatic selection of the optimal temporal lags for constructing the time delay embeddings, and can be generalized to compute a multivariate transfer entropy measure for reconstructing networks with non-redundant information sharing among nodes.
items.authors: Kirkley, Alec*

items.title: Collective Noise Filtering in Complex Networks
items.abstract: Networks are powerful representations of complex systems across scales and domains. However, real-world network data are often corrupted by noise in the edge weights, incomplete observations, and sampling biases, all of which undermine the reliability of downstream analyses. Standard approaches that filter noise independently at the individual edge level are fundamentally flawed, as both signal and noise often exhibit dependencies across many edges. As a solution, we introduce the Network Wiener Filter, a principled method for collective edge-level noise filtering that leverages network topology together with explicit noise characterization. Our framework reduces noise in observed edge weights and also predicts weights for missing edges. It applies seamlessly to binary, weighted, signed, and directed networks. We demonstrate the broad efficacy of the Network Wiener Filter in two applications from distinct domains: the Enron email communication network as a social setting and the pairwise genetic interaction network of the yeast \textit{Saccharomyces cerevisiae} as a biological setting, noting compelling evidence of enhanced signal-to-noise ratio in both. In the Enron network, our method effectively mitigates sampling noise arising from short temporal windows, while in the genetic interaction network, the filtered result shows improved agreement with established biological benchmarks. By enabling principled, error-aware reconstruction of network structure, the Network Wiener Filter advances a shift toward network science that embraces data imperfection as an inherent feature---and learns to navigate it effectively.
items.authors: Zhao, Tingyu*

items.title: A Review of Coarse-Graining Methods: Insights from Physics, Network Science, and Computer Science
items.abstract: Coarse-graining methods have become essential in the analysis of large and complex networks, offering ways to reduce dimensionality while preserving key structural and dynamical properties. These methods span across multiple disciplines, each with distinct goals and methodologies. In Physics, coarse-graining often focuses on preserving eigenvalues and is typically grounded in robust theoretical frameworks [1, 2]. In contrast, Computer Science prioritizes scalability, developing methods that handle larger graphs efficiently for storage and exploration. Recently, there has also been significant interest in adapting these methods to Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to reduce training costs while maintaining performance. Meanwhile, Network Science emphasizes preserving topological features such as degree distributions, motifs, and community structures, frequently linking these properties to dynamical processes like diffusion or synchronization. Despite the extensive body of work in this area, selecting the most suitable coarse-graining method for a given problem remains challenging. Many methods lack theoretical guarantees, raising concerns about their applicability to systems beyond their original domains. To address these gaps, a group of young researchers joined in 2023 and 2025 in the AccelNet-Multinet collabathons held in Zaragoza and Boston to tackle this problem. This collaboration resulted in a comprehensive review of coarse-graining methods and the creation of a Python package—CoarseNet—implementing over 25 algorithms. In this work, we present the results of our review, offering a detailed comparison of coarse-graining algorithms based on structural, topological, and dynamical properties. Specifically, we evaluate the ability of these methods to preserve features such as modularity, degree distribution, and spectral properties across diverse networks. We also examine their impact on dynamical processes, including diffusion models, random walks, and epidemic spreading. Our findings reveal trade-offs between computational efficiency and feature preservation, with certain methods excelling in capturing global structural patterns while others perform better in retaining local properties.
items.authors: Fernández Da Silva, Marco*

items.title: Equivalences between modularity and diverse low-dimensional representations
items.abstract: This work equates normalized and generalized versions of the modularity with variants of the k-means objective, spectral manifold learning objectives, and UMAP. These equivalences naturally lead to definitions of new representation objectives. As an example, we show that one of these objectives embeds brain-imaging data better than UMAP. Together, our results unify outwardly distinct representations across unsupervised learning, network science, and imaging neuroscience.
items.authors: Rubinov, Mikail*

items.title: Functional Graph Spectra
items.abstract: In this work I present a functional perspective on normalized graph spectra. By viewing the symmetric normalized eigenvalues of a graph as pointwise samples of an unknown spectral function, we can evaluate the spectrum at values other than 1,2,..n. This interpretation lets us 1) compare graphs of different sizes with the L2 norms of their respective functions; 2) derive objective functions for spectral graph reduction/editing; and 3) examine the distributions of spectral functions that arise from distributions of graphs. I will detail each of these, and present some data science applications as well as further questions that arise from treating graph spectra this way.
items.authors: Scott, Cory*

items.title: Expanding the policy toolbox: A global industry space captures regional diversification paths
items.abstract: It is well established that places (countries, cities, and regions) tend to diversify into new products, industries, and technologies that are related to their existing activities. In other words, this diversification and growth are driven by a path-dependent process through which regions evolve by entering new economic sectors that share many capabilities with those already present. To model this process, a range of country-specific industry networks has been deployed to predict the entry of new industries into regions. In this paper, we focus on inter-industry labour flow networks, where nodes represent industries and edges correspond to the number of workers transitioning between them. Specifically, we analyse the Skill-Relatedness Network as proposed by Neffke et al. (2017), where edges measure the degree of skill overlap (relatedness) between two industries, inferred from the number of workers moving between them. The underlying idea is that when two industries share a high degree of knowledge, workers are more likely to transition between them, as the skills acquired in one industry are highly valued in the other. This type of model has been highly successful in the policy sphere as it provides quite specific recommendations on which new industries might be feasible in a region based on its current industrial base. However, to date, these models are country-specific, and require detailed longitudinal individual-level data that is only available in a few countries. Here, we propose to build a ‘global network’ which combines data on inter-industry job transitions from multiple countries. This network can then be used to predict potential diversification routes for regions in any country. As a first step, we collect and analyse several country-specific networks, including those of Germany, Sweden, the Netherlands, Ireland, and the UK. For all networks, the industry classification follows the 4-digit NACE 2 system. Building on the insights from these country-specific analyses, we then construct the global network by utilising a combination of network science techniques, ranging from community detection to link prediction. In particular, we implemented a machine learning-based link prediction framework, where a set of topological and similarity-based features was extracted from the country-specific networks to train supervised classification algorithms. To validate the model, we use an econometric approach and demonstrate that it can predict diversification patterns for a set of countries not included in the network construction, including both middle- and high-income countries. We believe that this model is an important step towards expanding the current state-of-the art methodological approaches to the policy toolkit beyond a small set of data-rich countries.
items.authors: Mazzarino, Laura*; Landman, Mattie ; O'Clery, Neave

items.title: Revisiting Industry Adjacency: from Organizational to Production
items.abstract: As the economy becomes more complex and innovation continually reshapes industrial boundaries, firms increasingly operate across multiple industries rather than within a single, well-defined domain. This raises an organizational question: how are industries combined within firms? From a classic perspective, a firm can be understood as an organizational entity that coordinates multiple economic activities under a common managerial and financial boundary. At the same time, new activities often emerge through the recombination of existing capabilities, and established firms frequently internalize these combinations through diversified organizational structures [1]. Here, we examine how industries are jointly organized within firms by analyzing operating segments—managerially defined units used for internal oversight and external reporting (10-K disclosures)—across 7,159 publicly listed firms between 2017 and 2022 in Compustat. Firms may report multiple segments when they conduct distinct business activities that are reviewed separately by top management. From this, we construct an industry co-occurrence network in which two industries are connected when the same firm operates segments in both. Figure 1 shows the resulting network at the 2-digit NAICS level (Fig.1A–B), how such bundling scales with firm size and sales (Fig.1C–D), and how diversification is distributed across the economy (Fig.1E). Several stylized facts emerge. (1) The network does not separate cleanly into distinct clusters as segment definitions follow managerial organization rather than industrial classification [2]. Segment labels such as “Platforms,” “Solutions,” or “Corporate,” for example, bundle heterogeneous activities that do not map one-to-one onto NAICS industries. (2) Larger firms operate across more segments (Fig. 1D) with highly diversified holding companies and conglomerates, such as Compass Diversified Holdings and Berkshire Hathaway, and technology firms such as Alphabet. At the industry level, knowledge-intensive sectors—particularly (51: Information) and (54: Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services)—frequently co-occur within the same firm, indicating redeployment of capabilities across adjacent domains. Additional stylized facts are explored in the remainder of the paper. Do more diversified firms transition more frequently across industries (entries and exits)? We quantify them using two standard measures — the Shannon entropy and the Herfindahl–Hirschman Index given the sales share of firm in industry and the number of industries that a firm belongs to (Fig. 1(E)). Conceptually, segment-based industry networks capture organizational scope — how firms bundle activities, allocate managerial attention, and redeploy capabilities — rather than production or technological similarity [2]. Simply put, segment NAICS encode organizational boundaries, whereas establishment-level NAICS encode production boundaries. Revisiting the measure will complement existing relatedness measures in regional science and economic complexity, and provides a structural view of cross-industry organization.
items.authors: Kwon, Hee Youn*; Lee, Joon Mahn; Park, Hyunwoo; Youn, Hyejin

items.title: Inferring firm-level supply chain networks with realistic systemic risk from industry sector-level data
items.abstract: Production networks form the structural backbone of modern economies, yet recent crises have exposed their intrinsic vulnerability. Quantifying the system-wide impact of local disruptions—i.e., systemic risk—requires detailed knowledge of supply chain networks (SCNs), since such risks depend sensitively on their underlying topological patterns. In practice, however, microscopic SCN data are rarely available [1], forcing systemic-risk estimates to rely on coarse sector-level information, such as input–output tables, and aggregate production data at the firm-level [2]. Here we assess the ability of maximum-entropy approaches [3] to reconstruct SCNs with realistic level of systemic risk, when only sector- and firm-level constraints are provided. Leveraging the unique availability of the complete production network of Ecuador, we benchmark four reconstruction models with different formulations and data requirements, by comparing the inferred structural and systemic-risk properties (particularly, the Economic Systemic Risk Index [4]) with those of the real network. We find that the model incorporating firm-specific input requirements disaggregated by sector [5] most accurately reproduces the systemic-risk content of the empirical network, underscoring the importance of capturing heterogeneity in firms’ input profiles. We further show how this information can be inferred from aggregate firm-level data and input-output tables. Our results identify the minimal sector-level information needed to statistically generate synthetic SCNs that faithfully encode firm-level systemic risk. The proposed methodology is model-based and fully interpretable, reconstructing the network from aggregate information without ever training on real network instances.
items.authors: Fessina, Massimiliano; Cimini, Giulio*; Squartini, Tiziano; Astudillo-Estevez, Pablo; Thurner, Stephan; Garlaschelli, Diego

items.title: Analyzing corporate exports with network science tools to predict firm growth
items.abstract: Network science methods have been extensively applied to various areas of economics, from finance to the development and industrialization of countries. Firm level investigations, however, are lagging behind, mostly because of the difficulties of obtaining data for micro units. From an economic viewpoint, however, a rich theoretical and empirical literature investigated the link between export diversification and firm performance. While prior theoretical works hinted at the key role of capability accumulation in shaping production activities and performance, product-level indicators able to forecast corporate growth are still lacking. In our work [1], we borrow tools from the economic complexity framework, which usually analyses country level capabilities and growth starting from the bipartite network which connects countries with the products they export. In particular, we examine which characteristics of a firm's export basket predict future performance. We analyze a unique longitudinal dataset that covers export and financial data for 12,852 Italian firms, and which allows us to define a bipartite firm-product network (see Figure 1, left). We find that firms exporting products typically exported by wealthier countries -- a proxy for greater product sophistication and market value [2] -- tend to experience higher growth and profit per employee (see Figure 1, right). Moreover, we find that diversification outside of a firm's core production area is positively associated with future growth, whereas diversification within the core is negatively associated. This is revealed by introducing novel measures of in-block and out-of-block diversification, based on algorithmically-detected production blocks. A key point is the use of suitable community detection algorithms, as the use of the blocks defined by the UN classifications does not lead to statistically relevant results. In other words, the data-driven methods based on network science are key to identifying the proper ecosystem of each company, defined by the set of products mainly exported by it and its competitors - their "production core". Our findings suggest that growth is driven not simply by how many products a firm exports, but also by where these products lie within the production ecosystem, at both local and global scales.
items.authors: De Stefano, Valerio; Mula, Maddalena; Mariani, Manuel Sebastian; Zaccaria, Andrea*

items.title: The structure of domestic production networks and production efficiency: the dual economy paradox
items.abstract: European economies exhibit substantial heterogeneity in the economic role played by foreign‑owned firms [1]. While these firms may introduce advanced technologies to less developed regions, multinational enterprises (MNEs) often rely on pre-existing supplier networks, which are frequently foreign-owned, rather than sourcing inputs from local firms. This limits the positive external effects of MNEs on productivity and growth [2]. Using the OECD Analytical AMNE database, this study examines the degree of integration between domestic and foreign-owned firms within individual economies at the sectoral level, focusing on supplier and sales linkages. As illustrated by the contrasting cases of Slovakia and Germany in Panels a) and b) of Figure 1, there is significant cross-country variation, with less developed economies displaying markedly weaker connections between the two groups of firms. We construct a measure of economic duality that captures the strength of these domestic-foreign linkages within domestic production networks, and demonstrate its relevance for economic policy. In the short run, higher duality -- reflecting weaker domestic-foreign connections -- is associated with investment patterns dominated by manufacturing activities, where efficiency gains from established international supplier networks and export‑oriented production play a central role. In the long run, however, limited supplier and sales relationships hinder the diffusion of positive externalities from foreign to domestic firms. Econometric analysis confirms this paradox, which is illustrated with a descriptive example in Panels c) and d) of Figure 1: while increasing duality is linked to improved resource efficiency in the short term, it is negatively associated with efficient resource allocation over longer horizons. These findings underscore the importance of strengthening domestic-foreign linkages to promote sustainable, endogenous economic growth [3]. [1] Nölke, A., and Vliegenthart, A. (2009). Enlarging the varieties of capitalism: The emergence of dependent market economies in East Central Europe. World Politics, 61(4):670-702. [2] Wang, J.-Y., and Blomström, M. (1992). Foreign investment and technology transfer: A simple model. European Economic Review, 36(1):137–155. [3] Romer, P. M. (1990). Endogenous technological change. Journal of Political Economy, 98(5/2):71-102.
items.authors: Braun, Erik*; Sebestyén, Tamás; Gyimesi, András; Longauer, Dóra; Iloskics, Zita

items.title: Supplier network connections to multinational enterprises enhance export performance of domestic firms
items.abstract: Multinational enterprises (MNEs) play a key role in modern economies, connecting production processes across countries and acting as powerful economic actors [1]. MNEs often possess more efficient production technologies and superior export capabilities compared to domestic firms, serving as crucial gatekeepers that enable local economies to enter global value chains [2]. While connecting with these MNEs offer opportunities for domestic firms to participate in GVCs, long-term economic development eventually depends on domestic firms becoming globally competitive [3]. This study investigates whether domestic firms learn from their supplier relationships with MNEs, improving their export performance as a result. Using firm-level data on national buyer-supplier ties and international trade patterns in Hungary (2016–2019), we show that firms supplying to MNEs are more likely to start exporting and achieve greater export growth compared to firms without such connections. From a policy perspective, these findings suggest that domestic firms can indeed benefit from MNEs through interfirm learning and access to international markets. However, the number of domestic firms capable of becoming MNE suppliers remains limited. Figure 1 illustrates the empirical framework (on the left) and provides early results from multiple regressions with export outcomes as the dependent variable (on the right), highlighting the positive effect of MNE connections on export performance.
items.authors: Sebestyén, Tamás*; Braun, Erik; Juhász, Sándor

items.title: Dynamics between air transport network structure and urban economic activity
items.abstract: Air transport networks provide long-distance connectivity between cities, yet their dynamic relationship with urban economic activity remains insufficiently understood from a network-structural perspective. This study examines the temporal interactions between air transport network structure and urban economic activity using a global panel covering the world’s 50 major airports between 2014 and 2024. Air transport network structure is characterised using the Global Airport Connectivity Index (GACI), which captures multiple dimensions of the structure of air transport network. Urban economic activity is proxied by satellite-derived night-time light (NTL) intensity, which has been widely used in the literature as a consistent and spatially comparable proxy for economic activity. To disentangle interaction directions and temporal scales, we employ a dynamic time-series framework combining seasonal adjustment, unit-root tests, short-run causality analysis based on the Toda–Yamamoto procedure, and long-run relationship analysis using autoregressive distributed lag (ARDL) models. Here, short-run dynamics refer to month-scale fluctuations, whereas long-run relationships capture equilibrium adjustments over multi-year horizons. The results reveal a clear contrast across time scales. In the short run, causal relationship is rarely detected in either direction, indicating strong inertia and limited immediate responses between the two systems. In the long run, however, we find robust evidence of stable co-evolution between air transport connectivity and urban economic activity. The spatial distribution of long-run relationships highlights pronounced regional heterogeneity. While bidirectional co-evolution dominates globally, cities in China exhibit a distinct network-led pattern in which changes in air transport network structure precede long-term adjustments in urban economic activity. These findings suggest that the interaction between aviation networks and urban economies is governed by slow-moving structural adjustment processes rather than immediate demand-driven responses. By integrating network connectivity measures with satellite-based economic proxies in a multi-scale dynamic framework, this study contributes to network science by clarifying how infrastructure networks and urban systems co-evolve across space and time.
items.authors: Sugishita, Kashin*; Kajikawa, Shunsuke; Hanaoka, Shinya

items.title: Correcting temporal bias in mobility data using time-use surveys
items.abstract: Mobility data generated by mobile phones have been routinely used to measure patterns of social and economic activity in cities [1]. Despite its potential, mobility data suffers from well-documented limitations that can reduce the accuracy of these insights. These include {\bf demographic biases}, where certain sub-populations are over- or under-represented in mobility data, and {\bf temporal biases}, such as sample ‘drift’ over time or changes in individuals’ mobile device usage, which may affect the behaviors captured in these datasets. In this paper, we address temporal bias by benchmarking the American Time Use Survey (ATUS) [2], a high-quality, demographically representative survey of self-reported activities in the U.S. (Figure 1a) against observed behavior recorded by mobility data (Figure 1b). Using this approach, we quantify the mismatch between activities recorded in mobility data with those reported in ATUS across time of day and income groups (Figure 1c). We then apply these differences to re-weight mobility data in a process similar to post-stratification, which is commonly used to address demographic bias. Specifically, we examine the extent to which reweighting mobility data according to the ATUS impacts estimates of experienced income segregation at thousands of points of interest (POIs) in 11 metro-areas in the U.S., estimated via the method proposed by Moro, et al. [3]. We show that, while overall levels of segregation remain similar after adjusting for the temporal bias, there are still some significant differences at specific types of POIs and times of day. Our results show that, although aggregate measures of income segregation are relatively stable, re-weighting mobility data using external benchmarks, such as the ATUS, can reveal significant shifts in specific places and income groups, underscoring the value of correcting for behavioral and temporal bias. Our work highlights the importance of correcting for temporal bias when using mobility data to measure urban inequality, which has the potential to skew understanding of large-scale patterns of human behavior in cities. [1] F. Xu, Q. Wang, E. Moro, et al., “Using human mobility data to quantify experienced urban inequalities,” Nat. Hum. Behav. pp. 1–11 (2025). [2] U.S. Bureau of Labor and Statistics, American Time Use Survey (2017). [3] E. Moro, D. Calacci, X. Dong, and A. Pentland, “Mobility patterns are associated with experienced income segregation in large US cities,” Nat. Commun. 12, 4633 (2021).
items.authors: Sanchez, Sarah*; Gibbs, Hamish; Yabe, Takahiro; O'Brien, Daniel; Moro, Esteban

items.title: Network Resilience under Chronic Disruption: Contested Road Networks in Ukraine
items.abstract: Modern transportation networks supporting military and civilian logistics increasingly operate under persistent adver- sarial disruption. In contested environments, infrastructure undergoes continuous degradation, intermittently punctuated by acute disruption surges. As full recovery may not be achievable, understanding resilience in this context requires network models that capture the temporal dynamics of both functional loss and restoration. We present a framework that integrates network science with GIS to analyze the resilience of transportation systems under chronic stress, with application to military logistics. Using real-world armed conflict data, we model the degradation and repair of Ukraine’s major road network over the three years of the 2022 Russo-Ukrainian war shown in Fig. 1. The network is constructed from GIS road data, with origin–destination pairs representing logistical entry points and active war fronts. Georeferenced conflict events are translated into road disruptions that evolve across both time and space. Distinct from traditional percolation models that treat edges as strictly connected or disconnected, our approach assigns paths a continuous functionality score based on travel-time increases. In this formulation, routes transition from fully functional to delayed and eventually disconnected as disruptions accumulate. Network performance is quantified by aggregating functionality scores across all origin–destination paths, generalizing binary percolation into a weighted, time-dependent framework suited to logistics applications. Through simulations of disruption and repair, we observe a transition from a collapsed to a functioning network state as repair capacity increases. Below a critical threshold, ongoing disruptions overwhelm restoration efforts and the network remains fragmented. Above this threshold, functionality recovers rapidly before entering a saturation regime in which additional repair capacity yields diminishing returns, consistent with a second-order phase transition show in Fig. 2. Furthermore, we examine restoration strategies under varying resource constraints. Priority-based repair, guided by network-science metrics such as betweenness centrality, consistently outperforms first-in-first-out restoration at equal capacity as shown in Fig. 3. Prioritization shifts the critical threshold, enabling recovery with fewer resources and enhancing robustness against cascading failures. We also find that temporary surges in repair capacity can efficiently move the system across the collapse–recovery boundary when applied at appropriate phases. However, poorly timed surges may degrade performance, underscoring the nontrivial interactions between network structure and recovery dynamics. These findings show that resilience in contested logistics depends not only on the amount of repair resources, but on phase-aware deployment and strategic prioritization. Maintaining the minimum repair capacity is essential, while effective prioritization of repairs provides the greatest performance gains. When prioritization is limited, resource surging can serve as a complementary strategy, but must be applied carefully to avoid negative effects. This framework provides a quantitative basis for designing resilient military and civilian critical infrastructure under persistent attack.
items.authors: Chung, Sukhwan*; Sardak, Daniel; Jin, Andrew; Linkov, Igor

items.title: Unveiling temporal patterns in the maritime tanker network
items.abstract: The global maritime oil tanker network is a critical infrastructure for the world's energy and fuel sector, handling an estimated 70% of global oil trade. Unlike other maritime industries, crude oil market volatility drives uncertainty in the tanker shipping sector, resulting in complex traversal patterns as ships respond to changing market conditions. Traditional complex systems analysis of maritime trade predominantly employs static network representations that mask the rich temporal information embedded in tanker movements. We introduce a dual-scale framework utilizing sequential motifs and Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD) to explore dynamical patterns at both individual vessel and fleet levels. Analyzing 192,000 voyage segments across four tanker classes representing 3,026 vessels from Jan 2016 to Feb 2020, our findings indicate that short-term route diversification---captured through sequential motifs---correlates significantly with the laden-ballast ratio, a routing performance measure defined as the proportion of time carrying cargo versus total travel time. High-performing vessels favor ABC motifs (visiting three distinct regions consecutively) over AAA motifs (remaining within single regions), achieving laden-ballast ratios approximately 50% higher. At the system level, we reconstruct directional cargo flows in and out of regional sectors over time. DMD of these flows reveals global seasonalities with a dominant period of 51.5 weeks, corresponding to an annual cycle. Phase analysis shows that major importing regions (East Asia, Europe) display synchronous annual demand, while export regions (Middle East, South America) exhibit complementary anti-synchronous cycles relative to the import regions. These temporal patterns, invisible to static analysis, reveal how complex temporality operates across multiple network scales, offering practical insights for operational optimization in maritime infrastructure.
items.authors: Teo, Kevin*; Kiss, Istvan ; Arnold, Naomi; Hone, Andrew ; Coulon, Michael; Santillana, Mauricio

items.title: Emergent Scaling Laws in Drainage Networks Shaped by Probabilistic Growth and Landscape Boundaries
items.abstract: Drainage networks formed by natural landscape evolution processes self-organize to exhibit universal scaling laws. However, the underlying mechanisms behind these emergent patterns remain unclear. We introduce a probabilistic model that simulates the headward growth of drainage networks through probabilistic decisions based on local network properties such as drainage area and channel length. The resulting networks robustly reproduce key scaling relationships observed in natural drainage systems, including power-law distributions of contributing area and upstream length, as well as Hack’s law, which describes the scaling between channel length and drainage basin area. Furthermore, the distribution of individual basin areas across a landscape is observed to follow a power law, with the scaling exponent increasing with boundary complexity. Our analysis of 25 island landscapes reveals a systematic increase in the basin area distribution exponent with increasing boundary complexity, quantified using fractal dimension and compactness metrics. To explain this relationship, we apply our probabilistic network evolution model to landscapes with varying boundary complexities and demonstrate that more complex boundaries influence network competition during growth, leading to basin size distributions with higher scaling exponents. Thus, the headward growth mechanism in the proposed model provides a unified explanation for both power-law scaling in basin areas and the positive correlation between the scaling exponent and the fractal dimension of landscape boundaries. These findings highlight the active role of boundaries in shaping basin area distributions and demonstrate how stochastic network growth mechanisms grounded in key physical processes can generate the universal scaling patterns observed in drainage networks.
items.authors: Borse, Dnyanesh*; Biswal, Basudev; Singh, Arvind

items.title: Causal discovery of housing-mobility relations in urban residential migration networks
items.abstract: Urban life is increasingly understood as the result of complex processes where neighborhood characteristic such as housing, physical infrastructure, employment opportunities, amenity access, and neighborhood context shape individuals’ social connections, access to opportunity, and patterns of residential mobility. A growing body of work adopts a network science perspective by representing cities as graphs whose nodes are neighborhoods and whose links encode flows of people, capital, businesses, or information [1]. However, most empirical data on urban networks are constructed from mobile devices and IoT streams, which emphasize high-frequency mobility dynamics over long-term residential change. Existing research therefore offers limited insight into how residential mobility networks are influenced by contextual factors at fine spatial scales, and largely relies on correlation-based analyses instead of explicit causal inference [2]. To address these gaps, this project develops a causal study of how housing, amenities, and demographics shape annual residential migration across census tracts in two large US cities. This work investigates the causal relations between neighborhood’s housing, amenity, and demographic characteristics and tract-level residential migration, under the hypothesis that housing conditions exert a causal influence on migration while population demographics and spatial location patterns act as confounders. This study is informed by prior work [3] that uses a reinforcement learning–based causal computing framework to reveal causal and confounding relations among urban elements; adopting a similar causal perspective and data setup, we extend the focus to annual residential mobility in large U.S. cities. We construct four classes of urban elements: housing conditions and population demographics from the U.S. Census Bureau, amenities from OpenStreetMap, and annual residential migration from MIGRATE [4]. Census tracts define the nodes of a migration network, edges connect tracts with nonzero annual flows, and tracts are stratified by income level to aggregate inflow and outflow volumes into tract-level migration variables. Using Greedy Equivalence Search with expert constraints, followed by Double Machine Learning for causal effect estimation, we infer causal diagrams among the four urban elements (i.e., demographics, amenities, housing conditions, and residential migration) for New York City, NY, and Houston, TX (2015–2019). Our method reveals consistent patterns where median household income drives migration inflow from high-income census tracts. Yet the strength and direction of causal effects vary across cities and time periods. For instance, in New York City, a higher share of residents receiving SNAP benefits leads to reduced migration inflows from mid- to high-income tracts, a pattern absent in Houston. These findings highlight both the promise of causal discovery for uncovering systematic forms of socioeconomic segregation and the sensitivity of inferred relationships to underlying urban conditions and data aggregation.
items.authors: Yang, Xiyu*; Gibbs, Hamish; Santillana, Mauricio; Moro, Esteban

items.title: Disassortative community structure can induce oscillations and travelling waves in discrete excitable systems
items.abstract: Networks of discrete-state excitable systems can be used as simple models of activity in functional brain networks, epidemic spreading, and the propagation of social behavior like protest activity or the adoption of innovations. The macroscopic dynamics of such networks has received much attention due to their ability to reproduce many of the experimental observations that support the hypothesis that the brain operates at criticality, the onset of self-sustained activity that occurs as the strength of the coupling between the excitable systems increases. Previous work studying the bifurcations that occur as the excitability of the network is increased has focused mostly on networks without community structure. We study a stochastic discrete-state model with a refractory period on a network with community structure, and study how the dynamics depends on both the structure of the coupling of the communities, and on the network’s assortativity, which we quantify by the ratio of intra- to inter-community coupling. Using a mean-field description of the system, we show that additional bifurcations occur as the community structure of the network turns from assortative to disassortative. Depending on the structure of the network of communities, various bifurcations can occur, including period-doubling bifurcations, Hopf bifurcations, and a heteroclinic bifurcation as complete disassortativity is approached. Some of these bifurcations can lead to traveling waves when the network of communities is coupled on a ring structure. We illustrate our theoretical results with numerical simulations on different networks.
items.authors: Kritpracha, Poom*; Taylor, Dane; Restrepo, Juan

items.title: Partial coalescence of random walks on networks
items.abstract: This work introduces a model of multiple random walkers whose dynamics interpolate between independent and coalescing random walks on networks. Links activate stochastically; walkers at a node independently choose to follow an available temporal link with jump probability p. Co-located walkers see the same activation events, meaning the steps they take can coincide. While individual node occupancies fluctuate, the distribution of occupancies is stationary and depends on p.
