We derive a master equation for occupancy and identify a new dynamical regime (“partial coalescence”) where nodes regularly find themselves empty of walkers no matter how many walkers there are.
items.authors: Mattsson, Carolina*; Coutinho, Bruno; Starnini, Michele

items.title: Evolution of Conditional Entropy for Diffusion Dynamics on Graphs
items.abstract: Diffusion processes on graphs underpin widely-used tools in network science and machine learning. Various information- theoretic have been used to study these processes: recent works in network science include studies of the entropy production, a measure of the divergence of a process from detailed balance, and Von Neumann entropy, originally introduced in the field of quantum information. While the steady-state behavior of diffusion processes is well understood based on Markov chain theory, comparatively few exact results describe their time evolution. Here [3], we characterized this evolution through conditional entropy. Let {Z(t)} denote the heat diffusion on a graph, modeled as a continuous-time Markov chain. We consider the conditional entropy curve H(Z(t) | Z(0)), i.e., an average of row-wise Shannon entropies of the heat kernel. We show that this quantity admits a natural thermodynamic interpretation: mass conservation yields a first-law analogue, and, on connected graphs, H(Z(t) | Z(0)) is monotone non-decreasing in time (a second-law analogue). We then obtain explicit spectral results for complete graphs, path graphs, and families of circulant graphs. These formulas confirm the thermodynamic intuition and yield interpretable structural comparisons. In particular, the entropy growth is strongly affected by how local or long-range the available steps are in circulant graphs: enlarging the step-set accelerates mixing, while holding degree fixed but changing step lengths still produces distinct transient regimes. Additionally, we show that conditional entropy remains informative on random graph ensembles. For ER graphs, fully analytical expressions are not available; we propose a mean-field approximation that captures the qualitative dependence on edge density and improves as density increases. Finally, we discuss asymptotic behavior and heuristically using path-graph and complete-graph curves as entropy growth bounds. Overall our work highlights that, from the perspective of conditional entropy, heat diffusion on graphs behaves similarly to heat diffusion on a solid medium in the real world. This work paves the way for the development and understanding of physics-inspired methods in applications such as detecting change points and periodic patterns in temporal networks.
items.authors: Koovely, Samuel*; Bovet, Alexandre

items.title: Tempological Control of Network Dynamics
items.abstract: The normal functioning of many complex systems requires them to operate near a prescribed dynamical state. To this end, built and natural systems alike–from flocking birds to power grids–employ a strategy of feedback control to stabilize desirable (but otherwise unstable) dynamical states. Unfortunately, this strategy requires continuous control input to counteract deviations from the functional state, which can be invasive and demand considerable resources. Here, for networked systems whose function relies on the synchronization of their components, we introduce tempological (temporal + topological) control–a noninvasive feedback control scheme that stabilizes synchronization without any control input. Instead, our strategy works by making sporadic but deliberate changes to the coupling network on-the-fly, based on the dynamical states of the nodes. We show that by implementing this control strategy, a temporal network can achieve synchronization even if all subnetworks (snapshots) are individually unstable. We demonstrate the utility of our approach numerically using two canonical nonlinear models for synchronization: the Kuramoto and Stuart-Landau systems. We also establish probabilistic guarantees on the success rate of “tempological” control. In particular, we develop a statistical theory that shows how—in the limit of large temporal networks, success is virtually guaranteed—with diversity of network snapshots overcoming the deficiencies of any snapshot in isolation.
items.authors: Zhang, Yuanzhao; Cornelius, Sean*

items.title: Analytical prediction of structure-function relationships in networks of Kuramoto oscillators
items.abstract: Mapping structural to functional connectivity remains a central challenge in the study of networked dynamical systems, particularly in computational neuroscience. Although the Kuramoto model provides a standard framework for relating network structure to function, most theoretical work has focused on how coarse network features affect collective synchronization, relying either on mean-field approaches in the thermodynamic limit or on low-dimensional reduction techniques. These approaches impose assumptions on system size or topology, leaving open how general network structure shapes pairwise correlations between oscillators. Here, we show that in the weak-coupling regime, pairwise correlations driven by local resonances provide a direct signature of topology that is valid for any number of oscillators. We consider a network of N Kuramoto oscillators with phases evolving in time, coupled through a weighted adjacency matrix K with global coupling and heterogeneous natural frequencies drawn from a Cauchy-Lorentz distribution. Using a second-order perturbative expansion in the rescaled coupling, we extract the stationary contributions to the correlation matrix defined over an integration time, in the long-time limit, by averaging over natural frequencies drawn from this distribution. This averaging weights the local resonant modes that generate stationary correlations. As illustrated in Figure 1a, stationary correlations in the weak-coupling regime arise solely from second-order indirect interactions, while first-order direct interactions vanish. As a result, structurally disconnected nodes (j,k) become functionally correlated through shared neighbors (l). This yields a closed-form prediction. The analytical predictions are validated by extensive numerical simulations across multiple network topologies. Figure 1b illustrates an application to the zebrafish structural connectome. At weak coupling, the analytical expression accurately reproduces the structure-function relationship observed in simulations. Up to the stall coupling strength, beyond which the approximation breaks down, the cosine similarity between the analytical prediction and the correlation matrix obtained from numerical integration of the Kuramoto dynamics remains high (0.977 ± 0.004). This robust correspondence suggests that the approach can be extended to other statistics, such as phase locking, and to broader classes of network dynamics.
items.authors: Légaré, Arthur*; Desrosiers, Patrick; Doyon, Nicolas

items.title: Learning-Driven Reorganization of Neural Tissue Networks
items.abstract: Neural systems exhibit spontaneous bursting activity that reflects structured, collective dynamics rather than independent neuronal firing [1]. Across brain regions and preparations, bursts have been implicated in communication, plasticity, and learning, yet how burst structure reorganizes during learning remains poorly understood. In particular, it is unclear whether learning modifies bursts merely in rate and timing, or whether it reshapes the underlying network states that bursts express. Here, we investigate learning-induced changes in bursting dynamics using dynamic functional connectivity (DFC) as a state-based representation of population activity [2]. We study dissociated cortical cultures embodied in our closed-loop training framework (Holistic Agential Learning, HAL), in which ongoing electrophysiological activity is monitored in real time and stimulation is adaptively applied to reinforce task-relevant behavior. Cultures are trained on a task in which bursts are reinforced to initiate on a specific side of the array. To probe how bursting reorganizes with learning, we represent the culture as a network whose nodes correspond to recording channels on the multielectrode array. Dynamic functional connectivity is estimated within and across bursts, allowing us to track how patterns of coordination between channels evolve as learning progresses. Bursts are treated as temporally localized network states, enabling comparisons of functional connectivity structure both within individual bursts and between successive bursts over training. Using this framework, we examine whether learning induces systematic changes in the spatial organization of functional connectivity, alters the internal structure of bursts associated with different initiation locations, and reshapes how activity propagates across the network. We further extend this analysis using directed measures of interaction to assess how information flow during bursts differs across learning stages and task conditions, consistent with state-dependent reorganization of network hubs and information processing [4]. Beyond the specific task studied here, this work demonstrates HAL as a general platform for probing learning, plasticity, and state-dependent information processing in adaptive biological networks under closed-loop control.
items.authors: Clawson, Wesley*; Grasso, Caitlin; Palmer, Sydney; Steadman, Danielle; Levin, Michael

items.title: Unveiling the importance of nonshortest paths in quantum networks
items.abstract: Quantum networks (QNs) exhibit stronger connectivity than predicted by classical percolation, yet the origin of this phenomenon remains unexplored. We apply a statistical physics model—concurrence percolation—to uncover the origin of stronger connectivity on hierarchical scale-free networks, the (U, V) flowers. These networks allow full analytical control over path connectivity through two adjustable path-length parameters, ≤V. This precise control enables us to determine critical exponents well beyond current simulation limits, revealing that classical and concurrence percolations, while both satisfying the hyperscaling relation, fall into distinct universality classes. This distinction arises from how they “superpose” parallel, nonshortest path contributions into overall connectivity. Concurrence percolation, unlike its classical counterpart, is sensitive to nonshortest paths and shows higher resilience to detours as these paths lengthen. This enhanced resilience is also observed in real-world hierarchical, scale-free internet networks. Our findings highlight a crucial principle for QN design: When nonshortest paths are abundant, they notably enhance QN connectivity beyond what is achievable with classical percolation.
items.authors: Sun, Hanlin*

items.title: Shortest-path percolation on scale-free networks
items.abstract: The shortest-path percolation (SPP) model aims at describing the consumption, and eventual exhaustion, of resources on networks [1]. Starting from a network with a macroscopic connected component, random pairs of nodes are sequentially selected, and, if the shortest-path length connecting the node pairs is smaller than a tunable budget parameter, then all edges along such a path are removed from the network. As edges are progressively removed, the network eventually breaks into multiple microscopic components, undergoing a percolation-like transition. It is known that SPP transition on Erdős–Rényi networks (ERNs) belongs to same universality class as of the ordinary bond percolation if the budget parameter is finite; for unbounded budget instead, the SPP transition becomes more abrupt than the ordinary percolation transition. By means of extensive numerical simulations and finite-size scaling analysis, we study the SPP transition on random scale-free networks (SFNs) characterized by power-law degree distributions. We find, in contrast with ordinary percolation, that the transition is identical to the one observed on ERNs, denoting independence from the degree exponent [2]. Still, we distinguish finite- and infinite-budget SPP universality classes. Our findings follow from the fact that the SPP process drastically homogenizes the heterogeneous structure of SFNs before the SPP transition occurs.
items.authors: Kim, Minsuk*; Cirigliano, Lorenzo; Castellano, Claudio; Sun, Hanlin; Jankowski, Robert; Poggialini, Anna; Radicchi, Filippo

items.title: Robustness of Directed Networks to Generalized Edge Set Removal
items.abstract: The robustness of networks to the failure of nodes and edges is a cornerstone of network analysis. Shortest path percolation (SPP) has recently been proposed to model the simultaneous failure of edges along shortest paths. At each time step in SPP, a random origin and destination node are chosen and all of the edges along one shortest path between them are removed if the path length is smaller than a parameter C [1]. Studies of this model have found that with finite C, SPP is in the same universality class as traditional edge percolation on both random Erdos-Renyi and scale-free networks, while with infinite C the transition is more abrupt [1, 2]. Further, an application to a weighted, directed, temporal, and multi-layer line graph representation of a flight network found a transition from a phase where passengers can successfully navigate the network to a disconnected phase where many itineraries are not satisfiable [3]. Inspired by this line of research, here we investigate generalized failure scenarios involving the removal of sets of edges of varying sizes from a network. We develop a general framework where we first define a universe of possible sets of edges, then a (dynamic) ranking function that gives a probability for simultaneous failure at a given time in the process. Recast in this framework, the universe of SPP is all of the available shortest paths, and the probability of choosing a path is that of first selecting its origin and destination nodes, then selecting the specific path among all shortest paths between that pair. In our framework, however, sets of edges may be defined not only by the network topology, but also via other mechanisms, such as dynamical processes (e.g., random walks), or based on data (e.g., observed walk statistics). Figure 1 shows preliminary results on a directed network built from the Wikispeedia game dataset, where users are placed on a random Wikipedia page and tasked with navigating internal hyperlinks to find a target page [4]. We use all of the approximately 51k observed walks in the dataset corresponding to successful games and construct a directed graph with approximately 4k nodes and 60k edges. Figure 1 shows results attacking this network using 5 attack strategies that sample a walk or path through the graph. The first 3 strategies attack the network by removing whole walks from the input data, essentially iteratively downsampling the observed walks. Observed walks are sampled for removal either (i) uniformly at random, (ii) based on their static length (ignoring removed edges), or (iii) based on dynamic length (accounting for removed edges). In contrast, the last 2 strategies attack the network based on (iv) random walks and (v) shortest paths. The random walk attack chooses a random node, then simulates a random walk from that node with length up to the length of a randomly sampled observed walk. The shortest path strategy chooses a random node and a random BFS descendant of that node, then removes a shortest path from the source to the descendant node (different from SPP only because the sampled target node depends on the sampled source node). Our preliminary results indicate that the random walk and shortest path based approaches are more effective at breaking up the largest weakly connected component of the network than removing observed walks (c-d). Of the 3 strategies based on downsampling the observed walks, only the dynamic length-based sampling is effective. The uniform walk and static-length-based approaches are qualitatively worse than all other methods, suggesting that substantial redundancy in the observed walks can lead to the same edges being sampled multiple times despite already having been removed from the network. In future work we plan to explore more sophisticated edge set sampling functions based on higher-order network science representations such as de Bruijn graphs. Further, since the size of the largest weakly connected component may not always be the most important factor in robustness of directed and weighted networks, we will consider alternative metrics. Finally, we will apply this framework to infrastructure and mobility systems, especially transportation. [1] Kim, & Radicchi, Phys. Rev. Lett., 133(4), 2024. https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevLett.133.047402 [2] Kim et al., arXiv preprint arXiv:2509.09142}, 2025. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2509.09142 [3] Kim, Tyler, & Radicchi, Nat. Comm., 16(1), 2025. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-63489-w [4] West, Pineau, & Precup, IJCAI'09, 2009. https://dl.acm.org/doi/abs/10.5555/1661445.1661702
items.authors: LaRock, Timothy*; Hackl, Jürgen

items.title: Feedback percolation on networks
items.abstract: Percolation models provide a fundamental framework for understanding connectivity, robustness, and phase transitions in complex networks. Most studies of percolation assume fixed activation probabilities for each node [1]. In many real-world systems, however, the activation probability of nodes and the size of the giant component often mutually influence each other. This feedback mechanism captures situations where local activation is influenced by global connectivity such as Hebbian plasticity in neural systems and the interplay between the environment and interactions in social and ecological systems. In this work, we introduce a feedback percolation model, in which the probability of activating sites dynamically depends on the size of the giant component. We study both positive and negative feedback in percolation models by varying the feedback strength $a$. We analyze the impact of the feedback on the size of the giant component both numerically and analytically. Under positive feedback, the system shows a continuous percolation transition independent of the value of $a$, followed by an additional discontinuous jump in the size of the giant component, whose location approaches the percolation threshold $p_c$ as the feedback strength increases. In contrast, negative feedback leads to a discontinuous transition with an increasing percolation threshold as the feedback strength $a$ increases. We also discuss how the feedback mechanism in percolation models provides insight into several variants of percolation models, including cascading failures in interdependent networks, bootstrap percolation, and $k$-core percolation.
items.authors: Jang, Hoseung*; Min, Byungjoon

items.title: Geometric alignment and inference of shortest paths in Soft random geometric graphs
items.abstract: The study of shortest paths is central to many dynamical processes on networks, from communication routing to biological signaling. Although shortest paths can be computed exactly in fully known networks in polynomial time, this task becomes challenging when the network structure is incomplete or noisy, as is often the case in real systems. Motivated by this challenge, we focus on understanding the geometric organization and inferability of shortest paths in Euclidean Soft random geometric graphs. We demonstrate that shortest paths display a pronounced geometric alignment, closely following the geodesic curves as measured by both node deviations and path stretch. This alignment strengthens with increasing system size but shows a surprising non-monotonic dependence on network connectivity. At intermediate average degrees, shortest paths are optimally aligned, whereas both sparse and highly connected regimes yield larger deviations. We explain this behavior through the competition between two opposing mechanisms: reduced hop counts improve alignment, while increasing edge lengths weaken it, and induce geometric detours. Building on this sufficiently strong alignment, we demonstrate that shortest path nodes can be directly inferred from their proximity to geodesics, even without full knowledge of the network. This geometry-based approach remains robust under substantial coordinate noise and can outperform traditional Dijkstra-based methods in partially observable networks. Our results provide insights into how geometry impacts the organization of shortest paths and provides an efficient framework for path inference in imperfectly known systems.
items.authors: Qiu, Zhihao; Balogh, Sámuel*; Liu, Xinhan; Van Mieghem, Piet; Kitsak, Maksim

items.title: Multi-State Bootstrap Percolation for Neuronal Networks
items.abstract: Multi-state activation cascades are ubiquitous in neuronal and social systems. Neurons can remain quiescent, emit isolated spikes, or enter high-intensity bursting states whose synaptic outputs are disproportionately strong. Analogously, collective action online and offline often proceeds through intermediate low-commitment participation (viewing, liking, sharing, small donations) before a subset of individuals transitions to high-commitment actions (organizing, protesting, sustained contribution). Classical bootstrap percolation captures only a single active state and therefore cannot represent how multi-level activity spreads or how intermediate activity can catalyze a global transition. We introduce multi-state bootstrap percolation (MSBP), a minimal extension of bootstrap percolation in which nodes can be inactive, weakly active, or strongly active, and we analyze MSBP on Erdos-Renyi random graphs. Each node sends a signal to its neighbors whose strength depends on its current state: zero if inactive, sigma between zero and one if weakly active, and one if strongly active. At each discrete update, a node becomes weakly active if the weighted sum of incoming signals from currently weak and strong neighbors exceeds a threshold k1, and becomes strongly active if that same weighted sum exceeds a higher threshold k2 greater than k1. The weak threshold induces an effective integer parameter kappa1, defined as the ceiling of k1 divided by sigma, which governs the minimal number of weak neighbors needed (in the absence of strong neighbors) to activate a node. Starting from a constant number of weak seeds, we establish two sharp connectivity regimes corresponding to distinct mechanisms for global activation. First, we prove a weak-driven percolation threshold at connection probability on the order of n to the power minus one over kappa1. If the connection probability is much smaller than this scale, then with high probability weak activation cannot expand beyond a vanishing neighborhood and the cascade dies out. If the connection probability is much larger than this scale, then weak activation expands, reaches a macroscopic fraction of the network, and ultimately triggers global strong activation, even though the seed set is constant size. This result formalizes the intuition that an intermediate weak state can lower the connectivity required for a global cascade: weak activation forms a sparse but extensive scaffold that eventually pushes many nodes over the stronger threshold. Second, we prove a strong-only threshold at connection probability on the order of n to the power minus one over k2. When the connection probability is much larger than this scale, strong activation becomes self-sustaining and can produce global strong activation without relying on any contribution from weak nodes. This recovers the classical bootstrap percolation scaling for the single-state model and clarifies how MSBP interpolates between two propagation mechanisms: cascades catalyzed by the weak level (controlled by kappa1 and thus by sigma and k1) versus cascades driven purely by strong activation (controlled by k2). Beyond threshold identification, we characterize transient behavior and non-asymptotic (finite size) trends via simulation. Once activation begins in the supercritical weak-driven regime, the number of active nodes grows approximately exponentially under mild conditions, consistent with a branching-like amplification of weak activation during early stages. As connectivity increases beyond the weak-driven threshold, three robust dynamical regimes emerge: (i) weak self-sustaining, in which weak activation proliferates while strong activation remains negligible until late in the process; (ii) mixed propagation, in which weak and strong activation spread in parallel and many nodes transition through the weak state before becoming strong; and (iii) strong self-sustaining, in which strong activation rapidly dominates and the role of weak nodes becomes secondary. We also quantify phase-transition signatures across network sizes for two events: full percolation (global strong activation) and self-sustaining strong contagion (strong percolation without weak assistance). In both cases, event probabilities transition sharply from near zero to near one as connectivity crosses a critical value, and the transition becomes steeper and shifts to smaller connection probabilities as network size increases. Importantly, the scaling of the critical probability depends on the event: weak-driven percolation scales with kappa1, whereas strong-only percolation scales with k2. Interpreted for neuronal circuits, weak activation can be seen as transient spiking that contributes subthreshold synaptic drive, while strong activation corresponds to bursting that delivers sustained drive. The parameter sigma captures the relative efficacy of a spike compared with a burst, and kappa1 expresses how many weak inputs are needed to compensate for one strong input. The weak-driven threshold therefore predicts when sparse spiking activity can percolate through a circuit and create the conditions for widespread bursting, even when the circuit is too sparse to support bursting directly. More broadly, MSBP provides a parsimonious mechanism by which intermediate engagement can reduce the connectivity required for large-scale cascades. The model and analysis naturally extend to other random-graph ensembles (such as configuration models with heavy-tailed degrees) and to heterogeneous thresholds or signal strengths across nodes, where intermediate activation may interact with degree heterogeneity to create multiple competing nucleation pathways. These directions connect MSBP to questions of robustness and fragility in networked dynamical systems and offer a bridge between mathematically tractable percolation theory and data-driven studies of multi-level activation in real networks.
items.authors: Spencer, Owen; Rahimian, Amin*; Streipert, Sabrina; Rubin, Jonathan

items.title: Relation Classification for Social Network Analysis in Data-Scarce Scenarios
items.abstract: Social network analysis allows us to understand how entities (e.g., people, organizations, or locations) are connected and how those connections influence social behavior and outcomes. Relation classification (RC) plays a crucial role in transforming unstructured text into structured knowledge. However, the volume of data is increasing rapidly, making it increasingly challenging to manually extract knowledge from the available unstructured data. As a result, the automatic extraction of named entities and relationships is key. Recent advances in RC have been driven by the use of pre-trained language models (PLMs) such as RoBERTa and large generative language models (LLMs) such as the LLaMA family. PLMs have demonstrated state-of-the-art capabilities but still require substantial amounts of high-quality labeled data [1]. Due to their large-scale pretraining, LLMs provide a zero- and few-shot approach to RC, reducing the need for time-consuming manual annotations. However, existing LLM-based approaches still struggle due to a low representation of RC tasks in instruction-tuning datasets and observed biases towards varying relation types. We present two state-of-the-art pipelines designed for data-scarce RC scenarios. In the zero-shot setting, we propose a true/false prompting framework that queries an LLM for each possible logical relation between two identified entities drawn from a closed set of relation types. This approach incorporates relevance elicitation to determine the most likely relation among those marked as "true." In the few-shot setting, we introduce a two-stage RC pipeline: (1) prompt the LLM to predict the most probable relation type, and (2) verify this prediction to mitigate over-prediction errors. This few-shot pipeline is fine-tuned using the parameter-efficient technique LoRA within a causal language modeling framework, enabling the model to jointly learn to predict both the relation type and its accompanying chain-of-thought (CoT) explanation. The CoT rationales used for fine-tuning are generated by a teacher model, explicitly guiding the student model to learn broader relational reasoning patterns beyond dataset-specific cues [2]. Across both settings, our models outperform existing techniques in their respective data regimes on the TACRED family of datasets. For instance, the zero-shot true/false prompting with relevance elicitation performance using larger models (e.g., models with more than 30B parameters) is comparable to the leadership benchmarks that require full training datasets. The fine-tuned pipeline shows that the confirmation model improves precision at the cost of recall, but greatly improves the micro-F1 scores and reaches state-of-the-art RC performance over multiple k-shot training samples from the ReTACRED dataset.
items.authors: Alemany, Sheila*; Sullivan-Pao, Danielle

items.title: Testing autonomous LLM agents as surrogates for humans in networked social dilemmas
items.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) hold great promise as artificial agents for simulating social and economic systems, and their use in generative agent-based models (GABMs) has rapidly expanded. Yet, it remains unclear what collective behavior emerges when multiple LLMs interact fully autonomously, and, crucially, whether such interactions can reproduce human behavior in controlled networked experiments. This question is particularly relevant in settings where network structure shapes strategic outcomes, and in light of recent concerns regarding LLM evaluation and reliability. Here, we address this gap using a canonical benchmark from network science: the iterated Prisoner's Dilemma played on networks. We construct a GABM that closely replicates a well-known laboratory experiment originally conducted with human participants, in which network structure was shown not to promote cooperation. Using the empirical results of this experiment as a reference, we systematically test whether populations of LLM-based agents reproduce human collective behavior when placed on the same networks and subjected to the same game rules and instructions. We simulate nine different LLMs interacting on regular and heterogeneous networks, explore the role of prompt design, and consider mixed populations combining LLM agents with random decision-makers to account for experimentally observed behavioral noise. Our main findings can be summarized as follows: - We successfully reproduce the original human experiment at the collective level using fully autonomous interacting LLM agents, under carefully controlled modeling and prompting assumptions. - In agreement with the human data, we confirm the absence of network reciprocity: network structure does not promote cooperation in this setting. - Once validated, the generative framework opens the door to systematic exploration of social dilemmas across payoff matrices and parameter regimes that would be impractical or impossible to study with human subjects alone. Taken together, our results show that GABMs can approximate collective human behavior in networked social dilemmas, while clarifying the conditions under which LLM agents can -and cannot- serve as reliable surrogates for humans in network experiments, thus enabling systematic exploration of fully autonomous and interacting generative agents.
items.authors: Ferraz de Arruda, Henrique*; Gracia-Lázaro, Carlos; Aleta, Alberto; Moreno, Yamir

items.title: Network Dynamics of Interacting Contagions on X: From Semantic Barriers to Temporal Spikes in COVID-19 Conspiracy Theories
items.abstract: The COVID-19 pandemic triggered a wave of conspiracy theories on social media, undermining public health efforts. Despite contagion literature's emphasis on viewing such phenomena through an ecology of contagions encompassing intercontagion interactions, the network dynamics of their spread and interactions remain underexplored. Absent before the virus, COVID-19 conspiracies make the pandemic's quick onset a natural experiment ideally positioned for investigating the ecology of contagions. We analyzed a dataset of 7,616,554 tweets from 7,416 users on Twitter/X, spanning from the outbreak's onset to July 2020, originally collected by Greve et al.. We reconstruct the social network based on retweets and replies, treating the diffusion of conspiracies as interacting complex contagions. Conspiracies were detected in tweets using a prompt-tuned BLOOMZ-1.7B large language model (LLM) from Vicinanza et al.. Using Cox proportional hazards models with time-varying covariates, we measured peer influence and adoption dynamics at the individual level, accounting for multiple exposures from neighbors and cross-conspiracy effects (e.g., how adopting one conspiracy affects the hazard for others). Results show hallmarks of complex contagion: the hazard of sharing increases with repeated exposures from multiple peers. At the individual level, adopting one conspiracy theory temporarily elevates the hazard for adopting others, followed by an exponential decay back to baseline rates seen in non-adopters (Figure 1a). Moreover, not all theories serve equally as gateways: common hoax theories—often the first adopted—exert a weaker influence, whereas extreme bioweapon theories have the strongest effect, despite spreading more slowly overall compared to the faster diffusion of hoaxes. We further explored the effect of semantic distances between the 13 identified COVID-19 conspiracies via hierarchical clustering. As visualized in Figure 1b, adoption speeds up within semantic clusters but initially slows when crossing to a different cluster, with speeds rebounding to match intra-cluster rates in the new group, suggesting semantic barriers form multiple "rabbit holes" that accelerate intra-cluster but slow cross-cluster adoption. We employ the hazard model within agent-based simulations (ABM) on the empirical network to explore counterfactual scenarios, examining the impacts of various intervention strategies and network topologies on contagion dynamics. Our results demonstrate that models overlooking inter-contagion interactions substantially underestimate the diffusion speed of conspiracy theories. Notably, the simulations reveal that mitigating early "gateway" hoaxes proves to be more effective than selectively blocking extreme narratives. In summary, this study advances network science by illuminating how semantic and temporal interactions drive conspiracy theories, with implications for mitigating harmful content online.
items.authors: Tschofenig, Fabian*; Rao, Hayagreeva; Greve, Henrich; Guilbeault, Douglas

items.title: Trapped by Personalization: LLM-Assisted Recommendations and Their Hidden Impact on Human-AI Ecosystems
items.abstract: Over the years, recommender systems have evolved from basic tools for organizing content into sophisticated systems that now play a significant role in our daily lives, even subtly shaping our behavior. By controlling what information we see, they can influence the version of the world we perceive---a dynamic that has raised concerns about their role in creating filter bubbles, radicalization, polarization, and social inequality. With the rapid advancement of technologies such as large language models (LLMs), more powerful personalizations have become possible, where these effects may become even more pronounced. Consequently, it is imperative to examine LLM-powered recommender systems within the broader context of human-AI ecosystems. In this study, we audit an LLM-assisted YouTube recommender system to analyze how LLMs can enhance personalization while also assessing their potential to amplify societal polarization. In our setting, the initial candidate videos are drawn directly from YouTube's sidebar recommendations based on a real user's watch history. These candidates are then reranked using a zero-shot, in-context LLM strategy to further personalize the recommendations. First, we find that integrating LLMs into recommender systems can unintentionally increase exposure to conspiratorial and extremist material. Second, we demonstrate that prompt-level regularization, applied within an in-context learning setup, can reduce the likelihood of promoting extreme content and improve the diversity of recommended videos, with only modest losses in relevance to user history. Our findings highlight both the promise and the risks of integrating LLMs into ranking pipelines, underscoring the importance of explicit design choices---such as instruction-level regularization---that can balance personalization against societal responsibility.
items.authors: Hosseinmardi, Homa*

items.title: Overstating Attitudes, Ignoring Networks: Large Language Model Biases in Simulating Misinformation Susceptibility
items.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as proxies for human judgment in computational social science, yet their capacity to reproduce misinformation susceptibility remains uncertain. We examine whether LLM-based “synthetic survey respondents,” prompted with structured profiles that include egocentric personal network features, demographic attributes, attitudes, and behaviors can simulate human patterns of misinformation belief and sharing. This setting is important as personal network composition has been shown to predict both belief in and sharing of false headlines [1], while such features are largely neglected in LLM social survey simulations [2]. Using three online survey datasets as ground truth spanning distinct contexts including public health, climate change, and politics, we evaluate LLM simulations along three criteria: (1) whether simulated belief and sharing outcomes approximate empirical distributions, (2) whether models recover observed predictor–outcome relationships, and (3) whether they preserve the estimated effects of features relative to human data. Across models and prompting settings, LLMs can capture broad distributional tendencies and produce simulated responses that are modestly aligned with human responses. However, they systematically misrepresent key relationships that matter for social inference. In particular, predictive models estimated on simulated outcomes exaggerate explained variance, and rely disproportionately on attitudinal and behavioral predictors while underutilizing network predictors, when compared to models estimated on human responses. Taken together, these findings suggest that LLM-based survey simulations, even when they resemble human distributions at a surface level, can yield systematically “cleaner” and more attitude-centric mappings than those observed in real survey responses, while neglecting network-based social context. We therefore argue that current LLM-based simulations are useful for identifying where model-generated judgments diverge from human patterns [3], rather than serving as substitutes for human data when modeling misinformation susceptibility with personal network features. [1] Facciani, M. \& Steenbuch-Traberg, C. (2024). Personal Network Composition and Cognitive Reflection Predict Susceptibility to Different Types of Misinformation. Connections, 44(1), 2024. 165-180. https://doi.org/10.21307/connections-2019.044 [2] Ma, W., Deng, C., Moossavi, A., Wang, L., Vosoughi, S., \& Yang, D. (2024, August). Simulated Misinformation Susceptibility (SMISTS): Enhancing Misinformation Research with Large Language Model Simulations. In Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics ACL 2024 (pp. 2774-2788). https://aclanthology.org/2024.findings-acl.162.pdf \\ [3] Anthis, J. R., Liu, R., Richardson, S. M., Kozlowski, A. C., Koch, B., Brynjolfsson, E., Evans, J. \& Bernstein, M. S. Position: LLM Social Simulations Are a Promising Research Method. In Forty-second International Conference on Machine Learning Position Paper Track.
items.authors: Choi, Eun Cheol*; Young, Lindsay; Ferrara, Emilio

items.title: Tracing Influence Across Interacting Networks
items.abstract: Propagation processes in interconnected systems are rarely confined to a single network layer. For example, social interactions influence epidemiological exposure, and failures in one infrastructure network can cascade into others. Modeling these phenomena requires representations that capture not only the internal structure of each layer but also the directional and heterogeneous couplings among layers. However, widely used multilayer representations, most notably supra-matrices, collapse these interactions into a single aggregated operator, discarding alignment structure, blurring directional effects, and masking multiway relationships that govern cross-layer influence. Although tensor representations are a natural alternative, direct tensorization of adjacency matrices or time slices typically stacks layers as independent modes, without enforcing directional alignment or a shared latent geometry across layers, which obscures cross-layer propagation structure and offers limited interpretability beyond matrix-based formulations. To address these limitations, we introduce a tensorized embedding framework designed to preserve the geometry, directionality, and coupling mechanisms that shape cross-layer propagation in complex interconnected networks. We study the problem of learning structured representations of propagation dynamics in interconnected networks to characterize how influence flows within and across layers. Our approach proceeds in three steps. (\textbf{Step~1}) We construct \textbf{directional embeddings} for each network layer via spectral decompositions that preserve orientation, flow asymmetry, and local structural organization. These embeddings define layer-specific latent spaces in which heterogeneous networks can be compared without suppressing intrinsic structural differences. (\textbf{Step~2}) We learn \textbf{alignment operators} that transform activity between embedding spaces, capturing how propagation effects transfer across layers. (\textbf{Step~3}) The resulting intra-layer embeddings and inter-layer alignments are organized into a \textbf{six-mode tensor representation}, which retains the native multiway structure of interconnected networks beyond matrix-based formulations. We apply \textbf{low-rank CP decomposition} to this tensor to identify dominant propagation components, enabling structured analysis of cross-layer influence patterns and their relative contributions. \textbf{Figure~1} summarizes this three-step pipeline. Across synthetic benchmarks and co-simulated multilayer systems, our tensorized representation achieves \textbf{28 - 35\% lower reconstruction error} and \textbf{19 - 26\% higher classification accuracy} than supra-matrix and naive concatenation baselines while producing more stable propagation-mode signatures. Conceptually, it offers a principled perspective on multilayer diffusion by treating interconnected networks as genuinely multiway systems rather than flattened composites. This approach provides new analytical tools for understanding how dynamics propagate across interacting networks and opens opportunities for developing control strategies that leverage layer-specific and cross-layer structure.
items.authors: Zhang, Zonghan*; Benbrahim, Marouane; Rashme, Tamanna; Zhang, Lei; Chen, Zhiqian

items.title: Stochastic block model reveals multi-order block structure of higher-order networks
items.abstract: Higher-order networks, naturally described as hypergraphs, are essential for modeling real-world systems involving interactions among three or more entities. Stochastic block models are principled frameworks for characterizing their mesoscale organization, yet these models for hypergraphs face a trade-off between expressive power and computational complexity. A recent simplification, Hy-MMSBM model [1], mitigates this complexity by assuming a single affinity pattern governs any interaction order. The assumption of such universal affinity patterns, however, may obscure higher-order structural details. Here we propose a multi-order stochastic block model, HyperMOSBM, that relaxes this assumption by introducing a multi-order block structure, in which different affinity patterns govern distinct subsets of interaction orders.
items.authors: Nakajima, Kazuki*; Sasaki, Yuya; Uno, Takeaki; Aida, Masaki

items.title: Unveiling the impact of cross-order hyperdegree correlations in contagion processes on hypergraphs
items.abstract: Recent years have seen growing interest in spreading phenomena that extend beyond pairwise interactions to include group interactions [1]. These interactions among groups of more than two individuals give rise to non-trivial phenomena including discontinuous phase transitions, hysteresis, explosive spreading, and bistability. Within higher-order network frameworks, nodes participate in hyperedges of different sizes, resulting in a vector of hyperdegrees rather than a single degree. Previous studies have shown that hyperdegree distributions strongly influence spreading dynamics; for example, heterogeneity in pairwise interactions can suppress bistability and explosive transitions [2]. However, hyperdegree distributions across different interaction orders are typically assumed to be independent. The correlations arising from joint hyperdegree distributions in heterogeneous systems remain largely unexplored, despite their potential to fundamentally alter which nodes act as hubs at different interaction orders. We address this gap by proposing a configuration model with joint hyperdegree distributions that explicitly accounts for cross-order degree correlations. We consider a higher-order network in which each node i participates in two orders of interactions: pairwise interactions associated with hyperdegree ki1 and three-body interactions associated with hyperdegree ki2. Correlations are quantified by the Pearson coefficient σ between a pairwise hyperdegrees and three-body hyperdegree. We develop an effective-degree model for the Susceptible–Infected–Susceptible (SIS) process that accurately captures dynamics on heterogeneous hypergraphs while accounting for cross-order correlations. Our model reproduces stochastic simulations across diverse correlation regimes, enabling systematic analysis of how cross-order correlations reshape epidemic spreading. We identify three fundamental mechanisms: (i) positive correlations lower epidemic thresholds by concentrating multiple interaction orders in the same hub nodes, creating transmission pathways that amplify outbreak potential; (ii) negative correlations temporally desynchronize pairwise and group transmission, with pairwise spreading establishing an initial infection base that precedes subsequent group amplification; and (iii) negative correlations invert the canonical hierarchical infection pattern where high-degree nodes are typically infected first and contagion cascades to lower-degree classes [3] instead causing high-degree group hubs to become infected later, reversing the expected temporal hierarchy for group interactions. These mechanisms have direct implications for intervention design. Optimal control strategies depend on both correlation structure and the relative strength of pairwise versus group transmission, with negatively correlated systems requiring adaptive strategies that shift between pairwise and group targeting. More broadly, our results show that the structure of higher-order interactions, beyond their mere existence, critically determines dynamics in hypernetwork, making inter-order correlations essential for accurate modelling and effective intervention across real-world systems.
items.authors: Guzman , Andres*; Malizia, Federico; Kiss, Istvan Z.

items.title: Erdős-Rényi Random Hypergraphs
items.abstract: Modeling networks with higher-order interactions has recently received increased attention in network science. Higher-order interactions have been shown to induce non-trivial dynamical phenomena and are crucial for understanding the functioning of real-world complex systems [1]. Despite this surge of interest, the network science literature still lacks an in-depth study of the arguably simplest random hypergraph model: the Erdős-Rényi Random Hypergraphs (ERH) [2]. Here, we treat the ERH in complete generality and present it from a maximum entropy perspective [3]. Sampling from a canonical ERH model involves walking over all of the $\binom{n}{l}$ hyperedges of size $l \in [n] \equiv\{1,2\cdots, n\}$---$n$ is the number of nodes---and choosing to place hyperedges independently with probability $p_{n,l}$ $\forall l\in[n]$. The microcanonical ERH, on the other hand, is the uniform distribution over the set of all hypergraphs satisfying the sharp constraint that the number of hyperedges for every $l$ is $m_{n,l}$. Our main contributions include obtaining the asymptotic scaling of the entropy of the model, studying the ensemble equivalence between the microcanonical and canonical ERH, the degree distribution, small-worldness, percolation (the emergence of the giant connected component (GCC)), and component size distribution. We additionally show that the ERH in the sparse regime is locally hypertree-like with vanishing hyper-edge overlaps, and derive further key structural results. We provide extensive numerical evidence for our theoretical derivations. To this end, we generalize the fast Erdős-Rényi sampling algorithm [4] and the Newman-Ziff percolation algorithm [5] to the case of non-uniform hypergraphs. Finally, motivated by the use of ERH as a null model, we propose an ansatz for the sequences $\{p_{n,l}\}$ and $\{m_{n,l}\}$, inferred from empirical data, and use it to compare trends observed in real-world hypergraphs with those produced by the ERH. [1] Battiston, F., et al. (2020). Networks beyond pairwise interactions: Structure and dynamics. Physics Reports, 874, 1–92. [2] Schmidt-Pruzan, J., et al. (1985). Component structure in the evolution of random hypergraphs. Combinatorica, 5(1), 81–94. [3] Jaynes, E. T. (1957). Information theory and statistical mechanics. Physical review, 106(4), 620. [4] Batagelj, V. and Brandes, U. (2005) Efficient generation of large random networks., Phys. Rev. E, 71, 036113. [5] Newman M. and Ziff R. M. (2000) Efficient Monte Carlo Algorithm and High-Precision Results for Percolation. Phys. Rev. Lett. 85, 4104.
items.authors: Sabhahit, Narayan*; Laber, Moritz; Hartle, Harrison ; Pieleanu, Luca; Schepers, Markus; Petri, Giovanni; Krioukov, Dmitri

items.title: Entropy-based models to randomise real-world hypergraphs
items.abstract: Many real-world systems are characterised by interactions involving more than two entities at a time [1, 2]. While hypergraphs provide a natural representation of such higher-order structures, the literature shows a limited presence of principled and analytically tractable null models. Here we introduce the framework of Exponential Random Hypergraphs (ERH), a direct extension of entropy-based random graph models to hypergraphs [3], formulated in terms of the incidence matrix. Within this framework, we derive a family of canonical and microcanonical null models that randomise higher-order connectivity while preserving selected structural constraints. In particular, the Hypergraph Configuration Model (HCM) preserves both node degrees and hyperedge sizes, generalising the configuration model to polyadic interactions [4]. The resulting ensembles are analytically tractable, scalable to large systems, and enables exact expressions for expected observables. We characterise the behaviour of ERH ensembles across sparse and dense regimes, identifying filling, resolution, and percolation thresholds that control the emergence of empty, parallel, and mutually connected hyperedges. Finally, we show how ERH models provide a natural benchmark for hypergraph-to-graph projections, see Fig. 1. By computing analytical expectations for projected observables, such as node overlap and assortativity, the HCM enables the detection of non-random higher-order organization in empirical data. Taken together, ERH models establish a unifying and practical null-model framework for higher-order network analysis. References [1] Federico Battiston et al., Physics Reports (2020). [2] Renaud Lambiotte et al., Nature Physics (2019). [3] Giulio Cimini et al., Nature Reviews Physics (2018). [4] Fabio Saracco et al., Communications Physics (2025).
items.authors: Saracco, Fabio*; Petri, Giovanni; Lambiotte, Renaud; Squartini, Tiziano

items.title: A geometric model for the emergence of nestedness in higher-order networked systems.
items.abstract: Since its inception, a central objective of network science has been to characterise emergent phenomena. In turn, this requires an understanding of how collective behaviours arise from the microscopic organisation of interactions. When systems encode interactions involving more than two entities, completely novel local features arise. A notable example of such a truly higher-order feature is hyperedge overlap. Nestedness, the extent to which smaller hyperedges are completely contained within larger ones, is a specific instance of hyperedge overlap that has been observed in numerous real-world networks. It is also known to strongly influence collective dynamics across a range of higher-order systems[1]. Despite the importance of nestedness, existing generative models for higher-order networks do not allow the principled generation of higher-order overlap. Typically, it is inserted into the system in an ad hoc fashion[2] or emerges only indirectly but cannot be controlled[3]. Our work introduces the first generative model of higher-order interactions in which nestedness both emerges naturally and is controllable. By assuming a latent geometric structure, our model allows nestedness to be directly tuned via a well-defined geometric parameter beta. The model draws on previous work to generalise the well-known network geometry model[4,5] which is derived from maximum-entropy principles. The use of a hidden-variable framework with a latent geometry has several advantages: the assumption of independence at the latent level allows the model to circumvent the issues of interdependence that have previously hindered analytical treatment higher-order overlap. Here we derive exact constraints imposed by geometry on hyperedge organisation and in the large network limit we show that overlap undergoes a smooth phase transition controlled by the system’s coupling to the underlying geometry: one regime in which nestedness remains finite and persists with system size, and another in which it vanishes asymptotically. Furthermore, our framework is highly general. While we focus here on unweighted hypergraphs, the model naturally extends to different notions of overlap for more general hypergraphs containing for example weights or double edges. Finally, our model provides a principled null model for empirical higher-order networks, providing the wider community with a tool to analyse systems with diverse overlap profiles.
items.authors: Nortier, Berné*

items.title: Interplay of size and degree heterogeneities in the simplicial Ising model on hypergraphs
items.abstract: Higher-order interaction networks~[1] in the real world are often heterogeneous in various aspects; yet, understanding how such heterogeneities shape collective phenomena remains limited. Here, we study the simplicial Ising model (SIM)~[2,3,4] on hypergraphs with heterogeneous distributions of hyperedge size and node degree. The SIM is a prototypical equilibrium model that describes beyond-pairwise nonlinear ordering via the so-called simplicial rule~[5], defined by the Hamiltonian: $\mathcal{H} = -\sum_{e\in\mathcal{E}} J_{|e|} \delta_{\{S_i|i \in e\}},$ where the Ising spins $S_i\in\{\pm1\}$ within the same hyperedge $e$ interact via the size-dependent ferromagnetic coupling $J_{|e|}\geq0$, and $\delta_{\{S_i\}}$ is the higher-order Kronecker delta, equal to 1 if all variables in the set are identical and 0 otherwise (Fig.~1a). Previous works have shown that the SIM~[3,4] supports a rich variety of phase transitions. Here, we systematically extend these studies to general hypergraphs with arbitrary size and degree distributions by developing a cavity-method framework, focusing on the roles of size and degree heterogeneities as well as inter-size degree correlation (ISDC). The main results are as follows: (i) In regular uniform hypergraphs, transitions shift from continuous to discontinuous as either hyperedge size or node degree increases (Fig.~1b), confirming and extending the results of Ref.~[3]. (ii) When pairs and groups coexist, continuous--discontinuous double transitions can occur as temperature decreases (Fig.~1c); the continuous transition at higher temperature is primarily driven by pairs, while the discontinuous one is driven by groups (Fig.~1d). (iii) In contrast, with the coexistence of hubs and non-hubs, the transition nature can shift from discontinuous to continuous and back (Fig.~1e); notably, this suggests that the introduction of hubs can promote continuous transitions, distinct from the effect of large groups (Fig.~1c). (iv) Under power-law distributions, this contrast intensifies: size heterogeneity sharpens the transition (Fig.~1f), while degree heterogeneity softens it (Fig.~1g). In addition, degree heterogeneity alone can induce double transitions (Fig.~1g, $\gamma_k=4.5$). (v) Finally, with negative ISDCs, where group-hubs are unlikely to be pair-hubs, group-driven transitions are delayed, broadening the intermediate partially-ordered phase (Fig.~1h). Our results illustrate the intricacies of the interplay between size and degree heterogeneities on collective properties of higher-order interactions. [1] F. Battiston \textit{et al.}, Nat. Phys. \textbf{17}, 1093 (2021). [2] T. Robiglio \textit{et al.}, Phys. Rev. Lett. \textbf{134}, 137401 (2025). [3] G. Son \textit{et al.}, arXiv:2411.19080 (2024). [4] T. Robiglio \textit{et al.}, Phys. Rev. E \textbf{112}, L022301 (2025). [5] I. Iacopini \textit{et al.}, Nat. Commun. \textbf{10}, 2485 (2019).
items.authors: Son, Gangmin*; Battiston, Federico; Lee, Deok-Sun; Goh, Kwang-Il

items.title: Link Prediction with Untrained Message Passing Layers
items.abstract: In this work, we explore the use of untrained message passing layers in graph neural networks for link prediction. The untrained message passing layers we consider are derived from widely used graph neural network architectures by removing trainable parameters and nonlinearities in their respective message passing layers. Experimentally, we find that untrained message passing layers can lead to competitive and even superior link prediction performance compared to fully trained message passing layers while being more efficient, especially in the presence of high-dimensional features. We also provide a theoretical analysis of untrained message passing layers in the context of link prediction and show that the inner product of features produced by untrained message passing layers relate to common neighbour and path-based topological measures which are widely used for link prediction. As such, untrained message passing layers offer a more efficient alternative to trained message passing layers in link prediction tasks, with clearer theoretical links to classical path-based heuristics.
items.authors: Qarkaxhija, Lisi*; Wegner, Anatol; Scholtes, Ingo

items.title: The Self-Loop Paradox: Investigating the Impact of Self-loops on GNNs
items.abstract: Artificial neural networks make predictions for samples based on their features. Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) extend standard neural networks by incorporating the graph topology that connects multiple samples in addition to each sample’s features. When making a prediction for each node, many MPNN models include the features of each node for its own prediction by adding a self-loop to each node, i.e. by modifying the graph topology. A one-layer MPNN can pass messages from each node back to itself and, thereby, use each node’s own features for its prediction because of this added self-loop. However, if the MPNN consists of more than one layer, messages about each node’s own feature can also return to its origin via cycles in the graph topology. Intuition suggests that this “backflow” of messages should be larger in graphs with self-loops compared to graphs without. In this work, we counter this intuition and show that for certain MPNN architectures, each node’s information gain from itself can be smaller in graphs with self-loops compared to the same graphs without. We call this phenomenon the self-loop paradox and explain its cause analytically with the changes in graph topology introduced by adding self-loops to all nodes.
items.authors: Lampert, Moritz*; Scholtes, Ingo

items.title: Epidemic Source Detection as a New Benchmark Task for Evaluating GNNs
items.abstract: Epidemic source detection on networks has been an active research area since its introduction in 2010 [1]. The task is to infer the true source given the network and a snapshot of the epidemic states of the nodes. The field has experienced renewed interest with the emergence of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), leading to promising results but also raising questions regarding benchmarking practices and experimental reproducibility. For instance, several recent studies do not compare against well-established source detection methods such as the Soft Margin Estimator (SME) [2], making it difficult to assess the relative advantage of GNN-based approaches. Motivated by these observations, we review existing GNN-based solutions to source detection in a new paper [3] and present a clear and reproducible experimental evaluation of GNNs and relevant benchmarks. Building on our prior work on traditional source detection methods [4], we initially set out to challenge the effectiveness of GNNs for source detection. However, our results indicate that, with appropriate architectural choices, GNN-based models outperform traditional benchmarks, including SME and our Monte Carlo mean-field (MCMF) approach [4], across a range of network topologies [3]. Based on these findings, our primary objective is to propose epidemic source detection as a benchmark task for evaluating GNN architectures. As a node-selection problem, source detection differs from both node classification and standard graph classification. In particular, single-source detection can be formulated as a multi-class prediction problem, with model outputs representing a probability distribution over nodes. A notable feature of this task is that training and test data can be generated synthetically, enabling controlled and scalable experimental setups. This flexibility opens the door to new research questions, such as how model performance scales with training data size. Establishing epidemic source detection as a benchmark task could benefit everyone: GNN researchers would gain an additional and practically relevant evaluation task, while source detection research could benefit from increased methodological scrutiny and unified benchmarks enabling more structured progress. As an illustration of real-world relevance, we report results on identifying the source country of the 2009 H1N1 pandemic using the global airline network and simulated epidemic data from a simple susceptible–infectious (SI) model (Figure 1).
items.authors: Sterchi, Martin*; Brack, Nathan; Hilfiker, Lorenz

items.title: Scalable Construction of Spectrally Near-Optimal Networks via Reinforcement Learning
items.abstract: The algebraic connectivity (lambda_2) of a simple graph, G(V,E) with N = |V| nodes and M = |E| edges, fundamentally governs the robustness of networked systems, controlling synchronization speeds in power grids, consensus convergence in distributed computing, and information diffusion rates [1]. While maximizing lambda_2 is NP-hard [2], greedy spectral heuristics, such as maximizing Effective Resistance (ER) or Fiedler Vector (FV) separation, can produce high-quality topologies. However, these methods require expensive eigendecompositions or matrix inversions (O(N^3)), resulting in a prohibitive total complexity of O(M N^3) or O(N^5) for dense graphs. Conversely, scalable O(N^2) constructive models like the Watts-Strogatz Small World (SW) often fail to avoid spectral bottlenecks, yielding suboptimal robustness. We introduce a Two-Phase Hybrid Framework that delivers high algebraic connectivity with O(N^2) inference, bridging the gap between spectral quality and computational scalability. Phase I (Deterministic Skeleton): We propose a deterministic strategy that approximates high spectral quality via local geometric proxies. By prioritizing edges with maximal chordal distance and disjoint neighborhoods, we facilitate isotropic expansion, promoting robust, monotonic connectivity scaling across arbitrary edge densities. Phase II (RL Refinement): To surpass deterministic limits, we employ a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) agent trained via Reinforcement Learning to add residual edges. Crucially, we introduce a Top-K Guided Policy: the deterministic heuristic from Phase I prunes the search space to the top K candidates, allowing the RL agent to focus on learning subtle topological optimizations within a high-quality subspace. This imparts guided flexibility to the pipeline without sacrificing scalability. We benchmark our method across validation scales N in {32,64,128,256}, where greedy spectral baselines remain tractable for direct comparison, since ER and FV heuristics recompute a Laplacian eigensolver at each greedy edge-addition step. Results show that our O(N^2) hybrid pipeline achieves 95--97\% of the greedy ER baseline's lambda_2 while consistently outperforming Small-World graphs by 10--20%. We further evaluate scalability up to N = 2,048, observing quadratic runtime scaling and sustained lambda_2 gains of approximately 15% over Small-World baselines.
items.authors: Hwang, Junsung*; Liu, Ji

items.title: Strategic Origins of Network Structure
items.abstract: A longstanding question in network science is to connect the dynamics of network formation with the dynamical processes that unfold on those networks. We develop a generative model that connects the dynamics of interactions and graph structure on a fundamental level. Our model applies to all agents and organism that rely on reproduction, imitation, or learning and which then experience functional benefits or ``payoffs'' from their interactions. This model encodes topological structures of cooperative interactions from generic mutual benefit such as enhanced stability, enhanced functionality, or lower overall system's free energy. It specifically captures the tension between cooperative and competitive dynamics such as competition for binding sites or pathological states. Building on this new theory model, we develop a physics-aware Graph Neural Network (GNN) which we then apply in a transductive way to real network datasets like Protein-Protein interaction. For this, we derive a differentiable loss function that captures the fundamental connection between structure and dynamics developed in our theory. We then use self-supervised learning on synthetic data to train a GNN. Once pre-trained, the GNN can be used for common graph prediction tasks like link prediction and node classification from graph structure as its only input. The GNN thus encodes behavioral information about competitive and cooperative dynamics that occur in cellular or human social networks. This physics-aware GNN has full interpretability built in from the ground up. This pre-trained model archives state of the art performance on link prediction in protein-protein interaction networks. Given its unique structure, our model supports entirely new prediction tasks such as predicting the dominant payoff structure faced by agents, provides interpretable labels labels for edges and entire graphs, and distance from equilibria (dynamic balance). Since the model is pretrained on synthetic data it can be productively used in settings with few known data points---both small networks or settings with few graphs---and with very low inference latency given its small size. Our physics-informed model acts as a rigorous motif detector while rejecting the statistical noise of standard link predictor models. Our findings suggest that coevolutionary systems are information compressors. Agents, through their local interactions and rewiring, ``wash out'' the fine-grained details of the incentive structure, collapsing a continuous space of incentives into a discrete set of stable topologies (Universality Classes). This suggests that the standard game theory assumption that small shifts in payoff parameters matters is wrong; instead, different payoff parameters collapse onto the same network structure and our GNN is performing ``thermodynamic denoising.''
items.authors: Riedl, Christoph*; Smead, Rory

items.title: Reinforcement Learning for Respondent-Driven Sampling
items.abstract: Respondent-driven sampling (RDS) is widely used to study hidden or hard-to-reach populations by incentivizing study participants to recruit their social connections. The success and efficiency of RDS can depend critically on the nature of the incentives, including their number, value, call to action, etc. Standard RDS uses an incentive structure that is set a priori and held fixed throughout the study. Thus, it does not make use of accumulating information on which incentives are effective and for whom. We propose a reinforcement learning (RL) based adaptive RDS study design in which the incentives are tailored over time to maximize cumulative utility during the study. We show that these designs are more efficient, cost-effective, and can generate new insights into the social structure of hidden populations. In addition, we develop methods for valid post-study inference.
items.authors: Weltz, Justin*; Yoon, Angela; Zhang, Yichi; Volfovsky, Alexander; Laber, Eric h

items.title: Publication preferences reveal structural diversity of academic fields
items.abstract: Publication decisions shape scholarly communities, yet the value of publishing in a particular venue is far from universal: a journal widely viewed as “top-tier” in one field may be peripheral in another, and researchers differ in how they balance prestige, audience, cost, and potential impact. Studying these preferences directly has been difficult because bibliometric data conflate aspiration with the ability to realize those aspirations. To address this, we developed an online survey platform where respondents select venues they aspire to publish in and then make pairwise comparisons among them. Using data from 3,600 participants across 13 academic fields, we obtained consideration sets (venues participants hope or want to publish in) and combined them with comparison data to generate quantitative preference rankings. We examine how these rankings co-vary with career stage, gender, publication records, and institutional prestige to characterize individual- and field-level patterns in publication preferences. Our results reveal substantial structural diversity: some fields exhibit strong consensus around a few central venues (e.g., 96% of economists selected American Economic Review), whereas others, such as Engineering and Computer Science, display broad and fragmented consideration sets. Fig. 1 illustrates field heterogeneity through venue accumulation curves (Fig. 1A), within-field overlap between top venues (Fig. 1B and C), and ranking network visualization (Fig. 1D). Regression analyses further show that researchers at more prestigious institutions both publish in and prefer higher-ranked venues at the field and aggregate levels, suggesting alignment between institutional position and preference formation. By allowing preference structures to arise directly from individual judgments, our approach extends prior work on journal rankings and disciplinary structures and provides a richer view of how academic hierarchies, field cultures, and researcher attributes jointly shape the publishing landscape.
items.authors: Landgren, Ekaterina*

items.title: Interdisciplinarity Revealed by Transitive Reduction of Citation Networks
items.abstract: We investigate the impact of transitive reduction on citation networks. Our hypothesis is that documents which lose fewer citations under transitive reduction are likely to be interdisciplinary, while a large loss of citations suggests a document is primarily cited within a single discipline. We test this hypothesis by using an artificial model of a citation network and by using data on citations from three sources: academic papers, court decisions and patents. Where needed, we applied modularity-based clustering techniques on a network defined using bibliographic coupling to classify documents by topic. A cluster-dependent measure was then used to classify the nodes as interdisciplinary or intradisciplinary. Our results provide strong support for our hypothesis in three of the four cases, with somewhat weaker but still positive support in the case of patents.
items.authors: AlMuhanna, Haya*

items.title: The social structure of scientific disagreement
items.abstract: Healthy disagreement among peers is vital to the production of scientific knowledge. Disagreement is also social. Whether a scientist disagrees with a peer requires being aware of the target (visibility ), sharing domain expertise to deeply engage (topical similarity ), and confidence to do so without sacrificing social position (social forces). We present a data-driven investigation into the social structure of debate in the scientific literature. Two forms of disagreement are examined. The first are 2,000 critical letters [1] that appear in journals of the American Physical Society (APS) and other journals. The second is more than 23,000 “contrasting” (and 350,000 “supporting”) citations sourced from Scite.ai. We use these data to represent severe and mild disagreement, respectively. Disagreement in science tends to be local. “Contrasting” citations are more often issued within the same venue and field, and to more topically similar papers than neutral ones (Fig 1.a). Notably, the most highly-cited (visible) papers are less likely than expected to receive a contrasting citation, suggesting that visibility is not the sole driver of disagreement (Fig 1.b). This extends past work which observed that critical letters target visible and topically-similar papers [1]. Social forces shape disagreement. Exploratory analysis finds that senior authors are disproportionately involved compared to other authors published in the same journal (Fig 1.c), and are more often men and from Europe (results not shown). Focusing on the case of APS, the “social distance” spanned by authors writing and receiving critical letters is greater than the baseline of neutral citations (Fig 1.d); no difference is observed for “contrasting” citations (not shown). Disagreement is structurally distinct from neutral citation. We build a journal citation network and contrast the weighted degree and eigenvector centrality of journals using only edges of each citation type. “Contrasting” citations are more dissimilar to mentioning citations than are supporting across both node centrality metrics (Fig 1.e). Collectively, these results suggest that disagreement in science is directed locally against similar papers, but not against direct or near collaborators. Further, disagreement is a structurally distinct form of scholarly communication
items.authors: Murray, Dakota*; Liu, Yixuan

items.title: Field-Specific Networks: How Disciplines Shape the Geography of Global Scientific Recognition
items.abstract: Global science is an interconnected system of knowledge production, recognition, and exchange that underpins innovation, collaboration, and the diffusion of ideas across national boundaries. Mapping the structure of this system is essential for understanding participation dynamics, structural barriers to equity, pathways of knowledge flow, and pathways through which scientific influence travels across countries and fields. However, much of the existing literature maps global science using volume-based indicators (e.g. publication counts, co-authorships, or raw citation totals) that conflate scale with relational importance. This "volume problem" causes high-output countries to appear artificially central, obscuring the structure of scientific relationships and masking meaningful patterns of recognition, dependence, and exclusion [1]. Recent scholarship suggests that the structure of global science exhibits increasing fragmentation rather than conforming to a canonical core-periphery structure [2,3,4]. To address this limitation, recent work has shifted toward relational measures of scientific recognition. In particular, citation preference networks capture realized patterns of recognition and reveal which countries systematically value whose contributions, independent of production scale. Using this approach, prior research demonstrates that the global scientific recognition network exhibits increasing fragmentation over time, rather than converging toward a stable core–periphery structure [2,3,4]. Yet national scientific recognition is not uniform across disciplines. Aggregate, cross-field analyses obscure substantial disciplinary heterogeneity: countries may occupy divergent positions across fields, emerging areas reorganize international hierarchies, and mechanisms of geographic stratification operate differently across scientific domains. Understanding the fragmentation of global science and the diffusion of ideas therefore requires mapping citation preference networks at the field level, where the structure and dynamics of scientific recognition are most directly expressed. Here, we use a probabilistic, rank-based measure to quantify whether one country systematically over- or under-cites another within each disciplinary domain [4], We analyze 76,080,360 OpenAlex publications spanning 26 fields from 1990–2022. Field-level citation preference networks exhibit pronounced heterogeneity. Network size ranges from ~70 countries in infrastructure-intensive fields (e.g., physics, chemistry) to over 100 in applied domains (e.g., medicine, engineering), while density varies from 0.054 to 0.119, with physical sciences most dense and social sciences and humanities least dense.
