Field-level analysis reveals many citation preferences absent from aggregate networks, particularly in computer science and physics & astronomy (30–40%), indicating recognition structures that depart from canonical core–periphery patterns; arts & humanities largely remain a subgraph (15%). Despite overall stability in community structure, field-specific deviations are substantial: China is markedly more central in computer science, physics is highly centralized, and medicine exhibits a polycentric topology. Understanding disciplinary variation in global network structure enables countries to identify strategic niches---such as regional specialization in medicine or infrastructure development in computer science---where barriers to international recognition are lower.
items.authors: Gao, Jianjian*; Gates, Alexander J.

items.title: The Sources and Impacts of Idiosyncrasy in Peer Review
items.abstract: A common folk theory holds that success in peer review is down to the luck of the draw, that is, that outcomes depend heavily on which reviewers and editors a manuscript happens to receive. We call this editorial idiosyncrasy: the variation in how different editors respond to the same work, reflecting individual preferences for certain topics, methods, or interpretations. If such idiosyncrasies substantially influence publication decisions, they also shape scientific careers and the trajectory of research itself. However, the empirical literature on peer review has focused primarily on social biases and replicability [1], leaving idiosyncrasy largely unmeasured—partly because confidentiality makes detailed data scarce, and partly because measuring it requires a specific structure of editor-manuscript assignments. This work investigates editorial idiosyncrasy at Science by leveraging the interconnected structure of the journal’s initial evaluation stage. These data, acquired through a data use agreement with Science’s publishers, enabled us to create a bipartite network connecting 48,800 manuscripts with only 443 eminent, interdisciplinary scholars (Fig. 1a), called the Board of Reviewing Editors (BoRE). In the initial review stage, BoRE members provide a rapid and shallow review of manuscripts, returning ratings from 1 to 10 to the presiding editors. Most submissions are sent to one BoRE (62%), but 18,444 articles are sent to two or more, allowing us to quantify systematic differences in how different reviewers rate the same manuscript. We examine whether such idiosyncrasy is present in the initial evaluation stage at Science, how this matters to later paper outcomes, and if decision makers appear to compensate for idiosyncratic rating tendencies. By modeling BoRE ratings as a mixture of fixed paper qualities, BoRE members’ idiosyncratic rating baselines and noise, we show that more than one third (34%) of the BoRE exhibit idiosyncrasy, meaning their baseline is significantly above or below the population mean. In total, 134 reviewers rate papers consistently higher than average and only 15 reviewers exhibit lower-than-average rating tendencies (p < 0.01). To investigate whether these idiosyncracies matter, we use matching procedures from causal inference. Manuscripts sent to BoRE with negative baselines (n = 3, 675) are 4% less likely to go out to review (p = 0.000045) and 1.3% less likely to be accepted (p = 0.017) than papers which received only neutral advisors. By comparison, manuscripts sent to BoRE with positive baselines (n = 28, 666) are 1.7% more likely to go out to review (p = 0.00041) and have a 0.9% advantage for acceptance (p = 0.0014). Using matching, we reveal evidence that editors partially compensate for BoRE rating idiosyncrasies, estimating that the 1.7% advantage and 4% penalty would balloon to 7.3% and 7.7% respectively without the influence of the presiding editor (Fig. 1b). Editor compensation can also be seen in the predictive power of paper-level fixed effects from our model, representing the rating each paper would receive if BoRE idiosyncrasies were accounted for. These ”true” paper quality coefficients better model papers’ probabilities of being sent out to peer review, and later acceptance, than the raw BoRE ratings (Fig. 1c), indicating that presiding editors adjust ratings toward these values when making their decisions. This work reveals evidence of idiosyncrasy in the initial evaluation stage at the journal Science, and shows that while rating idiosyncrasies impact later paper outcomes, editors compensate for a substantial portion of this effect. The kind of idiosyncrasy revealed here is likely a general phenomenon present in other review-based systems such as conference and grant review. However, in systems without a high density of ratings per reviewer, the compensatory behavior we describe is not possible, suggesting that idiosyncrasy substantially shapes outcomes in scientific peer review at large.
items.authors: Barnes, Kathryn (Kate)*; Clauset, Aaron; Larremore, Daniel

items.title: Following the Laplacian Trail: A Journey in Time Through Scientific Network Evolution
items.abstract: Scientific progress is deeply intertwined with the story of humankind. By analyzing the global evolution of science in time, we can understand how strongly global events can influence its growth rate. These events span from technological big discoveries to conflicts and economic cycles [1,2]. In this work we use OpenAlex, a large-scale bibliographic database, to map the research space by creating a bipartite network between scientific works and the topics associated with them, and by projecting on the topics space. We obtain in this way a weighted and undirected co-occurrence network. We study the evolution of the structure of the network through an analysis of the Laplacian Spectrum [3]. Several indicators are analyzed, like eigenvalues variance distribution, effective dimension evolution and community detection from eigenvectors. Our results show some robust structural signatures that can be found in practically all indicators that come from the Laplacian analysis, and that are clearly connected to big global events. But, especially focusing on the last decades, more subtle features emerge from the combined analysis of the indicators. If from one side they delineate a network that has stabilized its dimension, from the other they show a very complex reciprocal relationship between science growth and science stagnation, and global events [4]. [1] L. Bornmann and R. Mutz, J. Ass. Inf. Sci Tech. 66, 2215 (2015). [2] L. Bornmann, R. Hanuschild and R. Mutz, Hum. Soc. Sci. Comm. 8, 224 (2021). [3] P.N. McGraw and M. Menzinger, Phys. Rev. E 77, 031102 (2008). [4] Paola Gallo, Sara Venturini, Alessandra Urbinati, Jessica Davis and Alessandro Vespignani, Following the Laplacian Trail: A Journey in Time Through Scientific Network Evolution, in preparation (2026).
items.authors: Gallo, Paola*; Venturini, Sara; Urbinati, Alessandra; Davis, Jessica; Vespignani, Alessandro

items.title: Dataset re-purposing fuels AI breakthroughs
items.abstract: Data is the primary driving force of AI research. New datasets enable not only the development of models with greater capacity, stronger performance, and broader generalization, but also deeper understanding of how models behave and learn. Sometimes, however, existing datasets are creatively repurposed to trigger innovation—much like old, familiar drugs are repurposed for unexpected treatments. We ask whether this form of creative reuse drives scientific innovation: Can it spark the emergence of new research areas, or does it instead result in isolated, low-impact work? We measure dataset repurposing through a network-science lens by integrating (i) a bipartite dataset–paper network with (ii) a semantic embedding space of paper content. This framework allows us to quantify how far a focal paper moves a shared dataset into new topical regions relative to earlier dataset neighbors. To operationalize this study, we construct a large-scale bipartite dataset–paper network using data from Papers With Code. We derive a paper–paper network in which nodes represent papers and an edge is formed when two papers use the same dataset. The resulting network covers over 10,000 AI papers and 1,689 datasets. To quantify \emph{dataset repurposing}, we embed each paper into a semantic space derived from large-scale language-model embeddings of paper abstracts. Repurposing is then defined as the semantic distance between a focal paper and prior papers that used the same datasets. Intuitively, repurposing measures how far a paper pushes a shared dataset into new topical regions relative to its existing usage neighborhood. Across this network, we find that higher repurposing is strongly associated with \emph{disruptive} scientific impact. A one-standard-deviation increase in repurposing corresponds to a 10.2\% increase in 3-year disruption, even after controlling for dataset frequency, dataset-combination atypicality, topical novelty, venue, and team characteristics. In contrast, repurposing shows no robust short-term citation advantage, highlighting its high-risk, high-reward nature. We further study \emph{repurposing propagation} by asking whether subsequent papers that reuse the same datasets move toward the focal paper in semantic space, effectively rewiring the local paper--paper network neighborhood. Propagation is relatively rare, but when it occurs it is consequential: papers that successfully pull later work toward their semantic position receive both higher citations and greater disruption. Team-level analysis suggests that institutional research strength and academia-industry collaboration increase both repurposing and propagation success. Our findings reveal new mechanisms through which specific forms of data utilization drive scientific impact, offering valuable insights for researchers, policymakers, and data curators.
items.authors: Yu, Yulin*; Ahn, Yong-Yeol ; Romero, Daniel

items.title: Reconstructing the early spatial spread of pandemic respiratory viruses in the United States
items.abstract: Background & aims of study: Understanding the geographic spread of emerging respiratory viruses is critical for pandemic preparedness and response, yet the early spatiotemporal dynamics of SARS-CoV-2 and the 2009 H1N1 pandemic influenza (H1N1pdm influenza) in the United States (US) remain unclear. Here, we aim to reconstruct the early spatial spread of the last two pandemics and compare their transmission pathways across the US. We further simulate the effect of aircraft-based wastewater surveillance and subsequent local transmission control on curbing the initial spatial spread of future novel respiratory viruses. Methods & results: We developed and validated an ensemble inference framework integrating airline travel, commuting flows, and pathogen superspreading potential to reconstruct transmission networks of novel respiratory pathogens, with associated uncertainty for inferred transmission pathways. Leveraging city-level influenza-like illness (ILI) records from medical claims and estimated county-level COVID-19 infections accounting for underreporting, we inferred the transmission pathways of SARS-CoV-2 and H1N1pdm influenza across US Metropolitan Statistical Areas (MSAs). The two viruses exhibited distinct spatial spread networks; however, both pandemics established local transmission in most MSAs within weeks, driven by several common hubs. The inference results for SARS-CoV-2 agreed with several findings from independent genomic studies in the US. While mobility flows partially explain the inferred transmission pathways, stochastic dynamics introduce substantial uncertainty in transmission routes, undermining the inference accuracy of heuristics based solely on mobility. Simulations suggest that both sufficient coverage of wastewater surveillance at transmission hubs and effective local interventions are needed to curtail the initial spatial spread of future outbreaks. Implications: Our study provides a generalizable framework to infer early epidemic dynamics that may be applied to other pathogens. By combining mechanistic modeling, mobility integration, and ensemble-based inference, this approach provides a scalable, data-efficient method to resolve spatial transmission patterns in the critical early window. We find that stochastic dynamics play an important role during the early spatial spread of emerging respiratory viruses. Our findings highlight the rapid spread of pandemic respiratory pathogens and the challenges of early-stage containment.
items.authors: Pei, Sen*

items.title: Reconstructing the Worldwide Airport Network using Open Source Data
items.abstract: While airport networks have often been studied using commercial datasets, reliance on proprietary sources limits the accessibility of air transportation research. Here, we introduce an open-source framework that reconstructs and analyzes the global airport network using only publicly available data, offering a fully reproducible alternative to commercial datasets. The methodology integrates heterogeneous open sources, including Wikipedia for route connectivity and passenger flows, OpenStreetMap for urban points of interest, and World Bank Open Data for demographic and economic indicators. The framework follows a two-step approach. First, we reconstruct the worldwide air transportation network from the Wikipedia Airlines and destinations tables (which list the possible destinations reachable from the airport described on each page), achieving approximately 94% coverage of global airports. Validation against the OAG commercial reference dataset reveals strong correlations across multiple network centrality measures, indicating a high level of topological fidelity in the reconstructed network. Second, leveraging the validated network structure, we introduce a predictive framework to estimate annual passenger volumes on individual routes. We evaluate several machine learning models, including Decision Tree, Random Forest, and XGBoost regressors, using a multiscale feature set that combines country-level attributes (e.g., language, currency, and economic indicators), local characteristics such as population within airport catchment areas and the density of nearby points of interest (e.g., hotels and museums), network structural features (e.g., node degree), and geographical descriptors, including inter-airport distance and indicators of whether two airports lie in the same hemisphere or on the same continent. An ensemble stacking model, trained and tested using Wikipedia passenger volumes, achieves R^2 values of 0.67–0.70 at the global scale and 0.88–0.90 for domestic U.S. routes. Our results show that open data can simultaneously reproduce the structural properties of a large-scale transportation network and support accurate edge-weight prediction. Future steps include testing the fully reconstructed passenger flow against OAG data and adding additional predictive features, such as the number of hospitals and universities.
items.authors: Santagata, Luca*; Urbinati, Alessandra ; Davis, Jessica ; Tizzoni, Michele; Srivastava, Ajitesh; Chinazzi, Matteo ; Vespignani, Alessandro

items.title: Disease-induced immunization is efficient in dense geometric networks
items.abstract: A critical aspect of infectious disease control lies in the achievement of population-level herd immunity, which also determines how the disease can spread upon a potential future outbreak among the residual set of non-immunized individuals. The effectiveness of herd immunity can be quantified by the size of the largest residual connected subgraph consisting of the remaining susceptible individuals after the population has been immunized. Importantly, the effectiveness of herd immunity is strongly influenced by the topology of the underlying contact network and, crucially, by the immunization process. Recently, Hiraoka et al. [1] compared two fundamentally different immunization strategies. First, vaccine-induced \emph{random immunization}, which involves a f fraction of randomly selected network nodes who receive immunity, reflecting mass vaccination campaigns (see Fig.1a). Second, disease-induced \emph{natural immunization}, which appears as individuals acquiring immunity after recovering from an earlier wave of an epidemic (see Fig.1b). Although both immunization strategies share the common goal of providing protection against an epidemic, their effects are often influenced by non-pharmaceutical interventions (NPIs). Such interventions typically aim to reduce interactions between individuals, thereby lowering the probability of pathogen transmission across the contact network. In this work, we explore the interplay between interventions and immunization outcomes using two distinct approaches. First, we model the contact networks as synthetic Geometric Inhomogeneous Random Graphs (GIRGs) [2], in which the average degree and degree heterogeneity can be implicitly controlled through model parameters. Second, we use the SafeGraph data [3] to analyze empirical state-level mobility networks from the U.S. before and during the COVID-19 pandemic. In this case, weekly origin-destination flows recorded in April 2019 and 2020 provide a natural experimental setup to reflects lockdown-induced reductions in the average degree. Interestingly, both in synthetic and empirical geometric networks, we discover a trend-reversal phenomenon: in sparse regimes, random immunization outperforms natural immunity, whereas in dense regimes the opposite is observed (see Fig.1c). This effect is entirely absent in maximally random networks, highlighting the critical role of spatial embeddedness. We also observe similar behavior in other geometric structures, and real geometric networks (see Fig.1d-e). In summary, we demonstrate that the average number of contacts in geometric networks is not just a scaling parameter but a structural determinant that can qualitatively alter the balance between vaccination- and disease-driven immunity. Dense networks favor natural immunization, while vaccination may become optimal when combined with contact-reducing interventions. These results highlight the importance of including network geometry and structural changes in epidemic models and might provide guidance for designing interventions that combine NPIs with vaccination strategies. [1] T. Hiraoka, et al., Strength and weakness of disease-induced herd immunity in networks, PNAS 122, 28, e2421460122, 2025. [2] K. Bringmann, R. Keusch, and J. Lengler, Geometric inhomogeneous random graphs, Theo. Comp. Sci., 760, 35–54, 2019. [3] Y. Kang et al., Multiscale dynamic human mobility flow dataset in the U.S. during the COVID-19 epidemic, Scientific Data 7, 390, 2020.
items.authors: Balogh, Sámuel; Ódor, Gergő; Karsai, Marton*

items.title: Mobility Networks Make Up for Missing Local Data in Epidemic Forecasting
items.abstract: Human mobility networks—capturing how people move between places and interact across communities—have become central to epidemic modeling [1, 2]. Passively collected smartphone traces now allow researchers to construct dynamic, weighted networks where nodes represent geographic units and edges encode population flows, enabling improved epidemic forecasting. A key question for public health practice is: \textit{when does investing in mobility data infrastructure and network-based modeling pay off for epidemic forecasting?} We address this question using COVID-19 data from Massachusetts towns (April 2020--April 2021), constructing weekly directed mobility networks from anonymized smartphone traces and linking them to official case reports. We compute dynamic network measures, both holistic—such as betweenness centrality of each town in the network—and local—such as within-town mobility—and evaluate their out-of-sample predictive value for one- to four-week-ahead forecasts. Our central finding is that the value of mobility network structure is \textbf{conditional on data availability}. When high-resolution local case histories are unavailable or delayed, incorporating network topology dramatically improves forecasts: out-of-sample Predict-$R^2$ rises from 0.60 (using only statewide incidence with town and time fixed effects) to 0.83--0.89 when adding network position interacted with macro-level prevalence. The largest gains occur during epidemic waves and rising phases, precisely when connectivity patterns shift rapidly, and bridge-like towns become conduits for spread. However, once granular town-level case data become available, simple autoregressive models—capturing current cases in each town—already achieve Predict-$R^2 \approx 0.90$, and network features add only marginal improvement ($\sim$0.5 percentage points). These patterns are reproduced in agent-based simulations where infections propagate on mobility-like networks, confirming that the conditional value of topology reflects a general mechanism rather than data idiosyncrasies: macro-scale prevalence activates the predictive salience of a place's position in the human mobility network. Our results offer actionable guidance: invest in mobility network infrastructure and real-time network computation early in an outbreak or when local surveillance is sparse; once high-frequency case reporting is established, rely on autoregressive baselines and use network metrics primarily as diagnostic tools to identify potential spillover pathways [3].
items.authors: Ilami, Sepehr*

items.title: Network Structure Breaks Homogeneous Mixing: Why Spatial Aggregation Distorts Epidemic Inference
items.abstract: Simple compartmental epidemic models deployed at state and national scales during COVID-19 systematically violated their core assumption of homogeneous mixing, yet the specific consequences for parameter inference and forecasting remained poorly quantified. We demonstrate through canonical network simulations, mobility-informed metapopulation models, and comprehensive COVID-19 empirical validation that spatial aggregation creates fundamental distortions in epidemiological inference that depend critically on network connectivity structure. Using metapopulation SIR models on canonical topologies (fully connected, star-shaped, unidirectional cycles), we show that aggregate epidemic curves can deviate dramatically from local dynamics even when all nodes follow identical trans- mission parameters. Large, sparsely connected networks produce spurious temporal patterns—including sustained plateaus in effective reproduction numbers (Rt) where none exist locally—that fundamentally mislead policy responses. Standard SIR models fitted to aggregated data systematically underestimate transmission rates (up to 55% bias), with error mag- nitude determined by the interplay between network topology, size, and mobility levels. Mobility-informed simulations using empirical SafeGraph data for Texas, Colorado, and Massachusetts confirm these artifacts emerge in realistic connectivity structures. State-level aggregation in large, sparse states produces multimodal epidemic curves violating homogeneous mixing assumptions, while Metropolitan Statistical Areas (MSAs) consistently preserve epidemic coherence across diverse network structures. COVID-19 validation across two years of US county-level data reveals systematic patterns predicted by network theory. MSAs exhibit 2.5-fold higher epidemic synchrony than states (county-aggregate correlations >0.8 vs <0.6, p < 0.001). Independent mobility community detection confirms MSAs align remarkably with transmission networks (NMI=0.965 for large MSAs vs 0.865 for states), providing mechanistic explanation for their epidemiological performance. This conver- gence demonstrates that functional connectivity, not administrative convenience, determines aggregation validity. We present quantitative criteria that enable the prospective selection of appropriate spatial-scale data aggregation where well-mixed SIR models may be reasonably used to characterize the dynamics of disease outbreaks, using a data-driven strategy that leverages human mobility data from mobile phones. Our findings reveal network connectivity as the fundamental determinant of whether spatial aggregation illuminates or obscures epidemic reality, with direct implications for the operational practices of agencies like CDC and ECDC that continue deploying compartmental models at state and national scales. We provide algorithmic protocols transforming spatial aggregation from ad hoc administrative convenience to epidemiologically grounded decision-making for future pandemic preparedness.
items.authors: Santillana, Mauricio*; Bhora, Yash; Das, Haridas; Pant, Binod; Kiss, Istvan; Parag, Kris; Moro, Esteban; Stolerman, Lucas

items.title: An analytical method to calculate epidemics threshold within spatial structure
items.abstract: Epidemic thresholds are a central tool in network epidemiology, commonly used to assess outbreak risk and guide interventions at national and global scales. On spatial and metapopulation networks, analytic thresholds are typically obtained from the spectral radius of a next-generation/reproduction operator. However, a single system-wide threshold compresses heterogeneous communities into one number and can be misleading for realistic seeding: even in a globally supercritical system, introductions in weakly connected or low-contact areas may still go extinct due to early-stage stochasticity. We develop an analytic framework that extends the notion of threshold into a spectrum of community- and structure-specific critical points, derived from the fixed-point equations of multi-type branching-process outbreak probabilities. Beyond the global singularity, we show that local “activation” points emerge and are detectable via the minor outbreak size (the expected size of finite outbreaks conditional on extinction). Using a Lyapunov–Schmidt reduction, we prove that weak coupling generically shifts these singularities off the real axis into complex-conjugate pairs: the real part sets the effective local activation threshold for a given community or multi-community mode, while the imaginary part quantifies the smoothing (“smearness”) of the transition, i.e., how sharply the mode turns on under coupling. The method is analytic and data-parsimonious, requiring only an estimated reproduction operator from surveillance and recurrent mobility data. Applied to real colocation-based mixing in Italy, it recovers local thresholds missed by the system-wide value and reveals dynamically cohesive versus fragmented regional structures. On high-resolution Texas data combining mobility with measles vaccination coverage, thresholds self-organize into clusters that align with metropolitan commuter-sheds rather than administrative borders, highlighting epidemiological units as dynamic mobility-defined regions.
items.authors: WANG, Boxuan*; Valdano, Eugenio

items.title: Road Network Renormalization with Preserved Higher-Order Dynamics
items.abstract: Renormalization of road networks is traditionally approached as a problem of topological simplification. However, mobility on transportation systems is fundamentally shaped by path-dependent dynamics, arising from sequential decision-making rather than static connectivity alone. Using the widely adopted Sioux Falls benchmark, we show that different network representations, while exhibiting similar large-scale structure, can induce different behavioral and visitation patterns. This highlights a key limitation of topology-driven simplification: reducing network complexity without accounting for memory aware movement (i.e. higher-order dependencies) can suppress essential behavioral structure, thereby compromising the fidelity of models used for decision making and policy analysis. To address this, we propose a renormalization framework that operates across network orders and induced spatial distributions, enabling both the principled evaluation and the construction of coarse-grained network representations that preserve higher-order mobility dynamics.
items.authors: Zhang, Chen*; Hackl, Jürgen

items.title: Harmonic morphisms and dynamical invariants in network renormalization
items.abstract: Renormalizing complex networks while preserving their dynamics remains a central open problem in network science. Although many renormalization schemes successfully compress structure, when and how coarse-graining preserves dy- namical invariants remains unclear, even for the simplest diffusion processes. Principled criteria to assess the effect of most existing approaches on dynamics are still wanting. Here we address this outstanding challenge: defining a probabilistic renormalization scheme for networks that preserves random-walk dynamics across scales. We show that this problem admits a precise solution by leveraging harmonic morphisms, a concept from algebraic graph theory bridging discrete probability and combinatorial geometry. We prove that harmonic morphisms represent the minimal condition under which a coarse-graining maps random walks on the original network to random walks on the renormalized one, up to a scale-dependent random time change. This establishes harmonic morphisms as the discrete analogue of conformal maps in field theory, providing a principled notion of dynamical equivalence for network renormalization. Building on this theoretical result, we introduce the harmonic degree, a quantitative diagnostic that measures how closely an arbitrary coarse-graining approximates a harmonic morphism. We apply this framework to major renormalization schemes—geometric, Laplacian-based, and graph-neural-network-based methods—across dozens of real-world networks. Our results show that harmonic-degree curves along renormalization flows act as dynamical fingerprints revealing when dynamical invariance is preserved, when it breaks, and whether it is recovered at larger scales. Doing so allows the characterisation of coarse-graining mechanisms into top-down, bottom-up, and learning-based categories. Remarkably, Laplacian renormalization spontaneously generates exact harmonic morphisms at specific scales in real networks, yielding perfect preservation of random-walk dynamics despite strong structural compression. Finally, we show that our framework naturally extends to higher-order networks by replacing graph Laplacians with Hodge Laplacians on simplicial complexes, where harmonicity becomes sensitive to intrinsic topological dimension. Together, our results provide a unified, dynamical perspective on network renormalization and offer practical tools to compare and design multi-scale representations of complex systems.
items.authors: Guadagnuolo, Francesco*; Nurisso, Marco; Petri, Giovanni; Allard, Antoine

items.title: Multiscale Network Renormalization
items.abstract: The representation of the internal architecture of all physical systems depends on the resolution at which they are observed and described. In spatially embedded systems, geometric coordinates provide a natural way to change the resolution level, indicating how to coarse-grain both the real system and any model of the latter, allowing consistent mappings across scales that lie at the foundation of the renormalization group (RG). By contrast, for complex networks with no explicit spatial embedding, multiple renormalization schemes exist [1], resulting in non-unique representations of the same system across different scales. The Multiscale Network Renormalization approach [2,3] has been recently introduced as a way to design random graph models that can represent the same network consistently under arbitrary aggregations of nodes. It seeks a probability distribution over graphs that is invariant under node aggregation (see Fig. 1), thereby representing a fixed point of a nontrivially generalized RG flow. The model comes with node variables that are additive upon node aggregation. It successfully replicates, at multiple hierarchical levels, the properties of several real-world networks [2,4,5] and lends itself naturally to the renormalization of directed graphs [3], which is otherwise problematic. It also allows one to infer the structural properties of a network at a hierarchical level that is different from the one at which empirical observations are available, opening new avenues for cross-scale network reconstruction [4]. Moreover, the approach can be applied to Machine Learning algorithms that take a graph as input and encode its structure onto output vectors that represent nodes in an abstract space [5,6]. In particular, under arbitrary coarse-grainings of the input graph, the multiscale method ensures statistical consistency of the embedding vector of a block-node with the sum of the embedding vectors of its constituent nodes. This guarantee enables the interpretable application of the basic properties of vector spaces (i.e. sum of vectors and multiplication of a vector by a scalar) to the latent space where node embeddings are identified. It turns out that several key network properties, including a large number of triangles, are successfully replicated already from embeddings of very low dimensionality, allowing for the generation of faithful replicas of the original networks at arbitrary resolution levels [5,6]. Finally, a purely abstract, annealed version of the model leads to infinite-mean node variables and spontaneously replicates, without any fitting parameters, several real-world network properties such as power-law degree distribution, finite local clustering coefficient, and disassortativity profiles [2,7]. Moreover, since node aggregation invariance is a form of discrete scale invariance, several unique properties emerge in the spectrum of the adjancency matrix, such as log-periodicity and complex scaling exponents [8]. References: [1] A. Gabrielli, D. Garlaschelli, S. P. Patil, M. A ́. Serrano, Nature Reviews Physics 7, 203-219 (2025). [2] E. Garuccio, M. Lalli, D. Garlaschelli, Physical Review Research 5(4), 043101 (2023). [3] M. Lalli and D. Garlaschelli, arXiv:2403.00235 (2024). [4] L. N. Ialongo, S. Bangma, F. Jansen, D. Garlaschelli, arXiv:2412.16122 (2024). [5] R. Milocco, F. Jansen, D. Garlaschelli, arXiv:2412.04354 (2024). [6] R. Milocco, F. Jansen, D. Garlaschelli, arXiv:2508.20706 (2025). [7] L. Avena, D. Garlaschelli., R. S. Hazra, M. Lalli, Journal of Applied Probability, https://doi.org/10.1017/jpr.2025.10037 (2025). [8] A. Catanzaro, R. S. Hazra, D. Garlaschelli, arXiv:2509.12407 (2025).
items.authors: Garlaschelli, Diego*

items.title: Scaling and multiscaling invariance in the Laplacian Renormalization Transformation
items.abstract: Complex networks are a simple way to illustrate diverse phenomena in biology, society, and engineering, yet their vast sizes and intricate connectivity are often obstacles to capturing essential structural features and organizing principles. Extracting insight from such systems requires systematic coarse-graining methods that preserve key features. The renormalization group (RG) approach, which reduces complexity by aggregating nodes and links, has been a potential candidate as a powerful method. Despite its promise, renormalization in complex networks faces challenges absent in regular or fractal lattices. Real-world networks, especially scale-free networks with power-law degree distributions $P(k) \sim k^{-\gamma_d}$, exhibit small-world properties that obscure spatial scales for coarse-graining. Their diameters grow as $\ln N$ or $\ln\ln N$, making characteristic length scales ill-defined in the RG transformation. To address these challenges, two main approaches have been developed, each targeting a different aspect of complex networks. Real-space approaches, such as box-covering methods, partition networks into subgraphs, treating each as a renormalized supernode. Although this method yields structural scaling relations, they neglect dynamical correlations. Recently, the Laplacian renormalization group (LRG) method has been proposed~[1]. This method analyzes Laplacian eigenmodes under diffusion dynamics to extract dynamical observables such as the spectral dimension~$d_s$, but it lacks a systematic framework for understanding the scaling relations between exponents regarding structural and dynamic features. This separation poses a fundamental limitation: in complex systems, spatial organization and dynamical behavior are intrinsically coupled. Recently, we extended the previous LRG framework to simultaneously capture structural and dynamical features within a unified spectral formalism [2]. The central idea is to embed the LRG framework into the standard RG framework via the Gaussian model defined in Laplacian eigen-space. This theoretical construction yields scaling relations that relate the thermal and magnetic exponents~($y_t$, $y_h$) to the spectral, fractal, random walk dimensions, and degree scaling exponent~($d_s$, $d_f$, $d_w$, $d_k$). These scaling relations establish the theoretical framework for the LRG in a mathematically explicit manner. The fractal and spectral dimensions, and degree scaling exponents are indispensable and complementary to characterizing the renormalization of complex networks. Next, based on the Gaussian model formalism, we construct a meta-graph algorithm that maps spectral modes back to real-space topology, enabling direct extraction of random walk dimensions. We apply this meta-graph algorithm to real-world networks, for instance, the Internet topologies at the AS level over several years, yeast regulatory networks, and the European power grid. The algorithm yields the scaling exponents that satisfy the theoretically predicted scaling relations while preserving structural features such as degree distributions across scales. These results support the soundness of our refined RG framework. Moreover, the framework unveils previously inaccessible features, including multiple-scaling behaviors in evolving networks and long-range coherence emerging from spectral renormalization. Our RG framework provides a powerful lens for understanding mesoscopic organization and dynamical coherence in heterogeneous networks.
items.authors: Kahng, Byungnam; Kim, Cook Hyun*

items.title: Equilibrium-preserving Laplacian Renormalization Group
items.abstract: Coarse graining is central to understanding large-scale organization in complex systems, yet a rigorous real-space renormalization group (RG) framework for networks and hypergraphs remains incomplete. Laplacian renormalization group (LRG) approaches [1] use diffusion time as a scaling parameter and yield a coarse-grained network as an effective structure governing diffusion dynamics, thereby overcoming limitations of earlier methods. However, LRG procedures are largely heuristic, and the RG transformations of the Laplacian and adjacency matrix have yet to be formulated. We introduce the equilibrium-preserving Laplacian renormalization group (EqLRG) [2], a principled RG framework that preserves the equilibrium state while accurately capturing late-time diffusion. Supernodes are defined as locally equilibrated node sets, formalized through a reduced basis spanning the slow Laplacian eigenmodes. This construction yields a unique basis transformation that preserves the zero-eigenvalue left and right eigenvectors of the Laplacian, ensuring equilibrium invariance under renormalization. Using this basis, we derive a renormalized Laplacian that reproduces the small-eigenvalue spectrum. Equilibrium-state flows further define a renormalized adjacency matrix, producing a consistent coarse-grained network with conserved mean connectivity. These results establish a principled real-space LRG scheme. We apply EqLRG to diffusion on hypergraphs in bipartite vertex–hyperedge form, enabling simultaneous renormalization of elements and interactions. Numerical validation shows that EqLRG accurately reproduces long-time diffusion and outperforms heuristic coarse-graining schemes [Fig. 1 (a,b,c)]. Recursive application reveals distinct large-scale flows: hypergraphs with finite spectral dimension, such as random (RN) and Barab\'{a}si-Albert (BA) hypertrees, flow toward effective Poissonian structures [Fig. 1 (d,e,f)], whereas Erd\H{o}s–R\'{e}nyi (ER) hypergraphs develop increasingly heterogeneous, power-law-like connectivity [Fig. 1 (g,h,i)]. These results clarify the roles of informational, structural, and dynamical scale invariance in higher-order networks. More broadly, EqLRG provides a rigorous foundation for multiscale analysis, enabling systematic complexity reduction while preserving essential dynamical properties. [1] P. Villegas, T. Gili, G. Caldarelli, and A. Gabrielli, Nat. Phys. 19, 445 (2023). [2] S. Yi, S.-G. Yang, K.-I. Goh, and D.-S. Lee, arXiv:2507.04977 (2025).
items.authors: Lee, Deok-Sun*; Yi, Sudo; Yang, Seong-Gyu; Goh, Kwang-Il

items.title: Scale invariance and statistical significance in complex weighted networks
items.abstract: Weighted networks appear in a wide range of social, biological, technological, and information systems. Determining whether observed structural patterns are statistically significant in weighted networks requires suitable null models (Fig. A). The most widely adopted choice is the Weighted Configuration Model (WCM), which extends the configuration model by interpreting weights as bundles of unit edges and preserving node strengths in the randomized ensemble. In many applications, the scale used to express weights is arbitrary (e.g., seconds vs. minutes, or thousands vs. millions of dollars), while standard weighted measures such as the Onnela weighted clustering coefficient, weighted eigenvector centrality, and weighted modularity are themselves scale invariant. This leads to a natural expectation: p-values obtained by comparing empirical scores to their corresponding null distributions should be independent of the chosen weight units. Here we show that this expectation is violated by the WCM: statistical significance under this model is scale dependent. The origin of this behavior lies in the multi-edge interpretation of weights, which induces distribution fluctuations whose standard deviation grows sublinearly with the rescaling factor A, causing the null distribution to shrink asymptotically as A⁻¹ᐟ². As a result, in the large-A limit, essentially any deviation becomes statistically significant, making inference depend on an arbitrary unit choice (Fig. B: WCM), as previously hinted by studies of scale effects in weighted null models. To restore scale invariance while preserving network sparsity, we adopt a separable two-step construction inspired by network-reconstruction frameworks. In this approach, topology is first randomized using a degree-based configuration model, and weights are then assigned to realized edges from a scale-invariant maximum-entropy (exponential) distribution, with means chosen to preserve node strengths in expectation. Across representative weighted networks from distinct domains and for key measures (maximum modularity, maximum L2-normalized eigenvector centrality, and weighted clustering), this two-step null model yields scale-flat distributions (Fig. B: CReMb) and thus scale-invariant p-values, enabling unbiased significance testing in complex weighted networks (Fig. C). Figure caption: (A) Schematic of the hypothesis-testing procedure: a network statistic is measured on the empirical network and compared to its distribution under a null model to obtain a p-value. (B) Standard deviation of null distributions for three weighted measures: maximum modularity, maximum eigenvector centrality, and weighted clustering coefficient, as a function of the rescaling factor A. Under the WCM, the null width decreases approximately as A⁻¹ᐟ², implying scale-dependent significance. In contrast, the separable two-step null (CReMb variant) yields approximately constant variance, indicating scale invariance. (C) Resulting p-values for a few networks under the WCM (dots) and the CReMb variant model (crosses).
items.authors: Nascimento Silva, Filipi*; Kojaku, Sadamori; Flammini, Alessandro; Radicchi, Filippo; Fortunato, Santo

items.title: The hidden structure of innovation networks
items.abstract: Understanding how innovation emerges requires uncovering the structure of collaborations among inventors, firms and institutions. Here, we employ the ORBIS Intellectual Property data~\citep{orbis} across the years 2020-2024 to construct the \emph{co-inventorship} and \emph{co-ownership} networks of three, strategic, technological domains, i.e. \emph{artificial intelligence}, \emph{biotechnology} and \emph{semiconductors}. For each domain and level of analysis, we focus on the 500 most cited actors. At the microscale, inventor networks appear as denser and more cohesive than organization networks. In order to investigate their mesoscale organization, we compare the results coming from modularity maximization with those coming from the minimization of the Bayesian Information Criterion, instantiated with both the Stochastic Block Model (SBM) and its degree-corrected variant (dcSBM)~\citep{karrer2011} - although the SBM is `agnostic' about the underlying structure to be detected, it is still block-wise homogeneous: the dcSBM, instead, refines the picture provided by the SBM by incorporating the degree heterogeneity. While modularity, consistently with its assortative bias, tends to cluster collaborations across all domains, the SBM and the dcSBM uncover a wider range of configurations, including non-assortative structures such as locally tree-like patterns. As our results clearly indicate, innovation networks exhibit different mesoscale structures at different levels: while inventor networks self-organize in modules mirroring small, recurrent teams of collaborations, organization networks appear as combinations of core-periphery structures, where few, dominating actors coordinate the most peripheral ones. Moreover, Lorenz curves of forward citations show a pervasive inequality in technological influence: inventor networks display a steadily high concentration; for what concerns organization networks, instead, the steepest inequality is observed in the domain of artificial intelligence - driven by a handful of corporate-academic alliances.
items.authors: Emer, Lorenzo; Gallo, Anna; Marzi, Mattia; Mina, Andrea; Squartini, Tiziano*; Vandin, Andrea

items.title: The Western Record Industry Band Collaboration Network
items.abstract: Cultural data is valuable for network science because it can provide rich attributed data for nodes and edges of complex networks representing various aspects of cultural production. In this abstract, we present a novel dataset focused on Western music. We build a band collaboration network using data from Discogs (https://www.discogs.com/). Discogs provides credit information for over 18 million records. In our network, we connect two bands -- including solo artists -- if they have a significant number of shared credits -- artists credited in their released records. We focus on Western music -- plus Russia and Japan -- as these are the countries which are well covered in the Discogs data. We perform a number of data cleaning steps to increase the data quality from Discogs -- disambiguating aliases, using only musical roles, identifying correct years of release/recording, adding country information, etc. At the moment of writing, the network includes 41,207 nodes and 193,980 edges, but the data coding and cleaning is still underway and these counts are posed to greatly increase once completed. The data will be ready by the time the conference happens. This work is a significant improvement over previous efforts focused on a single country [1]. The nodes of the network have rich attributes, including their country of origin, genres played, labels publishing their records, and years of cultural relevance (when the band publishes records). The dataset is valuable both for network and cultural analysis on the one hand, and for machine learning on graphs on the other. We show in Fig 1 two examples of such analyses. For cultural and network analysis, the network supports a number of research questions. For instance, by calculating the country entropy of the connections of a band in the network -- by estimating the probabilities of their links to go to bands of different countries, we can discuss the difference between nationally- vs internationally-important bands. One case is the one of Björk: the band is in the top 5 most central internationally, even if it does not appear in the top 10 most central bands in a network including only bands from Iceland -- its country of origin. Fig 1(a) also shows how we can use network distances to analyze and visualize relationships between music genres. We treat the number of releases a band makes in a given genre as a node attribute, and perform dimensionality reduction on the network to highlight how genres played by the same bands co-locate in this complex manifold. This can be used as the basis of a novel classification of musical genres. In a final relevant analysis at the intersection of network science and cultural data analytics, we can fit exponential random graphs to see which node attributes of the bands influence edge creation. We discover that the most important factor leading two bands to have significant artist-sharing relationship is having records produced by the same label (explaining 20% of the variance in the edge existence variable), followed by playing similar genres and hailing from the same country (both explaining 11% of the variance each). For machine learning on graphs, this dataset provides an ideal test scenario to perform Graph Neural Network (GNN) tasks. We can use the nodes attributes to predict a target one -- i.e. using label information to predict country of origin. By performing message-passing on the network, a GNN can outperform a Neural Network (NN) classifier which would only use the node attributes without topological information -- as Fig 1(b) shows. The precision-recall curves shows a clear superiority of GNN approaches over NN ones, providing a clean dataset for testing new GNN architectures. In summary, we are providing a valuable resources for network science, cultural data analytics, and machine learning, showing only a few of the potential interesting analyses that this dataset supports. The data will be made available on Zenodo once the process of data coding and cleaning is complete.
items.authors: Coscia, Michele*

items.title: Green building blocks reveal the complex anatomy of climate change mitigation technologies
items.abstract: Decarbonization is often framed as “deploying green technologies”, but the transition is not a monolithic shift. Instead, many climate-change mitigation technologies emerge when an existing nongreen technology (the problem/source domain) is augmented with modular components that reduce its carbon footprint. Building on theories of combinatorial innovation, we introduce a network representation of this process by identifying Green Building Blocks (GBBs)—reusable mitigation modules that can be recombined across domains. Using 8.4 million USPTO patents (1976–2022) and defining CCMT patents via the Y02/Y04 tagging scheme, we pair each green patent with functionally similar nongreen patents using semantic text embeddings, and validate the functional-similarity ranking against expert human judgments (normalized correlation ~ 0.81). We then extract “extra” CPC codes that appear on the green patent but not on its nongreen match, denoise these signals with a CPC2vec similarity model, and cluster the resulting additions (UMAP + HDBSCAN) into 82 GBBs; clustering shared codes yields 193 nongreen source fields. The resulting bipartite source–GBB network maps which mitigation modules can be applied to which incumbent technologies, illustrating cases where broadly applicable GBBs (e.g., fuel cells) contrast with narrowly specialized solutions. We find that the source–GBB adjacency matrix is nested: a small set of general-purpose GBBs connects to many sources, while most GBBs are specialized, and some sources have only a short “menu” of decarbonization options. This structure is not merely descriptive. At the micro level, a firm’s prior activity in GBBs relevant to a CCMT class predicts entry into that class, with the GBB signal over three times stronger than source-field experience, and with strong complementarity between the two. At the meso level, we treat alliance formation as link prediction: for 5,947 high-patenting firms, we compute “missing-GBB” complementarity against 7,190 potential partner institutions and show that green co-assignment alliances concentrate among the most complementary pairs. Finally, the most complementary partners are frequently cross-border—optimal partners for ~84% of US, 87% of German, and 92% of Chinese firms are foreign—suggesting that frictions in international collaboration may materially narrow the feasible option set for rapid mitigation innovation.
items.authors: LI, YANG*; Neffke, Frank

items.title: Idea Timing: Structure as the Hidden Variable in Innovation
items.abstract: Technological evolution is often punctuated by disruptive events [1]. To understand this punctuated evolution, we examine how community structure evolves in networks of U.S. patent technology codes. We construct technology networks from U.S. patent data between 1865 and 2014, where nodes represent technology codes and edges capture their co-occurrences within patents. We find that densely interconnected codes self-organize into modules whose life cycles exhibit birth, growth, division, and recombination [2]. Coarse-graining these communities reveals system-level reconfigurations, including war-driven recombinations and the emergence of semiconductor clusters, corresponding to disruptive events in the technological space. We observe that macro-level structures undergo punctuated reconfiguration, even as micro-level structures evolve more continuously (Fig. 1a–c). To quantify these structural shifts, we measure the stability of community structure across time using Normalized Mutual Information (NMI). By aggregating forward- and backward-looking NMI, we define a measure of structural stability. Declines in this stability mark discrete transitions between stable regimes, often with forward-backward asymmetries (Fig. 1d–e). We apply this framework to rolling five-year patent networks from 1976 to 2014 and analyze these transition impacts on innovation. Tracking NMI-based stability over time, we classify years into transition- and stable-regime reference points: end-point years are local minima where declining stability reverses to recovery within the z < 0 range; start-point years are the immediately following years; and peak years are all local maxima within positive-stability intervals between transitions (Fig. 1f–g). These macro-level reconfigurations follow a punctuated pattern with episodic shifts [3]. Linking these transition periods to innovation outcomes, we find that patents granted at transition start points outperform those at end points in both deviation and annualized change of annual average five-year forward citations (Fig. 1h–i) as well as in market value (Fig. 1j). In contrast, peak years outperform other years not within transitions. These findings show that innovation advantage depends not only on cumulative processes, but on entry into episodes of network reorganization. Our framework reveals how innovation impact varies across phases of structural transition. [1] Dosi, G. Technological paradigms and technological trajectories. Research Policy 11, 147–162 (1982). [2] Palla, G., Barab´asi, A.-L. & Vicsek, T. Quantifying social group evolution. Nature 446, 664–667 (2007). [3] Gould, S. J. & Eldredge, N. Punctuated equilibria. Paleobiology 3, 115–151 (1977).
items.authors: Yang, Seolmin*; Jeong, Dawoon; Ahn, Yong-Yeol; Youn, Hyejin

items.title: Structural Diversity and Network Versatility are Higher in Informal Structures of Collaboration
items.abstract: Most scholarly exchange occurs outside formal co-authorship, through informal collaborations that provide advice, feedback, and guidance but remain largely invisible in publication-based records. We make this “invisible college” visible by extracting informal ties from acknowledgment sections of published papers and compare their importance vis-à-vis with the more formal coauthorship ties. Using all articles published in Political Science journals between 2003 and 2024 (129,750 articles and 85,653 authors), we reconstruct co-authorship and acknowledgment networks and compare their structural diversity. Following past research on how the structure of the network might influence the diversity of information one has access to, we operationalize structural diversity using ego-network fragmentation after ego removal, the number of long ties that the ego enables, and the number of stochastic block model partitions of the network that it connects. Additionally, to assess whether structural diversity corresponds to substantive diversity, we measure topical diversity in authors’ research as measured through structural topic modeling. We find systematic differences between formal and informal collaboration networks. Informal networks expose researchers in comparable meso-scale structural positions (e.g., K-core or SBM communities) to significantly more topic-diverse environments. Moreover, we also observe how the ego-centered structural diversity positions is associated with greater integration of diverse knowledge, an effect that is stronger for informal collaborations. These ties allow scholars to draw on advice and feedback from a broader and more topically diverse set of colleagues, while also revealing unequal access to informal networking opportunities. Finally, our results show that structural diversity in informal collaboration networks captures meaningful variation in access to diverse knowledge and may help explain subsequent academic outcomes.
items.authors: Danus, Lluis*; Dinneen, William; Torreblanca, Carolina; Grossman, Guy; Gonzalez-Bailon, Sandra

items.title: Beyond Skills and Similarity: Multilayer Networks and Relational Mechanisms in Team Formation
items.abstract: Understanding how teams form in networked environments is a foundational problem in network science, organizational research, and computational social science [1]. Prior work has often explained team assembly through individual attributes—such as competence, expertise, or demographic similarity—while treating relational structure as background context. However, teams emerge within evolving social networks, where prior relationships, trust, and interaction history may play a decisive role. This raises a core question: Which network mechanisms most strongly shape collaborator selection when individuals assemble teams over time? This paper examines team formation by integrating multi-theoretical, multi-level (MTML) frameworks [2] with data-driven network modeling. We analyze team assembly processes across four distinct empirical settings, where individuals form teams while embedded in pre-existing collaboration networks: student projects, software development teams, scientific collaborations, and space crews. The resulting consolidated, open-access database captures individual attributes, dyadic relationship quality, evolving network structure, and downstream team outcomes, enabling comparative analysis across contexts. To model team formation as an endogenous network process, we employ Exponential Random Graph Models (ERGMs) and Multi-Level ERGMs (MLERGMs) [3]. These models allow us to simultaneously account for individual-level attributes (e.g., competence, experience, similarity), dyadic relational features (e.g., prior collaboration quality), and higher-order structural dependencies. Rather than treating team membership as a purely rational or attribute-driven choice, this approach models collaborator selection as a probabilistic process shaped by embedded social relations. Across all four settings, we find a consistent and robust pattern: the quality of prior relationships is a significantly stronger predictor of collaborator selection than either individual competence or attribute similarity. Individuals are substantially more likely to choose collaborators with whom they have high-quality prior ties, even when alternative candidates exhibit greater expertise or closer similarity. These effects persist across model specifications and levels of analysis, highlighting relational embeddedness as a central mechanism of team assembly. This work demonstrates the value of MLERGM frameworks for studying team formation as a multilevel network phenomenon, contributing to a more network-centered understanding of how collaborative groups form and persist. [1] Contractor, N. (2013). Some assembly required: leveraging Web science to understand and enable team assembly. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A: Mathematical, Physical and Engineering Sciences, 371(1987), 20120385. [2] Contractor, N. S., Wasserman, S., & Faust, K. (2006). Testing multitheoretical, multilevel hypotheses about organizational networks: An analytic framework and empirical example. Academy of Management Review, 31(3), 681-703. [3] Krivitsky, P. N., Koehly, L. M., & Marcum, C. S. (2020). Exponential-family random graph models for multi-layer networks. psychometrika, 85(3), 630-659.
items.authors: Xia, Jiarui; Contractor, Noshir; Gomez-Zara, Diego*

items.title: The impact of self-interactions on the interactome
items.abstract: Proteins that assemble into complexes with identical copies of themselves (homomerizing proteins) play a central role on the structure and function of organisms across all domains of life. The resulting homomers shape network structure and evolution and form the basis of a wide range of protein complexes by gene duplication. Yet, we lack network models of gene duplication that incorporate the role of homomerizing proteins. Here, we present and analytically solve a family of gene duplication models that explicitly follow the evolution of homomerizing proteins. Our results demonstrate that homomerizing proteins have a profound impact on the network patterns through network evolution, diversifying the network structure at all scales, starting from degree sequence and clustering to higher-order network patterns of all sizes. As a result, homomerizing proteins can be efficiently inferred from the rest of the interactome, outperforming alternative methods such as AlphaFold [1], as evidenced by our large-scale experimental validations for human proteins, HuRI [2]. Our methods are applicable to any organism and serve as a starting point towards a more complete understanding of the evolution of the structure and function of the interactome.
items.authors: Kovacs, Istvan*; Zhao, Tingyu

items.title: Molecular networks elucidate the epigenetic landscape of food
items.abstract: The Human Genome Project showed that genetic variation alone cannot explain heterogeneity across individuals and populations. Instead, gene expression is dynamically regulated by epigenetic processes, through which many environmental factors act—most notably diet. Historical evidence, such as the Dutch famine, demonstrates that nutritional exposure can induce persistent, even transgenerational, epigenetic effects [1]. Yet, despite extensive epidemiological links between diet and epigenetic regulation, including the role of folate and B vitamins in one-carbon metabolism, a unifying, holistic framework linking food-derived molecules to epigenetic mechanisms is still lacking. Here, we introduce EpiNet, a curated epigenetic interactome built by integrating public databases, validated through specialized Large Language Models (LLMs) (Fig. 1a). EpiNet comprises 1,240 proteins organized in ten epigenetic hallmarks, forming a dense submodule of the protein-protein interaction (PPI) network. Moving beyond expert-defined associations, we apply a network-based reclassification that offers a finer-grained modular organization of epigenetic mechanisms and their intrinsic interdependencies (Fig. 1b). We embedded nutrition onto this epigenetic landscape by reconstructing the chemical and molecular profiles of 1,003 ingredients across 92,246 recipes worldwide. Using network proximity analysis, we identified over 1,000 food-derived compounds with predicted epigenetic activity, enabling the ranking of compounds, ingredients, recipes, and cuisines according to their potential to modulate epigenetic activity (Fig. 1c). Finally, we show that flaxseed consumption—classified as epigenetically active by our framework—is associated with increased survival across ten NHANES IV cycles (1998–2018) (Fig. 1d). Together, these results establish the first network-based framework for the epigenetic landscape of food, linking molecular insights to epidemiological outcomes. By moving beyond isolated nutrient–pathway associations, our approach highlights diet as a central modulator of epigenetic regulation, providing a foundation for precision nutrition in disease prevention and healthy aging. [1] Heijmans, B. T. et al. Persistent epigenetic differences associated with prenatal exposure to famine in humans. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U. S. A. 105, 17046–17049 (2008).
items.authors: Lombardo, Salvo Danilo*; Sebek, Michael; Menichetti, Giulia

items.title: Host-specific recovery of the healthy human gut microbiota after antibiotics
items.abstract: Following disruption by antibiotic treatment, the human gut microbiota undergoes a recovery process that can extend over several weeks to months, typically restoring broad community features such as species diversity and converging toward a general healthy state. However, it remains unclear whether these recovery trajectories reflect a host-specific process where each microbiome tends to re- establish its own original pre-treatment composition (“host-specific recovery”), or instead represent the assembly and engraftment of taxa commonly found in healthy individuals, leading to a more host-independent post-treatment configuration. Because the microbiome plays a critical role in health and disease, understanding the natural dynamics determine when it can be shifted versus when it remains host-specific resilient is essential for developing effective interventions. Here, we quantify the extent to which each individual’s post-antibiotic state resembles their own baseline compared to alternative baselines and to randomized assemblages, and found that approximately 70% of individuals exhibit clear host-specific recovery. Moreover, we show that the recovery process unfolds in two distinct phases: a rapid rebound of species that survive antibiotic exposure and thrive again within days, and a slower reassembly phase involving the gradual colonization of new taxa over weeks to months. Yet even when focusing solely on the slower reassembly phase, a substantial host-specific signal remains: about 45% of individuals still display host-specific recovery, reflecting the higher likelihood of reassembling returning baseline taxa than of incorporating taxa not previously present. Taken together, these results suggest that post-antibiotic community assembly and recolonization are not random processes, but are structured by ecological and biological selection mechanisms. One plausible interpretation is that historical contingency contributes to this structure: following antibiotic perturbation, the resulting community composition may not be determined solely by contemporary environmental conditions. Rather, it may be constrained by the pre-existing ecological context, defined by the interaction network linking antibiotic-surviving species with both recolonizing taxa that were present prior to perturbation and genuinely novel colonizers. To examine how historical contingency could enhance the colonization probability of both newly introduced species and taxa lost during antibiotic perturbation, we developed a numerical simulation based on the generalized Lotka–Volterra model. Using a top-down approach that does not require explicit knowledge of the underlying interaction network, we show that certain species are preferentially able to engraft after antibiotics, relative to others. This preferential engraftment arises from their interactions with species that survive the perturbation. Importantly, when the interactions between candidate colonizing species and antibiotic-surviving taxa are removed, this advantage is abolished, indicating that network-mediated interactions are necessary for the observed effect. Using our method, we detect a significant positive effect in more than 40% of individuals across two independent studies. These results are consistent with the hypothesis that historical contingency contributes to host-specific microbiome recovery following antibiotic perturbation.
items.authors: Kahn, Shaya*; Bashan, Amir

items.title: Network-driven discovery of food compounds for aging therapeutics
items.abstract: Network medicine has become an effective tool for disease classification and drug design through mapping the protein-protein interaction network, identifying disease modules, and uncovering complex network relationships among gene expressions and disease phenotypes [1]. Although aging is not a disease, a recent study found that the hallmarks of aging - biological processes causing functional decline and vulnerability to disease - also form modules in the human interactome that can be leveraged for repurposing drugs to slow aging [2]. However, despite the essential role of food in human longevity, the impact of food compounds on aging remains largely unexplored in network medicine. In this work, we developed a novel systematic pipeline to identify food compounds conducive to longevity, essentially treating food chemicals as drugs. We combine data on the protein-protein interaction network, the targets of food compounds, and expression data for aging genes to determine a list of food chemicals with statistically significant impact on aging hallmark genes in the positive (pro-longevity) direction, leveraging network proximity and a transcription-based metric called pAGE (pro-age). We first validate that a significant portion of the compounds obtained from the pipeline have literature evidence for extending lifespan and propose others as candidates for cell line experiments. Second, we conduct an enrichment analysis of the compounds between pro-longevity and unhealthy diets. Third, we find that pro-longevity compounds have structural similarity through a learned embedding of the food compound database. Finally, we construct the mechanism of action for food compounds, illustrating that the network medicine pipeline uncovers biological network pathways that cannot be retrieved through compound targets alone. [1] Barabási, A. L., Gulbahce, N., & Loscalzo, J. (2011). Network medicine: a network-based approach to human disease. Nature reviews genetics, 12(1), 56-68. [2] Gross, B., Ehlert, J., Gladyshev, V. N., Loscalzo, J., & Barabási, A. L. (2025). Network-driven discovery of repurposable drugs targeting hallmarks of aging. ArXiv, arXiv-2509.
items.authors: Kerdelian, Garo*; Gross, Bnaya; Barabási, Albert-László

items.title: From Molecular Networks to Diet: Identifying Synergistic Vitamin Effects in Myocardial Infarction
items.abstract: Diet is a major modifiable determinant of cardiovascular disease risk, yet nutritional research has largely relied on reductionist paradigms that evaluate individual nutrients in isolation. Such approaches overlook the inherently networked nature of biological systems and the fact that dietary components act as coordinated, multi-target perturbations of molecular pathways. Here, we present a network medicine framework that integrates molecular interaction networks with population-level dietary networks to identify synergistic combinations of vitamins modulating myocardial infarction (MI) risk. Our central hypothesis is that cardiovascular pathophysiology is organized into localized disease modules within the human protein–protein interaction network, and that vitamins influence disease risk by targeting proteins within or proximal to these modules. To test this hypothesis, we construct tissue-contextualized protein interaction networks and identify disease modules associated with key cardiovascular phenotypes, including thrombosis, inflammation, fibrosis, and vascular remodeling. We quantify the mechanistic relevance of individual vitamins by computing network proximity between vitamin target sets and disease modules, benchmarking observed distances against degree-matched null models to prioritize vitamins whose molecular targets are topologically aligned with disease-relevant neighborhoods. Because vitamins are consumed as part of complex dietary patterns, we next model vitamin co-consumption using Gaussian graphical models applied to large-scale population dietary and biomarker data. These probabilistic networks capture conditional dependencies among vitamins, disentangling direct nutrient relationships from spurious correlations driven by overall diet quality or shared food sources. Community detection on the inferred vitamin networks reveals synergistic vitamin groups that co-occur in real diets and are jointly associated with reduced myocardial infarction prevalence through modulation of disease-relevant phenotypes. These communities define network-informed dietary scores that provide a data-driven alternative to expert-defined nutrient indices commonly used in public health. Finally, we connect molecular and population-scale networks by mapping vitamin communities onto food composition data and performing in silico food substitution analyses constrained within consumption categories. This approach enables targeted increases in protective vitamin communities while preserving the overall structure of food macrogroups that reflect behavioral and cultural dietary habits. Together, these results illustrate how network science can translate molecular mechanisms into realistic, population-level dietary interventions, providing a scalable and mechanism-driven framework for identifying synergistic nutritional strategies to reduce chronic disease risk.
items.authors: Aldana, Andres*

items.title: Prioritizing the disease multiome with variant-coupled multilayer quantitative trait loci networks
items.abstract: Background The influence of genetic variants on molecular phenotypes, measured by molecular quantitative trait loci (molQTLs), offer invaluable insights into the functional consequences of genetics. While molQTLs have been used extensively in genetic epidemiology, no studies have so far attempted to analyze these relationships at the systems level by mapping trait-specific variant-omic associations onto large-scale multilayer networks. We hypothesize that deploying diffusion-based multilayer network prioritization algorithms on multilayer molQTL networks coupled by single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) can provide unique mechanistic insights into the long-range and coordinated effects of genetic variants on multiple types of molecular traits. Methods We obtained blood- and plasma-based summary-level molQTL data for gene expression (eQTL), DNA methylation (meQTL), and protein abundance (pQTL) from the eQTLGen, EPIGen, and UK Biobank Pharma Proteomics consortia, respectively. We built a trait-agnostic heterogeneous multilayer network in which each network layer is an undirected bipartite molQTL network, with cis- and trans-QTLs represented as edges between SNPs and transcripts, CpG sites or proteins, interconnected by shared SNPs. We identified differentially expressed transcripts, CpG sites, and proteins in multiple cancer types in The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA). We used the resulting differentially expressed omics as seed nodes in a random walk with restarts on universal heterogeneous and multiplex networks (RWR-MH) algorithm to jointly prioritize transcripts, CpG sites, and proteins for each cancer type. We developed mixed-seed and leave-one-omic modality-out benchmarking approaches, and used the area under the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) and precision-recall (PR) curves to assess prediction performance. Results The resulting multilayer molQTL network included an eQTL layer with 725,803 SNPs and 9,929 transcripts, a meQTL layer with 209,138 SNPs and 247,429 CpG sites, and a pQTL layer with 11,624 SNPs and 2,525 proteins. All layers had a significant proportion of nodes in the largest connected component (73.6% for eQTL, 73.7% for meQTL, and 95.1% for pQTL). The mixed-seed benchmark, which used differentially expressed transcripts and proteins and differentially methylated CpG sites (|FC|>1; FDR<0.05) as seeds, achieved an AUPRC of 0.877 for breast cancer, identifying a balanced mix of omics, including non-seeds. In the leave-one-omic modality-out benchmark, which used disease-associated omics from one modality as seeds and aimed to identify disease-associated omics from the remaining two modalities, using CpGs, transcripts and proteins as seeds resulted in an increased prediction performance with AUROCs of 0.578, 0.657 and 0.731, respectively, for identifying disease-associated transcript/protein, CpG/protein, and transcript/CpG sets in breast cancer. Conclusions We propose a novel approach to integrate molQTLs using multilayer networks and prioritize disease-associated transcripts, CpG sites and proteins using a multilayer network diffusion algorithm. Our preliminary findings suggest the potential utility of our method to uncover the coordinated effects of molQTLs at the global level in a trait-specific way, paving the way to identify multi-omic diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers that work in tandem.
items.authors: Halu, Arda*

items.title: Discovering Communities in Continuous-Time Temporal Networks by Optimizing L-Modularity
items.abstract: Community detection is a fundamental problem in network analysis, with many applications in various fields. Extending community detection to the temporal setting with exact temporal accuracy, as required by real-world dynamic data, necessitates methods specifically adapted to the temporal nature of interactions. We introduce LAGO, a novel method for uncovering dynamic communities by greedy optimization of Longitudinal Modularity, a specific adaptation of Modularity for continuous-time networks. Unlike prior approaches that rely on time discretization or assume rigid community evolution, LAGO captures the precise moments when nodes enter and exit communities. We evaluate LAGO on synthetic benchmarks and real-world datasets, demonstrating its ability to efficiently uncover temporally and topologically coherent communities.
items.authors: Brabant, Victor*

items.title: Engagement steady states and volatility in temporal interaction networks
items.abstract: Temporal networks provide a natural framework for studying how interactions evolve in complex systems, from organizations and online communities to transportation and communication infrastructures~\cite{science}. While much work has focused on structural properties or diffusion processes, less attention has been given to low-dimensional engagement summaries that remain comparable as network size and activity fluctuate. This work proposes two temporal engagement indices for weighted interaction networks: a Temporal Network Intensity index that captures average edge activity, and a Temporal Dominance Inequality index that captures how unevenly activity is distributed across nodes.
