Interaction networks are modeled as sequences of weekly snapshots with edge weights capturing the volume of one-to-one communication. The dominance index applies a Gini-style inequality measure to nodes’ weighted degrees, quantifying how concentrated engagement is among a few actors. Using six real-world temporal interaction networks (organizational email, online forums, and Wikipedia communication), the study shows that both indices are remarkably stationary over time. For each network, more than 80\% of week-to-week changes in intensity fall below 10\% despite large swings in the number of active nodes and edges, and probability distributions place each network in a narrow region of the (intensity, inequality) plane, suggesting engagement ``steady states''~\cite{miller2019constant}. Weekly index pairs also serve as a compact fingerprint: a simple multi-class classifier infers the underlying network from (intensity, inequality) with F1 scores around 0.85–0.95, even when time-series lengths differ. An important exception is the Enron managers’ email network before the company’s collapse. There, intensity is higher and both indices show persistent large week-to-week changes, so neither can be treated as stationary. This contrast suggests that departures from a network’s typical engagement steady state may provide early signals of organizational stress or structural change, complementing change-point detection methods that operate directly on network topology. Viewed together, these results show that temporal interaction networks often self-organize into stable engagement regimes that are robust to size fluctuations, and that simple intensity–inequality indices can both characterize these regimes and highlight rare periods of instability.
items.authors: Miller, Hadar; Mokryn, Osnat*

items.title: Modeling Continuous-time Interval Graphs using Marked Hawkes Process Network Models
items.abstract: In many application settings involving temporal networks, edges denote quantities that have durations, such as lengths of phone calls between two people. Such temporal networks with edge durations have often been referred to as interval graphs [1], as edges are active over time intervals. In continuous-time interval graphs, each edge is observed through timestamped events represented by a quadruplet (𝑢,𝑣,𝑡,𝑚), indicating a directed interaction from node 𝑢 to 𝑣 that starts at time 𝑡 with a duration of length 𝑚, as shown in Figure 1. Most existing continuous-time network models are based on relational event models, which only model instantaneous events of the form (𝑢,𝑣,𝑡) ignoring their duration. For many types of interactions, including phone calls and meetings, modeling temporal networks requires capturing not only when interactions occur but also how long they last. These edge durations can be viewed as marks accompanying the timestamped relational events. We propose a general approach for modeling continuous-time interval graphs by using marked Hawkes processes to jointly model edge start times (using the Hawkes process event times) and edge durations (using the marks). We apply our modeling framework to extend two recently proposed continuous-time network models, the Multivariate Community Hawkes (MULCH) model [2] and the Latent Space Hawkes (LSH) model [3], to interval graphs. MULCH is based on a stochastic block model, where each node belongs to one of 𝐾 latent blocks, and the probability of forming edges between nodes depends only on their latent blocks. LSH is based on the latent space model, where each node is represented by a vector in a continuous latent space, and the probability of forming edges between nodes depends on the distance between their latent vectors. For both MULCH and LSH, we consider two different latent modeling approaches: a single shared latent representation, where edge start times and durations are modeled by the same latent block or vector, and a dual latent representation, where edge start times and durations are each modeled by separate latent blocks or vectors. We apply our proposed MULCH-Interval and LSH-Interval models to a variety of real network data sets with different types of edges with durations, including phone calls and bike share rentals. Our models achieve competitive prediction accuracy for both edge start times and durations, with the dual latent representations usually resulting in higher prediction accuracy. Moreover, our models offer strong interpretability for model-based exploratory analysis. For example, in the network of bike share rentals, we find that pairs of bike stations with longer durations of rentals are generally placed further apart in the latent space for LSH-Interval with dual latent spaces, which reveals some of the geographical structure of the bike stations, as it takes longer to bike between further stations. Thus, our models are useful both for prediction and yielding meaningful insights when used for exploratory analysis on real-world data. References [1] Holme, Petter, and Jari Saramäki. Temporal networks. Physics Reports 519.3 (2012): 97–125. [2] Soliman, Hadeel et al. The Multivariate Community Hawkes model for dependent relational events in continuous-time networks. International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2022. [3] Huang, Zhipeng et al. A mutually exciting latent space Hawkes process model for continuous-time networks. International Conference on Uncertainty in Artificial Intelligence. PMLR, 2022.
items.authors: Huang, Zhipeng*; Soliman, Hadeel; Xu, Kevin

items.title: Generating realistic temporal networks with periodic RWIG
items.abstract: Many complex systems, such as social, transportation and biological systems, are inherently dynamic, with interactions that change over time. Representing these systems as temporal networks through sequences of time-ordered graphs $G_0, \dots, G_{K-1}$ encodes the timing and order of interactions, which directly affects the spread of dynamical processes such as epidemics and information. This representation allows us to track how connectivity patterns change over time, detect recurring interaction structures and predict both future network changes and the outcomes of dynamical processes. Developing simple and interpretable \emph{generative models} for temporal networks remains an open challenge. Ideally, such models should (i) reproduce realistic contact sequences, (ii) allow analytical treatment, and (iii) shed light on the mechanisms shaping both the topological and temporal structure of networks. In this work, we study the \emph{Random Walkers Induced Temporal Graph (RWIG) model} [1], in which $M$ independent walkers traverse a fixed underlying graph $G$ with $N$ nodes in discrete time steps. Each walker $w_i$ moves according to a Markov process defined by an $N {\times} N$ transition matrix $P_i$ (see Figure 1a). At each time step $k$, walkers occupying the same node generate links according to the \emph{co-location principle}, forming the temporal contact graph $G_k$ (see Figure 1b-c). A physical interpretation of RWIG is a collection of individuals moving through space (e.g. city ) where each node of the underlying graph is a physical location. We focus on the \emph{inverse problem}: given only a single $K$-length contact sequence $G_0, \dots, G_{K-1}$ and without access to the actual movements of the walkers, our goal is to infer the initial transition matrices $P_1, \dots, P_M$ that can reproduce the observed contacts. Motivated by empirical observations that many real-world interactions exhibit bursts, daily or weekly cycles and quasi-periodic patterns, we model the transition matrices of the walkers as \emph{$p$-periodic}, assigning each walker $i$ a sequence of steady-state distributions $\tilde{s}_i[0], \dots, \tilde{s}_i[p-1]$ satisfying \[ \tilde{s}_i[(k+1) \bmod p] = \tilde{s}_i[k] P_i, \quad k = 0, \dots, p-1, \] with each vector $\tilde{s}_i[l]$ being a steady state of $P_i^p$. This formulation ensures that the inferred transition matrices $P_i$ capture the quasi-periodic patterns observed in real temporal networks. Building on our previous results in [2], we prove that when all walkers share the same transition matrix $P_i {=} P$ and $M {\ge} N$, both the number of states $N$ and the steady-state distributions at each period $k$ can be derived as the roots of an $M$-degree polynomial $p_M(z)$ in the complex variable $z$: \[ p_M(z) = \sum_{r=0}^{M} a_r z^r. \] The coefficients $a_0, \dots, a_M$ are defined recursively with $a_M = 1$ and $a_r = - \frac{1}{M-r} \sum_{l=r+1}^{M} a_l \, \sigma_{l,k}$ for $r = 0, \dots, M-1$, where $\sigma_{l,k}$ is the empirical probability of a contact involving $l$ walkers at times $k, k+p, k+2p, ...$. When each walker $w_i$ has its own transition matrix $P_i$, we show that the steady-state distributions and matrices $P_1, \dots, P_M$ can be recovered numerically from the observed probabilities of contacts between walkers. As a result, we propose a \textit{periodic RWIG model} that infers both the period $p$ and the corresponding $p$-periodic transition matrices from observed temporal contact data. We validate the periodic RWIG model on both artificial and empirical datasets, including school, conference and hospital face-to-face interaction networks (e.g. see Figure 1d). The results demonstrate that RWIG can reproduce realistic temporal networks while preserving the observed contact probabilities between walkers, suggesting that many networks can be approximated by a small number of periodic graph sequences reflecting quasi-periodic activity patterns.
items.authors: Shvydun, Sergey*

items.title: Multistable active phases in complex contagion with group plasticity
items.abstract: Higher-order contagion models show that group-based reinforcement can trigger discontinuous transitions, yet these results are typically derived on static hypergraphs that neglect temporal group dynamics. Here, we demonstrate that temporality is essential for correctly characterizing complex contagion by developing an analytically solvable approximate master equation framework that incorporates group switching. Our theory interpolates continuously between quenched and mean-field limits and reveals that increasing group switching rates can induce a discontinuous transition, enlarge the bistable region, and, under sufficiently strong nonlinearities, parallel exposures, or large group sizes, reverse its influence on the invasion threshold, thereby hindering contagion. Mapping empirical group switching rates and group-size heterogeneity to theory reveals the emergence of four distinct dynamical phases, combining continuous or discontinuous transitions with localized or delocalized activity. Remarkably, in localized regimes, we uncover multistable active phases in which multiple endemic states coexist, extending classical bistability. Furthermore, the emergence of discontinuous transitions requires stronger nonlinear reinforcement than previously thought, highlighting that both static-network simulations and mean-field analyses can yield qualitatively misleading predictions.
items.authors: Lamata Otín, Santiago*; Malizia, Federico; Keating, Leah A.; St-Onge, Guillaume ; Latora, Vito; Gómez Gardeñes, Jesús; Hébert-Dufresne, Laurent

items.title: The interplay of network structure and correlated infectious traits in epidemic models
items.abstract: Individual contributions to the spread of an epidemic vary widely due to an individual's location in a social network and their intrinsic ability to transmit or contract diseases. While the effect of heterogeneous population structure and infection rates is well-understood, less studied is the impact of population-level covariance between susceptibility and transmissibility, despite empirical evidence showing that both susceptibility and transmission vary across individuals. We introduce a mathematical modeling framework incorporating population subgroups, each with their own joint distribution of susceptibility and transmissibility. We apply this framework to the susceptible-infected-recovered (SIR) model to examine the effect of community structure and degree heterogeneity. We derive analytical expressions for the basic reproduction number, which, when reduced, corroborates prior results and validate these results with numerical simulations. We pair these estimates with simulations exploring first, the temporal dynamics of this model with the homogeneous SIR model, and second, implications for effective social intervention. This analysis provides a foundation for future studies exploring the interplay between structural and dynamical heterogeneity in infectious disease transmission.
items.authors: Gupta, Abhay*; Landry, Nicholas

items.title: To trace or not to trace: analytical insights from network-based contact-tracing models
items.abstract: Contact tracing is one of the most important control measures deployed during epidemics and pandemics, relying on the identification of contacts of known infected individuals [1,2]. A network-based description is therefore essential for its accurate modeling [3,4]. Pairwise models have been used extensively to study contact tracing, but existing analytical results typically rely on a strong decoupling assumption—most commonly that contact tracing operates on a much faster timescale than disease transmission [3]. Here, we relax this assumption and provide a full analytical characterization of the epidemic threshold without requiring fast tracing. Our analysis employs a fast-variables approach [5], capturing the rapid early stabilization of key network quantities, including the average number of susceptible neighbors per infected individual and the average number of infected neighbors of a typical tracing-triggering node. Inspired by social adoption dynamics, we further introduce an extended tracing mechanism in which infected individuals may be traced not only via direct contact with a tracingtriggering neighbor, but also indirectly as part of a triple containing two such nodes. Using the resulting pairwise model and analytical threshold condition, we map out the interplay between disease dynamics and the critical contact tracing efficiency required to prevent epidemic invasion. We additionally show that this critical tracing rate is strongly model dependent: in particular, contact tracing vanishes from the outbreak threshold in a classical mean-field formulation that neglects network structure. Together, these results yield a rigorous and tractable threshold condition for contact tracing on networks, extending the applicability of pairwise models and providing new insight into the interplay between disease progression and tracing processes.
items.authors: de Meijere, Giulia; Pugliese, Andrea; Gonzalez, Gerardo; Simon, Peter; Kiss, Istvan*

items.title: Modeling diffusion in networks with communities: a multitype branching process approach
items.abstract: The dynamics of diffusion in complex networks are widely studied to understand how entities, such as information, diseases, or behaviors, spread in an interconnected environment. Complex networks often present community structure, and tools to analyze diffusion processes on networks with communities are needed. We present our recent work showing how a multitype branching process model can be used to shed light on the interplay between community structure and disease (or information) spread [1]. We model the diffusion of information using the popular independent cascade model (ICM), where node infections occur in discrete time. Nodes can be in three states: inactive, active, or removed. Active nodes attempt to activate their network neighbors once, before becoming removed themselves in the next time step. The process continues until there are no active nodes to carry on the process. We show how, by using limited information about the network---the degree distribution within and between communities (see Fig.~\ref{fig:model}a)) --- we can use probability-generating functions (PGFs) to track the number of active nodes in a two-community network $\G{N}{t}(s_1,s_2) = \sum_{n=0}^\infty \sum_{m=0}^\infty P[N_1(t)=n,N_2(t)=m] s_1^n s_2^m$, where $N_1(t)$ and $N_2(t)$ are the random variables for the number of active nodes in communities 1 and 2 by generation $t$. This PGF is particularly useful in deriving expressions for the probability of extinction. Additionally, we are able to derive new quantities specific to spreading processes in networks with communities, such as the the probability of (re)introduction of a disease into a community where it either has never spread before or stopped spreading in the past, which would be hard to analytically calculate otherwise. Furthermore, these tools alow us to probe the impact of the community structure on spreading behavior. Figure~\ref{fig:model}b) and c) show the impact of community structure for a two-community network. Community 1 and 2 have average degrees of $3$ and $7$, respectively, with their degree distributions following an approximately log-normal distribution. We can easily isolate the effect of communities on the cascade size distribution by removing the inter-community links (confining the spread to the seed community) and comparing this to the case where we allow spreading between communities. We can note from Fig.~\ref{fig:model}b) that it is indeed due to the community structure that community 1 sees a change in its cascade size distribution, where the inter community links provide a means of linking to the higher, on average, degree nodes in community 2. However, if we seed activations in community 2 (Fig.~\ref{fig:model}c)) we see no such discernible benefit for community 2 from being connected to community 1, where the inter community links provide a means of linking to the lower, on average, degree nodes in community 1, which does not have a qualitative effect on the cascade size distributions shape.
items.authors: O'Sullivan, David *; Pena, Caroline; Dubovskaya, Alina

items.title: Correlated heterogeneity in simple contagions mimics complex contagions
items.abstract: The study of epidemic spreading in structured populations has been an impactful domain of network science since its inception. Recent work has found that a variety of infection kernels---functions specifying infection likelihood given exposure to infected individuals---underlie spreading phenomena in networks, from the ``simple" contagion kernels characteristic of infectious diseases to ``complex" contagion kernels commonly associated with the spread of social behaviors~[1]. However, determining which type of contagion is spreading in a population is difficult. For example, recent studies have shown that interacting simple contagions [2] and uncertain transmission settings [3] may be confounded with complex contagions. We show that heterogeneity in simple contagion kernels, when correlated with population structure, can produce spreading dynamics resembling complex contagions. In particular, when subpopulations---each with its own simple infection kernel---are organized into communities, the resulting infection patterns can mimic complex contagion dynamics. Using recent nonparametric contagion models [4], we infer infection kernels from time-series data on nodal states. We find that assuming a single infection kernel for an entire population can yield an inferred kernel displaying the nonlinear behavior commonly associated with complex contagions, even when only simple contagion kernels are present.
items.authors: Kaiser, Daniel*; Tang, Katerina; Thompson, William; Young, Jean-Gabriel; Hébert-Dufresne, Laurent; Landry, Nicholas

items.title: The impact of household physical distancing on the transmission of SARS-CoV-2
items.abstract: Households constitute a fundamental mesoscale unit of epidemic spreading, where transmission dynamics are governed by highly structured and adaptive contact networks. How these contact patterns are spontaneously reshaped by behavioral responses following the introduction of an infection, however, remains poorly quantified and is often neglected in epidemic models. To address this issue, we analyzed a large prospective household transmission study of SARS-CoV-2 in the United States (April 2020–April 2021), combining detailed behavioral data and longitudinal testing data to quantify how contact network topology and its temporal reconfiguration following infection awareness shape epidemic outcomes (Figure 1). Using Exponential Random Graph Models (ERGMs), we infer physical contact networks within 280 households at multiple time points, capturing effects of household size, age-specific mixing, contacts clustering, and role asymmetry associated with index cases. We find that awareness of infection induces a sharp, non-uniform rewiring of household networks, characterized by a 77% reduction in contact density and selective avoidance of index cases, while preserving age- structured interaction patterns and higher-order network motifs. We embed the inferred networks into an individual-based, two-level mixing epidemic model that explicitly couples within-household transmission along network edges with community-level exposure. Calibrating the model via Bayesian inference to longitudinal infection data collected in the study households, we quantify how the timing of behavioral network adaptation modulates secondary attack risk. Counterfactual simulations show that initiating contact reductions at symptom onset, rather than after test confirmation, can reduce secondary household infections by over 25% in medium-sized households, with pronounced age-specific effects. Our results highlight the central role of adaptive, small-scale contact networks in epidemic dynamics and demonstrate how network inference combined with mechanistic spreading models can bridge behavioral responses and transmission outcomes. Beyond COVID-19, this framework provides a generalizable approach for incorporating empirically grounded, time-varying network structure into epidemic models.
items.authors: Coletti, Pietro*

items.title: Generating function framework for spatiotemporal outbreak analytics
items.abstract: Branching processes and their solutions via probability generating functions (PGFs) are a cornerstone of network science and network epidemiology. They enable the calculation of various quantities like extinction probabilities and outbreak size distributions for transmission networks [1]. However, these efficient analytical approaches have yet to be extended to operational public health tools essential for real-time forecasting and public health decision-making. Here, we develop a broadly applicable, computationally efficient framework for spatiotemporal outbreak prediction and inference leveraging generating functions. Building on Ref. [2], we formalize a novel generating function methodology to encode and extract joint distributions of arbitrary observables, for example, cases and hospitalizations, across space and time (Fig 1, panel a). To make this framework practical and efficient in computational epidemiology settings, we leverage the cumulant generating function to characterize the first-order cumulants (mean, covariance) of observables, and use moment-matching to model high-dimensional joint distributions. The resulting approach enables the principled Bayesian inference of important disease parameters for structured epidemic models, obtaining similar posterior distributions as state-of-the-art approximate Bayesian computation (ABC), but at a fraction of the computational cost (Fig 1, panel b). We further demonstrate accurate predictions for complex metapopulation networks, extending the framework’s applicability to the spatiotemporal spread of epidemics (Fig 1, panel c). By transforming generating functions into a computational and statistical inference engine, we provide a pragmatic bridge between theoretical network models and operational public health tools, enabling rapid, data-driven outbreak response.
items.authors: Boudreau, Mariah*; St-Onge, Guillaume

items.title: Nanoscale networks for information processing
items.abstract: Most of today's activities rely on hardware and algorithms designed to process information. Artificial Intelligence has become so pervasive that it is now applied to even the simplest tasks. Yet this ubiquity brings with it serious concerns about its cost in terms of resources. At the same time chip manufacturing is proximate to reach its physical limits. For these reasons, unconventional computing strategies that rely on a physics-based approach are receiving significant research attention. Among the most promising are those leveraging devices built from disordered networks of nano-materials [1]. Such systems compute directly at the matter level, with information processing emerging from the material’s own physical structure and dynamic behavior under electrical stimulation. Indeed, these computational substrates are complex networked systems at the nanoscale. While structural properties vary across distinct resolution scales, functional behavior emerges from the collective dynamics of many interacting elements, rather than from the sum of individual properties. Despite rapid progress in the development of novel disordered functional nanomaterials and related devices [1,2,3,4,5], network science has so far played only a marginal role in their analysis and design, even though it offers substantial potential benefits. In this talk, we show how the convergence of network science and nanotechnology enables new strategies for the analysis, modeling, and design of complex nanoscale systems within a network-centered theoretical framework [1,3,4]. Such an approach allows the realization of functions through structure, supporting novel physics-based concepts of information processing in unconventional computing architectures.
items.authors: Cipollini, Davide*

items.title: Quantum Network-Based Prediction of Cancer Driver Genes
items.abstract: Identification of cancer driver genes is fundamental for the development of targeted therapeutic interventions. The integration of mutational profiles with protein-protein interaction (PPI) networks offers a promising avenue for their detection, but scaling to large network datasets is computationally demanding. Quantum computing offers compact representations and potential complexity reductions. Motivated by the classical method of Gumpinger *et al.*, in this work we introduce a supervised quantum framework that combines mutation scores with network topology via a novel state preparation scheme, *Quantum Multi-order Moment Embedding* (QMME). QMME encodes low-order statistical moments over the mutation scores of a node’s immediate and second-order neighbors, and encodes this information into quantum states. These are used as inputs to a kernel-based quantum binary classifier that discriminates known driver genes from others. Simulations on an empirical PPI network demonstrate competitive performance, with a 12.6% recall gain over a classical baseline. The pipeline performs explicit quantum state preparation and requires no classical training, enabling an efficient, nearly end-to-end quantum workflow. A brief complexity analysis suggests the approach could achieve a quantum speedup in network-based cancer gene prediction. This work underscores the potential of supervised quantum graph learning frameworks to advance biological discovery.
items.authors: Coutinho, Bruno*

items.title: Hyperedge Classification for Predicting High-Order Drug Interactions in Pharmacological Networks
items.abstract: High-order networks exhibit emergent relations that cannot be reduced to dyadic interactions or modeled through traditional graph-based techniques. Pharmacological networks represent a notable example of such high-order complexity, where adverse outcomes frequently arise when multiple drugs are co-administered. These complex drug-drug interactions impose a significant burden on healthcare systems, contributing to annual costs exceeding $177 billion in the United States. In this study, we model the pharmacological system as a hypergraph, where nodes represent drugs and hyperedges encode interacting drug combinations of size two to five. We cast the prediction of myopathy-inducing combinations as a hyperedge prediction task, allowing direct modeling of multi-drug interactions beyond pairwise relationships. Each drug is characterized using Morgan fingerprints to capture structural features and multi-hot vectors of known protein targets to represent biological mechanisms. We employ a hybrid propagation strategy that integrates feature-based similarity with co-occurrence information from observed hyperedges, allowing the learned embeddings to reflect both molecular similarity and real-world co-administration patterns. Building on these embeddings, we introduce an intra-hyperedge self-attention mechanism to model context-dependent interactions within each drug combination. Drug features are first projected into a latent space, after which the attention module assigns importance weights to individual drugs by jointly considering their intrinsic features and the surrounding hypergraph structure, including co-occurrence and neighborhood information. This enables the model to identify influential drugs within a combination while capturing higher-order interaction effects. A gated fusion layer then integrates intrinsic and contextual representations, and a permutation-invariant pooling operation produces interpretable hyperedge-level embeddings that reflect the relative contribution of each drug. The results demonstrate stable predictive performance across folds (accuracy ≈ 0.89$, F1 ≈ 0.89$, AUC ≈ 0.96$), providing evidence that the model can effectively capture high-order pharmacological risk and generate reliable estimates for previously unseen drug combinations. Although only a limited number of studies have addressed high-order drug interactions, our model achieves a performance that is among the strongest reported for this prediction task.
items.authors: Shirmohammady, Naeem*; Kafaie, Somayeh

items.title: Generative Modelling of Traffic Distributions in Sparse Communication Networks
items.abstract: Traffic on sparse infrastructure networks is governed by a small number of long-tailed flows that concentrate load on a few links, increasing both cost and failure risk. National optical backbones, consisting of hundreds of low-degree nodes, are a key example. However, their traffic matrices are extremely sparse, proprietary, and usually available at most as a single static snapshot. In this data-limited setting, researchers rely on simple distributional assumptions that underestimate extreme flows and distort estimates of capacity, congestion, and robustness. To address this, we show that (i) traffic on sparse backbones is multimodal, (ii) modelling the bulk and the tail separately is both necessary and sufficient, and (iii) additional node features (geolocation, population, data-centre locations) only marginally improve performance. Can a generative model produce realistic synthetic traffic samples in this data-scarce regime? In this work, we develop a hybrid Gaussian Mixture–Extreme Value Theory (GMM–EVT) model [1, 2]. The bulk distribution of origin–destination flows is learned using a finite mixture of Gaussians, while extreme upper-tail behaviour is modelled via peaks-over-threshold Generalised Pareto distributions. The model is evaluated on a single traffic snapshot from the 106-node British Telecommunications (BT) backbone, with traffic defined as the sum of inflows and outflows across non-zero, asymmetric source–destination (SD) pairs. We assess model performance by comparing the observed and synthetic traffic distributions using RMSE (root mean squared error). Five components minimise error and are selected as optimal. How does GMM-EVT compare to existing traffic models? GMM–EVT achieves the lowest error on UK backbone traffic (RMSE = 0.02), even outperforming the lognormal distribution (0.03), the standard assumption and state-of-the-art model for Internet traffic [3]. Domain-specific heuristics perform substantially worse as gravity models (0.31), data-centre and peering heuristics (0.28), and uniform distributions (0.25) incur errors an order of magnitude higher [4, 5]. We further investigate whether contextual features improve distributional fidelity and evaluate supervised baselines that are widely adopted by operators (linear regression, random forests, neural networks, gradient boosting [6]) using geolocation, population, data-centre and Internet Exchange Point features, and node embeddings. While these methods outperform heuristics (0.16), they still fail to match GMM–EVT’s performance. Finally, we demonstrate that the unified GMM–EVT formulation is necessary, as it reduces error relative to GMM-only (0.03) or EVT-only (0.13) variants. Does the 5-component GMM-EVT structure generalise to other sparse backbones? We validate on a 105-node Telefonica backbone with substantially different traffic characteristics [7]. The GMM-EVT model generalises to this network with operator-specific parameters and achieves an equally low error (0.02). Overall, this approach (i) challenges single-family distributional assumptions that underestimate traffic extremes and consequently infrastructure costs, (ii) indicates a recurrent stratification of traffic into distinct statistical regimes across operators, and (iii) enables privacy-preserving sharing by allowing operators to release model parameters without exposing source–destination flows. [1] C. M. Bishop, “Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning,” Springer, (2006). [2] S. J. Savory, “Extreme values in optical fiber communication systems,” OFC, (2020). [3] M. Alasmar et al., “Internet traffic volumes are not Gaussian—They are log-normal,” IEEE/ACM Trans. Netw., (2021). [4] A. Dwivedi et al., “Traffic model for USA long-distance optical network,” OFC, (2000). [5] S. K. Patri et al., “Planning optical networks for unexpected traffic growth,” ECOC, (2020). [6] D. Szostak et al., “ML classification and regression approaches for optical network traffic prediction,” Electronics, (2021). [7] J. M. R. Moscoso et al., “TEFNET24: reference packet optical network topology for edge to core transport,” JOCN, (2024).
items.authors: Ahuja, Akanksha *; N. Herzberg, Sam; Rafel, Albert; Wright, Paul ; Lord, Andrew; J. Savory, Seb

items.title: AIProxiMed: AI-Powered Network Proximity Modeling for Therapeutic Discovery across Pharmacological and Nutritional Chemical Spaces
items.abstract: Drug–target interaction (DTI) prediction is a central challenge in drug discovery and has traditionally relied on experimental assays. Although highly reliable, wet-lab experiments are costly, labor-intensive, and difficult to scale. To overcome these limitations, a variety of computational approaches have been developed. Classical computational strategies, such as hand-crafted similarity measures and descriptor-based QSAR models, typically rely on fixed feature representations and predominantly local inferences in the chemical or target space, limiting their capacity to incorporate modern representation learning or leverage global interaction context. Recent advances in deep learning have enabled end-to-end DTI prediction directly from molecular and protein representations; yet, many existing models depend on the availability of large labeled datasets, overlook the global structure of interaction networks, and struggle to generalize to unseen molecules under data sparsity and sampling bias. To bridge these gaps, we introduce AIProxiMed, an AI-powered, network-based framework for estimating ligand–target proximity within drug–target interaction networks. Drawing on network science, drugs and targets are represented as nodes in an interaction graph, enabling DTI prediction to be naturally formulated as a distance-prediction problem, where distances are learned directly from molecular representations rather than computed from observed interactions. AIProxiMed encodes molecular structures using a chemical language model to produce high-dimensional semantic representations, which are then mapped to predict discrete, ordered graph distances between compounds and biological targets. These distances serve as proxies for relative ligand–protein binding propensity. By casting proximity prediction as an ordinal regression problem rather than an unordered multiclass classification task, the framework aligns learning objectives with the hierarchical structure of interaction networks and avoids modeling adjacent proximity levels as mutually independent classes, thereby mitigating the effects of sparse and noisy supervision. We then apply AIProxiMed for therapeutic target discovery in heart failure. The trained model is used to screen both approved drugs and food-derived small molecules, prioritizing candidates that are proximal in the drug–target network to disease-associated proteins such as the clinically relevant AT2T1, ACE and HMG-CoA reductase. By integrating molecular semantics, network topology, and ordinal regression, AIProxiMed provides a scalable and data-efficient framework for compound prioritization, enabling network-informed discovery across pharmacological and nutritional chemical spaces.
items.authors: Xu, Hao*; Menichetti, Giulia; Loscalzo, Joseph

items.title: Learning by Surprise: Surplexity for Mitigating Model Collapse in Generative AI
items.abstract: Recent research has investigated the risks associated with a self-consuming loop, often referred to as autophagy process, where Large Generative Models (LGMs) are recursively fine-tuned on their own outputs. The process can lead to a phenomenon called model collapse, characterised by a significant loss of linguistic diversity. However, as noted by Schaeffer et al., there are many alternative definitions of collapse as well as many open questions. We focus on three issues: (i) Model collapse is typically detected through metrics defined on generated content, but we lack a method to characterise a collapsed model as a probability model; (ii) while it is known that fine-tuning a model on synthetic data causes collapse, we lack a principled understanding of which properties of training documents contribute to collapse; (iii) methods for mitigating model collapse assume knowledge about whether training documents are synthetic or human-authored: an assumption that is difficult to validate in real-world settings. Using three textual datasets, we consider different fine-tuning scenarios based on the composition of the training set, simulating the autophagy process over 10 steps. In response to issue (i), we propose a metric for a collapsed LGM based on the probability distributions it generates. Then we address issue (ii) by proposing a new definition of the class of documents responsible for collapse related to the model's surprise: documents that are not surprising lead to collapse, while documents that are surprising do not. Given a model and a document, we define surplexity as the extent to which the model is surprised by the document. We then use surplexity to address issue (iii), mitigating collapse by filtering training items by the level of surprise they elicit (see Figure a), finding that the strategy is as effective in mitigating model collapse while requiring no knowledge of whether documents are human-authored or AI-generated. We extend our characterisation based on next-token probabilities into a network representation (see Figure b), obtaining a network that captures the growing inequality in the probability distribution of a single prompt. This approach enables us to characterise model collapse in terms of network metrics and, additionally, provides a framework for comparing the phenomenon of autophagy feedback loops in generative AI with other ecosystems.
items.authors: Gambetta , Daniele *; Gezici, Gizem; Giannotti, Fosca ; Pedreschi, Dino ; Knott, Alistair ; Pappalardo, Luca

items.title: Mapping inequities in global vaccine sentiment research
items.abstract: Negative public sentiment towards vaccination (PSV) poses significant challenges to the effectiveness of immunization programs, with dramatic effects on morbidity and mortality for vaccine-preventable diseases [1]. Yet, global health initiatives are heavily influenced by economic and geopolitical factors [2] and by disruptive events, such as the COVID-19 pandemic [3], rather than countries’ epidemiological or healthcare needs. This study examines global patterns and drivers of PSV research on five vaccines -polio, measles, human papillomavirus, influenza, and SARS-CoV-2- and evaluates the COVID-19 pandemic’s impact on research volume, focus, and distribution. We conducted a machine-learning-assisted global literature search on PSV with no geographic, language, or time restrictions (Figure 1). Using NLP and network analysis, we constructed vaccine-specific, directed weighted country networks (5-year intervals) linking authors’ countries to studied countries. From these networks, we derived node-level indicators of domestic, inbound, and outbound research. These network-based measures were integrated into regression models to assess how economic, epidemiological, and temporal factors shape the global production and direction of PSV research. We analysed 13,287 articles and detected consistent literature growth from 1980 onwards, with vaccine-specific peaks following key licensing events. After 2020, publication volumes rose above projections for influenza (32%; 95%CI = 20%, 46%) but declined for polio (-56%; 95%CI = -68%, -26%) and measles (-17%; 95%CI = -33%, 9%). Although PSV research had global coverage, its distribution was markedly imbalanced, largely shaped by country-specific economic factors. A few high-income countries (HIC) produced 72% of publications and the likelihood of a country being studied varied by income and vaccine. Foreign authorship also increased as the income of the studied country decreased (over 75% in LMIC vs. below 50% in HIC). PSV research reveals persistent inequities, with a misalignment between countries leading research and populations most in need of its outcomes. These inequities, further exacerbated by COVID-19 disruptions, reflect systematic imbalances in global health. Our findings underscore the need to decolonise research by fostering leadership, agenda-setting, and accountability that centres on the necessities of affected communities. Achieving this will require funding and publication reforms that promote equitable collaborations and elevate local priorities alongside long-standing global health objectives.
items.authors: Balsamo, Duilio*; Offeddu, Vittoria; Aghamohammadi, Zhina; Chiavenna, Chiara; Leone, Laura Pasqua; D’Agnese, Elena; Sharma, Deepak; Torbica, Aleksandra; Shayegh, Soheil; Andreu-Perez, Javier; Melegaro, Alessia

items.title: The software space of science
items.abstract: Science advances not only through the accumulation of facts but also the evolution of tools. Yet tool use is difficult to study: tools are tacitly transmitted and their adoption is not systematically tracked. The widespread use of software in research offers a rare opportunity, as software mentions in scientific articles can be identified and traced over time. Here we map the software space of science by analyzing 1.3 million publications across 170 disciplines from 2004 to 2021. We construct a network of 520 software tools linked by disciplinary co-usage, with link strength weighted by proximity based on Revealed Comparative Advantage (RCA). The resulting network reveals a structured landscape where tools cluster into functional communities, from computing and statistics to wet lab and dry lab applications. Each discipline carries a distinct signature of used tools reflecting its investigative character. Tracking disciplinary tool ecologies over time uncovers divergent patterns. Data-intensive fields show progressive consolidation around dominant platforms, while laboratory-based sciences retain varied, task-specific toolkits. Yet across most domains, software usage has grown increasingly stable, suggesting a broad trend toward instrumental settlement. This stability could indicate field maturity or technological lock-in. Disentangling these interpretations requires linking tool patterns to scientific outcomes, a question our software space now makes accessible.
items.authors: Wu, Zhouming*; Murray, Dakota

items.title: Quantifying Ageing Effect in Scientific and Innovative Careers
items.abstract: Recent advances in science and innovation have accumulated growing empirical evidence that old innovators continue to exert a lasting impact on science and technology. Citations, funding and awards are increasingly directed towards senior innovators, resulting in newcomers experiencing greater delays before achieving recognition. Despite the broad implications of this ageing phenomenon for scientific production, communication, and evaluation, our quantitative understanding of the underlying mechanisms remains limited. Here, we systematically quantify the ageing phenomenon across multiple innovative domains, covering seven decades of scientific careers and four decades of patent inventors. We find a marked increase in the average age of innovators who receive recognition, measured by incoming citations. We tested three commonly proposed explanations and found them insufficient. Instead, we identify preferential attachment and network densification as the causal drivers of ageing, and introduce a simple mechanistic model that not only reproduces the observed patterns but also reveals how structural feedback shapes the dynamics of career ageing. Analysis of the model demonstrates a direct link between ageing and network densification, providing a basis for forecasting future recognition patterns once the magnitude of densification is known. These findings contribute to a new empirical and theoretical framework for understanding career dynamics and inform policy design to support early career innovators.
items.authors: Yang, Yangfanyu*; Yang, Alex Jie; Yin, Yian

items.title: SciSciGPT: Advancing Human-AI Collaboration in the Science of Science
items.abstract: Recent advances in the Science of Science increasingly rely on large-scale data describing scholarly production, collaboration, and impact, which are naturally represented as complex heterogeneous networks. While network-based approaches have yielded fundamental insights into the structure and dynamics of scientific systems, the growing scale of data and methodological complexity has raised substantial barriers to efficient, reproducible, and exploratory research. To tackle this challenge, here we introduce SciSciGPT, an open-source, prototype artificial intelligence (AI) collaborator that uses the domain of Science of Science as a testbed to explore the potential of large language model (LLM)-powered research tools. SciSciGPT is a multi-agent AI system structured by a collaboration network of specialized agents, which automates complex workflows, supports diverse analytical approaches, accelerates research prototyping and iteration, and facilitates reproducibility. Through case studies, we demonstrate its ability to streamline a wide range of empirical and analytical research tasks, including network construction, visualization, statistical analysis, and regression, while highlighting its broader potential to advance research. We further propose an LLM agent capability maturity model for human-AI collaboration, envisioning a roadmap to further improve and expand upon frameworks like SciSciGPT. As AI capabilities continue to evolve, frameworks like SciSciGPT may play increasingly pivotal roles in scientific research and discovery. At the same time, these new advances also raise critical challenges, from ensuring transparency and ethical use to balancing human and AI contributions. Addressing these issues may shape the future of scientific inquiry and inform how we train the next generation of scientists to thrive in an increasingly AI-integrated research ecosystem. [SciSciGPT Full paper https://www.nature.com/articles/s43588-025-00906-6] [SciSciGPT Demo Video https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cRA2G5HOL-w]
items.authors: Shao, Erzhuo*; Wang, Yifang; Qian, Yifan; Pan, Zhenyu; Liu, Han; Wang, Dashun

items.title: The Rise of Large Language Models and the Direction and Impact of US Federal Research Funding
items.abstract: Federal research funding shapes the direction, diversity, and impact of the US scientific enterprise. At the same time, large language models (LLMs) are rapidly diffusing into scientific practice, holding immense promise while raising widespread concerns. Despite growing attention to AI use in scientific writing and evaluation, little is known about how the rise of LLMs is reshaping the public funding landscape. Here, we combine two complementary levels of analyses to examine the LLM involvement at key stages where scientific ideas compete for public support: confidential NSF and NIH proposal submissions from two large US R1 universities, including funded, unfunded, and pending proposals, and the full population of publicly released NSF and NIH awards. We apply an established detection approach to estimate LLM involvement and relate it to the semantic distinctiveness of proposed ideas, proposal success, and downstream publication outcomes. We find that the use of LLMs rises sharply beginning in 2023 and exhibits a bimodal distribution, indicating a clear split between minimal and substantive use. Across both private submissions and public awards, higher LLM involvement is consistently associated with lower semantic distinctiveness, as measured using network-based embeddings, positioning projects closer to recently funded work within the same agency. The consequences of this shift are strongly agency-dependent. In both proposal selection and downstream outcomes, LLM use disproportionately affects NIH-funded research: it is positively associated with proposal success and with higher subsequent publication output at NIH, whereas no comparable associations are observed at NSF. Notably, the amplification in output at NIH is concentrated in non-hit papers rather than in the most highly cited work. Together, these findings provide large-scale evidence that the rise of LLMs is already reshaping how scientific ideas are positioned, selected, and translated into publicly funded research. More broadly, they highlight how generative AI can subtly alter the composition and consequences of public investment in science, with implications for portfolio governance, research diversity, the stewardship of public funding, and the long-run impact of science.
items.authors: Qian, Yifan*; Wen, Zhe; Furnas, Alexander; Bai, Yue; Shao, Erzhuo; Wang, Dashun

items.title: Funding the Frontier: Visualizing the Broad Impact of Science and Science Funding
items.abstract: Understanding the broad impact of science and science funding is critical to ensuring that science investments and policies align with societal needs. Existing research links science funding to the output of scientific publications but largely leaves out the downstream uses of science and the myriad ways in which investing in science may impact human society. As funders seek to allocate scarce funding resources across a complex research landscape, there is an urgent need for informative and transparent tools that allow for comprehensive assessments and visualization of the impact of funding. Here we present Funding the Frontier (FtF ), a visual analysis system for researchers, funders, policymakers, university leaders, and the broad public to analyze multidimensional impacts of funding and make informed decisions regarding research investments and opportunities. The system is built on a massive data collection that connects 7M research grants to 140M scientific publications, 160M patents, 10.9M policy documents, 800K clinical trials, and 5.8M newsfeeds, with 1.8B citation linkages among these entities, systematically linking science funding to its downstream impacts. As such, Funding the Frontier is distinguished by its multifaceted impact analysis framework. The system incorporates diverse impact metrics and predictive models that forecast future investment opportunities into an array of coordinated views, allowing for easy exploration of funding and its outcomes. We evaluate the effectiveness and usability of the system using case studies and expert interviews. Feedback suggests that our system not only fulfills the primary analysis needs of its target users, but the rich datasets of the complex science ecosystem and the proposed analysis framework also open new avenues for both visualization and the science of science research.
items.authors: Wang, Yifang*; Qian, Yifan; Qi, Xiaoyu; Yin, Yian; Dang, Shengqi; Qian, Ziqing; Jones, Benjamin; Cao, Nan; Wang, Dashun

items.title: Urban semantic: detecting dynamic land use through human mobility patterns
items.abstract: Human mobility research has leveraged large-scale digital traces to reveal hidden urban structures, with recent advances applying embedding techniques like Word2Vec to POI sequences, Place2Vec for location co-occurrence, and neural network embeddings to define urban functions. However, these methods treat space as static, either ignoring or aggregating temporal information. Place2Vec captures spatial co-occurrence but cannot encode how a location's function changes throughout the day, preventing understanding of how urban spaces transform functionally across time. We build on these foundations by proposing a novel embedding framework that captures the dynamic interplay between space and time in human mobility, expanding analysis beyond single-city studies to diverse urban contexts. Using mobility data from Cuebiq, we apply our framework across European cities (London, Turin, Rome, Cardiff), Latin American cities (Buenos Aires, Bogotá, Mexico City, Rio de Janeiro), and US cities (Boston, Bay Area, Chicago, Dallas), enabling cross-cultural comparison of temporal urban dynamics. Our methodology treats each trajectory as a sentence where tokens represent administrative zones visited at specific hours (e.g., "A\_8 B\_12 C\_18"). Applying Word2Vec learns vector representations for each place-hour combination based on co-occurrence patterns across trajectories. We cluster these embeddings using spherical K-Means to identify groups of place-hour combinations appearing in similar mobility contexts, revealing how zones shift between different functional contexts throughout the day (Figure 1A). To validate that clusters correspond to real-world urban functions, we integrate Global Human Settlement Layer satellite imagery, classifying areas as Residential, Non-Residential, and Open Space at 10m resolution. We compute relative land use by dividing each zone's proportions by city-wide distributions (Figure 1B), then characterise each embedding cluster through three metrics per land-use type: average relative land use, tendency to be over-represented (relative land use $>$ 1), and consistency (variance), creating 9-dimensional vectors. We analyse how zones transition through mobility contexts by tracking cluster assignments hourly across weekdays and weekends. Clustering these temporal profiles identifies groups following similar daily activation patterns: some maintain high residential character with near-parity non-residential use on weekends, while others shift from weekday work activity to weekend residential use (Figure 1C). We distinguish embedding clusters (place-time combinations with similar mobility contexts) from zone clusters (zones based on temporal transitions). To examine spatial inequality, we incorporate socioeconomic indicators like the Index of Concentration at the Extremes for US cities and equivalent measures internationally. By analysing trajectory sequences, we track how different socioeconomic groups navigate urban contexts over time. Even when different groups visit the same zone, they may do so at different times when it serves different functions: residential during hours dominated by low-income visitors but shifting to commercial functions for high-income groups. This cross-city analysis reveals how temporal accessibility patterns vary across cultural and infrastructural contexts, exposing universal versus city-specific disparities in urban space experience beyond static land-use categories.
items.authors: Iyer, Nandini*; Luca, Massimiliano; Langle Chimal, Ollin; Gonzalez, Marta; Di Clemente, Riccardo

items.title: Causal inference in the city: Improving Urban Policy Evaluation Through Mobility-Aware Methods
items.abstract: Evaluating urban policy interventions is difficult because cities are highly interconnected and policy effects can spill beyond geographic boundaries, violating the no interference assumptions behind standard quasi experimental designs. We introduce a mobility aware causal inference framework that explicitly accounts for behavioral connectivity by constructing a mobility flow network from large scale, anonymized location data. We use community structure in this network to redefine exposure, assigning treatment and control groups by behavioral proximity rather than physical distance. We then apply the Synthetic Control Method to build counterfactual trajectories and estimate effects in the presence of overlapping citywide changes. Across multiple interventions, this approach improves placebo performance and produces more robust and interpretable impact estimates than conventional distance based designs.
items.authors: Joseph, Bijin*; Gibbs, Hamish; Yabe, Takahiro; Moro, Esteban

items.title: Beyond Demographics: Creating Interpretable Lifestyle Embeddings Across US Cities
items.abstract: The dynamics of urban life arise from complex interactions between individuals and the built environment, which shape networks of social interaction, economic production, consumption, and civic participation. Traditionally, urban scientists have relied on the residential demographics of neighborhoods to study complex phenomena such as the structure of social networks, inequalities in access to amenities, or individuals' economic opportunities in cities. Behavioral data, including GPS traces from mobile phones, offer insights into "what people do away from home", providing novel perspectives on urban dynamics not available from traditional demographic data. Despite opportunities to improve urban decision-making, behavioral data can be challenging to use, limited by restrictions on data sharing, and can risk individual privacy. Rather than using behavioral data directly, there is increasing interest in providing access to low-dimensional representations of behavioral data in the form of embeddings, which capture the structure of behavioral datasets while increasing ease of use and reducing risks to individual privacy. Though promising, many embedding methods suffer from a lack of explainability, a crucial requirement to support decision-making using behavioral data. In this study, we present a set of interpretable behavioral features derived from GPS mobility data which reveal latent regularities in mobility behavior across neighborhoods and over time. Using non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) applied to a mobility dataset of ~30B visits across 20 US cities between 2019 and 2022, we identify 12 latent behavioral features capturing regularities in urban mobility behavior such as Weekend Shopping, Morning Commute, or Campus Life. Importantly, we show that these behavioral features offer stable, interpretable behavioral dimensions which can be incorporated in models of urban phenomena to complement existing demographic data. Finally, we show that adding lifestyle embeddings to demographic baselines improves prediction for selected outcomes in health, employment, civic engagement, and access to opportunity.
items.authors: Gibbs, Hamish*; Moro, Esteban

items.title: Unsupervised Embedding of Mobility Networks Reveals Invisible Barriers in US Cities
items.abstract: Many visible borders are present within our cities, such as highways, railways, rivers, and other administrative bound- aries. These boundaries can unintentionally act as physical barriers, hindering social interaction. These tangible divisions are complemented by invisible barriers arising from demographic stratification, institutional gatekeeping, and socioeconomic disparities, which collectively intensify social segregation. Traditional methods of identifying and an- alyzing these invisible boundaries have relied primarily on qualitative observations, anecdotal evidence, or static census data, limiting our understanding of how residents navigate and experience urban spaces. This study examines mobility networks across 11 U.S. cities to identify both visible and invisible borders that generate unexpectedly low movement patterns, utilizing unsupervised embedding of human mobility trajectories to uncover their underlying characteristics.
items.authors: Weng, Guangyuan*; Kim, Minsuk; Ahn, Yong-Yeol; Moro, Esteban

items.title: The urban impact of algorithmic navigation
items.abstract: The collective impact of navigation services is unclear: while widely used in urban mobility, they can also unintentionally reshape traffic flows and amplify emission inequalities. In this work, we introduce a simulation-based framework for evaluating the impact of navigation services, integrating real-world GPS data, road networks, and route recommendations offered by leading navigation services' APIs. The results demonstrate a universal pattern of amplified conformity: increasing adoption rates of navigation services cause a reduction of route diversity. Although navigation services recommendations can help reduce CO2 emissions when their adoption rate is low, these benefits diminish or even disappear when the adoption rate is high and exceeds a certain city- and service-dependent threshold. To isolate the core dynamics of traffic and emissions concentration, we replicate our experiments in simplified mobility settings, confirming that these patterns emerge independently of real-world urban complexity. Overall, our findings reveal how widespread algorithmic routing can induce emergent network-level concentration, with important implications for the efficiency and equity of urban mobility systems.
items.authors: Cornacchia, Giuliano*; Nanni, Mirco; Pedreschi, Dino; Pappalardo, Luca

items.title: The urban impact of AI: modelling feedback loops in location-based recommender systems
items.abstract: Location-based recommender systems are increasingly embedded in location-based services, shaping individual mobility decisions in urban environments. While their predictive accuracy has been extensively studied, less attention has been paid to their systemic impact on urban dynamics. In this work, we introduce a simulation framework to model the human–AI feedback loop underpinning next-venue recommendation, capturing how algorithmic suggestions influence individual behaviour, which in turn reshapes the data used to retrain the models. Our simulations, grounded in real-world mobility data, systematically explore the effects of algorithmic adoption across a range of recommendation strategies. We find that while recommender systems consistently increase individual-level diversity in visited venues, they may simultaneously amplify collective inequality by concentrating visits on a limited subset of popular places. This divergence extends to the structure of social co-location networks, revealing broader implications for urban accessibility and spatial segregation. Our framework operationalizes the feedback loop in next-venue recommendation and offers a novel lens through which to assess the societal impact of AI-assisted mobility—providing a computational tool to anticipate future risks, evaluate regulatory interventions, and inform the design of ethic algorithmic systems.
items.authors: Mauro, Giovanni*

items.title: Adaptive Weight Control of Power Grid Networks via Reinforcement Learning
items.abstract: Transport processes in weighted networks are strongly affected by the distribution of link weights. When a link fails—effectively reducing its weight to zero—it may trigger further failures and lead to an unstable system. Such cascading breakdowns are observed in power grids [1] and other infrastructures. While changing the network topology can enhance stability, it cannot respond dynamically once instability arises. Consequently, adapting the weights of the remaining links is a more practical approach. Yet, determining the optimal weight configuration over all links is computationally demanding. Here, we introduce the Adaptive Weight Controller (AWC) algorithm within a reinforcement learning framework, which autonomously tunes link weights to drive the transport dynamics back to stable states after network attacks or spontaneous failures. We apply the AWC algorithm to a realistic representation of the UK power grid [2]. Frequency synchronization in power grids is described by the swing equation, a modified second-order Kuramoto model [3,4], given by $m_i\ddot{\theta}_{i} = P_{i} - \gamma_i \dot{\theta}_{i} + \sum_{j\neq i}^N{K_{ij}\sin(\theta_{j} - \theta_{i})}$, where $m_i$ denotes inertia, $\gamma_i$ the damping coefficient, $P_i$ the power and $K_{ij}$ the coupling strength (link weight). When a link $\ell$ is removed, it causes quantified frequency fluctuations. {\small $$\Xi^{(\ell)} \equiv \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T dt \left[ \frac{1}{\sum_i m_i} \sum_i m_i \dot{\theta}_i^2(t) - \left( \frac{1}{\sum_i m_i} \sum_i m_i \dot{\theta}_i(t) \right)^2 \right],$$} where $T$ is the total simulation time, set to 10 seconds. The AWC algorithm aims to minimize $\Xi^{(\ell)}$ by continuously adapting the weights of the links. Figure 1(a) presents the frequency deviations $\Xi^{(\ell)}$ resulting from the failure of individual links, highlighting a strongly heterogeneous response pattern. Across 105 failure cases, the AWC algorithm yields an average 53\% reduction in $\Xi^{(\ell)}$, selectively targeting high-impact failures while avoiding unnecessary interventions for small disturbances [Figure 1(b)]. In the illustrative example (failure of the link between buses 46 and 47, indicated by red arrows), the algorithm quickly damps oscillations within a few seconds [Figure 1(c)] by adaptively and bidirectionally tuning the link weights [Figure 1(d)]. These findings demonstrate that reinforcement learning can successfully handle the exponentially large search space of weight configurations to recover target dynamics following topological disturbances. We anticipate that this approach generalizes beyond power grids to other networked systems that demand fast stabilization after disruptions, such as transportation networks and communication infrastructures.
items.authors: Park, Sangjoon*; Choi, Hoyun; Lee, Yongsun; Jo, Seungchan; Kahng, Byungnam

items.title: A Koopmanian approach to spectral and geometric properties of dynamics on networks
items.abstract: Initially developed by Koopman and von Neumann in the early 1930s, Koopman theory is a mathematical framework representing any dynamical system in an operator formalism similar to quantum mechanics. Despite prominent data-driven applications in soft robotics and fluid mechanics, Koopman theory has only begun to show its full potential for dynamics of complex systems. For example, recent work on phase oscillators linked their constants of motion and symmetries to network motifs via properties of the Koopman operator, which governs the time evolution of functions on the state space. This operator is linear, albeit infinite-dimensional, thereby allowing the use of spectral methods. In this project, we adopt a more general perspective and seek the families of dynamical systems that admit specific Koopman eigenfunctions. First, we show that each Koopman eigenfunction constrains the component of the vector field normal to the corresponding level sets. Hence, the components of the vector field tangent to the intersection of the level sets of all eigenfunctions remain unrestricted. From this geometric perspective, we identified two general families of dynamics on networks where the rank deficiency of the weighted adjacency matrix plays a central role. These families encompass widely used models such as recurrent neural networks (RNNs) with affine eigenfunctions and the Lotka-Volterra model with monomial eigenfunctions. In all cases, each left singular vector of the weight matrix of null singular value implies a specific Koopman eigenfunction. Given a weighted adjacency matrix of rank r, these eigenfunctions define a generally nonlinear change of variables suitable for the exact dimension reduction of the system to r non-autonomous equations. The reductions also hold approximately for dynamics on complex networks whose weight matrix has low effective rank, a feature that is widespread in empirical networks. Our results link structural, spectral, and geometrical properties of dynamics on networks, and highlight the relevance of operator-theoretic approaches in the study of complex systems.
items.authors: Claveau, Benjamin*; Thibeault, Vincent; Allard, Antoine; Desrosiers, Patrick

items.title: Weighted Digraph Dowker Persistence and Applications in Dynamical Systems
items.abstract: By their nature it is difficult to differentiate chaotic dynamical systems through measurement. In recent years, work has begun on using methods of Topological Data Analysis (TDA) to qualitatively type dynamical data by approximating the topology of the underlying attracting set. This comes with the additional challenges of high dimensionality incurring computational complexity along with the lack of directional information encoded in the approximated topology. Due to the latter fact, standard methods of TDA for this dynamical data do not differentiate between periodic cycles and non-periodic cycles in the attractor. We present a framework to address both of these challenges. We begin by binning the dynamical data, and capturing the sequential information in the form of a coarse-grained weighted and directed network. We then calculate the persistent Dowker homology of the asymmetric network, encoding spatial and temporal information. Analytically, we highlight the differences in periodic and non-periodic cycles by providing a full characterization of their one-dimensional Dowker persistences. We prove how the homologies of graph wedge sums can be described in terms of the wedge component homologies. Finally, we generalize our characterization to cactus graphs with arbitrary edge weights and orientations. Our analytical results give insight into how our method captures temporal information in its asymmetry, producing a persistence framework robust to noise and sensitive to dynamical structure.
items.authors: Timofeyev, Tobias*; Potvin, Christopher; Jones, Benjamin; Kurianski, Kristin; Lopez, Miguel; Tanweer, Sunia

items.title: Observing network dynamics by a small number of sentinel nodes
items.abstract: See the pdf abstract.
items.authors: MacLaren, Neil; Barzel, Baruch; Masuda, Naoki*

items.title: Emergent Topology of Optimal Networks for Synchrony
items.abstract: Real-world networks, whether shaped by evolution or intelligent design, are typically optimized for functionality while adhering to physical, geometric, or budget constraints. Yet tools to identify such structures remain limited [1]. Unlike classical synchronization studies that vary a global coupling constant on a fixed graph [2], our framework co-optimizes topology and weights under a hard coupling budget, revealing fundamentally different synchronization phenomenology. This presentation will cover our recent work [3] in which we develop a gradient-based optimization framework to identify synchrony-optimal weighted networks under a constrained coupling budget. As shown in Fig. 1A, the resulting networks exhibit counterintuitive features: they are sparse, bipartite, elongated, and extremely monophilic (i.e., the neighbors of any node are similar to one another while differing from the node itself) [3]. Dynamics unfolding over optimal networks provably lack a synchronization threshold; arbitrarily small budgets lead to non-zero synchronization (see the top panel of Fig. 1B), even without diverging degrees or dense connectivity. Instead, we observe a sharp onset of global phase-locking as the budget exceeds a calculable critical value (see the black dash in Fig. 1B), exhibiting critical scaling at the transition. These findings are matched by ``constructive'' theory: a nonlinear differential equation identifies which pairs of nodes are coupled (the black curve in Fig. 1C), while variational analysis prescribes the budget allocated to each node (the areas under the blue curves in Fig. 1C). This theory reveals two admissible pairing branches—reconciling prior conflicting numerical results—and predicts which is globally optimal, consistent with our gradient-based optimization. Our results have implications for power grids, neuromorphic computing, and other coupled oscillator technologies. [1] Arenas, A., Díaz-Guilera, A., Kurths, J., Moreno, Y. and Zhou, C., 2008. Synchronization in Complex Networks. Physics Reports, 469(3), pp.93-153. [2] Skardal, P.S., Taylor, D. and Sun, J. (2014) Optimal synchronization of complex networks, Physical Review Letters, 113(14), p. 144101. [3] Mikaberidze, G. and Taylor, D., 2025. Emergent Topology of Optimal Networks for Synchrony. arXiv preprint arXiv:2509.18279.
items.authors: Mikaberidze, Guram*; Taylor, Dane

items.title: Early Predictor for the Onset of Critical Transitions in Networked Dynamical Systems
items.abstract: Numerous natural and human-made systems exhibit critical transitions whereby slow changes in environmental conditions spark abrupt shifts to a qualitatively distinct state, entailing severe consequences [1]. Examples include rapid global warming, extinction events, and power outages [2]. Real-world complex systems often comprise hundreds or thousands of interacting entities, and prevention or management strategies for critical transitions would benefit from knowledge of the point at which such transitions occur. However, most established methods [2] focus on low-dimensional systems, do not estimate the point of onset of transitions, or rely on prior knowledge of network connectivity or governing dynamics. We address this gap using a machine-learning framework that combines graph isomorphism networks for highdimensional interacting systems with gated recurrent units for time series analysis [3]. The resulting model is trained to predict the parameter value at which a critical transition occurs using only short time series from observed nodes, and without knowledge of connectivity or dynamics. We benchmark the method on a diverse set of model systems exhibiting both smooth (second-order) and explosive (first-order) transitions: synchronization in coupled Kuramoto oscillators, abrupt collapse of resource biomass in ecosystems, and failure of Wilson-Cowan neuronal networks. The model accurately anticipates critical transitions well in advance of their onset and remains robust to noise, nonstationarity, and partial observability [3]. Finally, applying the method to empirical vegetation and rainfall data using transfer learning, we predict forest-to-savannah transitions in African ecosystems (Fig. 1). [1] M. Scheffer, J. Bascompte, W. A. Brock, V. Brovkin, S. R. Carpenter, V. Dakos, H. Held, E. H. van Nes, M. Rietkerk, and G. Sugihara, Early-warning signals for critical transitions, Nature 461, 53-59 (2009). [2] I. Franovi´c, S. Eydam, and D. Eroglu, Regime switching in coupled nonlinear systems: Sources, prediction, and control—Minireview and perspective on the Focus Issue, Chaos 34, 120401 (2024). [3] Z. Liu, X. Zhang, X. Ru, T.-T. Gao, J. M. Moore, and G. Yan, Early Predictor for the Onset of Critical Transitions in Networked Dynamical Systems, Physical Review X 14, 031009 (2024). Figure 1: Predicting abrupt transitions in empirical vegetated ecosystems. (a) Empirical vegetation data used to fine-tune a model after training on synthetic systems (transfer learning), showing average tree cover as a function of annual rainfall $\varepsilon$. (b,c) Predicted transition points for two areas from a previously unseen district at different lead distances. Horizontal lines span the interval from the data window used for prediction (blue dots) to the predicted transition point. Colour represents prediction error. Insets show magnified views. In (a-c), dashed vertical lines mark critical transitions $\varepsilon_C$.
items.authors: Liu, Zijia; Zhang, Xiaozhu; Ru, Xiaolei; Gao, Ting-Ting; Moore, Jack*; Yan, Gang

items.title: Upward mobility in the green sector for frontline fossil fuel workers
items.abstract: The green sector is often viewed as a source of new jobs for frontline fossil fuel (FFF) workers, yet concerns persist about uncompetitive salaries. This view focuses narrowly on immediate horizontal transitions into similar green jobs, overlooking the sector’s potential to enable long-term career mobility. As an emerging industry, the green sector may offer greater opportunities for advancement into management and engineering roles than mature fossil fuel sectors. Wage comparisons, therefore, should account for differences in upward mobility. Using data on 190,648 FFF workers in the U.S., we analyze career trajectories after leaving their first fossil fuel job. Over their career, FFF workers who, at any point, transition into the green sector (green switchers) earn 4\% more than those who remain in fossil fuel jobs. This is partly driven by higher upward mobility in the green sector. Green switchers are twice as likely to hold managerial jobs and 2.5 times as likely to hold science and engineering (SE) jobs compared to peers who remain in the fossil fuel sector. Within these occupational categories, green sector workers earn 10\% and 30\% more, respectively, than their fossil fuel counterparts. To secure these roles, workers adopt distinct reskilling strategies: skill diversification raises the odds of managerial advancement, while skill deepening into engineering-relevant skills increases the likelihood of entering SE occupations. These findings inform reskilling policies that promote both upward mobility and horizontal flexibility for FFF workers.
items.authors: CHANG, QING*

items.title: Resilience of Labor Markets Under the COVID-19 Shock: The Role of Job Connectivity
items.abstract: Economic complementarities shape resilience of labor market systems, cities with greater job connectivity experience markedly lower unemployment when encountering shocks. However, most studies on labor market resilience focus on occupation-homogeneous networks without disaggregating different types of occupations, which may bias the assessment of resilience dynamics. In this study, we expand the research framework by moving beyond treating all occupations as homogeneous and instead analyzing structural job connectivity across broad occupational categories defined by two-digit Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) codes. By constructing occupation networks for 45 U.S. metropolitan areas, we explore how job connectivity shapes resilience mechanisms in response to the Covid-19 shock.
