The preliminary results show that the role of job connectivity in labor market resilience is context-dependent—mitigating unemployment in stable environments but amplifying vulnerability during severe disruptions. Finally, our research enhances the richness of granular unemployment data.
items.authors: Li, Wendi*; Moro, Esteban; Frank, Morgan; Ahn, Yong-Yeol

items.title: Labor Market Networks and Policy Failure: Why Opportunity Zones Didn’t Help Residents
items.abstract: The Opportunity Zone program offered tax incentives for $75 billion invested in 8,762 designated low-income census tracts. Despite substantial capital deployment, evaluations till the year 2020 find null effects on resident employment and poverty Freedman et al. (2023). We first extend he evaluation till 2022 with extended results and further explain the null effect through network analysis of metropolitan labor markets Busso et al. (2013). Using LODES commuting flow data (21 million annual tract-pair observations, 2013–2022), we construct labor market networks where nodes are census tracts and edges are worker flows. Replicating Freedman et al.’s inverse probability weighting methodology extended to 2022, we confirm null effects on resident employment (+0.00%, p > 0.90), poverty (-0.13%), income (+0.21%), and—newly documented—workplace employment in designated tracts (+0.14%). We then apply six network diagnostic methods to explain these null results. Designated tracts occupy severely disadvantaged network positions: (1) Only 6.7% are in the network core versus 32.2% of non-designated tracts (k-core decomposition); (2) In-degree centrality is 75% lower (8.9 vs 34.6, p < 0.001), indicating few workers commute to these areas; (3) Community detection reveals 71% concentrate in two segregated, low- income communities with only 15% of flows crossing boundaries (vs 29% for non-designated); (4) Structural constraint is 10% higher (Burt’s measure, p < 0.001); (5) ERGMs confirm strong income homophily (β = −0.21, p < 0.001) that designation does not disrupt (interaction β = 0.08, p = 0.28); (6) Network exposure to OZ predicts worse employment (β = −0.045, p < 0.001), indicating negative spillovers. Figure 1 visualizes community segregation in Chicago. Designated tracts cluster in peripheral, low-income communities despite geographic dispersion. This network-based segregation—distinct from spatial clustering—prevents investment from integrating disadvantaged residents into mainstream labor markets. Our findings demonstrate that network position determines policy effectiveness. Investment in peripheral, constrained, segregated tracts cannot overcome structural barriers to benefit diffusion. Future place-based policies must map network structure and target bridging positions, not just geographic distress.
items.authors: NARAYAN, KISHAN*

items.title: Global Propagation of Economic Policy Uncertainty and Geopolitical Risk: A Complex Network Perspective via Complex Hilbert PCA
items.abstract: Economic policy uncertainty (EPU) and geopolitical risk (GPR) propagate across countries through complex, time-lag, and asymmetric interactions. Traditional approaches based on real-valued correlations or causality tests are limited in their ability to capture such lead–lag structures at a global scale, as they typically rely on pairwise analysis and explicit model assumptions. In this study, we propose a complex-network-based framework that embeds temporal precedence directly into the network representation. By employing Complex Hilbert Principal Component Analysis (CHPCA), we construct weighted and directed networks that jointly encode the strength and phase of interdependencies among countries. This approach enables a global, model-free characterization of uncertainty propagation in the international system. We analyze monthly newspaper-based EPU and GPR indices for 20 countries from January 1997 to December 2024 (Fig. 1, left). Each time series is transformed into its analytic signal using the Hilbert transform and the complex correlation matrix. Complex Hilbert Principal Component Analysis is applied to the resulting complex correlation matrix. Unlike standard PCA or causality-based methods, CHPCA preserves phase information and embeds lead–lag relationships directly into a multivariate correlation structure, enabling simultaneous analysis of all countries without pairwise model specification. Based on complex correlations, we construct fully weighted, directed networks in which nodes represent countries and edges encode both the magnitude of interdependence and the direction implied by phase differences. Directionality in this context reflects systematic temporal precedence and directional influence, rather than causal identification. To further characterize the structure of uncertainty propagation, we apply the Helmholtz–Hodge (HH) decomposition to the directed networks, separating potential-driven source–sink structures from cyclic (rotational) flows. The constructed networks exhibit pronounced asymmetries and dynamic reconfigurations over time. Periods associated with major global shocks—most notably the September 11 terrorist attacks in 2001 and the Russia–Ukraine conflict in 2022—coincide with endogenous structural changes in network topology and edge weights, rather than being imposed ex post. The HH potential highlights dominant sources of uncertainty transmission within the global system (Fig. 1, right). In particular, China’s EPU emerges as a significant upstream driver of the GPR in Egypt and Israel, while Russia’s EPU plays a leading role in shaping the GPR dynamics in Taiwan, Japan, and China. These source–sink patterns are obscured in raw directed networks but become explicit through the HH decomposition. From a network science perspective, our results suggest that global uncertainty behaves as a directed flow system, with a small number of nodes acting as influential sources that depend on geopolitical conditions and economic uncertainty. By embedding lead–lag information into complex-valued networks, the proposed framework reveals structural features that are invisible in real-valued or symmetric analyses. More broadly, the methodology provides a general approach for analyzing delayed and directional interactions in multivariate dynamical systems, with potential applications beyond economic and geopolitical contexts.
items.authors: Souma, Wataru*

items.title: Networks and Corporate Board Appointments
items.abstract: Diversity in leadership positions, including corporate boards, is an important aspect of equality. It is important because it is the key to better decision-making and innovation and, above all, it paves the way for future generations to participate and shape our society. Many studies emphasize the importance of visibility of role models and the effect that connectivity has on the success of minorities in leadership. However, the connectivity of firms, the dynamics of the adoption of minorities into leadership positions, and the long-term effects in terms of group dynamics and visibility are not well understood. In the CBRC project we analyze large datasets of firms and their corporate board members in the EU and Japan over time. We analyze how connectivity on the organizational and personal level influences how board composition changes, how legislation on board composition impacts this process, and we analyze how the composition of the board influences decision making, firm profitability, as well as governance. Preliminary results for the EU suggest that firm characteristics together with the firms' networks provide a good indicator for a board's appointment decisions. In addition to performing an empirical analysis, we use observed appointment decisions, networks, and personal characteristics to build network models that describe the dynamics of diversity on corporate boards. These models are used to analyze the influence of connectivity, visibility, as well as homophily on the dynamics of board appointments. They are also used to analyze the effectiveness of counterfactual intervention mechanisms as well as structural changes to the system. In practice, we often observe a bias with respect to firm size for women. The results of our simulation illustrate how such bias that typically translates to visibility can prolong the process towards a more equal composition on corporate boards.
items.authors: Raddant, Matthias*

items.title: Reconstructing temporal multi-relational firm networks at scale using LLMs
items.abstract: The semiconductor industry is foundational to nearly every modern technology, yet its global firm-level network remains only partially observable. Any reconstruction of such an economic system is necessarily incomplete, as available data sources provide biased and fragmentary views of underlying supplier, partnership, and ownership relations. This poses persistent challenges for both scientific analysis and policy design. Existing studies rely heavily on proprietary databases, which are costly, slow to update, and ill-suited to capturing rapidly evolving dependencies. Here, we demonstrate that Large Language Models (LLMs), applied to open, first-party web data, can reconstruct the semiconductor firm network at scale while explicitly accounting for partial observability. By analyzing 170 million webpages from semiconductor companies, we reconstruct a temporal network (2015–2025) comprising over 1,300 consistently linked firms connected by more than 3,300 directed edges. Our framework targets a multi-relational structure, using LLMs to distinguish supplier–buyer flows, strategic partnerships, and ownership links. Validation against human-annotated ground truth yields high classification performance (F1-score: 0.78 for link existence and 0.76 for link type). Rather than treating either the web-derived network or commercial datasets (e.g., S&P Capital IQ) as ground truth, we address the more fundamental problem of inferring the full, unobserved network from overlapping but incomplete data sources. We model the intersection of these independent partial views within a probabilistic framework and apply Maximum Likelihood Estimation to estimate the true scale of the latent global semiconductor network. This provides a novel network-theoretic approach to quantifying unobserved economic activity and assessing the completeness of digital footprint data. The reconstructed network enables the study of salient industry-wide dynamics. We observe a temporary 9% decline in network edges during the 2022 chip shortage. Most notably, NVIDIA exhibits a sharp increase across all considered network centrality measures after 2021, coinciding with the inflection point of the generative AI boom (Fig. 1a), confirming its emerging role as a nexus supplier. Beyond technological shifts, we detect clear geopolitical signals: by 2025, the share of new links connecting Chinese firms to European partners declines sharply, consistent with intensifying trade frictions. Finally, we trace the evolving regional alignment of lead firms (Fig. 1b), illustrating how web-derived economic networks can capture rapid structural adjustments in high-stakes industries.
items.authors: Köse, Seyda*

items.title: Modeling neurodegenerative disease as a spreading process on brain networks
items.abstract: Neurodegenerative diseases---such as Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s disease---are characterized by the progressive appearance of pathological proteins in new brain regions over time. Experimental evidence suggests that these proteins propagate along anatomical connections between neurons, motivating the idea that disease progression can be understood as a dynamical process on a brain network. However, network-based models of neurodegeneration have remained relatively simple and have rarely been tested against longitudinal data in a principled way. We formulate network-based dynamical systems to capture the spatiotemporal progression of protein pathology in the brain. Using high–temporal-resolution histopathology data from mouse models, we fit these models to data using Bayesian Markov chain Monte Carlo. We compare a hierarchy of models of increasing complexity: (i) diffusive spreading along network connections, (ii) spreading with independent local growth dynamics, and (iii) spreading with growth and an additional slower \emph{decay} process, leading to a reduction in pathology at later stages of the disease. We find that diffusion-only and diffusion–growth models fail to reproduce observed regional trajectories. In contrast, the full model incorporating both growth and decay accurately captures the spatiotemporal dynamics of disease progression, achieving an R^2 = 0.84 at the maximum a posteriori estimate. The Bayesian framework allows tractable inference of 2$N$ region-specific parameters and direct comparison of competing transport mechanisms and network null models. Analysis of inferred parameters reveals strong regional heterogeneity. Growth and decay rates vary independently across brain regions. Correlating these parameters with regional gene expression shows that decay rates, but not growth rates, align with dopaminergic pathways central to Parkinson’s disease. This result suggests that disease-relevant vulnerability may be more strongly associated with the loss or clearance of pathology than with its initial accumulation. Together, these findings demonstrate that neurodegenerative progression is governed by multiple timescales on brain networks, and that incorporating decay is essential for explaining longitudinal data. More broadly, this work positions neurodegeneration as a concrete inference problem in network dynamical systems and highlights an underutilized application domain for network science.
items.authors: Alexandersen, Christoffer*

items.title: Message flux imbalance under copy-spread-annihilate dynamics predicts edge weight asymmetries on the mammal connectome
items.abstract: Regions of the mammal brain form nodes in a densely interconnected communication network linked by white matter axons. However, the network is not symmetric: it possesses asymmetries in edge weights between node pairs (as well as non-reciprocal edges). Here we investigate white matter connection strength asymmetries, which have so far not been predicted by a simple generative model. While the exponential distance rule [1] predicts mean edge weight for connected pairs based on empirical tract lengths, this rule does not explain weight asymmetries. Using a dynamic copy-spread-annihilate (CSA) message-passing model, we predict weight asymmetries from simulated message flux over bi-directional edges. Our CSA model is a synchronous Markovian process where messages arriving coincidentally at a node are deleted, while surviving messages spread to all nearest neighbors [2]. We simulated message-passing under the model on the pairwise-complete mouse brain connectome with all weights set to 1 and all spatial and tract length information removed. Message flow generates consistent flux differences across edges that predict empirical weight differences of those edges. For the largest ≈ 5% of weight differences, sign (Spearman’s ρ_avg = 0.30, p_avg < 0.001) and signed magnitude (ρ_avg = 0.22, p_avg < 0.005) are well predicted. These results hold over a range of values of the sole model parameter, message load (i.e., the number of new messages injected at randomly-chosen open nodes each timestep). Adding Gaussian- or exponential-distributed noise to weight values eliminates the effect. No significant prediction of weight asymmetries is achieved by static measures of network structure such as search information [3] and hub-set-orientation prevalence [4] nor by measures of message flux under alternative dynamic models including a random-walk-annihilate (RWA) model, the susceptible-excited-refractory (SER) model [5], and mean first-passage time. We suggest that weight asymmetries are due in part to the ways in which the brain’s evolved topology biases the spread of messages across the network, though other factors such as differences in regional function surely also contribute to these asymmetries. [1] M. Ercsey-Ravasz, N. Markov, C. Lamy, D.C. Van Essen, K. Knoblauch, Z. Toroczkai, and H. Kennedy, Neuron 80, 184 (2013). [2] Y. Hao and D.J. Graham, Network Neuroscience 4, 1055 (2020). [3] C. Seguin, Y. Tian, and A. Zalesky, Network Neuroscience 4, 980 (2020). [4] P. Moretti and M.-T. Hütt, Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences USA 117, 18332 (2020). [5] M. Müller-Linow, C.C. Hilgetag, and M.-T. Hütt, PLoS Computational Biology 4, e1000190 (2008).
items.authors: Hong, Randy; Hao, Yan; Graham, Daniel*

items.title: Social environment dynamics exhibit distinguishable trajectories, distinct mental health outcomes, and dissociable brain network connectivity
items.abstract: Better social support is associated with lower morbidity and mortality, better mental health, better cognitive performance, greater life satisfaction, and differences in brain network organization[1-3]. Beyond just the quality, instability in the social environment, especially during adolescence, has been linked to greater risk of psychopathology later in life[3]. To assess the intersecting associations between social environment dynamics, mental health, and brain network organization, we cluster thousands of adolescents (N=4664) from the ABCD Dataset by applying the Louvain algorithm for modularity maximization to a subject x subject similarity matrix defined by 10 measures of their social environment recorded over four years (see Figure 1a). The resulting three clusters represent social envirotype trajectories[2], i.e., distinct patterns of change in the social environment. At all time points and across all but two measures, Cluster 1 exhibits a worse social envirotype than both Clusters 2 and 3 (all ps for this and all subsequent tests <0.04 using ANOVA and post-hoc Tukey's test, corrected for multiple comparisons); Cluster 3 has the best social envirotype, scoring better than both Cluster 1 and 2 for at least three time points across all but two measures (see Figure 1a). Next, we evaluated potentially confounding demographic features of the clusters, finding that Cluster 3 had higher income and education and less pubertal maturation than the other two clusters, although there were no differences in age. These social envirotype trajectories are associated with both distinct mental health outcomes as well as distinct changes in mental health over adolescence, with Cluster 1 having worse and Cluster 3 having better mental health. Changes in Cluster 1's mental health exhibit a trade-off, while Cluster 2's mental health has the largest improvement and Cluster 3 sees the largest decline (see Figure 1b for composite measures). We then assessed whether these social envirotype trajectories exhibit dissociable patterns of brain network connectivity by performing edge-wise t-tests, correcting for multiple comparisons, then using a space-preserving `spin' test to summarize results at the level of brain system. We found that Cluster 1 displayed greater connectivity of Somatosensory regions (e.g. SMh, Aud, Vis) while Cluster 3 was distinguishable by more extreme connectivity in transmodal systems (e.g. CO, CP, DMN, DAN; see Figure 1c for group-level summary and d for pairwise comparisons). Additionally, we leverage the longitudinality of the data to perform a pseudo-intervention analysis to identify which kinds of changes in the social environment are associated with the largest changes in mental health. Finally, we assess how the patterns of brain network connectivity associated with better mental health look different for different social environment dynamics. This work points towards opportunities for interventions on the social environment to promote better mental health and wellbeing as well as a richer understanding of normative network neurodevelopment.
items.authors: Merritt, Haily*; Gonzalez, Marlen; Teixeira, Andreia Sofia

items.title: Brain network science modelling of sparse neural networks enables Transformers and LLMs to perform as fully connected
items.abstract: This study aims to enlarge our current knowledge on the application of brain-inspired network science principles for training artificial neural networks (ANNs) with sparse connectivity. Dynamic sparse training (DST) emulates the synaptic turnover of real brain networks, reducing the computational demands of training and inference in ANNs. However, existing DST methods face difficulties in maintaining peak performance at high connectivity sparsity levels. The Cannistraci-Hebb training (CHT) is a brain-inspired method that is used in DST for growing synaptic connectivity in sparse neural networks. CHT leverages a gradient-free, topology-driven link regrowth mechanism, which has been shown to achieve ultrasparse (1% connectivity or lower) advantage across various tasks compared to fully connected networks. Yet, CHT suffers two main drawbacks: (i) its time complexity is O(N · d^3)- N node network size, d node degree - hence it can be efficiently applied only to ultra-sparse networks. (ii) it rigidly selects top link prediction scores, which is inappropriate for the early training epochs, when the network topology presents many unreliable connections. Here, we design the first brain-inspired network model - termed bipartite receptive field (BRF) - to initialize the connectivity of sparse artificial neural networks. Then, we propose a matrix multiplication GPU-friendly approximation of the CH link predictor, which reduces the computational complexity to O(N^3), enabling a fast implementation of link prediction in large-scale models. Moreover, we introduce the Cannistraci-Hebb training soft rule (CHTs), which adopts a flexible strategy for sampling connections in both link removal and regrowth, balancing the exploration and exploitation of network topology. Additionally, we propose a sigmoid-based gradual density decay strategy, leading to an advanced framework referred to as CHTss. Empirical results show that BRF offers performance advantages over previous network science models. Using 1% of connections, CHTs outperforms fully connected networks in MLP architectures on visual classification tasks, compressing some networks to less than 30% of the nodes. Using 5% of the connections, CHTss outperforms fully connected networks in two Transformer-based machine translation tasks. Finally, with only 30% of the connections, both CHTs and CHTss achieve superior performance over other dynamic sparse training methods, and perform on par with—or even surpass—their fully connected counterparts in language modeling across various sparsity levels within the LLaMA model family.
items.authors: Zhang, Yingtao; Cerretti , Diego ; Zhao, Jialin ; Liao, Ziheng; Wu, Wenjing; Micheli, Umberto; Cannistraci, Carlo*

items.title: Glioblastoma invasion reorganizes network structure and information flow in in vitro neuronal networks
items.abstract: Glioblastoma (GBM) is the most aggressive primary brain tumor and is frequently accompanied by severe neurological symptoms, including epilepsy and cognitive impairment. Although hyperexcitability associated with glioblastoma is often attributed to increased excitatory drive or local disinhibition, the neurological symptoms often persist following surgical resection~[1], indicating that glioblastoma induces durable and self-sustaining changes in the surrounding neural circuitry. However, mechanistic understanding of how glioblastoma reshapes neuronal network structure in the tumor periphery remains limited. In this work, we investigated how invasion by glioblastoma reorganizes the structure and dynamics of the neuronal network using a compartmentalized \textit{in vitro} platform illustrated in Fig.\ 1A. Neuron somata and glioblastoma were cultured in separate wells connected by axon-guiding microchannels, allowing long-term observation of tumor–neuron interactions. The system was placed on top of high-density microelectrode arrays to permit simultaneous electrophysiological recordings from the entire network. Leveraging this platform, we performed effective connectivity inference [2] at multiple stages of glioblastoma invasion. We found that glioblastoma infiltration drives a collapse of modular network organization and the emergence of broadcasting hub neurons (Fig.\ 1B). These hubs played a distinct functional role during population bursts: they were largely silent at burst onset and became selectively active at later stages of the burst. The reorganization of network structure was also accompanied by changes in information dynamics: glioblastoma confined information transfer predominantly to population bursts, while information integration shifted toward predominantly redundant processing. To mechanistically interpret these observations, we developed an \textit{in silico} spiking neural network model coupled to a glioblastoma-driven global modulatory variable. We showed that the experimentally observed population dynamics can be recapitulated, as illustrated in Fig.\ 1C, when hub neurons possess distinct intrinsic properties—specifically, reduced baseline excitability combined with stronger coupling to glioblastoma-mediated signals. Together, our results suggest that glioblastoma reshapes neural activity not merely by homogeneously elevating excitability, but by reorganizing the network structure and redistributing computational roles across neurons.
items.authors: Amos, Giulia; Jordi, Luc; Miccoli, Mariangela; Ahuja, Karan; Gerber, Alexandra; Hutter, Gregor; Voros, Janos; Tringides, Christina M.; Vasiliauskaite, Vaiva*

items.title: Leveraging Social Networks to Improve Adherence to a Prescribed Diet
items.abstract: Dietary recommendations are typically prescribed at the individual level, despite strong evidence that eating behaviors are socially embedded and shaped by social networks. Even emerging precision nutrition approaches—that tailor dietary guidance based on individual biology and health—rarely incorporate social or structural environments, potentially contributing to persistent challenges in diet adherence. We address this gap by developing a computational model to examine how social network characteristics influence adherence to a prescribed healthy dietary pattern. We constructed an agent-based model representing adults in Los Angeles County, where each agent is embedded in a multi-layered social network spanning household, workplace, and community contexts. Agents choose between a Mediterranean–DASH (MED-DASH)–aligned diet and a Typical American Diet (TAD), with baseline preferences shaped by normative influence from close and weak social ties and by event-based social influence during shared meals, capturing mechanisms of social modeling and mimicry. At each eating occasion, agents probabilistically select a diet based on personal preferences, individual constraints (e.g., food budget, foods available at home or other contexts), and both longer-term social norms and immediate social cues. Using simulation studies, we examined how diet adherence varies with social network characteristics and evaluated social network–based intervention strategies that increase MED-DASH adoption among socially connected individuals and co-eaters. Results indicate that diet adherence is highly sensitive to social network context. Household networks exerted the strongest influence on adherence, followed by workplace and community settings. Across contexts, greater prevalence of healthy eating among social ties substantially improved adherence, including among individuals whose baseline norms favored unhealthy diets. Notably, multiple weak ties adhering to a healthy diet could outweigh the influence of a single strong tie adhering to an unhealthy diet, highlighting the importance of network composition over isolated relationships. These findings frame diet adherence as a socially distributed process and offer a computational foundation for extending precision nutrition to households and social groups.
items.authors: de la Haye, Kayla*

items.title: Social Influence in Large Language Models (LLMs) Groups During Cognitive Tasks
items.abstract: With an increasing prevalence of LLMs in our society, it has been a growing interest in understanding the emergence of collective intelligence (CI) behavior [1]. Albeit CI has been studied widely across human and non-human animal groups (e.g., honeybees, birds), it is rather unclear as to whether the dynamics of collective decision-making can also be observed in LLMs. We sought to examine how social interaction shapes the performance of the collective and the individual using previously approaches to studying CI behaviors in human groups [2].This was done by using a one-shot cognitive assessment: the Number Line Estimation (NLE) task, a widely used instrument for evaluating mathematical competence in children and adolescents [3]. We first carried out simulations on the ego-network associated with LLMs of varying model families and sizes exposed to synthetically generated neighbours’ answers from a Gaussian distribution. We then tested numerous combinations of the parameters, neighbours’ variance σ and neighbours’ mean μ. Following this, we derived a functional form that captures how different configurations of neighbourhood’s performance constraint the ego’s performance. We found that agents integrate social information with a tendency to either maintain complete conviction in their initial answer or fully adherence to the social consensus, with the switch probability being determined by a predictable combination of size of neighbours, neighbours’ variance and errors. This phenomenon can be understood as a critical transition point from stubbornness to possible adoption. Intuitively, agents appear more willing to abandon their initial estimates when surrounded by many neighbors who largely agree with one another, treating such convergence as a signal of reliable collective information. In addition, we repeated the experiment but allowed multiple LLM agents interacting together instead of providing synthetically generated answers, and discovered a significant compensatory effects of social influence. For larger models, social learning via information exchange harms initially accurate agents, but it reduces the errors of the originally inaccurate ones, with a transition point that is invariant to network topology. This finding is contrastive to our expectation that larger-sized models can more resistant against external opinions. All in all, our findings capture the mechanisms and conditions that characterize how networked LLM agents may learn to become better at cognitive tasks. Specifically, this work contributes to the large body of scholarly work on the potential of social learning between LLMs. Our analytic characterization will subsequently enable us to formulate predictions of macroscopic performance in larger networked populations during multi-shot cognitive tasks, encompassing both homogeneous (identically parameterized agents) and heterogeneous ensembles (agents instantiated from distinct model classes). [1] Schoenegger, P. et al., Wisdom of the silicon crowd: LLM ensemble prediction capabilities rival human crowd accuracy. Sci. Adv. 10, eadp1528 (2024). https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.adp1528 [2] Wall, J. L. et al. (2016). Children can accurately monitor and control their number-line estimation performance. Developmental Psychology, 52(10), 1493–1502. https://doi.org/10.1037/dev0000180 [3] Woolley, A. W., Chabris, C. F., Pentland, A., Hashmi, N., & Malone, T. W. (2010). Evidence for a collective intelligence factor in the performance of human groups. Science, 330(6004), 686 688. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1193147
items.authors: Borges, Henrique M.; Yeung, Wang Ngai*; Deshpande, Raj; Di Clemente, Riccardo; Petri, Giovanni

items.title: Manipulating Collective Opinion through Network Intervention
items.abstract: Political polarization—where opinions on political issues diverge into opposing camps—has intensified worldwide, posing a serious threat to the effective functioning of democratic institutions. Because large-scale empirical studies of opinion formation are often impractical, mathematical modeling plays a crucial role in understanding these dynamics. Although existing models successfully reproduce polarization phenomena [1], the effects of structural interventions by social network providers remain poorly understood. Developing a rigorous understanding of these effects is essential for evaluating the risks and unintended consequences of interventions aimed at shaping public discourse, such as efforts to mitigate polarization. In this study, we investigate whether collective opinion can be manipulated by temporarily altering the social network structure, which is a feasible action for platform providers [2]. We consider a simple mathematical model for opinion formation within a social network with $N$ individuals (users) who exchange opinions on a binary issue such as gun control or abortion: $ \dot{x_i} = -x_i + \kappa \sum_{j=1}^N a_{ij} g( |x_i-x_j| ) h(x_j)$, where $x_i$ is the opinion of a user $i$, $\dot{x_i}$ is the time derivative of $x_i$, $\kappa$ is the strength of social interaction between users, and $a_{ij}$ represents the entries of the adjacency matrix of the social network. The sign and magnitude of $x_i$ indicate the user’s stance, i.e., agreement or disagreement, on the issue and the strength of the opinion, respectively. The homophily function $g( |x_i-x_j| )$ describes the tendency of individuals to interact with people who have similar opinions, and the controversy function $h(x_j)$ describes the social reinforcement regarding the issue. Using analytical tools such as mean-field approximation and linear stability analysis, we can analytically identify conditions and tipping points for abrupt opinion shifts. Numerical simulations show that reducing network density---an intervention intended to mitigate polarization---can unintentionally amplify radicalized states. Moreover, network interventions by inferring user stance can drive transitions from polarized states to a targeted radicalized opinion state. Our results highlight the potential risks of large-scale network interventions and underscore the need for theoretically informed design to foster resilient and inclusive public debate in an increasingly networked world.
items.authors: Hata, Shigefumi; Lambiotte, Renaud; Nakao, Hiroya; Kobayashi, Ryota*

items.title: Beyond Dyads: The Roles of Triadic Closure and Real-Time Co-Presence in the Transmission of Healthy Eating Habits
items.abstract: While social influence is a known driver of behavior change, the precise structural and temporal mechanisms governing this transmission remain under-explored, especially the involvement of social closure in small groups. This study utilizes real-time data from a randomized controlled trial among more than 8000 hospital employees conducted in the workplace over four years between 2016-20 [1] to investigate how stimulus-driven healthy eating habits spill over through social networks [2,3]. Specifically, we move beyond dyadic analysis to examine the roles of triadic structure and physical co-presence in mediating the spread of Healthy Purchasing Scores (HPS), a measure of healthy eating scaled between 0(less healthy) - 100(healthier) derived from traffic-light labeling of each item purchased in the workplace. Drawing on theories of network closure and transitivity, we hypothesize that tendencies towards homophily within closed triads (where ”a friend of a friend is also a friend”) create stronger pressures for conformity in eating behaviors than open triads or dyads, thereby accelerating behavior adoption. To further understand the mechanism, we use more than 29 million continuous-time cafeteria purchase records to construct undirected “real-time” triads—instances where all three actors were physically co-present and interacting during a meal, as well as ”artificial” triads where closure is induced over multiple occasions between three dyads during the purchasing year. This approach disentangles two distinct mechanisms: 1. Structural Transmission: Sequential, one-on-one influence occurring across the broader network over time. 2. Realtime Conformity: Immediate social modeling and peer influence occurring during shared eating occasions in small groups. This allows us to test the hypothesis that real-time co-presence in closed triads exerts a significantly stronger influence on HPS adoption than non-co-present (i.e. artificial) closed triads. Our preliminary findings suggest that within-closed-triad HPS (µ= 68.7; SD=8.6) is higher (healthier) than dyadic HPS (µ= 67.1; SD=10.5), and that HPS within triads rises over the 4 years of the study, increasing from µ= 66.6 (SD= 8.6) in 2016 to µ= 72.6 (SD=7.9) in 2019. To investigate these trends, we employ Separable Temporal Exponential Random Graph Models (STERGMs) to decompose network evolution into distinct formation and persistence processes. By adding real-time co-presence as a time-varying covariate, we further isolate immediate conformity from long-term structural transmission. By identifying where and when influence occurs, this research provides a mechanistic foundation for optimizing the impact, equity, and cost-effectiveness of network-based intervention strategies regarding public health and potentially other subjects.
items.authors: Li, Yifan*

items.title: Learning Social Mechanisms for Link Prediction in Signed Social Networks
items.abstract: Signed networks have emerged as a powerful framework in social network analysis to capture friendships (positive) or antagonistic relationships (negative). Structural balance theory and status theory have become the most used frameworks to study these relationships. Structural balance postulates that relationships follow patterns such as ”an enemy of my friend is my enemy” and ”an enemy of my enemy is my friend,” to reduce social tension. Status theory interprets directed signed links as reflecting hierarchical relationships. However, such theories fail to explain the existence of imbalanced triads in social networks, and do not incorporate competing structures or patterns including homophily, reciprocity, and popularity. To try to understand better the interconnectedness of these different mechanisms, we use a logistic regression classifier to combine node and edge features into an edge sign prediction task. Our aim is to uncover how the different signed edges and meso-scale structures contribute to a better signed link prediction (see Figure 1 (a)). For this purpose, we use a temporal high school network dataset among 888 students [1]. We apply inductive learning, partitioning the network by course, and training the model on one course while testing and validating on the others to avoid data leakage. Our results show strong inductive capability, with high balanced accuracy, AUC and F1-scores when predicting link signs in held-out courses, for different training courses and snapshots (see Figure 1 (b)). Additionally, by analyzing the top 10 most important feature coefficients across training courses, we identify consistent predictors: reciprocal tie strength, friend influence, and positive out-degree predict positive edges, while negative out-degree and negative reciprocal links predict negative edges (see Figure 1 (c)). Finally, some triad based features predict edge signs that violate both structural balance and/or status theory (BBpp and FFpp), suggesting that other mechanisms may contribute to signed tie creation. Ongoing work focuses on how these analysis may differ from using state of the art signed graph embeddings that incorporate both nodes and edge features, and whether the same social patterns or discover complementary structures are captured. Our preliminary findings identify the most significant social pattern features, offering guidance for improving the performance and explainability of current machine and deep learning models for link sign prediction, which typically rely solely on structural balance and status theory.
items.authors: Brázia, João*; González-Casado, Miguel; Sánchez, Angel; Francisco, Alexandre; Teixeira, Andreia Sofia

items.title: Nested hyperedges promote the onset of collective transitions but suppress explosive behavior
items.abstract: Higher-order networks often exhibit striking nonlinear behaviors, such as explosive transitions and bistability, yet how these effects arise depends strongly on the microscopic organization of interactions. Here, we study an SIS process in which pairwise and three-body interactions coexist, focusing on inter-order hyperedge overlap, which is defined as the fraction of links that are embedded in three-node groups. This structural measure captures how tightly the two contagion channels are nested within each other. Using a mean-field model that explicitly tracks node, pair, and group states, we derive analytical conditions for the epidemic threshold and for the onset of backward bifurcation, corresponding to the emergence of bistability. We show that increasing inter-order overlap smooths the transition: it lowers the epidemic threshold while suppressing explosive behavior. Simulations on random regular hypergraphs confirm these results and reveal that overlap suppresses discontinuous transitions by reducing the structural independence between pairwise and group transmission pathways. Additionally, we provide a microscopic, mechanistic explanation for this effect. Furthermore, we show that these findings remain consistent for higher-order Kuramoto dynamics. Overall, our results reveal a general topological mechanism through which higher-order structure controls the emergence of nonlinear collective behavior.
items.authors: Malizia, Federico*; Guzmán, Andrés; Battiston, Federico; Kiss, Istvan Zoltan

items.title: Low-Dimensional Phase Diagram of Higher-Order Networked Systems
items.abstract: Tipping points in nonlinear cooperative systems are fundamental to understanding — and potentially controlling — critical transitions, such as the shift from survival to extinction. However, analytically estimating these tipping points remains a major challenge due to the high dimensionality, complex interaction topologies, and diverse dynamical rules of real-world networks, particularly those with higher-order interactions. In this work, we introduce a general dimension reduction framework that captures arbitrary higher-order interactions by projecting their influence onto a low-dimensional state representation. This method enables an intuitive yet rigorous characterization of phase transitions and yields accurate predictions of tipping points in complex, high-dimensional networked systems.
items.authors: Qin, Jia-Jie*; Yan, Gang

items.title: Rumor propagation on hypergraphs
items.abstract: See attached file.
items.authors: Oliveira, Kleber; Traversa, Pietro*; Ferraz de Arruda, Guilherme; Moreno, Yamir

items.title: Reducibility of higher-order to pairwise interactions: Social impact models on hypergraphs
items.abstract: We show that general social impact models with higher-order interactions on hypergraphs can be exactly reduced to a pairwise interactions model on a weighted projected network. This reduction is made by a mapping that preserves the microscopic flipping probabilities. For hypergraph-voter models, we compute the weights of the projected network both analytically and numerically across several hypergraph ensembles, and we characterize their ordering dynamics through simulations of both higher-order and reduced dynamics. For a linear social impact function, the weights of the projected network are time independent, allowing us to develop a Pair Approximation that predicts macroscopic observables independent of those weights. The macroscopic dynamics is thus equivalent to that of the classical voter model on the unweighted projected network. For a nonlinear social impact function, the weights of the projected network depend on the instantaneous system configuration. Nevertheless, the classical nonlinear voter model on the unweighted projected network still reproduces the main macroscopic trends for enough connected hypergraphs.
items.authors: Llabrés, Jaume*; Toral, Raúl; San Miguel, Maxi; Vázquez, Federico

items.title: Higher-order interactions enhance dynamic range in networks of excitable systems
items.abstract: We study the effect of cooperative higher-order interactions on the response of a network of excitable systems to an external stimulus. In various applications, such as in models of functional brain networks, the effectiveness of such a network is measured with the dynamic range, the range of stimuli over which the response can be inferred. In models of functional brain networks in which nodes are coupled with pairwise interactions, it has been found that the dynamic range is maximized when the strength of the interactions is such that the network is at the cusp of a phase transition between quiescence and self-sustained activity [1]. The brain has been hypothesized to operate in this so-called critical regime, in which other measures of information processing are also optimized [2]. However, these previous analyses assume pairwise non-cooperative interactions, while cooperative or higher-order interactions are prevalent in the brain. In order to explore the effect of higher-order interactions on the dynamic range of networks of excitable systems, we extend the Kinouchi-Copelli model [1] to include higher-order (triadic) interactions, where two nodes need to be simultaneously active in order to excite another via a triadic interaction. We conduct numerical simulations on randomly generated networks that include both pairwise and triadic interactions with strengths K2 and K3. We find that the dynamic range becomes the largest when the strength of higher order interactions K3 is such that the network response is close to a subcritical pitchfork bifurcation. Moreover, even away from this bifurcation, the dynamic range attains values that are higher than the maximum value attained with pairwise interactions alone. We validate our numerical results by developing a mean-field theory, from which we obtain semi-analytical results for the dynamic range. The results of this theory are shown in Fig. 1, which shows the dynamic range as a function of K2 and K3. To explore the generality of our results, we validate them on another model where each node is excited stochastically with a probability that depends on their input, which is a combination of pairwise and triadic interaction terms. The results of this different model agree qualitatively with those of the Kinouchi-Copelli model. We also extend our analyses for random networks to networks with structured higher-order interactions, and determine the factors that increase the dynamic range. Our results show that higher-order interactions in networks of excitable systems can enhance their information pro- cessing capabilities, both by increasing the dynamic range and by enlarging the region of parameter space where large values of the dynamic range can be found.
items.authors: Restrepo, Juan*; Taylor, Dane; Kritpracha, Poom

items.title: Task-Focused Dynamic Network Inference from Node Attribute Time Series
items.abstract: Many systems of scientific and societal importance, ranging from brain connectivity to ecological interactions and financial trust networks, are inherently dynamic. Both the attributes of individual entities and the patterns of interaction between them evolve over time, leading to complex dependencies that are most naturally represented as dynamic networks. Yet in most real-world settings, the underlying network structure is not observed directly, and must instead be inferred from time series of node-level measurements \cite{HolmeSaramaki2012,KolarXing2010}. Network reconstruction has a long history in neuroscience, biology, and economics, but most existing approaches assume static topologies or require past observations of edges. These assumptions limit their ability to capture the transient, heterogeneous, and evolving relationships that shape real-world dynamics. We present \textbf{DyNRI}, a framework for \emph{task-focused dynamic network inference} that reconstructs evolving interaction networks directly from node attribute time series. DyNRI builds on Neural Relational Inference (NRI) \cite{Kipf2018} by extending it to time-varying settings. The model employs an encoder–decoder architecture in which the encoder infers distributions over latent edges at each time step using Gumbel-Softmax sampling, and the decoder autoregressively predicts future node attributes conditioned on the inferred dynamic graphs. By aligning network inference with the task of multi-step forecasting, DyNRI learns structures that are not only plausible explanations of past data but also useful for prediction. This distinguishes our approach from purely unsupervised structure discovery and grounds inference in predictive utility. A major challenge in this domain is evaluation: ground-truth dynamic graphs are rarely available. To address this, we introduce a co-evolutionary simulation framework that generates coupled dynamics of node attributes and connectivity. In our simulator, nodes move in a latent space according to a stochastic policy that balances attraction to the centroid of their local neighborhood with independent goal-directed movement. Edges are formed based on $k$-nearest neighbors subject to a distance threshold, ensuring that attribute similarity drives connectivity while connectivity, in turn, influences subsequent attribute updates. This bi-directional coupling produces realistic, non-trivial dynamics where attributes and structure mutually influence each other, providing a principled testbed for dynamic inference methods. Experiments on synthetic data suggest that DyNRI can recover meaningful dynamic structures without access to ground-truth edges, while maintaining prediction accuracy comparable to or exceeding baselines. Ongoing work is extending these experiments to ecological datasets involving animal movement and implicit interactions within groups, and building a reinforcement learning framework that uses DyNRI as a base policy to infer networks in settings with little training data.
items.authors: Banerji, Namrata*; Berger-Wolf, Tanya

items.title: Network Reconstruction via Jeffreys Prior under Missing Sufficient Statistics
items.abstract: The problem of network reconstruction when only a few published information are known is a challenge in network science. Solving this problem well brings great benefits because the information provided can help policy makers make strategic decisions. The traditional fitness model method achieves relatively good results, however, this method only uses one variable: GDP in case of countries or total asset in case of firms to reconstruct the network. This method often ignores additional structural or contextual features. In this paper, we propose improvements with the similarity of category features. Two different methods have been proposed. The first method uses maximum likelihood with additional constraint to find a solution. The second method, used under limited input information conditions, proposes using medium entropy to find the suitable solution among all the solutions that satisfy the constraint. Our approach provides a principled and scalable strategy for building networks, which can exploit maximize potential of the available published information. Three different datasets were applied: ELEnet16, BACI (CPEII) and UN Comtrade to demonstrate the effectiveness of the method. The results are measured by ROC AUC, PR AUC, AIC and BIC. We find that economic regions are the variable that play an important role in network reconstruction for the above dataset.
items.authors: Duong, Duc*; Garlaschelli, Diego

items.title: UniqueRank: Identifying Important and Difficult-to-Replace Nodes in Attributed Graphs
items.abstract: Identifying important nodes within a graph is a critical task across a wide range of real-world applications. A persistent challenge is that the removal of important nodes can greatly compromise a network’s ability to perform its intended function. However, many methods for identifying critical nodes overlook the fact that networks are often resilient and can adapt to disruptions by substituting the removed nodes with alternative nodes located nearby. For example, in a criminal organization, if a central leader is removed, another operative with similar capabilities may step into the leadership role, with network connections subsequently rerouting to preserve the operational structure. In such cases, while the initial removal may temporarily destabilize the system, the long-term impact on overall effectiveness may be minimal if a suitable replacement is readily available. To address this gap, we focus on identifying nodes whose removal and subsequent attempted replacement result in a significant decrease in the network’s overall efficiency. Numerous methods have been developed to rank the importance of nodes, with PageRank being one of the most widely used. More recent approaches, such as AttriRank, extend these methods by integrating node attributes—such as organizational membership—into the ranking process, which captures richer contextual information. We introduce UniqueRank, an efficient method that builds on AttriRank. Like PageRank and AttriRank, we adopt a random walk perspective. Unlike prior methods, which assign equal transition probabilities to all neighbors, UniqueRank increases the likelihood of transitioning to neighbors with more distinct attributes. Each edge (i, j) receives a weight defined as the maximum attribute difference (minimum inverse similarity, defined by a radial basis function applied to attribute differences) between node j and its neighbors, capturing a local neighborhood at most two hops from i. Therefore, movement from node i to a neighbor j is proportional to how different j’s attributes are, relative to other neighbors. The top UniqueRank nodes form a robust set of candidates that are both critical and hard to replace. A secondary refinement step is then applied to select the final set of top-ranked nodes that maximize both structural importance and attribute uniqueness. We rigorously evaluate efficiency reduction of top UniqueRank nodes across diverse domains—real criminal networks, social networks, and supply chain networks—and find that removing and then attempting to replace these top-ranked nodes consistently leads to larger efficiency reduction in the neighborhood of the removed node when compared to other widely used and traditional ranking methods. We measure efficiency reduction by quantifying the relative decrease in shortest path distances between node pairs after a top-ranked node is removed and replaced. Our results hold across different attribute-similarity and node-distance criteria for assessing a node’s suitability as a replacement for a removed node. The presentation will also include results with a novel baseline method, an ablation study, and observations on an extension to biomolecule analysis.
items.authors: Cai, Erica; Miller, Benjamin*; Simek, Olga; Smith, Christopher

items.title: Toward Fairness in Network Algorithms: Rankings by Biased Random Walks
items.abstract: Ranking algorithms such as PageRank play a central role in ordering information and identifying influential nodes in networks, but they can also perpetuate existing biases, reducing the visibility of minority groups. While prior work has examined structural network features of unfair rankings, fewer studies have proposed concrete interventions to improve fairness. We address this gap by introducing a biased variant of PageRank that modifies the random walk transition probabilities based on node in-degree. By tuning a bias exponent, the algorithm can favour transitions toward either high- or low-degree nodes, producing different rankings on the same network. Focusing on networks with binary attributes, we evaluate group fairness by comparing the representation of minority nodes across ranking segments to their expected proportion. Experiments on 15 real-world networks show that standard PageRank consistently performs worse than the proposed Biased-PageRank in terms of fairness. Analysis using null models suggests that unfairness largely arises from degree distribution differences between groups. Overall, our results demonstrate that selecting an appropriate bias exponent can substantially improve ranking fairness, highlighting the importance of explicitly fairness-aware ranking algorithms.
items.authors: Salvai, Elisabetta*; Aarup Dalsgaard, Jacob; Gallo, Luca; Petri, Giovanni; Sinatra, Roberta

items.title: Bayesian Resolution of Discrepant Self-Reported Network Ties
items.abstract: Most social network analysis assumes an objective network of shared social ties, typically measured as self-reports from research subjects. Subjects often give discrepant reports on ties, however, and there is no standard way to resolve these discrepancies. We develop a method to address this pervasive and important problem. Building on work by Butts, 2003, we develop a Bayesian model that leverages patterns of agreement among respondents across multiple relations on the same set of subjects. The multiple relations allow a natural context for pseudo-replication, allowing us to estimate false positive and false negative rates for each individual and to find a posterior distribution for the objective network. Our work is motivated by the pervasive problem of discrepant reports in social network research and developed in the context of an exemplary set of six undirected social relations among middle school students in the Food Activity Screens and Teens (FAST) study (Sirard et al, 2024). The FAST study investigates multiple social relations among U.S. middle school students in several schools. Observed reporting patterns including small numbers of dramatic under-reporters and an even smaller number of dramatic over-reporters motivates our use of a novel class of flexible priors to allow for these realistic aberrant reporting behaviors. In simulations and model checks, we find that our method improves on both the extreme heuristic methods commonly used to resolve discordant reports, and on previous statistical methods (see Figure 1). We also provide posterior predictive checks which illustrate the strength of model performance; comparison across 6 relations in 4 datasets allows reflection on circumstances when the models perform more or less well.
items.authors: Gile, Krista*

items.title: Simultaneous Inference of Network Structure and Dynamics: How Much Data is Needed?
items.abstract: Understanding complex systems requires knowledge of both their network structure, which captures the interactions among components, and their dynamical mechanisms, which govern system evolution. While recent advances have enabled data-driven inference of either network topology or governing equations, an approach capable of recovering both simultaneously has remained elusive. Equally unresolved is the fundamental question of how much data is minimally required to achieve reliable inference. Here we present a unified framework that jointly infers both network topology and governing equations directly from time-series data, combining interpretable symbolic representations with computational adaptability. By applying this framework to coupled systems, we reveal the fundamental data limits for accurate inference and identify a phase transition from practically inferable to non-inferable regimes as system size increases. Our analysis further quantifies the information content of observational data for different time windows and sampling rates, linking it to the achievable inferability. These findings lay down both the theoretical foundations and practical guidelines for data-driven inference of interconnected dynamical systems across diverse scientific domains.
items.authors: Sun, Yiting*; Gao, Tingting; Yan, Gang

items.title: Distributed Self-Control of Dynamical Networks by Adaptive Link Weight Adjustments
items.abstract: Conventional control theory considers controlling the behavior of a dynamical system toward a desired state by injecting externally designed inputs into the system. Meanwhile, most complex systems exhibit self-organization through local information exchanges among individual dynamical components that are embedded within a complex network of interactions. The self-organizing dynamics of those systems are realized in a highly distributed manner using locally available information only, and therefore, their behaviors have not been discussed much from a control theoretic viewpoint. Meanwhile, adaptive networks, i.e., dynamical networks whose states and topologies coevolve at similar time scales, can offer a promising theoretical framework in which a distributed form of ``self-control'' can be formulated as adaptive link weight adjustments. Here we propose and study a set of models of self-controlling dynamical networks that can self-organize their own states into a desired one through distributed adaptive link weight adjustments. We demonstrate the feasibility of both of the first-order and second-order approaches. Numerical experiments show that the second-order approach works significantly better than the first-order one, achieving a substantially faster and more robust convergence with less transient behaviors. The performance difference is more manifested for systems with faster adaptive dynamics. However, we also found that the second-order approach was highly sensitive to noise added to the system's states, illustrating a non-trivial trade-off between the two self-control approaches.
items.authors: Sayama, Hiroki*

items.title: A broad class of network motifs enables constants of motion in phase dynamics
items.abstract: Partial integrability in phase-oscillator dynamics is typically examined for identically connected oscillators or groups thereof [1,2,3]. Yet, the precise connectivity conditions that ensure conserved quantities on general networks remain unclear. Using Koopman theory [4], we rigorously derive conditions for the existence of distinct conserved quantities in the Kuramoto model with heterogeneous phase lags on any weighted, directed, and signed graph [5]. In the process, we establish a criterion for Lie symmetries in general dynamical systems in terms of the Koopman generator, allowing us to identify symmetries that generate new conserved quantities. These results reveal a broad class of network motifs that support conserved quantities and we detect these motifs in hundreds of complex empirical networks. Our results provide a rigorous theoretical application of Koopman's framework to nonlinear dynamics on complex networks and new possibilities for preserving phase relationships over time in complex systems through a careful design of their structure.
items.authors: Thibeault, Vincent*; Claveau, Benjamin; Allard, Antoine; Desrosiers, Patrick

items.title: Disentangling Spatial and Noise Structures in Network Processes
items.abstract: Real-world dynamical processes exhibit variability that is poorly captured by deterministic models. In practice, fitting such models directly to data often risks conflating unmodeled stochasticity with structural components of the model, leading to parameter estimates that absorb noise and lose physical meaning. Instead, stochastic process models provide a principled alternative by explicitly separating deterministic dynamics from (structured) random forcing. Our work aims to untangle the effects of system topology, the dynamics governing evolution on that topology, and the statistical properties of external perturbations. In particular, we fill the gap between network inference from heat-diffusion trajectories and SDE-based graph kernels, to address the identifiability of spatial operators and noise structure from spatio-temporal observations.
items.authors: Nocet-Binois, Esteban*; Hackl, Jürgen

items.title: Deep Graph Generation for Microcanonical Ensemble with Arbitrary Topological Constraints
items.abstract:
Randomized graph ensembles serve as essential null models in network science for discerning statistically significant structural features of real-world networks. These ensembles are typically constructed either as 	extit{canonical}, where a topological observable $M(G)$ is constrained on average ($\langle M(G) angle = m$), or as 	extit{microcanonical}, where the constraint is satisfied exactly for every realization ($M(G) = m$). Exponential Random Graph (ERG) model is the standard framework for generating canonical ensembles, utilizing a control parameter $\alpha$ conjugate to $M(G)$ to bias the sampling probability $P(G) \sim \exp[-\alpha M(G)]$ [1]. In practice, sampling from ERGs relies on Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods, such as Metropolis-Hastings algorithms with degree-preserving rewiring. However, the mapping between target $m$ and parameter $\alpha$ is often analytically intractable, requiring exhaustive search. Moreover, the resulting ensembles exhibit finite variance in $M(G)$, and mixing time requires large number of rewiring steps. Recent studies have begun to explore deep reinforcement learning for graph generation [2]. In this work, we introduce a deep graph generation (DGG) sampling, a reinforcement learning framework designed to generate microcanonical ensembles directly, bypassing the limitations of MCMC sampling. Unlike MCMC, which relies on stochastic accept and reject, we formulate the graph generation as a sequential rewiring selection process as shown in Fig.~ef{fig:fig1}(a). A graph neural network (GNN) agent, trained via Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), takes the adjacency matrix and the target $m$ as input and autoregressively selects the optimal edge swaps to drive the graph to the exact target state $M(G)=m$. We demonstrate the efficacy of the DGG by focusing on degree-degree correlation, or assortativity $r$. The trained agent successfully generates microcanonical ensembles across a broad spectrum of network sizes ($N \in [10^2, 10^4]$), sparsity (mean degree $\langle k angle \in [3, 10]$), and degree distributions (Poisson and power-law) over the full range of assortativity $r \in [-0.8, 0.8]$. Crucially, DGG maintains topological randomness, exhibiting entropy~[3] comparable to MCMC sampling (Fig.~ef{fig:fig1}(b)), while requiring an order of magnitude fewer rewiring steps. Our results suggest that deep reinforcement learning offers a scalable, flexible, and efficient alternative for constructing rigorous null models in network science. 	extbf{(a) Schematic comparison of ensemble generation methods.} ERG sampling requires proper conjugate parameter $\alpha^*$ and perform random rewiring for canonical ensemble. DGG sampling directly utilizes the target assortativity $r^*$ and a GNN agent performs optimal rewiring, resulting in microcanonical ensemble. 	extbf{(b) Entropy and efficiency.} The entropy of DGG generated microcanonical ensembles (blue) closely matches that of MCMC canonical ensembles (orange). The inset highlights that DGG satisfies more strict constraints with fewer rewirings compared to the ERG. 
oindent[1] Park, J., \& Newman, M. E. (2004). Statistical mechanics of networks. Phys. Rev. E. 70(6), 066117. \ 
oindent[2] Darvariu, V.-A., Hailes, S., \& Musolesi, M. (2024). Graph Reinforcement Learning for Combinatorial Optimization: A Survey and Unifying Perspective. Transact. Mach. Learn. Res. \ 
oindent[3] Bianconi, G. (2007). The entropy of randomized network ensembles. Europhys. Lett., 81(2), 28005.
items.authors: Choi, Hoyun*; Jo, Junghyo; Lee, Deok-Sun

items.title: Networked Quantum–Classical Interdependency
items.abstract: Interdependency---the mutual reliance and interaction between interconnected systems---is a fundamental factor determining performance and functionality of complex networked systems. In quantum networks, the quantum hardware intrinsically depends on classical communication for signaling, feedback and resource allocation, yielding a tightly coupled architecture. While this tight quantum–classical coupling is recognized as a critical bottleneck limiting the reliable distribution of high-quality entanglement, prevailing analytical models typically oversimplify this interaction, leaving performance boundaries of quantum networks largely unexplored. Here, we introduce a quantitative framework revealing that this interdependency is not merely a bottleneck but a fundamental driver of emergent, network-wide collective phenomena. We show that the quantum–classical interdependency induces phase transitions in entanglement distribution—either mixed-order (discontinuous) or second-order (continuous)—depending critically on the specific quantum control protocol and noise levels. To reveal the underlying mechanism, we introduce a novel metric—the linear gain coefficient—which analytically captures the role of the quantum protocol and accurately predicts both the transition type and network robustness, as validated by quantum computer simulations. Our framework provides fundamental insights into quantum–classical interdependency and offers a predictive tool to guide the design of resilient quantum architectures capable of reliable, long-distance entanglement distribution.
items.authors: Meng, Xiangyi*

items.title: Matching and mixing: Matchability of graphs under Markovian error
items.abstract: We consider the problem of graph matching for a sequence of graphs generated under a time-dependent Markov chain noise model. Our edgelighter error model, a variant of the classical lamplighter random walk, iteratively corrupts the graph $G_0$ with edge-dependent noise, creating a sequence of noisy graph copies $(G_t)$. Much of the graph matching literature is focused on anonymization thresholds in edge-independent noise settings, and we establish novel anonymization thresholds in this edge-dependent noise setting when matching $G_0$ and $G_t$. Moreover, we also compare this anonymization threshold with the mixing properties of the Markov chain noise model. We show that when $G_0$ is drawn from a sufficiently flat graph model (e.g., the Erdos–Renyi model), the graph matching anonymization threshold and the mixing time of the edgelighter walk are both of order $\Theta(n^2\log n)$. We further demonstrate that for more structured model for $G_0$ (e.g., the Stochastic Block Model), graph matching anonymization can occur in $O(n^\alpha\log n)$ time for some $\alpha<2$, indicating that anonymization can occur before the Markov chain noise model globally mixes. Through extensive simulations, we verify our theoretical bounds in the settings of Erdos–Renyi random graphs and stochastic block model random graphs, and explore our findings on real-world datasets derived from a Facebook friendship network and a European research institution email communication network.
items.authors: Li, Zhirui; Levin, Keith; Zhao, Zhiang; Lyzinski, Vince*

items.title: Beyond Direct Links: Structural Mediation in Multipartite Networks
items.abstract: Many complex systems are naturally represented by entities interacting within different layers encoding different types of relationships. Yet, network analyses often focus on direct connections in each single layer, ignoring how these interactions are impacted by information contained in the other ones. Here, we introduce a network methodology to quantify how additional information coming from another layer mediates direct relationships and reshapes node relevance. Some systems can be represented as networks with three levels of information: an attribute level, whose internal relationships are our object of interest; a mediator level, whose structure gives us the context in which the relationships are shaped; and an entity level, that connects to attributes and mediators. For example: a network with species (entities) connected to the habitats they live in (mediators) and labeled with their phenotypes (attributes). This formulation allows us to explicitly examine how the roles of nodes at the attribute level change when the mediator level structure is included. We propose a framework that identifies the most relevant nodes, both considering direct relationships and those that emerge through the mediator level. We begin by characterizing the direct relationships. Starting from the bipartite network linking entities to attributes, we build the attribute-attribute network through contraction, obtaining direct relationships between attributes. For the mediated relationships, we start from the entity-mediator bipartite network and we build its entity-entity contraction, where two entities are linked if they share a mediator, such that proximity now reflects a shared embedding in the mediating structure. Each entity is then annotated with its attributes, forming overlapping subsets within the network. We compute average shortest-path distances within and between these attribute subsets to quantify how close they are in the entity network, when paths are constrained by the mediator-imposed structure. This yields a distance matrix, from which we derive an attribute-attribute network whose links are weighted according to its inter-attribute distance. Finally, we compute centrality measures for all nodes in both networks to see how important they are, considering direct or indirect connections. This allows us to isolate how structural mediation alters relational patterns and node relevance. These effects are captured in the metric Delta C, defined as the difference between indirect and direct centrality, and in a two-dimensional space defined by direct versus indirect centrality, which better describes the information gained through mediation. We have applied this framework to three different cases: microbial phenotypes structured by the ecological context, scientific impact mediated by collaboration networks and musical artist popularity shaped by social interactions. For all three, we show that the indirect structural context systematically reorganizes attribute relationships and centrality rankings. For the music data set, and using eigenvector centrality, the social structure mediation highlights popular artists at the time of data extraction, such as Bruno Mars, while direct associations capture canonical artists, such as the Beatles. Similar shifts can be observed in the biological and literature datasets, showing that these mediating layers can fundamentally alter how central a node is. Our results show that the inclusion of indirect structural mediation reveals information that is inaccessible when we only consider direct relationships, offering an approach to help clarify how multi level interactions shape relevance and organization in complex networks.
items.authors: Duarte, Pedro*; Durazzi, Francesco; Remondini, Daniel

items.title: Parameter Stability as a Diagnostic of Network Model Misspecification
items.abstract: Much of scientific modeling is concerned with accurate and reproducible estimation of model parameters. In explanatory modeling of complex systems, for example, such estimation helps to understand the importance of the basic building blocks of these systems. Much of network science has adopted a similar explanatory outlook, although studies have traditionally focused on the network statistics (values of constraints) rather than the underlying parameters in generative models. This has partially reflected a practical challenge: in many cases, the generative model of individual networks has remained implicit, which has made such parameter values difficult to estimate. Seminal work by Squartini and Garlaschelli has adopted the formalism of maximum-entropy (exponential-random-graph) models to explicate the parametric generative models of canonical network features (including in- and out-degrees), primarily to generate null models of real networks [1]. Here, we adopt this work to explore the parametric aspect of this formalism. We specifically examine the emergence of a phenomenon analogous to collinearity in classic linear regression. Collinearity (or multicollinearity) denotes a situation when the presence of correlated regressors results in high variance of parameter values, across multiple estimates. Though this phenomenon is well-established in classical statistics, it has been less explored in network science. Here, we consider a nonlinear version of this phenomenon within the maximum entropy framework. We adopt maximum entropy models that maximize entropy over network ensembles subject to specified constraints, specifically node degrees. Each constraint is assigned a Lagrange multiplier that encodes its contribution to reproducing desired network statistics. Our central aim is to quantify the effects of constraint similarity on parameter stability. We generated ensembles of perturbed networks by adding Gaussian noise to edge weights, fit models to each perturbed sample, and computed the coefficient of variation for each fitted parameter across all network samples. We quantified similarity of individual constraints using normalized mutual information (NMI). Across pairs of constraints, we observed a consistent relationship: increased constraint overlap was associated with increased mean parameter CV, suggesting that constraint similarity induces parameter instability. Here, we focus on in- and out-degree constraints across three example networks from neuroscience. The NMI between in-degree and out-degree constraints is 0, and correspondingly, the coefficient of variation of their fitted parameters is quite small, demonstrating that co-non-linearity is minimal in these data. However, as parameters become more similar to those of the out-degree constraint, we observe a super-exponential rise in parameter variation. This relationship highlights how parameter stability sensitively detects even subtle constraint overlap. In sum, we developed a practical diagnostic for estimating the stability of parameters, and used it to investigate the effects on constraint overlap on the reproducibility of individual parameters. We found a robust and stereotyped increase in parameter instability with constraint similarity, indicating that this instability can serve as a quantitative signature of misspecified models. Ultimately, this approach complements existing model-selection and goodness-of-fit practices by shifting attention from constraint values to the estimation of the parameters that generate them. In this way, it offers a principled approach for advance principled specification and estimation of explanatory models.
items.authors: Volkmann, Henry*; Rubinov, Mikail

items.title: The Two Sides of VC Attention: Investor Visibility and Startup Memorability
items.abstract: In an attention economy, getting venture funding is about capturing attention. Here, we propose a two-sided attention alignment framework that examines investor visibility and startup memorability. Our integrated findings demonstrate that two key factors matter most: the startup's ability to generate media buzz and the investor's social visibility and network reach.
