On the investor side, our analysis of nearly 500 U.S. software startups (founded 2011–2016) shows that ventures with more VCs on the board and board members active on Twitter secure greater subsequent funding - yet these features do not translate into higher sales. Conversely, ventures without VCs on the board and those with boards whose members are not active on Twitter exhibit higher asset turnover efficiency, indicating a stronger conversion of assets into revenue [1]. This suggests a tradeoff between attention-driven funding and operational performance. On the startup side, in a study of 197 U.K. micro/nano startups (1995–2004), we demonstrate that media memorability—operationalized through news semantic networks and the Semantic Brand Score (prevalence, distinctiveness, connectivity)—increases the probability of receiving VC funding beyond what media coverage and tone alone can explain [2]. In other words, how you're remembered matters more than how often you're mentioned. Methodologically, both studies quantify attention using network and text-based measures, moving beyond simple counts of mentions or followers. Practically, our findings suggest two implications: boards should cultivate credible, value-adding social presence, rather than visibility for its own sake; and founders should seek media coverage that builds a distinctive, well-connected image, rather than pursuing mention volume alone.
items.authors: Fronzetti Colladon, Andrea*; Grippa, Francesca

items.title: Evolution and determinants of firm-level systemic risk in local production networks
items.abstract: Recent crises like the COVID-19 pandemic and geopolitical tensions have exposed vulnerabilities and caused disruptions of supply chains, leading to product shortages, increased costs, and economic instability. This has prompted increasing efforts to assess systemic risk, namely the effects of firm disruptions on entire economies. However, the ability of firms to react to crises by rewiring their supply links has been largely overlooked, limiting our understanding of production networks resilience. Here, we study dynamics and determinants of firm-level systemic risk in the Hungarian production network from 2015 to 2022. We use as benchmark the stripe-corrected gravity model [1], a heuristic maximum entropy null model that generates an ensemble of production networks at equilibrium, by preserving the total input (demand) and output (supply) of each firm at the sector level. By computing the Economic Systemic Risk Index (ESRI) [2] we can see how the empirical values align well with the null model until the onset of the pandemic, as shown in Figure 1 A. It becomes significantly smaller afterwards, as the adaptive behavior of firms leads to a more resilient economy – both in absolute terms and with respect to equilibrium values. Furthermore we show that the fairly stable set of firms with highest systemic risk undergoes a structural change during COVID-19, as those enabling economic exchanges become key players in the economy – a result which can be seen in Figure 1 B and that is not reproduced by the null model. Moreover, regressions highlight how firms’ international trade volume (being a subject of disruption) becomes a significant predictor of their systemic risk. However, international links cannot provide an unequivocal explanation for the observed trends, as imports and exports have opposing effects on local systemic risk through the supply and demand channels.
items.authors: Mancini, Anna*

items.title: Assessing (im)balance in signed brain networks
items.abstract: Many complex systems (be they financial, natural, or social) are composed of units (such as stocks, neurons, or agents) whose joint activity can be represented as a multivariate time series. An issue of both practical and theoretical importance concerns the possibility of inferring the presence of a static relationship between any two units solely from their dynamic state. This contribution tackles such an issue within the frame of traditional hypothesis testing: briefly speaking, our suggestion is that of linking any two units if behaving in a sufficiently similar way. To achieve such a goal, we project a multivariate time series onto a signed graph by i) comparing the empirical properties of the former with those expected under a suitable benchmark and ii) linking any two units with a positive (negative) edge in case the corresponding series shares a significantly large number of concordant (discordant) values. To define our benchmarks, we adopt an information-theoretic approach that is rooted into the constrained maximisation of Shannon entropy, a procedure inducing an ensemble of multivariate time series that preserves some of the empirical properties on average, while randomising everything else. We showcase the possible applications of our method by addressing one of the most timely issues in the domain of neurosciences, i.e. that of determining if brain networks are frustrated or not. As our results suggest, this is indeed the case, with the major contribution to the underlying negative subgraph coming from the subcortical structures (and, to a lesser extent, from the limbic regions). At the mesoscopic level, the minimisation of the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC), instantiated with the Signed Stochastic Block Model, reveals that brain areas gather into modules aligning with the statistical variant of the Relaxed Balance Theory; besides, the revealed modules are capable of capturing up to the 80% of certain Yeo's regions. Notice that minimising the frustration (F) leads to isolate a number of communities as well: in this second case, however, these modules appear as quite uninformative as six of them are very small groups of nodes fragmenting a single Yeo's region in a quite unrealistic fashion, while the seventh and the eighth ones gather all the remaining regions together. It is, thus, of interest wondering which principle is obeyed by the brain to self-organise its functioning: Yeo's partition aligns (better) with the one returned by BIC minimisation, an advantage of which is that of gathering signed connections in a maximally "agnostic" way - differently from F minimisation, that seeks for the group-wise structure best aligning with the Traditional Balance Theory.
items.authors: Di Vece, Marzio; Agrimi, Emanuele; Tatullo, Samuele; Gili, Tommaso; Ibanez-Berganza, Miguel; Squartini, Tiziano*

items.title: Predictability of human movement in multilayer mobility networks based on industry sectors
items.abstract: Understanding human movement patterns is important for a myriad of applications including social/cognitive science, urban design, disease control, and response planning for extreme weather events. In this study, we investigate the extent to which human movements are predictable by using statistical techniques and machine learning (ML) to predict the structural patterns of multilayer networks in which the layers encode the different industry sectors including movements, e.g., to locations associated with education, health, food, retail trade, and so on. We construct these time-varying, multiplex networks using the approach developed in that utilizes the SafeGraph dataset (which contains weekly human movements derived from mobile-device GPS locations) and converts that data into time-varying multilayer networks in which nodes represent geographical regions called census tracts (CTs), and for each industry category, directed edges encode the observed movements between CTs during a given week. Given such data, we train ML models to predict the network layers in- and out-degrees (as well as those for layer-aggregated networks), using two types of model input data: social factors obtained from the U.S. Census and infrastructure information obtained from Safegraph (e.g., the number of schools in a given CT). Our study compares and contrasts the performance of different predictive models, the effects of different training procedures, and reveals which industry categories for movement are easier/harder to predict.
items.authors: Sejunti, Maisha Islam*; Butler, Melissa ; Hu, Yingjie ; Taylor, Dane

items.title: Modeling and Learning Dynamics of Online–Offline Social Systems on Networks
items.abstract: With the increasing use of social media, the boundary between the online and offline worlds becomes blurred. Activities in one domain strongly influence the other. From the online world, we see social media transforming offline life, from everyday decisions to the escalation of conflicts. Conversely, offline events in the physical world drive online discussion and engagement. It is thus essential to understand the mechanisms underlying the coupled dynamics of online and offline social activities. We present our attempts at disentangling this problem from two perspectives. (1) Model-driven: We propose a framework for modeling the population-level dynamics of participation on coupled online and offline layers, where agents interact through online networks. We develop corresponding mean-field approximations that allow outburst analysis using the reproductive number. To link mathematical models with the real world, we complement our study with data-driven approaches. (2) Data-driven: First, motivated by the high activity and vast influence of bots on the internet, we infer mathematical models for information spread among multi-agent large language models (LLMs) in response to real-world events, where mean-field models have proven to be an effective framework. Second, we use system identification, specifically Weak Form Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics (WSINDy), to learn models from noisy data, allowing us to identify effective continuum models as approximations of stochastic processes on networks. Overall, our studies demonstrate initial steps toward bridging simulation, modeling, and data-driven learning for coupled online–offline social dynamics. [1] Moyi Tian, P. Jeffrey Brantingham, and Nancy Rodríguez. Modelling the spillover from online engagement to offline protest: stochastic dynamics and mean-field approximations on networks. Journal of Complex Networks, in press (2025).
items.authors: Tian, Moyi*; Rodríguez, Nancy; Bortz, David; Mohler, George; Brantingham, P. Jeffrey

items.title: The dynamics of the Reddit collective action leading to the GameStop short squeeze
items.abstract: Online social media has transformed financial markets by accelerating the spread of information, sentiment and investment strategies, and by enabling new forms of collective behavior among retail investors [1,2]. While these platforms can enhance price discovery, they also amplify behavioral biases and shared narratives, particularly during periods of market stress and heightened attention. This study [1] investigates the January 2021 GameStop (GME) short squeeze, largely driven by the Reddit community WallStreetBets (WSB), to determine whether users merely reacted to ongoing market movements, or actively coordinated a financial collective action, therefore anticipating market dynamics. We analyze the temporal interplay among stock price movements, trading volume, WSB activity, and public attention using both linear methods (Granger causality, Vector Autoregression) and nonlinear techniques (Detrended Cross-Correlation Analysis, Convergent Cross-Mapping). Our results show that WSB activity systematically forecast price and volume surges in GME and related stocks, and that the episode unfolded through three distinct phases: discussion, action, and visibility. A sharp transition occurred on January 13, when WSB activity began to anticipate changes in GME trading volume, marking the shift from decentralized online debate to coordinated market action. At this stage, the collective financial exposure of WSB users became economically significant, and the subsequent surge of broader investor participation amplified the market impact. The event entered a global visibility phase following Elon Musk’s January 27 tweet. Overall, we show that online communities can act as catalysts for coordinated collective action with direct, measurable effects on financial markets. These findings highlight the growing systemic relevance of socially coordinated retail investors and underline the importance of fine-grained temporal analysis for understanding the evolving structure and dynamics of modern financial systems.
items.authors: Desiderio, Antonio; Aiello, Luca; Cimini, Giulio*; Alessandretti, Laura

items.title: When a New Line Helps and the Others Harm: Network Determinants of Grid Stability
items.abstract: Modern power grids face growing structural complexity driven by increased renewable and distributed power generation, creating substantial challenges for maintaining grid stability. While a new transmission line construction seems to reinforce the power grid stability, the consequences of the new line placement for the synchronization performance of the power grid remain elusive. Critically, reinforcing a wrong line could trigger catastrophic chain reactions, small overloads escalating to cascading blackouts, where well intentioned additions paradoxically worsen system performance (Braess’s paradox). We investigate how transmission line additions affect synchronization across scenarios using the second order Kuramoto model and basin stability analysis with systematically varied network size, power distribution, and topology. Our comprehensive evaluation reveals a robust placement principle: connecting power centers of generation and consumption clusters consistently improves stability across all configurations by up to 75%. Critically, we identify context dependent phenomena where suboptimal placements specifically, connecting nodes at cluster edges trigger Braess’s paradox, degrading stability by up to 49%. These findings contradict the conventional belief that additional transmission will naturally strengthen grids, demonstrating that poorly positioned lines can paradoxically reduce stability. Our results highlight critical considerations for transmission planning through comprehensive observations across diverse network topologies and operational conditions.
items.authors: CHOI, YUNJU*; Kim, Heetae; Lee, Daekyung

items.title: The Functional Advantage of Dimeric Architecture in Photosystem II
items.abstract: Green plants capture sunlight and convert it into chemical energy through photosynthesis. This process relies on Photosystem II (PSII), where light energy absorbed by chlorophylls is transferred to reaction centers (RCs) for charge separation. PSII consists of protein subunits that hold numerous chlorophylls, forming an energy transfer network that guides excitation energy toward two RCs. This complex exhibits a symmetric and dimeric architecture with two identical RCs [1](Fig 1(a)), yet the functional advantage of this structural arrangement remains unclear. Here, we apply network analysis to investigate how this dimeric structure contributes to robust performance under various RC failure scenarios. We modeled PSII as a network in which individual pigments serve as nodes and energy transfer pathways serve as weighted edges, with charge separation at RCs and heat dissipation representing terminal sinks. By systematically analyzing network flow dynamics under different single-RC operational states, we quantified changes in energy transfer efficiency. We examined three functional states characterized by their charge separation rates: fully functional (0.33 ps−1, the open state)[2], partially inhibited (0.005 ps−1, the closed state)[3], and completely inactive (0 ps−1, the damaged state). Our analysis reveals that when one RC is closed(OC) or damaged(OD) while the other remains open, the overall efficiency is maintained at approximately 78.30 − 81.32% relative to when both RCs are open(OO)(Fig 1(b)). This robustness demonstrates that the open RC can effectively compensate for its closed or damaged counterpart. Furthermore, non- photochemical quenching (NPQ)—heat dissipation for photoprotection—increases as the system becomes more closed, indicating a balance between energy utilization and protective dissipation(Fig 1(c)). Critically, we identified the structural basis for this robustness by analyzing energy transfer pathways under different RC states. When both RCs are open, energy flows through the network via symmetric and distributed pathways across the dimeric structure(Fig 1(d)). However, when one RC becomes closed or damaged, a shortcut pathway emerges that directly connects nodes on the impaired side to those on the functional side, effectively rerouting excitation energy toward the open RC(Fig 1(e), red line). These findings demonstrate that PSII’s dimeric architecture functions as a fault-tolerant network, illustrating how symmetric redundancy in biological systems can ensure robust performance under component failure.
items.authors: Kim, Jonghoon*; Kim, Eunchul; Kim, Heetae

items.title: The Stage and the Closet: Visibility Regimes of Pornography on Twitter Networks
items.abstract: Social media trace data are central to computational social science, yet a growing literature warns that sharing logs reflect filtered visibility rather than actual consumption. We investigate how "visibility regimes"—shaped by stigma, platform governance, and economic incentives—distort sharing-based domain–similarity networks, and how these distortions can mislead inferences about underlying behavior. We use pornography sharing on Twitter (X) as an extreme test case. Pornography is widely consumed but highly stigmatized, subject to strict platform governance (e.g., age-gates, shadow bans), and tightly coupled to monetization models that reward visibility. Consequently, public sharing is a strategic, high-cost choice, restricted primarily to a small subset of accounts (creators, promotional networks) navigating these constraints. Employing large-scale domain-sharing data linked to estimated demographic indicators, we quantify visibility distortion by comparing volume, concentration (Gini), and group over-representation across creator-oriented (e.g., OnlyFans), consumer-oriented (e.g., Pornhub), and mainstream entertainment domains. We further construct a domain–similarity network where nodes represent domains and weighted edges encode the cosine similarity of their demographic profiles, allowing us to visualize a backbone of the most visible domains connected by strong similarity ties. The resulting network comprises 27,414 nodes and 111,481 edges, dominated by a giant component of 26,242 nodes. Within this structure, we visualize a backbone of the most visible domains connected by strong similarity ties. Our results suggest a sharp visibility filter. Relative to mainstream entertainment, porn platforms appear rare in public traces and highly concentrated in who shares them. Pornhub emerges as "The Closet": it generates high estimated traffic but exhibits low, moderately concentrated public sharing. In contrast, OnlyFans functions as "The Stage": despite lower traffic, it shows extraordinarily concentrated sharing consistent with a small promoter elite. Notably, inferred user profiles suggest OnlyFans sharers are strongly male-skewed and disproportionately Black relative to demographic baselines, indicating potential intersectional structures in stigma costs and promotional incentives. In the domain–similarity network, OnlyFans appears to occupy a distinct lifestyle cluster tied to sports and games, indicating the cultural worlds in which sexual visibility is normalized. We introduce visibility regimes as a network-level property of stigmatized behaviors and demonstrate how inequality, demographic skew, and network position can decouple promotion from consumption. This framework offers a lens for analyzing other high-cost or stigmatized visibility behaviors—such as misinformation, gambling, or self-harm content—that occupy distinct positions in the stigma–governance–incentive space.
items.authors: Lu, Hsiu-Chi*; Ichikawa, Kei

items.title: Optimal dismantling of directed networks
items.abstract: As a fundamental problem in network science, network dismantling focuses on identifying a set of critical nodes whose removal sharply reduces a network's connectivity and functionality. Potential applications include stopping rumor spread, blocking sentiment propagation, and controlling epidemics and pandemics. Previous studies have mainly focused on undirected networks, whereas many real-world networks are inherently directed, such as the World Wide Web and the global trade system. Moreover, the functionality of directed networks depends on the giant strongly connected component (GSCC), where nodes are mutually reachable. Considering both the directionality and heterogeneity of these networks, we propose a novel centrality measure—network incoherence (NI) centrality—and develop a trophic analysis-based dismantling (TAD) method, in which nodes are removed in descending order according to their NI centrality scores, aiming to efficiently dismantle directed networks by reducing the GSCC. When applied to a wide range of benchmark synthetic networks with varying degree heterogeneity and 15 real-world directed networks, our TAD method consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods. Significantly, TAD also induces the largest maximum avalanches during the dismantling process, highlighting its ability to capture structurally critical nodes. These findings provide new insight into the structure-function relationship of directed networks and inform the design of more resilient systems against perturbations.
items.authors: Liu, Xueming*

items.title: Uncovering simultaneous breakthroughs with a robust measure of disruptiveness
items.abstract: Progress in science and technology is punctuated by disruptive innovations and breakthroughs. Identifying such contributions has attracted significant attention, yet challenges remain, such as incorporating complex, indirect impacts and properly attributing disruptiveness when simultaneous discoveries happen. Addressing these challenges, we introduce an embedding-based measure of disruptiveness (EDM). Conceptually, EDM estimates how far the “future” of a focal study (represented by the embeddings of the papers that cite the focal study) is from the “past” (the embeddings of the references of the focal study). By quantifying the distance between these two representations (denoted as ∆ here), EDM captures the extent to which the subsequent scientific trajectory from the focal paper departs from prior work (see Fig. 1A). This approach not only leverages non-local, multi-step patterns in the citation network to capture a broader and richer spectrum of transformative influence, but also remains robust to noise while maintaining high resolution. Applying EDM to more than 50 million scientific publications, we demonstrate that the measure substantially improves upon a widely used local-structure-based indicator, the disruption index [1](denoted as D). First, EDM more effectively identifies canonical landmark contributions, including Nobel Prize–winning papers (Fig. 1B), primarily by being robust against the impact of structural artifacts such as simultaneous discovery or multi-part contributions (Fig. 1C). Second, EDM provides substantially higher resolution than the disruption index, which exhibits pronounced degeneracy from its reliance on paper counts. Third, the learned embedding space enables the identification of simultaneous discovery pairs, which tend to have similar future vectors (cited similarly). Using APS (American Physical Society) data, we identify approximately 50 such pairs, illustrating how shared future trajectories can reveal common underlying contributions that are difficult to detect using local measures alone (Fig. 1D). Together, these results demonstrate that EDM offers a quantitative framework for capturing transformative scientific influence by exploiting higher-order information encoded in the citation network.
items.authors: Kim, Munjung; Kojaku, Sadamori; Ahn, Yong-Yeol*

items.title: Robustness & Generalization of Uncertainty-Aware Message Passing Neural Networks
items.abstract: Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) are the central architecture in graph machine learning. Numerous studies have characterized their theoretical properties, such as expressivity and generalization. A central assumption in previous work is that node features and network structure are known with absolute certainty. However, in real-world applications, node features and network structure may be uncertain due to noise, finite measurement precision, or coarse-graining. Here, we address uncertainty in node features in the context of MPNNs [1]. First, we show how uncertainty in input node features translates into uncertainty in node embeddings and downstream predictions by adapting moment-propagation techniques to MPNNs. Second, we use the moments of the node embedding distribution to establish a node-wise certified adversarial robustness radii in L2-norm. Specifically, we compute a ball in feature space around each node such that, for feature perturbations within a ball of this radius, the node will still be correctly classified with a fixed probability. Finally, we establish generalization bounds for the Simplified Graph Convolution (SGC) architecture on inductive node-level tasks by defining a new optimal transport-based pseudo-metric, the Feature Convolution Distance (FCDp). SGC is an MPNN without nonlinear activation functions between hidden layers. FCD_p is defined in the space of nodes of attributed graphs. This distance allows us to extend existing results on covering number-based generalization bounds [2] for MPNNs to the case of uncertain node features. Our result bounds the maximal difference between the empirical loss distribution on a given dataset and the population loss distribution under the data-generating process in terms of properties of the sample space and the architecture. In all three cases—moment propagation, robustness, and generalization—we validate our theoretical contributions with experiments on seven real-world networks and synthetic data with a given noise-correlation structure. For moment propagation, we find that, when considering approximation error and computational efficiency, different methods are preferable depending on network size. For robustness, we find that we can establish certificates for a majority of nodes in the network, but these radii can be small compared to the size of adversarial attacks necessary to induce node misclassification, meaning the method tends to underestimate the robustness of MPNNs. For generalization, we show that FCD_1 in the space of nodes correlates strongly with the distance in embedding space and that SGC is Lipschitz continuous in this distance. In addition, we empirically test the tightness of the generalization bound. Together, these findings show that a proper treatment of node feature uncertainty in graph machine learning requires new theoretical tools but provides insights into model robustness and guarantees for within-distribution performance. [1] Chernikova A. et al. (2025) Uncertainty-Aware Message Passing Neural Networks; NPGML NeurIPS 2025 [2] Vasileiou A. et al. (2025) Survey on Generalization Theory for Graph Neural Networks; arXiv:2503.15650
items.authors: Chernikova, Alesia; Laber, Moritz*; Sabhahit, Narayan; Eliassi-Rad, Tina

items.title: Universal expansion of human mobility across urban scales
items.abstract: Human mobility reflects interactions between individuals and their environments, yet the influence of egocentric preferences—particularly home location—remains poorly understood. Using millions of anonymized mobile phone trajectories from multiple countries and demographic groups, we develop a network-based framework that decomposes individual mobility into spatial modules. We uncover a universal scaling relationship, the inflation law, in which the module radius increases sublinearly with distance from home, $r_c \sim d_c^\kappa$, consistently across regions and populations. Analysis of movements within and between modules reveals heterogeneous mobility dynamics, challenging assumptions of uniform movement behavior. Incorporating a biologically inspired switch mechanism, our model reproduces both the modular network structure and observed spatial patterns. These findings highlight the central role of home-centered constraints in shaping mobility and have implications for urban planning, traffic prediction, and disease modeling.
items.authors: Zhong, Lu*

items.title: Hypergraph reconstruction from dynamics
items.abstract: A plethora of methods have been developed in the past two decades to infer the underlying network structure of an interconnected system from its collective dynamics. However, methods capable of inferring nonpairwise interactions are only starting to appear. Here, we develop an inference algorithm based on sparse identification of nonlinear dynamics (SINDy) to reconstruct hypergraphs and simplicial complexes from time-series data. Our model-free method does not require information about node dynamics or coupling functions, making it applicable to complex systems that do not have a reliable mathematical description. We first benchmark the new method on synthetic data generated from Kuramoto and Lorenz dynamics. We then use it to infer the effective connectivity in the brain from resting-state EEG data, which reveals significant contributions from non-pairwise interactions in shaping the macroscopic brain dynamics.
items.authors: Zhang, Yuanzhao*

items.title: Controlling the spread of deception-based cyber-threats on time-varying networks
items.abstract: Deception-based attacks such as phishing, baiting, and file masquerading have become one of the most diffuse types of cyber-threats. These are designed around ingenious strategies that target human nature. For example, the classic phishing scheme tries, via trusted access, to lure victims to open a malicious link and/or to download a malware which might affect personal accounts and devices, reveal sensitive data, and, unbeknownst to the victims, allow the threat to spread further. The literature devoted at modelling these processes and gathering insights to contrast them is vast, but presents two main limitations. First, most of previous work neglects the temporal nature of online contact patterns focusing instead on aggregated networks. Second, most of the literature assumes users to be equally susceptible (i.e., gullible). Here, we study the efficacy of strategies aimed at controlling the spread of deception-based cyber-threats unfolding on online social networks. We model directed and temporal interactions between users using a family of activity-driven networks featuring different gullibility classes and tunable homophily levels among them. We simulate the spreading of cyber-threats using classic Susceptible-Infected-Susceptible (SIS) models. We explore and quantify the effectiveness of four control strategies. Akin to vaccination campaigns with a limited budget, each strategy selects a fraction of nodes with the aim to increase their awareness and provide protection from cyber-threats. The first strategy picks nodes randomly. The second assumes global knowledge of the system selecting nodes based on their activity. The third picks nodes via egocentric sampling by using the connections of a fraction of nodes that act as probes. The fourth selects nodes based on the outcome of standard security awareness tests (SAT), customarily used by institutions to probe, estimate, and raise the awareness of their workforce. We quantify the impact of each strategy by deriving analytically how they affect the spreading threshold. Analytical expressions are validated via large-scale numerical simulations (see Figure 1). Interestingly, we find that targeted strategies, focusing on key features of the population such as the activity, are extremely effective. Egocentric sampling strategies, though not as effective, emerge as clear second best despite not assuming any knowledge about the system. In addition, we find that networks characterized by highly homophilic interactions linked to gullibility might expand the range of transmissibility parameters that allows for macroscopic outbreaks. At the same time, they reduce the reach of these spreading events. Hence, rather isolated patches of the network formed by highly gullible individuals might provide fertile grounds for the propagation and survival of cyber-threats.
items.authors: Perra, Nicola*; Gozzi, Nicolo

items.title: Bounded-confidence models with two-state node activations
items.abstract: Please see attached pdf.
items.authors: Yu, Shilong; Chu, Weiqi; Masuda, Naoki*

items.title: Detecting Global Trade Vulnerabilities via Graph Neural Networks
items.abstract: The past century has witnessed an unprecedented globalization of the world economy, binding nations together through intricate commercial relationships. The destruction of trading ties -- whether due to accidents, conflicts, or policy changes -- can negatively impact the livelihood of millions of people, often significantly so. Understanding precisely how trade disruptions spread and which nations stand most vulnerable can provide valuable insights for policymakers aiming to protect their population from economic disasters. Traditional macroeconomic tools -- while useful for broad economic assessments -- are ill-suited to describe the systemic vulnerabilities lurking inside complex systems -- such as global trade. Complex systems exhibit behaviors that cannot be explained solely by analyzing their individual parts in isolation. We represent international trade as a complex network, to enhance the prediction of economic vulnerabilities. Specifically, we frame the problem as a node classification issue: given a network representing exports (edges) by countries (nodes), can we label a country as either "safe" or "at risk'"? With this framing, we can sketch an approach by deploying graph neural networks (GNN). We choose to use GNNs because they excel in the node classification task. We enrich the representation with a number of economically-relevant attributes on both nodes and edges, creating node-attributed multilayer directed networks, where layers represent exports in different commodities. The node and edge features we consider include: the economic complexity [1] of both the countries and the products, the geographical location of the countries, trade agreements, corruption levels, and more. For ablation purposes, we also make versions of this network without edge direction and represented as multi-graph instead of multilayer, to test how performance varies (Figure 1(a)). For the research question "Given the trade links a country has, what is the likelihood they will experience a trade disruption in a specific commodity class this year?" we define a disruption if an importer either stops buying a product or is paying more to get the same amount of that product. This is a hard question to answer, because disruptions are not necessarily embedded in the network -- like wars, natural disasters, and so on. Figure 1(b) nonetheless shows we can not only outperform a random classifier, but also a neural network that does not take the topology of the trade network into account. This advances the hypothesis that such trade disruptions might leave early warning signs in the choices countries make for their trading partners. The easier question "Given the trade links a country has, what is the likelihood they will experience any trade disruption this year?" provides us with more training data and therefore higher predictive power (Figure 1(c)). Also in this case, we can outperform both random expectation a neural network classifiers. While only a starting point, our work provides an interesting avenue to estimate the vulnerability of global trade, by using our prediction scores to create a global trade risk outlook. Such a risk score is meaningful, as we can see traces of real world events in it. With the Russo-Ukrainian war as a test case, we see that the countries we label as "high risk" are much more likely to be affected than the low risk countries (27.5% vs 4% for selected products highly dependent on Russia as an exporter). We can also see that the countries we predicted to be at risk but not being affected are those who are defying international sanctions, which validates our method as it shows the reasons for the failed predictions. In future work, we can expand our method by including more features such as conflicts, natural disasters, shared history (colonial or cultural similarities) and more. [1] Hausmann, Ricardo, César A. Hidalgo, Sebastián Bustos, Michele Coscia, and Alexander Simoes. The atlas of economic complexity: Mapping paths to prosperity. Mit Press, 2014.
items.authors: Fazzi, Gino Franco; Coscia, Michele*

items.title: Measuring the Scapegoat Mechanism in Temporal Online Networks via Concentration and Relinking Signatures
items.abstract: Scapegoating episodes on social media often combine abrupt attention concentration on a target with rapid reorganization of interaction patterns. Yet scapegoating is rarely operationalized in a way that is both measurable in temporal networks and comparable to mechanistic models. We present a mixed-methods protocol that bridges mimetic crisis theory and network science by (i) extracting a compact set of temporal “bridge metrics” from real online interaction networks and (ii) validating these signatures against a parsimonious small-world agent-based model (ABM). We analyze four Brazilian cancellation episodes on X/Twitter (2017–2025) and construct directed temporal interaction graphs from retweets, replies, quotes, and mentions. Edges are labeled by stance (e.g., accuser vs. skeptic/defender) to quantify segregation and cross-community overflow. We compute concentration and power-structure metrics including engagement Gini (per user and per tweet), in-degree and Freeman centralization, assortativity by stance, peak indicators (e.g., peak/median), top- 𝑘 k participation shares, and distance-to-victim measures that capture isolation residues. In parallel, we implement an ABM on a small-world topology in which collective tension grows endogenously, leaders emerge contingently, victims and substitute victims are selected, and a “rite” temporarily relieves tension while rewiring ties; we also vary the proportion of skeptic agents. To compare empirical and synthetic dynamics, we align empirical time series at the event peak and ABM series at the rite tick, and evaluate similarity across time-series trajectories and distributional summaries. Across cases, empirical networks exhibit star-like concentration profiles characterized by simultaneous increases in inequality (Gini) and centralization, consistent with focal convergence on a target. The ABM reproduces the qualitative regime shifts observed in the data—pre-rite multi-community structures followed by post-rite re-centralization—and skeptic agents systematically dampen spread and reduce stance assortativity. These results suggest that scapegoating is detectable through robust temporal signatures combining concentration and relinking/isolation patterns, enabling reproducible measurement and supporting early-warning monitoring rules for online harassment dynamics.
items.authors: Paes da Silva, Carlos*

items.title: How our bones build networks: interplay between architecture and growth
items.abstract: Many biological tissues are permeated by a physical network of fluid-filled channels that facilitates not only transport but also mediates signaling [1] and mechanosensation [2]. In bone, this network – known as lacunocanalicular network (LCN) (Fig. 1a) consists of lacunae, which serve as hubs (Fig. 1b), and canaliculi, narrow channels connecting them. This porosity network accommodates the osteocyte network (cell bodies and their dendrites). Characteristics of the osteocyte network are similar to the neural network of our brains: one cubic centimeter of human bone comprises 74 kilometers of canaliculi [3]. Our research focus is on human osteons - cylindrical building blocks of bone with a diameter of approximately 300 µm (Fig. 1a). During our lifetime, osteons are constantly formed: a cylindrical cavity is first created, then progressively filled by bone-forming cells, which deposit concentric layers of bone lamellae (Fig. 1d). The process terminates leaving a central canal, which remains open to house a blood vessel for nutrient supply. During osteon closure, some of the bone-forming cells get embedded into the bone matrix, while maintaining connections with cells at the bone surface, becoming osteocytes and thereby building up a network structure. Given the variability in the shape of osteons, we ask: How can bone formation velocity influence the resulting network architecture? Can we understand the network formation dynamics under the hypothesis that the existing network structure guides the embedding of new bone cells? We have developed an experimental workflow that transforms three-dimensional images of the LCN, acquired with confocal microscopy, into a spatial network graph consisting of tens of thousands of nodes and edges. Using the custom-built software TINA [3] we quantify network architecture in terms of density and spatial heterogeneity. Our analysis of human osteons with non-circular shape (Fig. 1c, d) revealed that regions with higher bone deposition (typically along the long axis of ellipsoidal geometries) exhibit denser network (compared to regions along the short axis). To explore the dynamics of bone deposition, we developed a mathematical model of network growth within an osteon. This model simulates the embedment of a bone-forming cell, becoming an osteocyte - i.e. creating a new hub in the network, under different hypotheses. Notably, when we assume that bone-forming cells with the weakest connectivity to the already existing network are preferentially becoming new network hubs, the model generates network architectures resembling those observed in experimental data. Our analysis of the bone network structure suggests that cells carry information about their position during their embedment. This enables them to actively adapt the network architecture to ensure sufficient connectivity to the source of nutrients in the central canal. Combined with the result from our model that the existing network guides the embedding of new cells, these findings indicate a strong dynamic interplay between network architecture and growth. [1] Mehrpooya, A., Challis, V.J. and Buenzli, P.R., 2024, Proceedings A 480, 20230906. [2] Van Tol, A.F., Schemenz, V., …, Willie, B.M. and Weinkamer, R., 2020, PNAS 117, 32251. [3] Repp, F., Kollmannsberger, …, Wagermaier, W. and Weinkamer, R., 2017, Bone Reports 6, 101.
items.authors: Tits, Alexandra; Fenwick, Jack; Buenzli, Pascal; Weinkamer, Richard*

items.title: Knowledge-Based Gene Set Intersection Graphs Reveal Functional Master Regulators by Measures of Centrality and Self-Similarity
items.abstract: Our current study leverages computational and network-science methods to reveal molecular insights into gene function by analyzing knowledge-based networks derived from biomedical literature. Specifically, we aimed to determine how individual gene functions can be inferred based on their topological integration into complex networks constructed from curated gene signatures reported in approximately 4,000 scientific publications (MSigDB C2: canonical pathways, chemical and genetic perturbations) [1,2]. Using Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) [3] topic modeling, we identified 27 distinct biomedical contexts, including prominent themes such as 'molecular cancer gene signatures', 'Wnt and TGFβ signaling pathways', and 'hematopoiesis and leukemogenesis'. Gene sets corresponding to each biomedical context were transformed into complex intersection-based networks, where modularity analyses and functional annotation uncovered biologically meaningful clusters. Comparative topological analyses across all networks revealed universal connectivity patterns linked to fundamental cellular processes, alongside specialized network configurations that reflect unique biomedical contexts. Centrality [4] and local self-similarity [5] measures were applied to pinpoint genes crucial to network structure and functionality. Perturbation analyses (network rewiring simulations) further assessed the reliability of these topological metrics, particularly highlighting understudied genes with potentially significant biological roles. We developed a user-friendly web application for interactive exploration of these networks. By connecting computational biology and network theory, our approach demonstrates how network topology can illuminate gene function and regulation in complex biological systems, contributing valuable tools and insights to the NetSci2026.
items.authors: Hause, Frank*

items.title: Intangible Atmospheres: Sonic Measurement and the Limits of Network Visualization in Urban Systems.
items.abstract: Network science relies heavily on visualization and measurement practices that assume relational structures can be rendered visible without substantial epistemic loss. This assumption has enabled powerful analytical methods, yet it becomes increasingly unstable in urban contexts where networks are structured by intangible forms of information such as latent variables, probabilistic relations, inferred dependencies, and temporally unstable states. In these settings, networks do not exist as fixed relational objects but as continuously computed and updated processes. This contributed talk examines how standard visualization and measurement techniques systematically mischaracterize such systems. Drawing on examples from urban sensor networks, algorithmic governance infrastructures, and probabilistic mobility models, the presentation distinguishes between networks that are difficult to visualize due to scale or density and networks that are structurally resistant to visualization because their core relations exceed stable spatial and visual embedding. As Newman notes, networks are abstractions that “strip away all but the most essential information,” a strength that becomes a limitation when essential dynamics are precisely what cannot be directly observed. Our analysis of noise sensing networks develops a typology of intangible properties including latentness, uncertainty, temporal drift, and inferential density, and shows how common techniques such as force directed layouts, modularity maximization, stress optimization, and planarity assumptions suppress these dynamics in favor of visually stable structures. In these cases, visualization substitutes legibility for structure, equating what can be rendered with what exists. To address this limitation, the presentation introduces sound as an alternative mode of network measurement. Sonic mappings encode temporal variation, uncertainty, and delay as time based signals, enabling relational dynamics that resist spatial embedding to be measured rather than visualized. Grounded in this perspective, our talk reframes network visualization as one instrument among others rather than a neutral representational surface. Rather than asking how to better visualize intangible networks, it asks what different sensing modalities are actually measuring and what they systematically leave behind. Following Kitchin’s argument that data infrastructures do not simply represent the city but actively “produce new forms of urban reality,” the presentation proposes strategies for making the limits of representation perceptible, including uncertainty encoding, distortion mapping, indicators of dimensional overflow, and temporal sonification. By analyzing noise sensing networks and their representation we aim to foreground the gap between computation, representation, and interpretation; this work contributes to network science by offering a framework for analyzing urban networks whose dynamics remain intangible and probabilistic.
items.authors: Martin Rivero , Paula *; Kurakin, Stanislav

items.title: Mapping the Bureaucratic Topology of Urban Data Access
items.abstract: The transition from Kevin Lynch's "legible city" (where urban form is cognitively and directly readable by inhabitants) to the "accessible city" of platform urbanism marks a fundamental epistemic shift in how urban knowledge is produced and distributed (Hamilton et al., 2014). Where Lynch's city waited to be read through embodied navigation, the smart city waits to be accessed through APIs, datasets, and remote sensing infrastructures. Yet this accessibility is unevenly distributed: while open data initiatives promise transparency, significant layers of urban information remain locked behind institutional, technical, and bureaucratic barriers. Data accessibility therefore emerges not as a binary condition but as a stratified privilege, structured by technological literacy, institutional position, security concerns and economic capacity. This raises a critical question for network science: if urban systems are increasingly mediated by data flows, what are the topological properties of the networks that govern access to those flows? This research investigates the network structure of data accessibility by treating the bureaucratic process of requesting urban datasets as itself a network to be mapped. Using Grand Paris open data portal as a starting point, we identify datasets relevant to urban design and development, then systematically contact stakeholders through email (such as municipal departments, private operators, state agencies) to request access to more granular or complete information layers. Over a two-month period, these email exchanges will generate a relational dataset: each request, response, referral, and non-response constitutes an edge in an emergent network of data governance. We analyse this network using standard centrality measures (degree, betweenness, closeness) to identify institutional gatekeepers; response time distributions to characterise bureaucratic friction; and referral chain analysis to map the topology of data silos. Qualitative coding of response content (justification types, tone, alternative pathways offered) supplements the quantitative structure. The resulting network visualisation represents what we call a "bureaucratic topology" (graph structure that reveals how urban data access is institutionally mediated), where bottlenecks occur and which actors occupy positions of informational control. Preliminary analysis suggests that data governance networks exhibit high clustering (siloed departments) combined with low global connectivity (few cross-institutional pathways), creating structural barriers to integrated urban practices. The governance of urban data infrastructures has typically been studied through qualitative policy analysis. This research proposes a methodological inversion: rather than analysing urban datasets, we analyse the institutional process of requesting them, treating beaurocratic correspondance as a primary data source. This action-based research approach generates a network that would otherwise remain invisible, one that no existing dataset documents. By applying graph methods to this emergent structure, the methodology enables quantitative comparison of data governance across cities, sectors and institutional contexts, offering a scalable and replicable frameworks for studying platform urbanism's hidden architecture. The work contributes to network science by demonstrating how network analysis can render legible the very systems that determine what urban information becomes accessible and how.
items.authors: KURAKIN, Stanislav*; Martin Rivero, Paula

items.title: Belief Coevolution in a Social Network of Generalist and Specialist Large Language Models
items.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) now generate a significant portion of online content. AI-generated text appears in official press releases, scientific articles, and social knowledge-sharing platforms. As LLMs become more accessible, they are increasingly used in multi-agent settings alongside humans, where collaboration occurs through sustained multi-turn interactions. This environment creates an ongoing feedback loop in which LLMs shape users’ beliefs while simultaneously adapting to user-generated content. However, we still lack a systematic understanding of how LLMs form and propagate beliefs. Moreover, existing research on interactions between LLMs focuses primarily on behavioral proxies (e.g., decisions LLMs make in collaborative tasks) rather than the underlying belief states that drive these behaviors. Additionally, experiments often examine settings where LLM agents interact in pairs or share information globally, overlooking network-mediated scenarios in which agents interact only with immediate neighbors. Belief dynamics are further complicated by the heterogeneity of LLMs’ expertise. Some LLMs are finetuned on domain-specific corpora (e.g., biomedical literature, cybersecurity reports), while others are optimized for particular capabilities (e.g., storytelling or coding). When such diverse agents interact within a social network, their expertise and social roles (how LLMs present themselves to others) create a complex interactive environment. We propose CoevolveSim, an agent-based framework for simulating the coevolution of beliefs in a social network composed of LLMs. At each iteration, an LLM agent receives a summary of its neighbors’ beliefs along with their social roles. The agent then updates its belief based on local information and broadcasts the updated belief to its neighbors in the next round. CoevolveSim disentangles the effects of three factors: (1) expertise: domain knowledge embedded through finetuning, (2) social roles: identity labels that signal credibility to neighbors, and (3) network structure: who interacts with whom. Using both global and local measures of influence, we identify conditions under which belief diffusion leads to (1) stable convergence, (2) sharp phase-transition-like cascades, or (3) fragmented disagreement. Our preliminary results on 16 generalist and specialist LLMs connected by Erdős–Rényi graphs show that generalist LLMs without finetuning but with social roles exhibit rapid belief convergence toward consensus, while specialist LLMs with finetuning and corresponding social roles resist updating beliefs, allowing errors to persist throughout interactions.
items.authors: Savcisens, Germans*; Dies, Samantha; Maynard, Courtney; Eliassi-Rad, Tina

items.title: When AI Becomes Urban Compass: Socio-Spatial Biases in LLM-based Place Recommendations
items.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly becoming everyday interfaces for decision-making, from choosing restaurants to planning trips. As these systems begin to mediate human interactions within urban networks, their biases may inadvertently reshape socio-spatial patterns. In this project, we present a systematic analysis of representation and demographic biases in LLM-based place recommendations. We prompt an LLM to generate restaurant recommendations across neighborhoods in different cities, including Boston and New York. For each query, we simulate realistic user profiles by sampling synthetic individuals from census data, each defined by a home location, age, sex, and household income. We conduct two complementary analyses: (1) Neighborhood-level analysis: We examine how match rates vary across urban areas with distinct socio-demographic compositions. Our results show that LLMs produce uneven match rates across neighborhoods, favoring areas with higher population density, larger proportions bachelor-degree residents, smaller proportions of black residents, and better walkability. These findings suggest that the LLM’s “mental map” of the city is biased toward well-documented, affluent, and digitally visible areas. (2) Demographic-level analysis: We examine how recommendations differ for users of different income, age, and gender groups, controlling for spatial location. Our results reveal that low-income users are steered toward low-spending, high-frequency, crowded places, whereas female users are directed slightly farther and to more frequented places. This reflects the inherent biases carried by LLMs, potentially reinforcing existing biases in urban space. By revealing how representation and demographic biases are interwoven in LLM-based place recommendations, we offer a new lens for understanding algorithmic mediation of urban life. As LLMs become increasingly embedded in human mobility and consumption, their internal representations may become a hidden layer of the city’s socio-spatial network, subtly steering where different groups go and what kinds of places they discover. Such biased mediation can potentially erode the network fabric, reinforcing inequalities through social segregation and uneven local business survival.
items.authors: Chen, Lin*; Moro, Esteban

items.title: Wikipedia and AI for Mapping Cultural Diaspora
items.abstract: Cultural diaspora emerges from long-term patterns of human mobility and institutional opportunity [1]. While prior work has examined cultural diffusion using historical datasets [2], much relevant information remains in unstructured sources. In this exploratory study, we structure data from public web sources using an AI-based pipeline to construct and analyze mobility networks to map cultural diaspora. Our goal is to assess whether AI tools can structure text data and classify entities in domain-specific fields to support systematic studies of how migration contributes to the globalization of cultural expressions. In particular, we use Wikipedia as a global knowledge repository that encodes historical information in a relatively standardized format. We use classical ballet as a case study because it is a historically rich and highly transnational performing art, with well-documented patterns of migration and artistic production that lend themselves to network analysis. Beyond data availability, ballet is a remarkable example of a dance form that evolved from an elite European tradition into a culturally mainstream global art, despite the wide diversity of dance practices worldwide. We scrape data from 518 ballet titles listed on the English Wikipedia’s ‘List of ballets by title’ (accessed March 15, 2024 [3]). To structure the data, we input the text body of Wikipedia ballet entries into GPT-based models via the OpenAI API. In specific, we use prompt templates that instruct the GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 models to identify named artists, creative roles, countries of activity, and production-related attributes from each Wikipedia entry and to output these fields in a fixed tabular format, which are then compiled into a structured CSV file and evaluated against a manually verified subset of entries. Through this process, we extracted 1,042 artist names spanning multiple creative roles (27% composers, 22% choreographers, 19% designers, 12% muses, 7% librettists, 2% regisseurs, and 11% unspecified). The resulting structured data enabled the construction of the geography-based artists network (Fig. 1a). The historical trajectory of ballet production highlights the migration of ballet’s creative center from France and Italy to Russia, Britain, and ultimately the United States, which currently dominates ballet production alongside increasing activity in other regions (Fig. 1b). Through network metrics, we identify a small set of globally connected artists that mediate a large fraction of transnational creative flows, sustaining ballet as a globally connected art form. Moreover, Wikipedia correctly captures canonical figures such as Igor Stravinsky and George Balanchine as high-centrality nodes, reflecting their roles in transferring artistic practices from Eastern Europe to the United States. This work demonstrates that narrative public web data, when structured with AI and combined with network analysis, can recover meaningful patterns of creative mobility and support large-scale studies of cultural diaspora across artistic fields.
items.authors: Herrera Guzman, Yessica*; Peng, You; Lamba, Manika

items.title: Investigating the Drivers of Information Source Popularity in Telegram Communities Through Forwarding Networks
items.abstract: The messaging app Telegram has become a popular digital space for information sharing outside of large social media platforms [1, 3]. In particular, Telegram’s channel function enables one-to-many broadcasting to hundreds of thousands of platform users [2], while message forwarding allows channels to exchange information. Forwarding between channels is frequent, accounting for a fifth to a third of all messages in public channels on average [1, 2]. Such forwards more frequently contain external links than non-forwarded messages, emphasizing Telegram’s role as an information hub [2]. Understanding how different information sources achieve visibility is fundamental to characterizing the information ecosystem of digital platforms. However, conventional approaches of measuring popularity rely on aggregate metrics such as the frequencies or total views of sources [3], which can mask fundamentally different diffusion mechanisms. We demonstrate that the combined analysis of network topology and engagement metrics reveals distinct pathways to visibility that engagement metrics alone cannot capture. Using message data from German and English public Telegram channels in the publicly available TeleScope dataset [1], we construct domain-specific forwarding networks where nodes represent channels and directed edges represent forwarding relationships. We identify distinct models of source popularity that differ in both network topology and forwarding behavior. Low-amplification reference patterns show frequent sharing but low to moderate forwarding, such as the British outlet ‘independent.co.uk’ (2% forwards), the German outlet ‘spiegel.de’ (6% forwards), or the US outlet ‘nypost.com’ (15% forwards). Hub-centric amplification sources achieve visibility through concentrated networks with moderate to high forwarding rates. For the US outlet ‘thegatewaypundit.com’ (31% forwards), a single channel originates 16% of all forwards and accounts for 42% of all views. For the German outlet ‘auf1.tv’ (Figure 1, left; 54% forwards), 41% of all forwards originate from one channel accounting for 83% of all views. Multi-community utility sources show distributed forwarding across many channels. For ‘youtube.com’ (Figure 1, right; 24% forwards), the top forwarding channel accounts for only 1% of forwards and the most popular channel for 5% of all views. These topological differences reflect platform engagement strategies: the outlet ‘auf1.tv’ actively leverages Telegram for distribution, while ‘youtube.com’ is passively referenced as an external information hub. Ultimately, our findings advance the empirical understanding of how Telegram is shaped as a platform within the digital information ecosystem. [1] Susmita Gangopadhyay et al. “TeleScope A Longitudinal Dataset for Investigating Online Discourse and Information Interaction on Telegram”. In: Proceedings of the ICWSM (June 2025). DOI: 10.1609/icwsm.v19i1.35945. [2] Mohamad Hoseini et al. “Characterizing Information Propagation in Fringe Communities on Telegram”. In: Proceedings of the ICWSM (May 2024). DOI: 10.1609/icwsm.v18i1.31336. [3] Silvan Wehrli et al. “Information Pathways and Voids in Critical German Online Communities During the COVID-19 Vaccination Discourse: Cross-Platform and Mixed Methods Analysis”. In: JMIR (Oct. 2025). DOI: 10.2196/76309.
items.authors: Wehrli, Silvan*; Irrgang, Christopher

items.title: Topological data analysis of multiplex Markov chains encoding brain activity across frequency bands
items.abstract: Alzheimer's Disease (AD), the most common of dementia, affected millions of people worldwide is becoming the burdensome not just for individual but for the whole society. In our work, we utilized tools from topological data analysis visualized through persistent barcodes or Persistence Diagrams to automate the detection and summary of convection cycles in Alzheimer's dataset that can arise for irreversible Multiplex Markov chains (MMCs) constructed by coupling together sets of Markov chains (MCs). Specifically, we place the TDA-interpolation through the total imbalance of induced network that is described as a single-layer net to explore the homological varieties. Our pathway had been proven as a robust approach in distinguishing normal people and AD patients via topological toolsets as well as a relaxation in computational cost by playing with just one-layer network. Alzheimer's disease, as well as the others progressive neuro-degenerative disease, is increasingly received high attention via various approaches. Among these methods, considering the corresponding models under the prism of multilayer network is carved through a bunch of recent deployments. However, combining between Topological Data Analysis (TDA) and Multiplex Markov chains (MMCs) is still unveiled. Our article will discover the structural changes in multiscale way and compare the differences in homological characteristics between the normal person group and Alzheimer's group. The survey is not just to make the visualization hinging on varying of coupling strength but also the time-steps for convergence. Furthermore, the birth- and the death-time of these groups will be emphasized via the Bottleneck distance, a critical measurement in computational topology. Our TDA-assisted approach makes a promise for opening a bunch of potential developments in combining various disciplines. Notably, our approach convinces the usefulness of the utilization of PH into portraying the structural changes in a MMCs via quantifying the total imbalance of networks. It not just unifies unprecedentedly these different disciplinary into the specific application, but also tackling the challenge of the computational cost as facing with a large number of nodes scattering into various layers.
items.authors: LE, MINH*

items.title: Community Detection in Gene Co-methylation Networks Reveals Modules Associated with Human Aging
items.abstract: DNA methylation patterns provide a powerful window into the biological aging process. In this work, we aim to identify genes and pathways associated with aging by constructing gene-level networks from human source DNA methylation data from 282 samples. We use k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) and linear correlation thresholding to construct the networks and apply Leiden community detection to recover gene modules. We then characterize the functional pathways of these modules and systematically compare the findings with those obtained from Weighted Gene Co-expression Network Analysis (WGCNA). Figure 1 demonstrates the pipeline implemented (top panel) and the results of this analysis (bottom panel). We begin with the normalized human blood methylation matrix and construct interpretable gene-level features by grouping CpGs by genes and extracting each gene’s first principal component. Using this gene-level representation, we implement two methods of constructing networks--kNN and linear thresholding. Leiden is then applied to identify modules in these networks; this is a flexible multi-scale framework: kNN allows tuning via k, linear thresholding via the correlation cutoff, and Leiden via its resolution parameter, enabling exploration of modular structure across scales. We identify age-associated gene modules by correlating module eigengenes with chronological age, followed by Gene Ontology (GO) and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) enrichment analyses across all three methods. The kNN-based approach reveals enrichment in pathways related to transcriptional regulation, neuronal differentiation, ion transport, and cardiomyopathy signaling, including Wnt, Hippo, and adrenergic pathways. The linear-thresholding method emphasizes neural development, neuron projection morphogenesis, and major signaling cascades such as mTOR, Wnt, and Hippo. In contrast, WGCNA identifies broader enrichments in RNA processing, synaptic signaling, and neurodegenerative pathways, such as MAP kinase signaling and transcription misregulation in cancer. Overall, integrating kNN and linear-threshold networks with Leiden yields a robust and adaptable paradigm for dissecting the modular architecture, pathway organization, and functional complexity of the aging methylome beyond the constraints of WGCNA.
items.authors: Sadat Fatemi Nasrollahi, Fatemeh; Vila Miñana, Meritxell*; Beshkar, Nikki; DeMeo, Dawn; Fortunato, Santo

items.title: Real-Space Renormalization and Sparsification of Complex Networks
items.abstract: Graph sparsification and renormalization are fundamental tools for reducing the complexity of networks and network models, both to make them more amenable to numerical simulation and to reveal how macroscopic behavior scales with system size [1]. Existing renormalization schemes rely either on heuristic blocking, which yields poorly controlled approximations, or on spectral constructions, which can show poor performance on heterogeneous networks. We introduce a general real-space sparsification and renormalization framework based on message passing, which provides a recipe for performing local surgery on networks---removal of nodes or edges---while preserving the values of physically meaningful quantities. The method can be applied to any network process that can be represented using message passing. As examples, we apply the approach to edge percolation and to the solution of the Ising model at finite temperature. We also show that when our method is applied to the calculation of effective conductance in networks, the well-known Spielman–Teng spectral sparsifier [2] emerges as a limiting case. [1] Andrea Gabrielli et al. Network renormalization. Preprint arxiv:2412.12988. 2024. [2] Daniel A. Spielman and Shang-Hua Teng. “Spectral sparsification of graphs”. In: SIAM J. Comput. 40 (2011), pp. 981–1025
items.authors: Sheeran, Cornelia*; Newman, Mark

items.title: Decolonizing Network Theory in Ecology and Sustainability
items.abstract: Ecology studies organisms interacting within their environments. The sustainability of these systems—including humans—depends on complex networks of interactions that continually regenerate the conditions for life. Yet European colonization and its legacies1 continue to destroy the sustainability of vast numbers of species and peoples through habitat annihilation, genocidal extraction, and the industrial disruption of the carbon and nitrogen cycles that sustain all life. The resulting crises—from climate change and biodiversity loss to poverty and political upheaval—have elevated ecological network theory to a central role in sustainability science. Yet this theory’s dominant forms remain deeply compromised by their blindness to their own role in producing these crises. Ecological and sustainability theory—including its most influential network formalisms—was forged in service to exploration, extraction, and imperial governance, promoting ideologies of scarcity, domination, and control. Core concepts such as logistic growth (Fig. 1), carrying capacity (K, Fig. 1), overpopulation,2 competition,3 and predation3 enshrined zero-sum logics while obscuring equally fundamental processes by which organisms actively enhance the capacities of environments to support life. From cyanobacteria’s oxygenation of the planet and plant–fungal mutualisms that built terrestrial ecosystems to the ongoing CO₂–O₂ cycling among plants and animals, provision, facilitation, and reciprocity are not peripheral—they are structurally central. Yet ecological network theory marginalizes these processes, mirroring the marginalization of non-Europeans and their knowledge from the construction of theory itself. Darwin summarized the roots of this framing and its framers when he predicted that “the civilized races of man will almost certainly exterminate, and replace, the savage races throughout the world …as we may hope.”4 This embrace of domination and competitive exclusion including White supremacy was seamlessly extended from European empires to all of nature, entrenching hierarchy, violence, and genocidal exclusion as ecological law. Network theory formalizes these extensions most pointedly by embracing logistic growth—and especially its fiction of carrying capacity (K, Fig. 1)—as a foundation for competition3 and random matrix theories5 of stability and neo-Malthusian theories of sustainability2 that deflect responsibility from colonial economies to impoverished colonized populations—explicitly “blaming the victim” for colonial plundering.1 Rather than fully decolonizing ecological theory, this talk identifies specific network-theoretic paradigms where colonial constructions of organisms, interactions, and networks formed by these nodes and links continue to cripple predictive understanding—and points toward more mechanistically grounded alternatives. First, I show how the logistic growth equation contrives scarcity and rigid limits as default conditions for organisms’ existence. Second, I revisit two-species competition and coexistence theories3 and contrast their definitional instabilities and empirical failures of phenomenological interactions with consumer-resource and nutrient regeneration mechanisms that capture a far fuller range of ecological interactions. Finally, I turn to whole-system stability, where classic network theory holds that competition, self-limitation, and simplicity stabilize ecosystems, whereas mutualism, diversity, and complexity destabilize them.3,5 I then show how mechanistic trophic and biogeochemical network models bypass some of these failures by explicitly representing material flows and reciprocal feedbacks.6 Overall, I describe how foundational ecological network theory and metaphors it inspires in business ecosystems and social hierarchies misapprehend organisms as takers rather than makers in a fixed, zero-sum world. Instead, organisms are nutrient transformers—producers as much as consumers—continually modifying, regenerating, and expanding the life-sustaining capacities of their environments. Centering this more generative network perspective not only improves predictive understanding of ecological sustainability; it also directly undermines colonial notions of scarcity, domination, and control that continue to mislead network theory in ecology and sustainability science. 1. Hickel, J. et al. Imperialist appropriation in the world economy: Drain from the global South through unequal exchange, 1990–2015. Glob. Environ. Chang. 73, 102467 (2022). 2. Ehrlich, P. R. & Holdren, J. P. Impact of Population Growth: Complacency concerning this component of man’s predicament is unjustified and counterproductive. Science 171:1212 (1971). 3. Levine, J. et al. Beyond pairwise mechanisms of species coexistence in complex communities. Nature 546:56 (2017). 4. Darwin, C. The Descent of Man. (1871). 5. May, R. M. Stability and Complexity in Model Ecosystems. (1974). 6. Martinez, N. D. Allometric Trophic Networks From Individuals to Socio-Ecosystems: Consumer–Resource Theory of the Ecological Elephant in the Room. Front. Ecol. Evol. 8:1 (2020).
items.authors: Martinez, Neo*

items.title: Degree Organization Score: A Baseline-Aware Measure for Evolving Networks
items.abstract: Understanding how network structure evolves over time is central to the analysis of social, technological, and biological systems. While degree-based measures such as inequality and centralization are widely used to characterize network structure, their absolute values are often difficult to interpret and compare across networks or time, as they lack explicit normalization against meaningful reference configurations. We introduce the Degree Organization Score (DOS), a continuous and distribution-agnostic measure that quantifies the relative organization of a network’s degree structure. DOS is defined by normalizing observed degree inequality, measured via the Gini coefficient, between two interpretable reference models: an Erdős–Rényi (ER) random graph, representing a stochastic baseline of minimal organized structure, and a star graph, representing an extreme reference of centralized degree organization.
items.authors: Das, Sujoy*

items.title: Graph-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning for Resilience-Oriented Decision-Making in Critical Infrastructure Systems
items.abstract: Critical infrastructure systems (CIS), such as water distribution networks, road networks, and power networks, are often referred to as lifelines because they are essential for supporting the daily functioning of communities. The rapid expansion of CIS driven by urbanization has increased their vulnerability to high-consequence hazards, including earthquakes, flooding, and wildfires, thereby highlighting the urgent need for effective resilience-enhancement strategies. The resilience of a CIS is commonly defined as its ability to recover functionality following disruptive events, and sound repair-sequence decisions play a crucial role in achieving rapid and efficient recovery. This study presents the development of a Graph Convolutional Neural Network--integrated Deep Reinforcement Learning (GCN--DRL) framework to support optimal repair decisions aimed at improving CIS resilience under multiple hazard scenarios. To demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed GCN--DRL model, a water distribution network (WDN) and a road network subjected to earthquake and flooding hazards are used as case studies. Resilience evaluation frameworks for both systems are first developed, explicitly capturing the dynamic evolution of system performance during post-event recovery. For the WDN, the performance indicator accounts for the relative importance of service nodes and the extent to which post-earthquake water demands are satisfied. For the road network, the performance indicator reflects network connectivity under road segment closures. Within the GCN--DRL framework, the GCN encodes the structural and operational information of both the WDN and the road network. Network topology and node-level performance states (i.e., water demand satisfaction levels and road segment availability) are provided as inputs to the GCN, which outputs action-specific reward values. These values are then used by the DRL agent to identify optimal repair sequences from a large action space, with the objective of maximizing overall system resilience. The repair strategies generated by the proposed GCN--DRL model are compared with those obtained using four conventional decision-making approaches. The results demonstrate that the GCN--DRL model consistently achieves the highest system resilience index (SRI) values and enables the fastest recovery of system performance. Furthermore, by leveraging transfer learning from pre-trained models, the proposed framework exhibits high computational efficiency when determining optimal repair sequences under new damage scenarios. Overall, the GCN--DRL model demonstrates strong robustness and generalizability, providing an effective decision-support tool for enhancing the resilience of interdependent infrastructure systems under multiple hazard conditions.
items.authors: Fan, Xudong*; Hackl, Jürgen; Yu, Xiong (Bill)
