items.title: Modeling Group-level Behavioral Responses in the COVID-19 Pandemic: The role of coordinated social bubbles
items.abstract: Human behavioral response plays a critical role in the transmission and containment of epidemics. While considerable research has examined the impact of behavioral responses at individual level, coordinated group-level responses have received less attention. This study explores how group-level behavioral dynamics, in the form of early formation of social \textit{bubbles}, contribute to explaining variations in epidemic trajectories among two sets of European countries. To capture these dynamics, we propose an agent-based computational model leveraging cooperative game theory to simulate the formation and transformation of pandemic-related social bubbles. Our model is validated using real-world infection data from the first wave of the COVID-19 pandemic. The results demonstrate that this compact, simple, and computationally efficient model effectively reproduces both epidemic trajectories and ordinal rankings observed in the selected countries. Further validation with mobility data and robustness tests indicates that the proposed framework possesses explanatory power regarding the recovery patterns of social interactions prior to the official lifting of non-pharmaceutical interventions.
items.authors: Zhu, Hongan*; Cao, Qingtao; Heydari, Pedram; Heydari, Babak

items.title: Network perturbation predicts shifts in chondrocyte bioenergetic pathways in response to extracellular aging cues
items.abstract: Introduction: Identifying the causal mediators of aging and tracing their downstream effects in complex, low-turnover tissues—like articular cartilage—remains a significant challenge. Recent advances in omics-based technologies have enabled comprehensive profiling of transcriptomic and proteomic alterations in aging tissues1,2, including those within the knee joint.3 Although differential expression analyses are becoming increasingly common in both aging and orthopaedic research, they fail to dynamically simulate the propagation of extracellular perturbations through tissue-specific molecular pathways. Here, we present a computational framework for in silico perturbation modeling designed to predict distinct transcriptional responses to age-specific extracellular environmental cues. We exemplify applications of this framework using articular chondrocytes exposed to secretomes derived from infrapatellar fat pads (IFPs)—integral components of the cartilage microenvironment—excised from the knee joints of young and aged animals. Methods: To establish a foundational model for aging-associated signaling in cartilage, publicly available transcriptomic profiles from knee joints of healthy and osteoarthritic adults4 were used to construct a weighted gene co-expression network.5 To identify physiologically relevant perturbation inputs, we performed mass spectrometry-based proteomics on media conditioned with IFPs excised from the knee joints of young and aged male mice (n=4/group), following IACUC approval. We previously found that male IFPs had a greater influence on markers of chondrogenicity in aged chondrocytes.6 Thus, only IFPs from male mice were used to generate conditioned media. All in vivo and in vitro studies were performed in accordance with the ARRIVE guidelines to ensure rigor, reproducibility, and transparent reporting. Using the SEPDB secreted protein database7 and the NicheNet ligand-target model8, we identified receptors corresponding to secreted proteins identified via mass spectrometry. We then modeled the intracellular propagation of these extracellular signals using the Random Walk with Restart (RWR) algorithm9 and evaluated the biological relevance of the network inference using Gene Ontology enrichment analysis on ranked gene list outputs. To validate the results from this network model, we benchmarked the in silico outputs against RNA-sequencing data from aged chondrocytes treated with IFP-conditioned media that we have previously reported.6 Results: A total of 1,294 genes were differentially expressed between healthy and osteoarthritic patients, of which 800 were upregulated and 494 were downregulated in osteoarthritic knee cartilage. This includes the downregulation of chondrogenic genes, such as Sox9, and upregulation of catabolic and fibrocartilaginous genes, such as Mmp13, Col1a1, and Fap (Fig. 1A). We then constructed a cartilage-specific transcriptional network using topological overlap matrices and retained the top 50,000 weighted interactions for subsequent analyses. Genes encoding for core components of mitochondrial complex I involved in oxidative phosphorylation, Ndufb9 and Ndufab1, emerged as top hub genes within the network (Fig. 1B). 30 secreted proteins were identified in IFP-conditioned media. After mapping these ligands to their corresponding receptors, we employed the RWR algorithm to simulate the impact of these extracellular ligands on intracellular transcriptional responses and generated a ranked list of genes based on their proximity to receptor seeds in the network. Through GO enrichment analysis of top-ranked genes from the RWR model and differentially expressed genes identified by RNA-sequencing of murine chondrocytes treated with IFP-conditioned media in vitro, we identified 50 pathways enriched in silico and 46 pathways enriched in vitro. Of these, 18 pathways overlapped between the two datasets. Subsequent semantic similarity analysis implicated an additional 9 pathways from the in silico dataset as having high functional similarity (semantic similarity score 0.70) with the overlapping pathways, leading to reclassification of these pathways as overlapping (Fig. 2). Notably, a substantial portion of the overlapping pathways were associated with mitochondrial dysfunction, including mitochondrial respiratory chain complex I assembly and cellular respiration, further supporting the biological validity of the computational model in capturing disease-relevant mitochondrial processes. Discussion: This study presents and validates a computational framework that integrates tissue-specific transcriptomic data, extracellular proteomic inputs, and network-based propagation modeling. This network-based approach enabled us to predict transcriptional consequences of age-associated extracellular cues in silico and revealed that mitochondrial dysfunction represents a convergent pathway disrupted in aged cartilage. Using cartilage-IFP crosstalk as an example, our application of the RWR method to simulate signal propagation from extracellular ligands through a gene co-expression network represents a novel approach for modeling paracrine signaling. Unlike conventional pathway enrichment methods, which are limited by predefined annotations, our diffusion-based approach captures context-specific interactions and permits the quantification of ligand-specific influence across diverse biological modules. Compared to existing methods, such as Cytotalk, which rely on cell-cell communication modeling from single-cell transcriptomic data10, our approach does not require cell-type resolution and, instead, leverages tissue-specific co-expression topology to infer downstream signaling. Collectively, our integrative framework bridges proteomic and transcriptomic layers to uncover how topological positioning of receptors governs tissue responses to paracrine cues. Significance: The insights from this model advance our mechanistic understanding of joint aging and provide a scalable approach to identify interventional targets in degenerative musculoskeletal disorders. Additionally, this computational pipeline can be extended to other age-related or disease-associated tissues to support hypothesis generation and therapeutic target discovery. References: 1Huang et al 2025 2Takasugi et al 2024 3Liu et al 2025 4Fisch et al 2018 5Langdelf & Horvath 2008 6D’Amico, McNeill, & Khay et al 2025 7Wang et al 2024 8Browaeys et al 2020 9Valdeolivas et al 2019 10Hu et al 2021 Acknowledgments: Funding provided in part by NIA R01AG089455 (FA and HI) and NIA R01AG090356 (HI).
items.authors: McNeill, Tyler*; Ambrosio, Fabrisia; Iijima, Hirotaka

items.title: Effects of central node selection for network robustness against localized attacks
items.abstract: Localized attacks on complex networks, where nodes are sequentially removed based on their topological or spatial proximity to a designated seed node, have garnered increasing attention across the fields. Existing studies of localized attacks average robustness over randomly chosen seeds, leaving the effect of the seed’s location largely unexplored; yet in spatially embedded networks, attacks initiated near the topological center can be far more damaging than those at the periphery. We introduce the Localized Attacks Vulnerability Index (LAVI), which quantifies seed-specific damage by the resulting link removals. Across extensive simulations, LAVI correlates more strongly with realized damage than standard centrality metrics and reveals vulnerability patterns those measures miss.
items.authors: Chujyo, Masaki*; Liu, Shu; Toriumi, Fujio

items.title: Structure-Dependent Effects of Frequency Shuffling on Synchronization
items.abstract: Synchronization is a universal phenomenon observed in various real-world systems, including power grids, neural networks, and cardiac systems [1, 2]. Given that synchronous states ensure stable and efficient operation, there has been significant interest in developing methods to enhance and maintain synchronization in these networks. Previous research has demonstrated that frequency shuffling, which involves oscillators intermittently exchanging their natural frequencies over time, can both enhance the degree of synchronization and reduce the relaxation time to the synchronized state [3]. However, these findings are based on globally coupled systems where every oscillator interacts with all others. To understand how network topology influences the effects of frequency shuffling on synchronization, we investigate a Kuramoto model on a one-dimensional periodic regular lattice with nearest-neighbor interactions. Frequency shuffling is implemented as a random exchange of oscillators’ natural frequencies occurring at a constant rate. Our results show that, unlike in all-to-all networks, the order parameter in a ring network exhibits a nonmonotonic dependence on the shuffling rate λ For small λ, increasing the shuffling rate enhances synchronization, consistent with previous results for all-to-all networks. At larger λ, however, the dynamics become qualitatively different. In addition to the synchronized state, new metastable fixed points emerge, characterized by nonzero winding numbers q, known as twisted states. As a result, while some realizations relax to the synchronized state, others become trapped in twisted states for extremely long times before eventually synchronizing. To confirm this mechanism, we numerically analyze the transition probabilities between the in-phase state (q = 0) and the typical twisted states (|q| = 1) by initializing the system in each configuration and counting the number of remaining or transitioning samples at a given time (Fig. 1, center and right panels). We find that transitions from the synchronized state to the twisted states become increasingly rare and eventually vanish as λ increases, whereas transitions from twisted to synchronized states persist with a finite probability. Importantly, the decreasing escape probability from the twisted states implies that the lifetime of these metastable states grows dramatically as the shuffling rate increases. Consequently, the relaxation time to synchronization increases with λ, in contrast to all-to-all networks, where larger shuffling rates monotonically accelerate synchronization. It results in a persistent but transient nonmonotonic dependence of the order parameter on λ, on finite time scales relevant to realistic systems (Fig. 1, left panel, black dashed line). These results demonstrate that network topology plays a crucial role in determining the effectiveness of frequency shuffling. In particular, in translationally invariant networks such as regular lattices, the emergence of twisted metastable states fundamentally alters the relaxation dynamics, limiting the benefits of frequency shuffling.
items.authors: Kim, Juseong*; Kim, Cook Hyun; Park, Jong-Min; Kim, Heetae

items.title: How Do Network Mechanisms Respond to Shocks? Local Projections Meet Relational Event Models
items.abstract: How do network formation mechanisms respond to external shocks? Existing approaches either compare static snapshots before and after perturbations—losing dynamic information—or detect that parameters change without characterizing response trajectories [1,2]. We introduce a framework combining Relational Event Models (REM) with Local Projections (LP) to estimate impulse response functions on mechanism parameters themselves. The two-step procedure first estimates REM parameters in moving windows, yielding time series of coefficients for reciprocity, homophily, and transitivity [3,4]. We then apply Jord` a’s [5] LP method to trace how these coefficients respond at multiple horizons following a shock. LP’s robustness to dynamic misspecification is valuable: we need not assume how parameters evolve, only that shocks have estimable effects. We validate through simulations demonstrating recovery of canonical IRF shapes—permanent shifts, tempo- rary spikes with exponential decay, and gradual transitions—under realistic network sizes and event densities. We establish power curves for shock detection and compare performance against naive pre/post estimation, showing LP+REM’s advantage in distinguishing temporary perturbations from permanent structural breaks. As empirical illustration, we apply the method to Twitter interactions among Turkish-American foreign policy experts (2010–2021). Figure 1 shows interaction volume with perturbations at political crisis points. LP+REM en- ables tracing how specific mechanisms—cross-national reciprocity, professional vs. national homophily—respond to bilateral crises, testing predictions from threat-rigidity theory versus epistemic community resilience. [1] Meijerink-Bosman et al. (2022). Dynamic relational event modeling. PLOS ONE, 17(8). [2] Haunss & Hollway (2023). Multimodal mechanisms of political discourse dynamics. Network Science, 11(1). [3] Butts (2008). A relational event framework for social action. Sociological Methodology, 38(1). [4] Lillo & Rizzini (2024). Modelling shock propagation in financial temporal networks. arXiv :2407.09340. [5] Jord` a (2005). Estimation and inference of impulse responses by local projections. AER, 95(1).
items.authors: Tapan, Yunus Emre*

items.title: Synchronization Enhancement by Phase Lag in Coupled Oscillators with Inertia
items.abstract: Coupled-oscillator systems with inertia offer a realistic framework for describing many physical systems, including electrical power grids. Although inertia is essential to capture realistic dynamical behavior, it suppresses global synchronization, so the system can be synchronized only at relatively large coupling strengths and in a discontinuous manner, often accompanied by the formation of several clusters locked in frequency [1–3]. The effect of phase lag has been extensively explored in the first-order Kuramoto model—most notably in the Sakaguchi–Kuramoto variant—where it is typically observed to suppress synchronization and modify the critical coupling threshold [4]. In this study, we demonstrate that introducing a phase lag to only a subset of oscillators can, conversely, promote synchronization in the second-order Kuramoto model with inertia. We consider a heterogeneous population comprising oscillators with no phase lag and those with a uniform, nonzero phase lag. The dynamics are investigated using a post-lag protocol, in which the phase lag is imposed only after the initially lag-free system has relaxed to a steady state. Figure 1 shows that introducing a phase lag in only part of the population alters the structure of the primary frequency-entrained cluster. For large inertia, numerical results indicate that the phase lag displaces the left boundary of the primary entrained cluster in the intrinsic-frequency domain toward more negative values. As a result, oscillators with negative natural frequencies—both drifting units and those originally forming secondary or higher-order clusters—are recruited into the synchronized state [2,3]. In contrast, oscillators with sufficiently large positive natural frequencies do not depinning, so the right boundary of the primary cluster stays fixed, yielding a net expansion of the primary synchronized cluster. These findings demonstrate that introducing a partial phase lag can qualitatively alter the configuration of synchronized clusters in inertia-oscillator systems, leading to an expansion of the central frequency-locked cluster in the large-inertia regime. By revealing how phase lag reshapes cluster boundaries without triggering depinning on the positive-frequency side, this work clarifies the impact of partial phase lag on synchronized cluster organization in the second-order Kuramoto model, providing guidance on synchronization control for inertial systems, such as power grids.
items.authors: Yi, Sudo*; Kim, Cook Hyun; Kim, Heetae; Kahng, B.

items.title: Operational time and criticality in physics co-authorship networks
items.abstract: Many real-world complex networks display either fractal or small-world structure, but rarely both. Prior work explains this incompatibility through static architectural constraints: fractality emerges from critical branching trees, whereas small-world structure requires supercritical expansion supported by long-range shortcuts. Here we show that this dichotomy is not merely structural but dynamical. Using more than a century of APS co-authorship data, we construct a sequence of network time slices by tuning aggregation to the percolation transition. Measuring a set of macro-level parameters -- including percolation observables and small-world indicators -- we find that all scale as power laws with the networks’ distance from criticality. Networks remain fractal near criticality but acquire small-world shortcuts only when aggregated beyond the critical point. We further show that the critical time scale emerging from this aggregation procedure constitutes an operational time to which the system self-organizes: citation avalanches obey dynamical scaling theory only when defined in this intrinsic time. These results indicate that static analyses are insufficient whenever the evolutionary time scale of a network is empirically accessible. A dynamical scaling framework is required to correctly identify and characterize critical and supercritical network states.
items.authors: Lietz, Haiko*; Mirelmi, Pouria

items.title: The Political Reorganization of Everyday Life? Uncovering Latent Lifestyle Structures via Digital Traces
items.abstract: A common claim in polarization research is that everyday life is increasingly organized along partisan lines: from what we consume online to how we understand ourselves, even non-political content is thought to reinforce political identity [1,2]. We treat this “political reorganization of everyday life” as an empirical hypothesis and test it using network models of online lifestyles inferred from large-scale digital traces. We use a decade-long panel of U.S. Twitter users with linked demographic and voter-registration information (2011–2022)[3]. For each year, we build a weighted bipartite network between demographic groups and web domains, where groups are defined by race, age, gender, region, and party, and edges encode how often a group shares a domain. Excluding news and mega-platform sites allows us to focus on everyday, lifestyle-oriented content (entertainment, shopping, local services, hobbies). From the group–domain matrices we apply non-negative matrix factorization to obtain K latent “lifestyle bundles,” and represent each group as a point in this bundle space. We then construct a yearly group–group lifestyle similarity network, whose edges reflect normalized correlations between bundle profiles. On these networks, we compute assortativity for race, age, gender, region, and party to compare how strongly each attribute structures lifestyle similarity. Regression models complement this by asking (1) how much variance in bundle memberships partisanship explains relative to other demographics, and (2)how strongly ideology predicts similarity ties once other features are controlled for. We also track these statistics over time to relate changes in sorting to major events such as elections and the COVID-19 pandemic. Conceptually, we treat online lifestyles as a networked object—a temporal stack of lifestyle similarity layers grounded in a demographic–domain bipartite graph—to scrutinize the “political reorganization” thesis. Methodologically, our combination of factor models, similarity networks, assortativity, and regression provides a template for extracting meso-scale lifestyle structure from digital traces. Substantively, preliminary results show that race often generates stronger assortativity than party, that sorting is not monotonic over time, and that cross-cutting lifestyle ties persist, suggesting that while politics matters, everyday online life does not always consolidate primarily along partisan lines. [1] DellaPosta, D. (2020). Pluralistic collapse: The “oil spill” model of mass opinion polarization. American Sociological Review, 85(3), 507-536. [2] Nyhan, B., Settle, J., Thorson, E., Wojcieszak, M., Barber´ a, P., Chen, A. Y., ... & Tucker, J. A. (2023). Like-minded sources on Facebook are prevalent but not polarizing. Nature, 620(7972), 137-144. [3] Yang, K. C., Goel, P., Quintana-Math´ e, A., Horgan, L., McCabe, S. D., Grinberg, N., ... & Lazer, D. (2025). DomainDemo: a dataset of domain-sharing activities among different demographic groups on Twitter. Scientific data, 12(1), 1251.
items.authors: Ichikawa, Kei*; Lu, Hsiu-Chi

items.title: On the Structure of China's High-Speed Rail-Aviation Composite Network
items.abstract: This study constructs China’s high-speed rail(CRH)-aviation composite network. By analyzing its topological properties and comparing them with those of the single-modal CRH and aviation networks, we identify the multiple network roles of transportation hubs and reveal the hub-periphery structural pattern. We collected schedule data from China’s CRH timetables and flight plans to construct the aviation and CRH networks. Nodes are cities with CRH stations, airports, or both; edges mean direct CRH or flight connectivity, directed and unweighted. Railway stations and airports within the same city are considered interconnected in the composite network, Topological analysis shows CRH network and aviation network both have scale-free and small-world features—key for efficient transport. Differences exist: The CRH network has extremely high clustering, ensuring high local efficiency. The aviation network has a lower clustering, and no clear communities, supporting long-distance travel. The composite network expands coverage by leveraging the complementary advantages of these two transportation modes. We then assigned multiple network roles to nodes based on their multiplex degrees and distributions. From a multiplex network perspective, we identified four distinct roles of national transportation hubs: Focused nodes (R1), Multiplex nodes (R2), Focused hubs (R3), and Multiplex hubs (R4). This analysis reveals the uneven distribution of these hub types across geographical space and network hierarchical structures. We further explored the different roles of air and rail transportation in organizing passenger network structures; the inherent differences between the two modes have led to the structural differentiation of China’s CRH-aviation composite network, forming a fundamental pattern consisting of a hub core and peripheral clusters. This study addresses the limitations of single-modal transportation network research. It uncovers the hub-peripheral structural pattern and uneven distribution characteristics of the composite network, providing theoretical support for the optimization of comprehensive transportation planning and the precise layout of hub systems. Future research will further explore the weighted properties of the composite network and the influencing factors of regional differences.
items.authors: Hongyu, Dong*; Haoxiang, Xia

items.title: The mesoscopic structure of the Carbon Trade Network across the last twenty years
items.abstract: As emissions from burning fossil fuels are the primary cause of global warming~[1], the last decades have witnessed a considerable effort to mitigate carbon emissions. At country level, the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change attributes emissions to countries producing goods and services~[2]; recent literature has, however, advocated the adoption of a consumption-aware accounting, that re-distributes the responsibility for the emissions between producers and users~[3]. Studies have attempted to understand to what extent a `consumption principle' may help reducing disparities in carbon accounting, estimating the amount of traded carbon via Multi-Regional Input-Output (MRIO) tables. As noticed by Caro et al.~[4], MRIO tables are very sensitive to the accuracy of the available data on trading sectors; interestingly enough, data requirements can be relaxed by considering aggregate measures: specifically, the amount of carbon embedded into a country export can be simply quantified by multiplying it by the related \emph{GDP-induced carbon intensity}, measuring the kilograms of carbon per dollar of GDP released by a given country during a given year. Inspired by the work of Caro and co-authors, we have constructed the Carbon Trade Network (CTN), i.e. the graph induced by the trade-embedded carbon exchanges between world countries, with the aim of investigating it over the past two decades. More specifically, we have limited ourselves to analyze the mesoscopic organization of the CTN Strongly Connected Component (SCC), by optimizing the binary, weighted, and enhanced variants of the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) on it. In all cases, the SCC is characterized by an additional, nested structure constituted of a core and a periphery - the core (periphery) subgraph being characterized by the highest (lowest) level of reciprocity (see fig.~1). In the purely binary case, the core block gathers the $84$ richest, and more polluting, countries (among which Brazil, Canada, China, Europe, India, Japan, South-Africa, USA); if, instead, we leave the BIC instantiated with the directed, binary variant of the Stochastic Block Model (bSBM) free to find the optimal number of groups, we recover ten modules - only five of which are assembled in the aforementioned core. Optimizing BIC instantiated with the directed, weighted variant of the Stochastic Block Model (wSBM) confirms the results obtained by running the bSBM, the subset of core-nodes concentrating the highest percentage of the total weight ($\simeq 10\%$) now including China, India, South-Africa, USA: an important exception is represented by Russia, no longer part of this subset - as the year considered for the present analysis is $2023$, this may be due to the sanctions suffered by Russia in consequence of the war with Ukraine. As a last exercise, we have instantiated BIC with the directed, enhanced variant of the Stochastic Block Model (eSBM), constraining both binary and weighted properties in a block-wise fashion: in such a case, the algorithm recovers China, India, Russia, USA as the nodes constituting the subgraph summing up $\simeq 10\%$ of the total weight (see fig.~1). As an additional exercise, we have individuated the countries playing the role of hubs (top exporters) and authorities (top importers) by running both the binary and weighted versions of the HITS algorithm: accounting for weights reveals that \emph{i)} China is becoming increasingly central in the category of authorities as well, closely followed by India; \emph{ii)} Vietnam is becoming the second most central country in the category of hubs.
items.authors: Ferraro, Alexander*; Squartini, Tiziano

items.title: Across-Visit Consensus Community Detection in Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) Gene Co-expression Temporal Network Data
items.abstract: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a complex autoimmune disease characterized by strong heterogeneity and time-varying immune dysregulation. While gene co-expression network analysis has proven useful for identifying disease-associated modules, most existing approaches rely on static or cross-sectional representations and do not explicitly leverage longitudinal structure. Here, we propose a time-aware multilayer network framework to identify stable gene communities and underexplored hub genes in longitudinal whole-blood transcriptomic data from SLE patients (GSE65391). GSE65391 is a publicly available longitudinal whole-blood gene expression dataset in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), where patients are profiled repeatedly across multiple clinical visits (i.e., the same individuals contribute samples at different time points), alongside healthy controls. This longitudinal design enables constructing visit-specific co-expression layers and then identifying time-stable communities by aggregating evidence across visits rather than relying on a single static network [1,2]. Gene co-expression networks are constructed independently for each clinical visit, yielding a sequence of visit-specific network layers defined over a shared set of genes. Instead of optimizing a coupled multislice modularity with explicit inter-layer identity edges [3], we adopt a consensus-across-time strategy [4]: communities are detected separately within each visit layer using the Leiden algorithm [5], and a weighted consensus graph is subsequently built based on gene–gene co-assignment fractions across layers. Re-clustering this consensus graph produces time-stable gene communities that summarize recurrent co-expression patterns across visits. Applying this framework, we identify eight robust communities corresponding to major immune processes, including interferon signaling, neutrophil activation, plasmablast-related programs, and platelet-associated pathways. Beyond these well-characterized structures, our approach highlights unexpected hub genes that exhibit high within-community centrality, despite having a limited prior association with SLE. To systematically assess novelty, we integrate automated literature and GWAS-based screening to quantify existing disease-specific evidence for each candidate hub, revealing several highly ranked genes with minimal representation in prior SLE studies [6,7]. We further verify that the identified hub genes remain highly central when analyses are restricted to female samples, which constitute the majority of the cohort, indicating that hub detection is not driven by a small subset of subjects. Together, these results demonstrate that time-aware consensus community detection provides a robust and interpretable alternative to identity-coupled multislice methods for longitudinal omics data, enabling the discovery of stable network structure and novel candidate regulators in complex autoimmune diseases. This abstract also aligns with our prior RA study in which network community structure was leveraged to prioritize underexplored hub genes via systematic evidence screening [8].
items.authors: Amirirad, Neda*

items.title: Multilayer Network Architecture of Global Politics: Combining Diplomatic Connectivity and Historical Sentiment
items.abstract: Global political structure emerges from multiple, overlapping relational layers, including formal diplomatic ties and his- torical relationships. Here, we construct two global networks spanning all 195 UN sovereign nations: a diplomatic network based on mutual embassy representation and common diplomatic membership, and a historical network derived from sentiment-weighted bilateral mentions in narrative foreign-relations texts - both based of the respective nations Foreign Relations Wiki-page. Under Louvain community detection the diplomatic layer produces fewer, more integrated communities, reflecting the unifying role of formal representation, whereas the historical layer yields a more fragmented partition, consistent with persistent geopolitical divisions encoded in historical sentiment. These partitions are used as reference structures to validate a multilayer integration model defined on a weighted, undirected graph, with edge weights formulated as: wij = σ(α · Eij + β · (Sij − 0.5)) where: Eij ∈ {0, 1} represents embassy connection. Sij ∈ [0, 1] represents sentiment score (0.5 if no sentiment link exists). σ(x) = 1/(1+e^{−x}) represents the sigmoid function. A systematic grid search over (α, β) reveals a smooth modularity landscape in which the degree of geopolitical division varies predictably with layer dominance. Emphasis on historical sentiment reinforces fragmented community structure, while increasing diplomatic weight collapses these divisions into more unified partitions - concluded on the basis of modularity-measures. All parameter configurations were evaluated across three independent runs with fixed random seed, yielding identical results and demonstrating robustness. Together, these results showcase a quantitative framework for integrating and weighting multiple network layers, enabling the unification of historical and diplomatic relations within a single model that captures contrasting geopolitical structures.
items.authors: Fazal, Benjamin *; Due, Smilla

items.title: Characterizing Open-Ended Evolution Through Undecidability Mechanisms in Random Boolean Networks
items.abstract: Open-ended evolution (OEE) is the sustained production of novel phenotypes rather than convergence to a fixed repertoire. In finite-state network dynamics, novelty is easily confounded with noise, while asymptotic diagnostics are often computationally intractable. We introduce a simple, substrate-agnostic metric, $\Omega$, that scores a trajectory by the residence-time-weighted contribution of each distinct attractor’s cycle length, rewarding persistent new cyclic phenotypes while penalizing both single-attractor dynamics and fleeting random jumps. Using Random Boolean Networks (RBNs) as a unifying testbed, we compare classical Boolean dynamics with biologically motivated non-classical mechanisms (probabilistic context switching, annealed rule mutation, paraconsistent logic, modal necessary/possible gating, and quantum-inspired superposition/entanglement) under deterministic homogeneous and stochastic heterogeneous updating schemes. As Figure 1 shows, in homogeneous structures PBN stochastic switching dominates in the sparse/near-critical regime, while modal gating supports sustained novelty at higher connectivity. These results suggest that OEE-relevant dynamics in networked regulatory models are promoted not by noise alone, but by structured, state-dependent uncertainty that continually creates new, persistent attractor families, supporting recent theoretical results showing that only self-referential mechanisms enable authentic OEE.
items.authors: Lopez-Diaz, Amahury*; Rivera Torres, Pedro Juan; Febres, Gerardo; Gershenson, Carlos

items.title: Understanding Urban-Rural Disparities in Mobility Inefficiency for Colombia, Mexico, and India
items.abstract: As urbanisation accelerates globally, understanding mobility disparities between urban and rural areas becomes crucial for equitable development. Previous approaches to defining urban-rural boundaries rely on population density thresholds [1] or built environment contiguity [2], yet these static methods fail to capture functional interdependencies between regions. This critical gap leaves policymakers without actionable insights about where and how mobility infrastructure fails to serve rural communities, particularly in Low and Middle-Income Countries where informal employment and multi-purpose trips shape travel patterns differently than in Global North contexts. We address this gap by investigating mobility inefficiency inequalities across Colombia, India, and Mexico using aggregated Location-Based Services data from November-December 2019 [3]. We develop a novel framework that defines urban-rural boundaries through network percolation of mobility patterns rather than static characteristics, capturing functional integration of settlements. Our classification shows strong agreement with established Global Urban Rural Settlement datasets, validating our mobility-based approach. We introduce mobility inefficiency as a metric measuring deviation between actual trip lengths and optimal shortest paths. Importantly, high inefficiency values do not imply poor individual routing choices but reveal aggregated systemic constraints such as congestion, limited infrastructure, geographic barriers, or trip chaining patterns. Our analysis reveals that rural areas consistently exhibit higher mobility inefficiency than urban areas, particularly for longer trips (>5km) and later in the day (Figure 1A visualises the case of Colombia). These disparities only emerge when analysing both trip distance and time of day simultaneously, while aggregate analyses mask important inequalities. For urban areas, we construct vector fields to analyse transit-efficiency alignment. We calculate public transit accessibility scores combining coverage area (convex hull of transit stops) and stop density from OpenStreetMap data, then create spatial gradient vectors showing how transit access and mobility efficiency vary across neighbouring areas. Our transit metric was validated against General Transit Feed Specification data in Bogotá and Mexico City, showing strong correlations during operational hours. Comparing angular differences between these vector fields reveals four distinct urban patterns, with Colombian cities showing stronger alignment likely due to Bus Rapid Transit systems (Figure 1B). For rural areas, we analyse all rural geohashes within 256km of major cities, linking each to its nearest urban centre. Using OLS regression, we examine how distance from city centre, population, directional orientation (east-west and north-south positioning), and proximity to alternative cities relate to inefficiency. Results reveal context-specific patterns: distance matters in Bogotá, population in New Delhi (Figure 1C), while directional patterns in all three cities align with documented geographic barriers and socioeconomic distributions. Our framework demonstrates that measuring mobility inequality requires both consistent metrics and context-appropriate analytical methods, providing actionable insights for targeted infrastructure investment.
items.authors: Iyer, Nandini*; Luca, Massimiliano; Di Clemente, Riccardo

items.title: The Specialization of Interdisciplinary Innovators in Science and Technology
items.abstract: Interdisciplinary research is widely recognized as essential for science and society, yet our quantitative understanding of who drives such work remains limited. Funding agencies have traditionally emphasized assembling teams of disciplinary specialists, while recent studies point to the importance of individual generalists. Analyzing 52 million publications and 7 million patents, we uncover a striking regularity: the timing of a researcher’s most interdisciplinary work follows a uniform distribution across the career, implying that interdisciplinarity is best understood as a persistent, individual-level trait. Building on this universal pattern, we derive a quantitative index, Λ that captures each researcher’s interdisciplinary specialization. Λ remains stable across careers and fields, is strongly associated with the production of highly interdisciplinary works across team sizes, and sharply predicts the quantity and impact of future interdisciplinary work. Large-scale analyses leveraging Λ further reveal that interdisciplinary output is specialized at the individual level: a small set of high-Λ innovators disproportionately drive boundary-spanning contributions; impact peaks when the interdisciplinarity of work aligns with individual Λ; and collaboration networks exhibit strong homophily in Λ, reinforcing this specialization. Together, these findings demonstrate that interdisciplinarity is not only a collective property of teams but also a measurable, persistent individual trait, offering a new framework for understanding how interdisciplinary knowledge is produced, evaluated, and rewarded.
items.authors: Kusumegi, Keigo*

items.title: Philanthropic Influence of Art Collectors on U.S. Art Museums
items.abstract: Art museum collections evolve through complex social relationships [1]. In particular, art collectors play a key role: their philanthropic donations sustain many museum holdings [2], yet their influence on artists’ institutional careers remains poorly quantified. Here, we compile a large-scale dataset of historical museum acquisitions from 15 major publicly funded art museums in the United States, selected to balance institutional prestige and geographic diversity. We extract artists and collectors names from unstructured text through an AI-human name disambiguation pipeline. The final data comprise 1,341,762 artworks linked to 100,686 artists, 47,123 collectors, and 3,640 organizations. Each artwork in the data includes its acquisition year, title of the artwork, artist name, and name of the collector, who donated or provided resources for the respective museum acquisition. From these data, we map the network of collectors linking artists with museums over time through philanthropic donations. We characterize connectivity in the museum network along two general dimensions: scope and frequency. Scope captures whether connections remain local, concentrated within a single museum, or global, spanning multiple institutions. Frequency captures whether these connections occur one-time, or repeat over time. Together, these dimensions define four connectivity types: local one-time, local repeat, global one-time, and global repeat. Applied jointly to collectors and artists, these dimensions describe how relationships between artists and museums are established and sustained. We find that 20% are local repeat collectors, who act as institutional anchors, supporting 56% of the long-term growth of museum collections. A 69% are one-time donors (local or global) who contribute occasionally with a 12% of total donations. In contrast, 11% are global repeat collectors responsible for 32% of total donations, who emerge as cultural connectors by linking museums and introducing artists into multiple institutional contexts. On the artist side, 75% of artists are distributed in only one museum, whereas 25% of artists achieve global institutional presence. We observe that wider artists’ visibility is closely associated with the giving patterns by global repeat collectors, who enable the circulation of artworks across institutions. Interestingly, global repeat collectors form connections with artists and institutions through mixed selective patterns (Fig. 1a), demonstrating that collectors’ donation choices can significantly influence artists’ institutional career success (Fig. 1b). Collectors’ selective connectivity evidences that they function as bridges, or gatekeepers, for artists’ visibility across institutions, forming or reinforcing the artists-museum connection. Overall, our network-based framework captures fundamental dimensions of collector-mediated connectivity, offering a quantitative lens on how artistic visibility is gained and why artists’ careers diverge within museum systems shaped by philanthropic giving. More broadly, this work points to new ways of turning cultural data into structured knowledge, creating building blocks that can feed into future AI systems, data science tools, and new forms of computational art.
items.authors: Herrera Guzman, Yessica*; Barabási, Albert-László

items.title: Graph evolution rules meet communities: assessing global and local patterns in the evolution of dynamic networks
items.abstract: Dynamic networks often exhibit modular structure, yet the mechanisms driving the evolution of their communities remain poorly understood. Graph evolution rules (GERs) capture recurrent local transformations in evolving networks in a data-driven and interpretable way. In this work, we combine GER mining with community detection to analyze network evolution at both global and mesoscopic scales. Using EvoMine and the Leiden algorithm, we derive evolutionary profiles that characterize networks and their communities. Our results show that communities may follow heterogeneous or uniform evolutionary patterns depending on the system, and that structurally connected communities tend to evolve similarly. This approach provides a fine-grained perspective on how local temporal processes shape community evolution.
items.authors: Galdeman, Alessia*; Zignani, Matteo; Gaito, Sabrina

items.title: Community Detection in Labeled Growing Hypergraphs
items.abstract: We consider a model of growing hypergraphs in which in which hyperedges form as noisy copies of prior hyperedges. Our model extends prior work [1] by incorporating binary community node labels. The formation of new edges is affected by homophily with respect to these labels. Unlike standard generative community detection methods which assume that edges form independently conditioned on node labels, our model instead explicitly assumes that new interactions are most likely to arrive between similar nodes which have previously iteracted. This generative model is thus well-suited for community-detection for higher-order data sets collected from systems which may be reasonably believed to exhibit path-dependence, in which which interactions happen in the future are causally influenced by which interactions occurred in the past. This setting is common in many higher-order social and information systems; for example, in a network of scientific collaborations, whether a group of researchers collaborates on a new paper depends strongly on whether or not that same group, or some variant thereof, has collaborated in the past. The community structures which result from this model are very different from those than which would be generated by standard stochastic blockmodel variants. To perform community detection under this model, we develop a likelihood-based inference method. Due to the computational complexity of computing the likelihood, we develop an approximation based on edge intersection sizes, which we show to have good empirical performance. We perform the maximization via simulated annealing. This approximated likelihood is highly correlated with the true labels of the graph, allowing the maximization of the approximate likelihood to yield an effective approximation of the true labels. The latent-variable structure of this model also allows us to estimate the genealogy of edges, giving an approximate branching process describing the emergence of new interactions represented in the data. On synthetic data, we find that likelihood maximization in this model can result in much higher-fidelity reconstruction of ground-truth communities than standard methods such as Louvain modularity maximization (Fig. 1(a)-(b)). We also apply our methods to two classes of node-labeled data sets which do not obey standard community detection assumptions: legislate cosponsorship in the US congress (tagged by party aﬀiliation) and co-authorship data from arXiv (tagged by inferred author gender). In sum, our results indicate that methods for community detection in edge-dependent hypergraphs can outperform standard methods for some systems, and suggest future work for modeling and inference in such systems.
items.authors: Chodrow, Philip*; Cataldo, Francis

items.title: Modeling the Effect of Social Contagion in Solar Adoption through Commuter and Socioeconomic Networks
items.abstract: The widespread adoption of residential solar panels is critical for the global energy transition, but its progress is hindered by challenges such as high customer acquisition costs and slowing demand, with a market contraction of 31% in 2024, the first contraction since 2017. While social contagion is known to be a powerful driver of increasing solar adoption, the exact mechanisms within this “peer effect” remain poorly distinguished. Specifically, a gap exists in understanding whether influence spreads primarily through Information Flow – that is, through interaction and connections – or through Socioeconomic Similarity (Homophily), where influence is stronger within groups of more similar socioeconomic characteristics, such as income, race, and education levels. This study aims to quantitatively distinguish between these two competing mechanisms for solar adoption, hypothesizing that if the principle of homophily – as opposed to information flow – was the dominant mechanism in social contagion for solar adoption, then a network model built on socioeconomic similarity would produce communities with more uniform solar adoption rates and correlate more strongly with real adoption data. Three networks were created at a U.S. county level to compare adoption: a Socioeconomic Similarity Network connecting geographically proximate counties based on a multi-dimensional Jenson-Shannon Divergence similarity score from socioeconomic profiles; an Information Flow Network from county-to-county commuter flows; and a State-Level Network for a geographic baseline. Then, a static analysis using the Louvain algorithm identified community structures and measured their homogeneity, and then a Susceptible-Infected-Resistant (SIR) epidemiological model was used to test which network structure's diffusion patterns best correlate with the real-world solar adoption data. Rather than designating beta as a global constant, the probability of transmission between two counties is made proportional to the strength of their connection and calculated as: P(transmission) = base_beta * (edge_weight / max(network_weight). Static analysis showed that the Socioeconomic Similarity network produced highly homogeneous clusters (lower intra-cluster standard deviation of adoption rates), confirming that adoption concentrates within specific socioeconomic groups. However, dynamic SIR simulations revealed that the Commuter Network was the only model to yield a strong, statistically significant positive correlation with real-world adoption rates, with the Commuter Network having correlation r = 0.18 (p < 0.01) to real-world results while the Socioeconomic Network had correlation r = -0.24. These findings suggest that while socioeconomic homophily dictates receptivity, information flow via commuter ties drives propagation. Commuter routes act as “weak ties” bridging distinct communities, while socioeconomic similarity represents “strong ties” required for validation. Policy interventions must therefore be hybrid: leveraging high-mobility commuter corridors for broad information dissemination while tailoring incentives to specific socioeconomic peer groups to ensure local uptake.
items.authors: Ray, Aritra*

items.title: FORGE: Foundational Optimization Representations from Graph Embeddings
items.abstract:
Combinatorial optimization (CO) problems are fundamental across science and engineering, yet learning-based approaches face critical limitations: they require extensive training data from solving numerous difficult instances, demand dedicated models for each problem distribution and task, and depend on the very solvers they aim to accelerate. These constraints severely limit scalability and generalization to new domains. We therefore introduce 	extsc{Forge}: 	extbf{F}oundational 	extbf{O}ptimization 	extbf{R}epresentations from 	extbf{G}raph 	extbf{E}mbeddings, a framework that addresses these limitations through unsupervised pre-training on diverse mixed-integer programming (MIP) instances. Unlike existing methods, 	extsc{Forge} does not require optimization solvers or optimal solutions during training. 
oindent	extbf{Core Innovation}: 	extsc{Forge} employs a vector-quantized graph autoencoder that learns structural representations at the instance level. Drawing inspiration from foundational models in NLP and computer vision, we introduce two key concepts to optimization: (1) a discrete vocabulary for representing MIP instances through vector quantization, and (2) preservation of global information that traditional GNN approaches fail to capture due to their inherent locality bias. 
oindent	extbf{Evaluation}: We demonstrate 	extsc{Forge}'s versatility in both unsupervised and supervised settings:  egin{itemize} \item 	extbf{Unsupervised}: 	extsc{Forge} embeddings effectively cluster unseen instances across diverse problem domains and sizes without any task-specific training. \item 	extbf{Supervised}: A single pre-trained 	extsc{Forge} model can be fine-tuned for multiple downstream tasks using minimal additional data. We evaluate on two distinct tasks: (1) predicting integrality gaps for cut generation, and (2) predicting variable hints for search guidance. Critically, the same pre-trained model with $\approx 3.25$M parameters generalizes across varied problem types and instance sizes. \end{itemize} 
oindent	extbf{Results}: Integrating 	extsc{Forge} predictions into Gurobi, a state-of-the-art commercial solver, consistently reduces primal gaps across both tasks and multiple problem domains. 	extsc{Forge} can also augment existing learning-based methods, improving their performance on instances these methods were specifically trained on but unseen by 	extsc{Forge}. 
oindent	extbf{Contributions}: We provide: (1) a general-purpose foundational model producing multi-level embeddings (instance, variable, and constraint), (2) unsupervised generalization across problem types, (3) supervised adaptability with minimal fine-tuning, (4) improved solver performance through integration, and (5) open-source code, pre-trained weights, and embeddings to foster further research.
items.authors: Shafi, Zohair*; Kadıoğlu, Serdar

items.title: Uncovering excitatory and inhibitory associations across nodes via stochastic generalization of Markov chains
items.abstract: Markov chains are widely recognized as a fundamental framework for analysing complex networks. The most representative example is Google’s PageRank algorithm, which evaluates node importance using the stationary distribution of a Markov chain. Similarly, the circulation of goods, services and money in economic networks—such as supply chains, input–output tables and international trade—can be represented as probability flows in Markov chains. Conventional Markov chains converge to a stationary state over time. However, actual circulations, for instance monetary flows in economic networks, are dynamic rather than static. To capture the dynamism of real-world flows, we propose a stochastic generalization of Markov chain (SGMC). We show that SGMC uncovers hidden patterns of associations across nodes at any distance, which are intrinsically related to community structure.
items.authors: Okamoto, Hiroshi*; Yamano, Hiroko; Sakata, Ichiro

items.title: Discovering Semi-Private Social Networks: Structural Bias from Private--Private Edge Censoring
items.abstract: Network data are often incomplete; methodological literature shows that how edges go missing can bias structural inference as much as, or more than, the number of missing edges. Some online social networks now exhibit a specific, privacy-driven observation regime when users ``lock'' their accounts: they remain reachable via public neighbors, but the edges among ``private'' users may be unobservable, creating a correlated pattern of missingness that differs from classic random-edge loss and from full actor nonresponse. Related work on mixed public/private profiles emphasizes that privacy can still permit substantial inference from visible structure, motivating a discoverability-oriented perspective on partial observation. We operationalize network discoverability by comparing two edge-loss mechanisms on a large real-world social network (~10,000 nodes, ~95,000 edges), holding the total number of removed edges fixed at each sparsification level. In a baseline condition, edges are removed uniformly at random. In a privacy-realistic condition, a random subset of nodes is designated private, and only private--private edges are censored, modeling platforms in which private users can still be encountered via public edges, but their mutual edges are hidden. We track local cohesion (global clustering/transitivity), global reachability (giant component size and diameter), degree-distribution shape (power-law exponent), fragmentation (number of connected components), and mesoscale structure (Louvain community count and Newman modularity). Despite identical edge-loss budgets, the two mechanisms produce sharply different trajectories: random edge removal preserves the degree-shape parameter and delays fragmentation, but rapidly inflates distances within a still-large giant component; privacy-driven censoring accelerates the loss of clustering, increases fragmentation, shrinks the giant component more quickly, and substantially shifts the fitted degree exponent. These results align with prior findings that measurement error and non-random missingness can systematically distort topology and inference. We conclude that discoverability in partially private social networks depends crucially on privacy semantics, not merely missingness rates, and we discuss implications for robust measurement and reconstruction under correlated edge censoring.
items.authors: ZINOVIEV, DMITRY*; Li, Michael

items.title: Gene Co-expression Network Rewiring Explains Variation in Immune-Mediated Fibrosis in wild Stickleback
items.abstract: Fibrosis is a complex, multigenic phenotype driven by sustained immune activation and tissue remodeling, making it well suited to network-based and machine-learning analyses that capture non-linear gene interactions. Rather than focusing on individual genes, understanding fibrosis requires identifying coordinated transcriptional programs and network structures that differentiate mild and severe outcomes across populations. We integrate head–kidney and liver transcriptomic data with fibrosis severity, parasite infection status, and ecological metadata from wild threespine stickleback (Gasterosteus aculeatus). Fibrosis was framed as a binary classification problem, enabling systematic comparison of linear and non-linear supervised models, including logistic regression with L1 and L2 regularization, random forests, and gradient-boosted decision trees (XGBoost). Models were evaluated with and without parasite load to assess robustness to infection-related confounding. XGBoost achieved the highest predictive performance (AUC = 0.748 with parasite load; 0.727 without), demonstrating that gene expression alone captures substantial fibrosis-related signal. Using XGBoost feature importance, we selected the top 50 genes for downstream analysis and resolved gene identities via BLAST alignment and Ensembl orthology mapping. To characterize higher-order organization, we constructed phenotype-specific gene co-expression networks and per- formed differential network analysis, identifying 1,748 statistically significant edges distinguishing high- and low-fibrosis states. Community detection revealed 13 densely connected gene modules, highlighting coordinated network rewiring associated with fibrosis severity. These results show that fibrosis variation in natural populations is governed by structured, gene network dynamics. Newly discovered genes from this analysis can uncover novel gene regulatory dynamics underlying immune responses to inflammatory diseases for various species.
items.authors: Urmi, Tamanna*

items.title: The Extended Community of Diet Culture Around Eating Disorders: A Network Analysis of Reddit Forums
items.abstract: Clinical studies suggest a history of dieting is a known risk factor for eating disorders (ED), yet the relationship between diet culture and ED in online spaces remains underexplored. While ED online communities offer insight into psychological and behavioral experiences about the illness, broader dieting culture---often shaped by restrictive eating norms and body hierarchies---may inadvertently promote disordered behaviors. We investigate the structural and thematic connections between ED and diet-culture online communities by combining network analyses with large language model (LLM)-assisted topic modeling. We analyzed posts and comments from 12 ED and 105 dieting or body-image forums on Reddit. We constructed two forum-level networks: a semantic similarity network, where edge weights are defined as the average cosine similarity between Sentence-BERT embeddings of posts across forum pairs, and a co-engagement intensity network, where edge weights capture normalized cross-posting and co-commenting activity from shared users based on each forum’s newest posts and associated comments. To identify clusters of forums with dense semantic or behavioral connectivity, we detected communities by optimizing the modularity objective function using the Louvain method, selecting the resolution parameter that maximized modularity. We further quantified between-group connectivity using average edge weight and assessed statistical significance via a resampling-based null model. To interpret these clusters, we examined the prevalence of 13 topics using annotations by advanced LLMs, aggregated into forum-level prevalences. Results show significantly higher semantic similarity and co-engagement intensity between ED and dieting forums than random topics. The detected community structures on both networks revealed extended communities in which a subset of dieting forums consistently clustered with ED forums. Within these communities, ED and ED-adjacent dieting forums shared elevated discussions of 'nutritional restriction', 'food cravings', 'body dissatisfaction', and 'family or social relationships'. These findings highlight the entanglement of ED and diet culture in online discourse and underscore the need for nuanced public health approaches to monitoring not only ED but general dieting discussions in digital spaces.
items.authors: Qiu, Jiaxing*; Levinson, Cheri; Kropko, Jonathan; Henry, Teague; Gates, Alexander

items.title: The Diffusion of Causal Language in Social Networks
items.abstract: Causal reasoning plays a central role in human cognition. It facilitates the ability to infer, predict, and manipulate outcomes within the environment, which in turn lays the foundation for a uniquely adaptive decision-making framework that is crucial in navigating complex problem-solving contexts. With the pervasive influence of social media platforms, these online social networks have become critical for disseminating information, shaping public beliefs, and influencing daily life. However, no study has examined the propagation of causal language within social networks. In this work, we analyze the dispersion of messages containing causal language against those without, within the milieu of a large online social network. With the entirety of messages over one complete day on Twitter along with two additional days for validation, and with our validated ensemble method for identifying causal language, our findings reveal that messages with causal language exhibit a more extensive reach than those without. Furthermore, our counterfactual analysis demonstrates that the effect of causal language on information diffusion is truly \textit{causal}. Moreover, our findings indicate that messages incorporating causal language manifest a higher ability to spread to out-groups compared to those without. These novel insights reveal the unique diffusion pattern of causal language within social networks, and suggest a potential to mitigate the echo chamber effect, while causal language could serve as a bridge for diverse perspectives.
items.authors: Shi, Zhuoyu*; Morstatter, Fred

items.title: Cascade Processes in Directed Hypergraphs and the Architecture of the Chemical Space
items.abstract: Directed hypergraphs allow us to capture multi-way interactions, and the development of methodologies on directed hypergraphs is an active area of investigation within network science. In this work, we develop a methodology for the analysis of connectivity in directed hypergraphs with an emphasis on the propagation of local connectivity and influence. This methodology is motivated by the logic of chemical reactions and captures how reactivity amongst nodes and hyperedges flows and accumulates. We note that this methodology may also be applied in general to hypergraphs beyond chemically-derived hypergraphs, in particular those that model system interactions which require specific prerequisite configurations or orderings before influence can propagate. We further introduce graph-theoretic metrics on the nodes and hyperedges of the hypergraph based on the application of our methodology, which unveil key drivers of the hypergraph system. Our metrics develop the reactive capital of nodes and hyperedges in relation to their capacity for production, the ease with which they can be produced, and their general association to other nodes and hyperedges in the directed hypergraph. As a playground, we extend our investigation to the application of modelling of chemical reactions, capturing the properties of organisation within the chemical space. We compare the results of our methodology, and in particular the metrics derived for nodes and hyperedges, between a chemical reaction dataset and a randomised null model of this data. This analysis illustrates how our method can be applied and also allows us to outline properties of the chemical space and its connectivity. The method we develop introduces an approach to modelling connectivity in directed hypergraphs, as well as offers a perspective of the organisation and traversal of directed hypergraph systems. Our methodology is a system-wide, exploratory method that captures the growth of possibilities in a system. Our method further quantifies how richly a system can be expanded in its inherent connectivity via selected subsets of nodes or hyperedges and in what depth, breadth, and ordering this can be done.
items.authors: Medina, Sofia*; LaRock, Timothy; Lambiotte, Renaud

items.title: Mapping Innovation: Geometrics of the Adjacent Possible
items.abstract: The adjacent possible captures how new configurations arise from existing components [1], yet its underlying geometry has remained implicit. We argue that this implicit geometry is central to understanding innovation in sociotechnical systems, where recombination occurs within institutions, conventions, and infrastructures accumulated through past collective action, distorting the space of possibilities into regions of differing feasibility and value. Here, we develop a network-based framework that makes this geometry explicit by formalizing the adjacent possible as a recombination network, building on prior views of innovation as recombination search [2]. Within this framework, we conceptualize technological innovation as a network of technology codes, where historically typical assemblies–frequently co-used components–can form dense clusters that compress effective distances, distort shortest paths, and may give rise to curvature in the surrounding possibility space. We operationalize curvature by comparing least-resistance paths between newly combined components, weighted by past co-use, to their expected distances in the absence of typicality (Fig. 1a), capturing sociotechnical inertia as a measurable mesoscopic network property. Using U.S. patent data, we illustrate curvature with Edison’s 1881 electric lighting system, where accumulated precedents compress the adjacent possible and lengthen least-resistance paths relative to expected paths (Fig. 1b); at the inventor level, iconic cases reveal systematic differences in structural contexts, with Edison’s inventions more often observed in high-curvature regions and Tesla’s in flatter regions of the possibility space (Fig. 1c–d). At scale, curvature is consistently associated with innovation outcomes: inventions observed in higher-curvature regions are more likely to be high-impact “hit” patents, defined as those in the top 5% of ten-year forward citations (Fig. 1e), and to exhibit greater market value (Fig. 1f). Together, these findings highlight curvature as a unifying network concept linking typicality, search, and value, offering a network-science perspective on where successful innovations are more likely to be observed, at curved peripheries of institutionalized knowledge. [1] Kauffman, S. A. (1993). The Origins of Order. Oxford University Press. [2] Fleming, L. (2001). Recombinant uncertainty in technological search. Management Science, 47(1), 117–132.
items.authors: Yang, Seolmin*; Youn, Hyejin

items.title: Identifying disturbances in power grids using reservoir computing
items.abstract: SEE PDF ATTACHED Power grids are large, complex networks in which generators must remain synchronized to maintain stable energy flow. Disturbances such as generator failures or power surges can quickly escalate into widespread outages if not promptly addressed, as demonstrated by the blackout across Spain and Portugal in April 2025. This project focuses on the identification and characterization of such disturbances using only prior observations of system dynamics. Although various methods exist for detecting these events, accurately identifying and responding to them remains a significant challenge due to the grid's size, speed, complexity, and inherently dynamic behavior. We model the grid using the second-order Kuramoto model, which captures the coupled dynamics of synchronous generators. To simulate disturbances, we apply sinusoidal functions to the frequency of selected generators, altering the behavior of the network. The Western Electricity Coordinating Council (WECC) 240-bus dataset, provided by NREL, serves as our test system, containing 146 generators and realistic network connectivity. Using reservoir computing, we can identify which generators are disturbed as well as the nature of the disturbances. Reservoir computing is a recurrent neural network based framework consisting of three layers: an input layer, a high-dimensional nonlinear “reservoir,” and a trainable readout layer. In our application, the input is the phase and frequency data from a system of N synchronous generators. The reservoir then transforms this data into a higher-dimensional space, and finally the readout layer is trained to learn a mapping from the reservoir states to the desired output, the underlying disturbances applied to each generator. Preliminary results demonstrate that our method can successfully identify disturbances in the WECC power grid. Using a delayed control scheme, we can not only identify but also suppress the disturbances. Current efforts focus on testing robustness under limited observability and reflecting realistic operating conditions. This project addresses a pressing challenge in contemporary energy systems through the integration of rigorous modeling, data-driven methods, and realistic test cases. The results not only confirm the feasibility of this approach but also point to numerous avenues for future research, underscoring its potential to make a substantial and enduring impact on the field.
items.authors: Obermayer, Erin*

items.title: Measuring Gendered Access Costs to the Philosophical Canon via Steiner Trees and Path Analysis
items.abstract: Background. The history of philosophy has often been narrated as an intellectual network centered on “Great Men” [1]. Feminist philosophy argues that core values such as reason and universality have been historically constructed around implicitly male subjects, topologically marginalizing female philosophers [2,3]. While recent quantitative studies show that articles about females generally exhibit lower centrality in hyperlink networks [4], philosophy’s intellectual system is uniquely shaped by a privileged core: the “canon.” In this system, what matters is not overall network centrality, but rather connectivity to the privileged core—access to authenticity. This study analyzes gender bias in access to the canon by simulating access through graph structures representing connectivity, using Steiner trees and shortest paths. Methodology. We constructed a cross-lingual intersection graph using Wikipedia and Wikidata [5], capturing the 1-hop neighborhood of 130 canonical 20th-century philosophers and the lateral connections among them. We ensured cross-lingual comparability by retaining only entities present in four target languages (EN, FR, DE, JA). We model the network as a directed graph and compute two substructures: (1) approximate minimum directed Steiner trees rooted at philosopher nodes to the canonical set, representing the minimal knowledge set required to reach the canon; and (2) unweighted shortest paths. We define a centrality score C(v, l) for a node v regarding a gender label l ∈ {f,m} based on the proportion of shortest paths from gendered sources s ∈ Vl to canonical targets t ∈ T that pass through v. Results. Contrary to the hypothesis that female philosophers incur higher access costs, our Steiner Tree analysis reveals that connectivity costs are nearly identical between genders across all datasets. Females do not suffer from lower network efficiency; they achieve canonical access through equally efficient but separate subnetworks. However, path composition differs drastically. Shortest paths originating from female philosophers contain other female entities at a rate 3.4 to 4.4 times higher than paths from males (Fig. 1). Permutation tests confirm this homophily is statistically significant (p < 0.01). Ranking analysis further identifies that female engagement is routed through specific hubs (e.g., “Feminism”, “Simone de Beauvoir”) that are peripheral in male paths. Contribution. By focusing on connectivity to the canon, we elucidate the structural dimension of gender bias. Bias in the history of philosophy manifests not as a disparity in efficiency, but as a structural segregation of pathways. Female philosophers rely on a distinct female genealogy to bridge the topological gap to the canon. [1] R. Collins. The sociology of philosophies. Belknap Press (1998). [2] M. Le Doeuff. The philosophical imaginary. Stanford Univ. Press (1989). [3] M. E. Waithe. In Methodological reflections on women’s contribution..., Springer (2020). [4] C. Wagner et al. ICWSM ’15, 454–463 (2015). [5] T. Suda, S. Hashimoto, Y. Takedomi. IPSJ Symp. Ser. Jinmoncom, 117–122 (2025).
items.authors: Suda, Towa*; Takedomi, Yuka; Hashimoto, Susumu

items.title: Rising Dispersion in Country-Level Academic Mobility Rankings from ORCID-Derived Flow Networks
items.abstract: We study whether the global academic mobility system has become more stratified in recent years by constructing country-to-country mobility flow networks from longitudinal affiliation episodes in the ORCID Public Data File (2025 release). From ORCID affiliation transitions, we build directed weighted networks where edge weights represent cross-border mobility volume between countries (country codes normalized via ISO 3166-1 mappings). To quantify structural position in these mobility networks, we compute country scores using SpringRank, a generative, flow-based ranking method for directed networks. We then perform a temporal analysis using centered sliding windows (e.g., 3-year windows centered at each year) to obtain yearly distributions of country SpringRank scores. To summarize system-level inequality, we track the weighted dispersion (weighted standard deviation) of country scores in each window, using total mobility volume as weights. Across the observation period (2007--2025), we observe a marked increase in score dispersion beginning around 2020, indicating a widening separation between countries that consistently occupy high-ranked positions in global mobility flows and those that remain peripheral. Because the dispersion metric aggregates over all countries, it provides a compact view of system-level stratification beyond individual country trajectories. These findings are consistent with an increasingly uneven mobility landscape, where a smaller set of countries concentrates a growing share of high-rank positions and mobility influence. Our dataset release and end-to-end pipeline enable independent verification and extension of the analysis.
items.authors: Li, Yifeng*

items.title: Universal Subtree-Size Scaling in Hyperbolic Minimum Spanning Trees
items.abstract:
Hyperbolic random graphs provide a geometric model for complex networks with hierarchical organization and scale-free degree statistics. A single parameter $\alpha$, $0.5 \leq \alpha \leq 1$, controls how strongly nodes deviate from a uniform distribution on the hyperbolic disk; varying $\alpha$ changes the degree exponent by reshaping how much mass concentrates near the origin [1]. From such graphs we extract the \emph{hyperbolic minimum spanning tree} (HMST): each node connects to its nearest neighbor with smaller radial coordinate (except the node of minimal radius). The HMST retains the scale-free signature of the ambient network: the CCDF of HMST degrees follows a power law proportional to $k^{-2\alpha}$, hence it is strongly $\alpha$-dependent [2,3]. In contrast, we observe a universality phenomenon: the HMST { f subtree size} distribution is remarkably insensitive to $\alpha$. Across a broad range of $\alpha$ values, the decay exponent stays confined to a narrow interval, approximately $	au\in[1,1.09]$, despite substantial changes in degree heterogeneity. This suggests that while $\alpha$ tunes local connectivity, the hierarchical decomposition induced by the HMST rule is governed by a robust geometric mechanism. A stochastic geometric description yields an equation for the expected subtree size. Let $S(r)$ denote the expected size of the entire subtree rooted at a node of radius $r$, including the node itself. Each direct child at radius $s>r$ contributes its own expected subtree $S(s)$, giving  egin{equation} S(r)=1+\int_{u=r}^{R} K(r,u)\,S(u)\,du,\qquad K(r,u)=\kappa_{\alpha}\,e^{-r/2}e^{u/2}, \label{eq:Volterra_subtree} \end{equation} which is a linear Volterra equation of the second kind with a separable kernel. Its solution implies an asymptotic power-law tail for the \emph{subtree size distribution}: if $\mathcal{S}$ denotes the (random) subtree size of a uniformly sampled node in the HMST, then $\Pr\{\mathcal{S}>s\}\propto s^{-	au(\alpha)}$ for large $s$, where $	au(\alpha) = \alpha/(\alpha^2-0.5 \alpha+0.5)$ . Numerical simulations for $\alpha=0.6,0.7,0.8,$ and $0.9$ confirm the theoretical prediction, showing close agreement between measured subtree size distributions and the analytical exponents $	au(\alpha)$. These findings suggest that HMST hierarchies obey a robust, nearly parameter-invariant scaling law, and highlight geometry-derived trees as promising backbones for navigation and robustness studies [3].  igskip {\small
