(i) Monte Carlo Mean Field (MCMF) is a Monte Carlo approach that uses a mean-field assumption [2], while IBA is based on deterministic update equations with the same mean-field assumption. (ii) The Soft Margin Estimator (SME) is inspired by Approximate Bayesian Computing with the key parameter a>0 [3]. Smaller values of a place more relative weight on simulation outcomes that match the observed snapshot more closely. Under the SI model, SME converges theoretically to optimal detection performance as $n\to\infty$ and $a\to 0$, effectively approaching direct Monte Carlo. (iii) A standard Graph Neural Network (GNN) using graph convolutional layers [4] which, in case of a temporal network, is trained on the static network projection. Figure 1 shows the first empirically demonstrated upper performance bound for the source detection problem, attained by SME on two small networks with progressively decreasing values of a (panels a-b). However, the number of simulations per node required to reach this performance plateau grows exponentially with network size, making it intractable for empirical networks (panels c-e). In contrast, MCMF and IBA perform reasonably well with little or no training data, respectively, but remain sub-optimal. Most strikingly, GNNs achieve near-optimal performance while also scaling rapidly.
items.authors: Brack, Nathan*

items.title: Graph-aware proper scoring rules for node selection tasks
items.abstract: Let $G=(V,E)$ be a graph with $N=|V|$ nodes. A \emph{node selection task} on $G$ consists of inferring a single node $i\in V$. This subsumes important graph learning problems such as epidemic source detection, influence maximisation, or fault detection. Any edge selection task can also be recast as a node selection task on the line graph. Many approaches to such problems are probabilistic (e.g., Monte Carlo methods or graph neural networks), which means they estimate $i$ by providing a probability distribution $\vec{p}\in\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^N$, $\|\vec{p}\|_1=1$, over $V$. Standard evaluation metrics in this setting such as top-$K$ error rate or the rank of $i$ are unsatisfactory for at least two reasons: (a)~these metrics do not take into account the graph structure when assessing how closely a prediction matches the observed true node, and (b)~they do not penalise unjustified overconfidence appropriately. We address this gap by introducing two graph-aware scores for node selection tasks: firstly, the \emph{resistance score} $S(\vec{p},i)=-\frac{N}{2}(\vec{p}-\vec{e}_i)^\top\Omega(\vec{p}-\vec{e}_i)$ where $\vec{e}_i$ is the $i$-th unit vector and $\Omega$ is the matrix of resistance distances between all pairs of nodes~[1]; secondly, the \emph{diffusion score} $S_\tau(\vec{p}, i)=\frac{1}{4}(\vec{p}-\vec{e}_i)^\top e^{-\frac{\tau}{2}(L-\mathbb{I})}(\vec{p}-\vec{e}_i)$ with scaling parameter $\tau\in(0,\infty)$ where $L$ denotes the Laplacian of $G$. We show that these scores are \emph{proper scoring rules} [2] in the statistical sense, i.e., they are optimised when $\vec{p}$ coincides with the true distribution of $i$, discouraging unjustified overconfidence. On the complete graph both scores reduce to the well-known multi-class Brier score for $N$ independent classes. The resistance score admits an intuitive interpretation in terms of the recently introduced variance of Devriendt et al. [1] and reduces on the path graph $P_N$ to the standard ranked probability score for ordinal data [3]. The diffusion score, meanwhile, behaves like the Brier score at different scales: it coincides with the Brier score for $\tau\to 0$, while for $\tau\to\infty$ it only assesses whether $\vec{p}$ is concentrated on the correct global cluster. In this sense, it connects to recent work on Laplacian coarse-graining [4]. Figure~\ref{fig:fig1} illustrates the improved discriminative ability of our scores in a toy example. Further simulation experiments for source detection on empirical networks demonstrate that our scores successfully distinguish between models that traditional metrics fail to separate. Moreover, we demonstrate that both scores can be efficiently approximated even for very large networks using techniques based on Krylov subspaces and Laplacian solvers as explored in [5]. Their use as loss functions in graph neural networks is left for future work.
items.authors: Hilfiker, Lorenz*; Brack, Nathan; Sterchi, Martin

items.title: Homophily and Classroom Composition in Adolescent Friendship Networks: Evidence from the Czech Republic
items.abstract: As adolescents spend a substantial portion of their daily lives in schools, peer relationships formed in classrooms play a central role in shaping their social, emotional, and academic development. Classroom friendships influence well-being, school engagement, and achievement, and they also provide early foundations for later-life weak ties with long-term social and economic relevance [3,4]. One of the most robust organizing principles of these networks is homophily—the tendency to form ties with similar others—particularly along dimensions such as gender, ethnicity, and socioeconomic status (SES). Despite its centrality, empirical evidence on how classroom composition moderates homophily remains inconsistent. Contact theory predicts that increased diversity promotes intergroup ties and weakens homophilic tendencies, whereas conflict theory suggests that larger or more salient minority presence intensifies within-group bonding and intergroup separation [1,2]. Existing studies provide support for both perspectives, pointing to a need for integrative network-based approaches capable of disentangling individual-level tendencies from contextual effects. We address this gap using a large-scale, multi-dataset analysis of friendship networks among 4,697 students nested in 228 lower-secondary classrooms in the Czech Republic, from six independent studies, including a nationally representative sample. Focusing on gender, ethnicity, and SES, we apply an attractiveness–mixing model [5] to estimate group-specific homophily while accounting for individual differences in tie attractiveness. Results from the studies are synthesized using Bayesian meta-analysis and meta-regression, allowing us to assess robustness across datasets and to evaluate classroom composition as a systematic moderator of homophilic tendencies. Our findings reveal substantial heterogeneity in homophily across social dimensions. Gender homophily is largely symmetric for boys and girls, while ethnic homophily is stronger among majority (Czech) students than among ethnic minorities. High-SES students exhibit pronounced homophilic tendencies, whereas low-SES students do not. Classroom composition plays a decisive role: a higher proportion of girls amplifies girl–girl homophily and attenuates boy–boy homophily, while a higher proportion of ethnic minority students increases ethnic homophily for both majority and minority students. These results reconcile elements of contact and conflict theories and demonstrate how meso-level network environments condition micro-level tie formation in adolescent social networks.
items.authors: Cartier van Dissel, Mauritz*; Lintner, Tomáš; Martin-Gutierrez, Samuel; Karimi, Fariba

items.title: Pairwise and higher-order triads in neuronal activity networks
items.abstract: In this study, we investigate the nature of the interactions between neurons in the visual system. To this end, we considered neuronal spiking activity from the Neuropixels Visual Coding dataset (n=32 mice) of the Allen Brain Institute. We focus on primary visual area (VISp) and secondary visual areas, also referred to as "higher-order" because they receive direct input from VISp, exchange information among themselves, and send outputs to multimodal and motor-related regions. First, we extract functional higher-order networks from multivariate time series of neuronal spiking activity. Here, we employ a novel symbolic Bayesian approach that identifies higher-order relations in multivariate time series by mapping the original signals into symbolic sequences. Using this method, we extract 2- and 3-motifs, represented as 2- and 3-hyperedges in the functional hypergraph. Then, we compute the fraction of higher-order triads (3-motifs, i.e., closed triangles) relative to pairwise triads (3-cliques, i.e., open triangles). Specifically, the three neurons may all belong to the same area 'A', to two different areas 'A' and 'B', or to three distinct areas 'A', 'B', and 'C'. Results show that neurons in the secondary areas of the visual system tend to exhibit a relatively larger fraction of higher-order triads compared to neurons in the primary visual area. However, when looking at interactions between primary and secondary areas, we found that higher-order triads are significantly more represented when they involve two neurons in the same area, whether primary or secondary. These findings support a potential role of common-input schemes in the origin of higher-order interactions in the brain.
items.authors: Civilini, Andrea*; Latora, Vito; De Vico Fallani, Fabrizio

items.title: Iterative AI CV optimisation drives similarity collapse and ranking instability in job matching
items.abstract: The abstract is in the attachments.
items.authors: Di Vece, Marzio *; Giannotti, Fosca; Pedreschi, Dino; Pappalardo, Luca

items.title: Emergence of giant component in percolated artificial neural networks
items.abstract: Despite the fact that major recent artificial intelligence breakthroughs rely on the introduction of new architectures [1], from a theoretical perspective, the structure-function relationship of such networks remains largely unexplored. In this work, we explore how sparsity affects functional robustness, particularly in the context of pruning techniques designed to reduce parameter count. Recent studies have demonstrated that training deep learning models can induce heavy-tailed degree distributions [2, 3], which are known to significantly influence network dynamics. These distributions are revealed by diverse pruning strategies. We explore the implications of such an observation through the framework of percolation theory applied to the MNIST dataset (Fig. 1a). Our results show that the formation of a giant connected component systematically precedes the recovery of network performance. Specifically, percolating edges in descending order of weight accelerates the emergence of a giant component compared to random Bernoulli percolation. This phenomenon correlates with an early recovery of network performance (Fig. 1b,c). Notably, by adapting the theoretical percolation threshold for random networks, we derived an accurate analytical prediction of the percolation threshold in layered networks. We show that in networks composed of successive same-sized layers, the threshold stays independent of the number of layers in the limit of large width compared to the number of layers. Thus, the threshold only depends on the width of the network and is given by ρ = 1/2n where n is the number of nodes of each layer (Fig. 1c, top panel). Our findings suggest that learning inherently promotes sparse and robust structures, offering insights into the implicit architectural biases of trained networks.
items.authors: Pontlevy, Adrien*; De Vico Fallani, Fabrizio

items.title: Structural reducibility of hypergraphs
items.abstract: Higher-order interactions provide a nuanced understanding of the relational structure of complex systems beyond traditional pairwise interactions. However, higher-order network analyses also incur more cumbersome interpretations and greater computational demands than their pairwise counterparts. Here we present an information-theoretic framework for determining the extent to which a hypergraph representation of a networked system is structurally redundant, and for identifying its most critical higher orders of interaction that allow us to remove these redundancies while preserving essential higher-order structure.
items.authors: Kirkley, Alec*; Felippe, Helcio; Battiston, Federico

items.title: Investigating the Effects of EEG Volume Conduction on Higher Order Interactions
items.abstract: Volume conduction refers to the spread of electrical fields through the conductive tissues of the head, whereby each scalp electrode records a linear mixture of activity from multiple underlying neural sources, preventing sensor-level EEG signals from directly reflecting localized generators [1]. While functional brain interactions are typically assessed using pairwise measures, such approaches cannot capture higher-order interactions (HOIs) that arise when the joint behaviour of multiple regions cannot be reduced to pairwise contributions. The O-information rate (OIR) enables the spectral characterization of collective brain interactions, distinguishing synergistic effects (OIR< 0) emerging only at the group level from redundant interactions (OIR> 0) explained by subgroups of regions [2]. Although the impact of volume conduction on bivariate connectivity measures is well known, its effect on HOIs remains largely unexplored. This work aims at investigating whether and to what extent HOI analyses performed at the EEG sensor level allow a meaningful interpretation of synergistic or redundant interactions in terms of the underlying interacting neural sources. Given M cortical sources as realizations of a stochastic process, Xt = [X1,t, . . . , XM,t], and L scalp sensors as realizations of Yt = [Y1,t, . . . , YL,t], source dynamics are modelled via a strictly causal vector autoregressive (VAR) process Xt =∑p k=1 AkXn−k + Un, where p is the model order, Ak is the M × M matrix of coefficients and Un are white and uncorrelated innovation processes. The mapping from source space to sensor space can be expressed as follows: Yt = LXt, where L is an L × M matrix representing the extent to which the sources are mixed [1]. Here, we investigate the effect of volume conduction on HOIs in two configurations of M = L = 3 interacting cortical sources, namely a common-child and a common-driver structure, by setting the diagonal elements of L to unity and the off-diagonal elements to α. Figure 1 shows that mixing induces instantaneous couplings that alter the OIR, introducing spurious spectral components and progressively shifting the interaction balance toward redundancy as α increases. Our findings suggest that stronger mixing drives the network toward redundancy-dominated time-domain interactions and introduces spurious redundant or synergistic spectral components.
items.authors: Spaccaforno, Salvatore*

items.title: Targeted County-Level Interventions Achieve Epidemic Control Without Statewide Lockdowns
items.abstract: When a new pathogen emerges, public health authorities must act rapidly to mitigate its spread while minimizing socioeconomic disruption. Despite extensive debate on localized epidemic control, no model-based study has systematically evaluated county-level interventions for statewide epidemic suppression in the United States. We present a metapopulation model that integrates county-level mobility data to identify epidemic hotspots and assess targeted intervention strategies for pandemic preparedness. We identify epidemic hotspots as counties that generate disproportionately large statewide epidemics when serving as outbreak origins. These hotspots align with population-dense and highly connected locations but provide sharper spatial contrast than traditional centrality metrics. Targeted interventions reducing the basic reproduction number (R0) only at hotspots achieve substantial epidemic control—reducing statewide epidemics by 60–90% in four representative states (Oklahoma, New York, Florida, and California)—without requiring broad lockdown measures. Hybrid strategies combining moderate reductions in R0 (10–30%) with partial mobility restrictions (30–80%) from hotspot counties, while preserving activity elsewhere, achieve control equivalent to full suppression at hotspots. This framework demonstrates that strategic, location-specific interventions can replace blanket pandemic responses. It provides state and county decision-makers with a quantitative tool for prospective pandemic planning, enabling rapid hotspot identification and intervention design grounded in empirical mobility networks.
items.authors: Das, Haridas; Hu, Tao; Santillana, Mauricio; Martins Stolerman, Lucas*

items.title: Integrating comparative protein biology with network medicine to elucidate the therapeutic role of plant chemicals for cardiovascular disease
items.abstract: Cardiovascular diseases remain the leading cause of death globally. Epidemiological studies demonstrate that plant-based diets provide cardioprotective benefits, yet our inability to predict precise molecular mechanisms limit precise dietary interventions. While network medicine offers a powerful framework for predicting how compounds modulate disease, the poor protein target characterization of plant chemicals creates severe data sparsity that prevents the systematic analysis of potential cardiovascular effects. We developed a computational framework that addresses this critical data limitation by integrating comparative protein biology with network medicine. This approach leverages the shared evolutionary origin and the co-evolutionary history of plants and humans, offering insight into how plant chemicals are recognized in human processes despite not being endogenously produced. Evolutionary conserved proteins and their associated functions between humans and plants account for 17{\%} of the human genome, allowing us to use the substantial body of plant biochemistry research to expand phytochemical-protein interaction data in humans. We identify a conserved homology module of 5,077 orthologous proteins within the human protein-protein interactome, creating a statistically significant largest connected component for use in network medicine analysis. For ten commonly consumed plants, we identify their plant chemicals and their human protein targets. We find that their targets are enriched within the homology module, indicating their importance to the modulation of the plant-human homology module. Additionally, the homology module is enriched with genes from cardiovascular disease modules. While the homology module contains many genes essential to eukaryotic life whose alternations typically associate with pathophenotypically severe Mendelian disorders or death rather than chronic, complex diseases, these orthologous proteins also include less connected proteins that contribute to CVD pathophysiology. By integrating plant-human homology with network medicine we create a systematic framework for predicting how plant chemicals affect cardiovascular health.
items.authors: Sebek, Michael*

items.title: Higher-order information-theoretic organization of motor modularity in health and stroke
items.abstract: Motor coordination subserving even simple activities of daily living requires navigating a high-dimensional space of muscle activations through nonlinear and task-dependent transformations. Despite this apparent complexity, the human motor system achieves robust, flexible, and transferable behavior across a wide range of tasks and contexts. A leading theoretical explanation for this efficiency is the existence of muscle modules: low-dimensional coordination patterns that constrain and organize muscle activity (d'Avella, A., & Bizzi, E.; 2005). Traditionally, muscle modules have been extracted using linear dimensionality-reduction techniques, which provide compact representations but are limited in their ability to characterize multivariate and higher-order dependencies among muscles. Here, we adopt a higher-order network perspective to investigate muscle coordination, leveraging information-theoretic tools that explicitly disentangle redundancy and synergy in multivariate interactions. We analyze publicly available data comprising electromyography (EMG) recordings from 12 upper-limb muscles during 30 distinct motor tasks, performed by 23 healthy controls and 21 individuals with mild stroke (Wrist/Hand Fugl-Meyer: 14-20) (Averta, et al., 2021). For each participant, we computed the O-information, a multivariate measure quantifying the balance between redundant and synergistic interactions across muscle subsets (Rosas et al.,2019). From the top-k multiplets, we constructed participant-specific hypergraphs capturing dominant redundancy- and synergy-based interactions (Figure 1A). Interpreting redundancy as reflecting stable muscle modules and synergy as a marker of inter-module coordination, we observed marked reorganization in stroke participants. At the group level, stroke patients exhibited increased redundancy alongside significantly reduced synergistic interactions (Figure 1B), consistent with more rigid or stereotyped motor control. Crucially, at the participant level, this imbalance was highly heterogeneous (Figure 1C), revealing substantial inter-individual variability in higher-order coordination patterns following stroke—variability that may inform individualized rehabilitation strategies. Together, these results demonstrate that higher-order information-theoretic networks provide a principled framework for characterizing motor coordination beyond pairwise interactions, revealing clinically relevant alterations in the balance between stability and flexibility of muscle control after stroke.
items.authors: Deshpande, Raj*; Huber, Maximilian; Yarossi, Matthew; Petri, Giovanni

items.title: Breakdown and recovery dynamics of gene regulatory networks
items.abstract: Recent single-cell experiments on Escherichia coli revealed that both breakdown and recovery exhibit long-tailed lag-time power-law distributions, with the complementary cumulative distribution function (CCDF) displaying a power-law decay with an exponent close to -2, associated with persister cells. This behavior occurs across various stress conditions, including starvation and antibiotic exposure, suggesting the existence of a universal underlying mechanism. Assuming that living cells are complex dynamical systems whose survival under stress emerges from collective interactions within gene regulatory networks (GRNs), we combine experimental data, network theory, and dynamical models to study breakdown and recovery time based on GRNs. Essentially, gene regulatory dynamics arise from intrinsic decay, nonlinear regulatory interactions, and basal activity. We modeled breakdown and recovery as the weakening and restoration of regulatory interactions in a random network and the E. coli regulatory network. Numerical simulations reveal a discontinuous transition between functional (gene expression) and non-functional (no gene expression) network states, leading to hysteresis between breakdown and recovery trajectories. Remarkably, the critical transition points can be predicted using the Gao–Barzel–Barabási universal resilience framework, which reduces the high-dimensional GRN dynamics to an effective one-dimensional control equation. Our results show that the complementary cumulative distributions of breakdown and recovery lag times emerge from network-level dynamics exhibiting power-law tails with exponents close to -2, in quantitative agreement with single-cell experiments. These results suggest that persistence, aging, and memory effects arise from network-level dynamics rather than molecular fine-tuning, which is analogous to interdependent networks. Together, these findings provide a unifying network-based explanation for stress responses in cells, bridging experimental microbiology and statistical physics.
items.authors: Aranha, Fellipe*; Levy, Orr; Sanhedrai, Hillel; Havlin, Shlomo; Medzhitov, Ruslan

items.title: Impact of top venues on creative careers in science and art
items.abstract: Career opportunities and recognition in creative fields are enhanced by access to top venues, such as publication in elite journals for scientists or invitations to exclusive biennials for artists. Yet, little is known about the magnitude of these effects or how selective access to venues reinforces discrimination based on gender, career stage, and nationality. We reconstruct the careers of over 3.4 million physicists and 1 million visual artists using annual performance data from Dimensions and Artfacts.net. Our results reveal a statistically significant venue-mediated effect associated with top venues in both science and art.
items.authors: Liu, Yixuan*; Dorantes-Gilardi, Rodrigo; Han, Larry; Barabási, Albert-László

items.title: DomiCoarse: dominance-based coarsening that preserves structure and dynamics in complex networks
items.abstract: The rapid growth of empirical network data has pushed many classical analysis and simulation techniques beyond computational feasibility, motivating the development of network coarsening methods that reduce system size while preserving essential structure and dynamics. Existing approaches typically rely on similarity-based aggregation, spectral embeddings, or community detection, often at the cost of interpretability, scalability, or dynamical fidelity. Here, we introduce a dominance-based coarsening framework in which nodes are hierarchically absorbed according to their structural influence (dominance), producing a reduced network that preserves both local and global connectivity patterns. Coarsening is performed through a new absorption mechanism whereby dominant nodes fractionally incorporate subdominant neighbors and inherit their interactions through a principled rewiring scheme, ensuring degree preservation and conservation of key structural and dynamical properties (see Fig 1a). Focusing on spreading dynamics (SIR), we show that the coarse-grained representation faithfully reproduces the temporal evolution observed on the original network, despite a substantial reduction in network size. In particular, the coarsened network captures both the timing and amplitude of the spreading process, indicating that the reduction does not distort the dominant dynamical pathways (Fig 1b). Beyond synthetic benchmarks, the dominance-based approach preserves key structural and spectral properties across a wide range of networks, enabling efficient simulations and multiscale analysis of large complex systems. By explicitly encoding hierarchical dominance rather than similarity, this new framework offers an interpretable and scalable alternative to existing coarsening techniques, opening new possibilities for the analysis of network dynamics at previously inaccessible scales.
items.authors: Engsig, Marcus*; Tejedor, Alejandro; Fernandez, Marco; Moreno, Yamir

items.title: The geometry of persuasion: Social judgement theory in a belief embedding space
items.abstract: How much can we be persuaded by an argument? Social Judgment Theory (SJT) predicts an inverted-U relationship between belief discrepancy and persuasion magnitude, whereby messages that are neither too similar to nor too distant from a receiver’s position can persuade the most. Yet empirical validation has largely been restricted to small-scale, tightly controlled surveys on specific issues. Here, we present systematic behavioral evidence for this prediction in a natural setting by integrating real-world debate behavior with a computational framework for measuring meaningful distance between beliefs and positions–belief embeddings. The embedding space functions as a continuous analogue of a belief network, where beliefs are connected by co-occurrences and embedding is trained by the empirical trajectories of users on this network. Using a large language model (LLM) fine-tuned on belief trajectories from 40,280 users across 59,986 debates on Debate.org, we map beliefs and individuals into a high-dimensional space where geometric distances encode the likelihood that a user holding one belief will hold another. We find that the relative discrepancy d_rel, reflecting the user's relative proximity to two opposing beliefs, robustly predicts both initial belief choices and post-debate belief changes. These changes take the form of persuasion (movement toward the farther belief, Fig.1b) and reversion (movement toward the closer belief, Fig.1c). Aggregating thousands of belief-change events, we observe a clear inverted-U pattern: although the probability of persuasion decreases with d_rel, the overall magnitude peaks at intermediate d_rel (Fig.1d,f), consistent with SJT. In contrast, the probability of reversion remains nearly constant, resulting in a magnitude that increases linearly with d_rel (Fig.1e,g). At the topic level, categories with moderate relative discrepancy exhibit larger magnitudes of persuasion, offering an explanation for why beliefs about “Entertainment”, “Arts”, shift more readily than those about “Religion” or “Philosophy”. These structured geometric patterns do not arise in pretrained base LLMs, which produce distance-insensitive predictions. This contrast demonstrates that the observed persuasion geometry is not an artifact of LLMs but emerges only when embeddings encode meaningful semantic and contextual relationships among beliefs. Together, our findings reveal how the geometry of latent belief space provides a quantitative framework for understanding belief change, offering a scalable approach to studying persuasion in natural environments and a foundation for modeling belief dynamics.
items.authors: Lee, Byunghwee*; An, Jisun; Kwak, Haewoon; Ahn, Yong-Yeol

items.title: Are node-activity models of networks graphons?
items.abstract: The abstract is provided as a PDF file. Due to certain limitations, we were unable to enter the abstract directly in the designated column. Please refer to the attached PDF for the full abstract. Thank you!
items.authors: ELDO, BISNA MARY*; MASUDA, NAOKI

items.title: Origins of the Fittest: Clonal interference in the World Aviation Network
items.abstract: Clonal interference, the competition between strains carrying different beneficial mutations, plays a crucial role in shaping evolutionary outcomes in asexual populations. Since the spread of new mutations is critical for CI, it is strongly influenced by the structure of the population. In particular, linear or planar structures restrict spread to slow, wave-like patterns, hindering adaptation, whereas long-range connections can rapidly distribute new strains, decreasing clonal competition. We investigate how this phenomenon unfolds in the the World Aviation Network (WAN), which shapes the population structure of microorganisms that use humans as hosts, including pathogens. Our novel analytical framework models the succession of adaptive mutations as a Markov Renewal Process. This process is built by leveraging epidemic modeling methods that have been very successful in predicting pathogen spread on the WAN, here used to model mutation spread. Or approach reveals how the interplay between mutation rate and network topology gives rise to evolutionary regimes: At low mutation rates, strains originating from globally central nodes have higher fixation probabilities, while at higher mutation rates, meso-scale and local properties become more important (see Panel A). Applied to the WAN, affluent, western regions are most likely origins of high-fitness lineages in the low-rate regime, shifting to more populous nations in Asia at high rates (see Panel B). Because of the logarithmic scaling of arrival time with traveler flux, reducing travel in low-travel, long-range links has usually a stronger impact on spreading behavior (per traveler). In the CI regime however, low-traffic nodes become particularly unlikely origins of new strains. The framework provides valuable insights how spatial structure shapes evolutionary outcome, with particular relevance for control strategies aiming to slow down the spread of high-fitness strains.
items.authors: Zachariae, Adrian*

items.title: Resonance in Social Media: Novelty, Network Position, and Diffusion in Online Mobilization
items.abstract: Research on framing alignment in social movements and protest mobilization has long emphasized the importance of constructing interpretations of the social world that resonate with broader audiences by appealing to shared interests, values, and beliefs [1]. However, despite extensive work on framing processes, less is known about the mechanisms through which particular messages achieve resonance [2], especially from a network perspective. Importantly, resonance has increasingly been understood as an emergent property of social interaction rather than a fixed attribute of messages, yet prior studies—while highlighting the role of emotional content, linguistic variation, and semantic novelty [2]—have rarely accounted for the relational structures through which these interactions unfold. To address this gap, we examine how semantic novelty and network position jointly shape message diffusion during large-scale protest mobilization. We investigate these dynamics using Twitter data from the 2019 Chilean estallido social, a wave of sustained, large-scale protest characterized by decentralized organization and the coexistence of multiple competing frames. This context provides a particularly good case for studying resonance as an emergent phenomenon, since no single actor or narrative dominated attention and message uptake depended on how frames circulated through the communication network. Using a dataset of 1,800,000 retweets from the #ChileDesperto, we construct a temporal interaction network of Twitter users, measuring message resonance as retweet counts, semantic novelty using a word embedding–based approach [3], and network position as authors’ cumulative in-degree prior to each tweet. Our preliminary results suggest that, while the analysis replicates previous findings of a curvilinear relationship between semantic novelty and diffusion, this relationship is systematically moderated by network position. As novelty increases, the relative advantage of centrality diminishes, allowing highly novel messages from peripheral actors to achieve levels of diffusion comparable to those of more central users. Together, these findings contribute to theoretical understandings of resonance in the framing literature and suggest plausible mechanisms through which peripheral actors may emerge as “crowdsourced elites”[4] in social media networks. [1] Snow et al. (1986). Frame Alignment Processes, Micromobilization, and Movement Participation. American Sociological Review, 51(4), 464–481. [2] McDonnell et al. (2017). A Theory of Resonance. Sociological Theory, 35(1), 1–14. [3] Shi and Evans (2020). Science and technology advance through surprise. ArXiv:1910.09370. [4] Papacharissi and de Fatima Oliveira (2012). Affective new and networked publics: The rhythms of news storytelling on #Egypt. Journal of Communication, 62, 266–282.
items.authors: Maldonado, Fabian; Gomez-Zara, Diego*

items.title: Who gets seen? Nationality bias in international art exhibitions
items.abstract: The global art world, despite its purported embrace of diversity and internationalism [1], frequently exhibits a discernible nationality bias. This phenomenon refers to the disproportionate inclusion, recognition, and visibility of artists and art originating from certain nations, predominantly Western ones, over those from other regions [2,3,4]. Existing research is predominantly qualitative or measures nationality bias using raw proportions unadjusted for population baselines, creating a need for large-scale computational analysis that systematically accounts for underlying artist distributions. We use publicly available datasets from [5] to examine patterns of nationality bias in global art exhibitions. Our sample consists of 22,338 artists from 177 nationalities who exhibited at least 10 times since 1985 in venues spanning 93 exhibiting countries. We first compare, for each country, the observed entropy of artist-nationality distributions across its exhibitions to a null model derived from Monte Carlo simulations in which artists are drawn from the global pool with equal probability at matched sample sizes. The divergence between the empirical entropy (blue line) and the random sampling from the global artist population (grey line) indicates systematic under-representation of nationality diversity in the global exhibition system (Fig. 1A). The inverted U-shaped trajectory of the empirical entropy shows that nations with the highest exhibition frequencies exhibit diminishing marginal returns to diversity despite their greater capacity. This naturally raises a question: To what extent does the over-/under-representation relationship between venue countries and artists' nationalities explain the systematic under-representation of nationality diversity? To address this question, we quantify the over-/under-representation of each artist's nationality within each venue country using standardized residuals from multinomial tests. This enables us to characterize the distribution of statistically significant home bias (Fig. 1D) and construct a network of over-representation patterns between countries (Fig. 1E). Through Monte Carlo simulations, we identify that home country preference emerges as the dominant bias mechanism (Pearson's r = 0.909, p-value<0.01), and community-based cultural affinities represent a secondary but significant influence that reinforces clustering beyond domestic favoritism (Pearson's r = 0.796, p-value<0.01) (Fig. 1B). Together, dual bias control yields a near-perfect fit, accounting for roughly 94% of the variance in nationality diversity (Fig. 1C). These findings suggest that policies aimed at increasing nationality diversity are likely to be constrained unless they address the preference-based mechanisms embedded in institutional selection processes and transnational cultural networks.
items.authors: Gao, Jianjian*; Gates, Alexander J.

items.title: From batch correction to covariate modeling in coexpression networks
items.abstract: Gene coexpression networks are widely used to model interrelationships between gene pairs in various biological contexts [1]. Because gene transcriptional activity can be measured under various conditions, technical factors can introduce unwanted variation in gene expression, which can spread through downstream analyses such as the inference of correlation networks. A standard approach for controlling these effects is to correct for batch [2], for example due to sequencing platforms and locations before computing correlations, akin to a partial correlation. However, this procedure does not correct for non-linear effects such batch effects introducing changes in correlations between genes, which is crucial for building robust correlation networks. In this presentation, we will discuss our recent work quantifying the magnitude and persistence of batch effects in correlation networks (Figure 1) even after batch correction with standard procedures such as LIMMA and discuss a method we developed called Coexpression Batch Reduction Adjustment (COBRA) that effectively addresses high-order batch effects by estimating association scores for each condition separately, for example, effects due to case/control as opposed to those induced by batch/location. We have applied our approach to build gene regulatory networks in thyroid cancer using The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) and have shown that the batch corrected networks estimated by COBRA reflect more meaningful biology for thyroid physiology than networks without batch correction. We have recently expanded our method to account for general covariate modeling in coexpression networks, which required a new formulation that results in positive semi-definite decomposition of covariate matrices, thereby expanding scope and interpretability of components decomposing coexpression matrices for each covariate. As coexpression networks continue to be used for comparisons between biological conditions either as a stand alone approach [1] or as input for machine learning methods [3], it is crucial to address unwanted variation induced by batch effects and isolate intervention effects to better estimate accurate effect sizes in health, disease, and due to treatment.
items.authors: Ben Guebila, Marouen*

items.title: Self-consistent analysis of the Higher-Order Kuramoto Model
items.abstract: The Kuramoto model on networks with higher-order interactions has recently been shown to exhibit bistability, explosive transitions, and rich dynamical behavior. However, most existing analytical approaches rely on all-to-all coupling or mean-field descriptions of network structure. While these methods accurately predict typical dynamics in the thermodynamic limit, they perform poorly for finite networks, specific network realizations, or particular oscillator frequency configurations. To address these limitations, we develop a self-consistent analytical framework for the Kuramoto model with higher-order interactions defined on hypergraphs. We introduce generalized local order parameters that capture the collective influence of both dyadic and triadic phase interactions and employ two complementary approximation schemes to obtain closed-form expressions for these local order parameters, which can be used to determine the global order parameter and the onset of synchronization. The first approximation, the Time-Averaged Theory (TAT), averages oscillator phases over time at equilibrium without averaging over oscillator frequencies, therefore capturing the effect of correlations between oscillator frequencies and network structure. The second approach, the Frequency Distribution Approximation (FDA), averages over both phase and frequency distributions. Using the FDA formalism, we perform a perturbative analysis near the critical point, yielding explicit expressions for the synchronization threshold in arbitrary finite-size networks and identifying a critical point that separates regimes with and without bistability. Analytical results show that triadic interactions significantly modify the bifurcation structure, introducing bistable regimes and hysteretic transitions. Numerical simulations on networks with homogeneous (uniform) and heterogeneous (power-law) degree distributions support the theoretical predictions and highlight differences in the applicability of the two approximation schemes. Overall, this framework provides a unified perspective on multi-body synchronization and emphasizes the role of higher-order network topology in controlling collective dynamics.
items.authors: Kumpeerakij, Chanin*; Restrepo, Juan

items.title: Behavior-driven dynamics of urban mobility networks improve epidemic forecasting
items.abstract: Urban mobility networks mediate infectious disease transmission by dynamically reshaping contact patterns across space and time. However, most epidemic models treat mobility networks as static or externally prescribed, overlooking how individual decision-making and local epidemic conditions reconfigure network structure. Here, we examine how behavioral heterogeneity drives the evolution of urban mobility networks and how explicitly forecasting these networks improves neighborhood-level epidemic predictions. Using New York City as a case study, we integrate survey-derived behavioral measures with high-resolution mobility data to characterize adaptive changes in inter-neighborhood mobility networks during the COVID-19 pandemic. Mobility networks are represented as weighted, directed origin–destination matrices constructed from aggregated, anonymized mobile device visits to points of interest (POIs). Behavioral attributes—including temporal discounting, loss aversion, and a newly constructed agency score—are measured through a series of financial and daily activity-based choice tasks for 816 individuals across 16 ZIP-code areas. These traits exhibit clear spatial clustering and are systematically associated with changes in mobility network structure, including edge weights and persistence across activity layers [1]. To model the temporal evolution of mobility networks, we develop a Graph Neural Network–Long Short-Term Memory (GNN–LSTM) framework that predicts weekly, activity-specific mobility matrices among 42 neighborhoods. The model integrates historical network states, local COVID-19 incidence, behavioral attributes, and socioeconomic covariates, enabling forecasting of mobility networks under changing epidemic conditions. The predicted mobility networks are then embedded within a networked SEIRV model to simulate disease transmission dynamics [2]. Retrospective analyses show that explicitly forecasting mobility networks—rather than relying on static or lagged flows—significantly improves short-term (1–4 week) epidemic forecasts, particularly during periods of rapid behavioral adaptation. Together, these findings demonstrate that mobility networks are not passive or static structures, but adaptive systems shaped by behavioral responses and epidemic pressure. Incorporating behavior-driven mobility network dynamics provides a principled pathway for improving epidemic forecasting and offers a general framework for studying coevolving networks in human–disease systems. [1] Yao, Qing, et al. "Person, place, or preference? Combining subjective and objective decision agency with choice preferences predicts behavior during major shifts in human mobility." medRxiv (2025): 2025-09. [2] Zhang, Renquan, et al. "Behavior-driven forecasts of neighborhood-level COVID-19 spread in New York City." PLOS Computational Biology 21.4 (2025): e1012979.
items.authors: Yao, Qing; Ashcroft-Jones, Sarah; Yang, Wan; Ruggeri, Kai; Pei, Sen*

items.title: Detection of Anomalous Delays in Real-time New York City Subway Data
items.abstract: The New York City subway, operated by the Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA), is one of the largest and well-used subway systems in the world, with around 472 stations, 22 train lines, and daily ridership of more than 3 million people [1]. As with any major transit system, delays on the subway are inevitable. Despite recent investments, as of 2024 the Office of the New York State Comptroller reported that about 18\% of trains did not reach their destination on time, with the majority of delays caused by infrastructure failures, planned maintenance work, and delays from police actions/medical responses [2]. The subway also has to contend with flooding from major rain events, which are anticipated to increase in frequency and severity due to climate change. When stations and lines are disrupted, MTA operators must make critical decisions about how to minimize system-wide impact and guarantee the safety of riders. These decisions must be made by human operators, who combine their experience and knowledge of the subway system with insights from computer-aided data analysis to make decisions. Our goal in this work is to use public subway schedule and real-time data to build a network-informed anomaly detection model for delays that not only identifies whether trains are on time, but also predicts the potential overall severity of a delay, accounting for cascade effects that individual local delays may have through the larger network. As a first step, here we show preliminary results on the pattern of delays on a typical Friday (October 26th, 2025) in the subway. We retrieve subway schedules and historical arrival and departure data from the public MTA Developer API [3]. In Figure 1 we show the subway network excluding the Staten Island Railway, which is not connected to the rest of the system by rail. Nodes are stations and colors represent the average arrival delay in minutes across all trains arriving at that station on October 26th, where darker colors indicate larger average delay. The stations with the largest average delays on this day were distributed throughout the system, with some near the extremes (Broad Channel on the Rockaway line) and others near the heart of the system (145th St in Harlem serving the A, B, C, and D lines). The middle plot shows a histogram of per-station delays, which follows a roughly normal distribution, with most stations having average delays of about 2 minutes, but a few having average delays of over 4 minutes. Since many stations in the subway serve multiple lines, in the right-hand plot we show the distribution of mean delay per station within each line, sorted along the vertical axis by the maximum average delay, shown by the right whisker. From this plot we can see that stations serving the F train had the widest distribution, with at least one station seeing a mean delay of more than 15 minutes and another seeing trains leave early on average, possibly due to previous delays. In contrast, the C train (8th Avenue Local) showed the closest to ideal behavior, with both mean and maximum station delays close to 0. As next steps, we plan to expand this analysis by characterizing delay patterns and correlations across different operational, temporal, and spatial resolutions. Then, we will develop models for anomalous delay detection that go beyond the difference between scheduled and real arrival times at individual stations to predict the potential for severe disruption across the network. We will connect ideas from the dynamics of cascading failures, path-based higher-order network science, and transportation engineering to develop efficient and effective predictive models that can be leveraged to assist in decision making for subway operators and riders alike. [1] MTA Website, Subway and bus ridership for 2024, accessed Dec 18, 2025. https://www.mta.info/agency/new-york-city-transit/subway-bus-ridership-2024 [2] Office of the New York State Comptroller Report 10-2026, Trends in New York City Subway Delays, September 2025, accessed Dec 18, 2025. https://www.osc.ny.gov/files/reports/pdf/report-10-2026.pdf [3] MTA Developer Resources, Realtime Data Feeds. Accessed December 18, 2025. https://api.mta.info/#/
items.authors: Ginige, Nipuna; LaRock, Timothy*; Hackl, Jürgen

items.title: Epidemic surveillance and control on mobility-driven metapopulation networks
items.abstract: Understanding how network structure shapes epidemic dynamics is critical for surveillance and intervention in spatially structured populations. Human mobility generates heterogeneous, directed contact networks that strongly influence outbreak timing, spatial spread, and the effectiveness of control strategies. Here, we present a general network-based framework that integrates fine-scale mobility networks with epidemic dynamics to support early outbreak detection and targeted intervention. We construct directed, weighted mobility networks at the Zip Code Tabulation Area (ZCTA) level using large-scale visitation and commuting data, where nodes represent communities and edge weights encode movement intensity. Focusing on pre-season mobility networks, we quantify how network structure—captured through centrality and connectivity measures—relates to epidemic outcomes such as outbreak onset, peak timing, and cumulative burden. These network features are embedded into a stochastic metapopulation SEIRS model, allowing transmission parameters and intervention processes to vary across nodes and time. Applying the framework to multi-year data on influenza, COVID-19, and RSV in Texas, we find that ZCTAs occupying central positions in mobility networks consistently experience earlier outbreak onset and higher disease burden. Incorporating network-informed risk metrics improves lead time for outbreak detection and enables the evaluation of surveillance placement and vaccine allocation strategies on the network. While Texas serves as a detailed case study, the framework is designed to be scalable and transferable across regions and pathogens. Overall, our results demonstrate how combining mobility-driven network structure with epidemic dynamics provides a unified, generalizable approach to surveillance and control on spatial contact networks. This work highlights the role of pre-season network organization in shaping epidemic trajectories and offers a generalizable methodology for network-based public health interventions.
items.authors: Herrera Diestra, Jose*

items.title: Designing interlayer coupling in multilayer networks of the human musculoskeletal system
items.abstract: The musculoskeletal system is characterized by the coexistence of diverse and complex interactions among heterogeneous entities. It involves multiple types of connections, and this heterogeneity highlights the limitations of existing network representations, such as bipartite and hypergraph models. Multilayer networks provide a natural way to address this limitation by incorporating interlayer dependencies. However, how to design interlayer couplings that preserve structurally meaningful differences between layers remains largely unexplored. In this study, we investigated several multilayer models with different interlayer coupling schemes and assessed their applicability to musculoskeletal systems. We compared full replica coupling, targeted partial coupling in which only bone replicas form interlayer links, and single-layer aggregation. Using a supra-matrix formulation, we analyzed the spectral properties of these models constructed from human musculoskeletal data and found that, unlike full coupling, partial coupling led to a non-trivial maximization of spectral entropy at intermediate interlayer coupling strengths. This behavior reflects a regime in which contributions from distinct structural modes are maximally differentiated, indicating a balanced interplay among heterogeneous components that would otherwise be concealed by full coupling or aggregation.
items.authors: yamano, hiroko*; De Domenico, Manlio

items.title: Bridging Silos: Visualizing Intra-University Collaboration Networks
items.abstract: Universities invest heavily in interdisciplinary research initiatives, such as cluster hiring and cross-department research centers. However, administrators often lack effective tools to target and evaluate these investments, and silos remain entrenched [1]. We address this gap by developing a visualization tool for analysis of intra-university collaboration. We construct internal collaboration networks by identifying faculty in the bibliometric database OpenAlex and extracting co-authorship relationships. So far, we have examined three institutions, including The Rockefeller University (Fig. 1a), Harvard Medical School (Fig. 1b), and Northeastern University. Specifically for Northeastern University, we manually curated a dataset of faculty and research units and built temporal network snapshots (Fig. 1c). Instead of focusing on cross-national or cross-university networks of collaboration, our approach reveals the mesoscale structure of collaboration inside institutions. The community clusters we identify align with departmental boundaries and confirm the presence of disciplinary silos. Our framework attempts to address a dual-audience design challenge: serving both administrators needing strategic insights and researchers seeking collaborators. The interactive prototype (Fig. 1d) implements Shneiderman's mantra [2] through force-directed layouts with filtering by unit, time, and collaboration strength, allowing comparison between departments and research centers. Preliminary findings reveal considerable variation in network topology. Rockefeller's laboratory-centric model produces tightly clustered communities, while Harvard Medical School's consolidated departments show more integrated structures with numerous bridging ties. Temporal snapshots from Northeastern University show how these patterns evolve (Fig. 1c), with inter-unit views (Fig. 1e) revealing collaboration boundaries across organizational units. Individual researchers also differ: some collaborate within their department, while others serve as cross-disciplinary bridges (Fig. 1f). These bridging positions do not appear on organizational charts but are key to knowledge flow and show untapped opportunities. This work shows a reproducible methodology applicable to any university. It supports current interdisciplinary efforts by making silos visible and uncovering hidden opportunities for collaboration.
items.authors: Liu, Zhuoqi*; Murray, Dakota; Lungeanu, Alina

items.title: Markovian ε-SIR on the complete graph
items.abstract: We investigate the continuous-time Markovian SIR epidemic with self-infection on the complete graph. We introduce a labelling of the state space based on Cantor’s zig-zag enumeration and show that, under this ordering, the infinitesimal generator of the process is an upper triangular matrix with a bidiagonal block structure. This structure allows us to derive an exact analytical solution for the time evolution of the state probabilities. However, given the complexity of the explicit solution, we derive an asymptotic expansion valid in the large-system limit, thus allowing a clearer understanding of the model’s limiting behavior as the system size becomes arbitrarily large. We then compare the exact dynamics of the Markovian SIR on the complete graph with the classical Kermack McKendrick (KM) approximation and also derive an analytical criterion to evaluate the accuracy of the approximation. Our analysis indicates that the KM model overestimates the number of infected individuals, in agreement with mean-field approximations. The discrepancy between Markovian SIR and KM is particularly significant near the epidemic threshold, consistent with theoretical predictions.
items.authors: Giovannelli, Benedetta*; Van Mieghem, Piet

items.title: Network-induced control of sustained oscillations in SIRS epidemics
items.abstract: Sustained oscillations in SIRS epidemic models are well understood in well-mixed populations when infectious and immune periods deviate from exponential waiting times. In contrast, the role of contact network structure in generating or suppressing such oscillations has received little attention. Existing work on small-world networks reports oscillations but does not relate them to effective transition-time distributions. This work investigates how network topology shapes oscillatory SIRS dynamics by simulating a stochastic SIRS process on self-similar modular hierarchical (SSMH) networks. These networks interpolate between random-like graphs and strongly modular, hierarchical structures while preserving system size and mean degree. A single structural parameter controls edge placement across hierarchical levels, reshaping effective distances between communities. Simulation results show that random-like networks support robust, coherent oscillations in global prevalence, whereas increasing hierarchical modularity desynchronizes outbreaks across communities and gradually destroys the global limit cycle, leading to damped or irregular fluctuations. These findings support an interpretation of epidemic oscillations as a synchronization phenomenon of individual SIRS cycles, where network topology promotes or inhibits global coherence by reshaping the time-to-infection distribution through changes in effective distances.
items.authors: Ropert, Samuel*; Perez-Acle, Tomas; Brockmann, Dirk

items.title: Fast inference for maximum entropy configuration models
items.abstract: Random graph models serve as null models to identify structural regularities in real-world networks. The Chung-Lu model is widely used but systematically overconnects hubs, creating spurious rich-club structures and inflated triangle counts. Maximum entropy models avoid these biases by generating maximally random networks subject to degree constraints, but their high computational complexity has limited adoption. We present FastMaxEnt, a method that accelerates parameter estimation for maximum entropy network models by 10 to 100 times relative to the popular NEMtropy algorithm. Rather than analytically computing gradients over all O(N^2) potential edges, FastMaxEnt estimates gradients by sampling a small number of networks from the current parameter estimate, reducing complexity to linear in the number of edges. Despite this approximation, the method achieves reliable parameter inference comparable to exact analytical approaches. We further improve performance through parametric interpolation, i.e., computing exact gradients for representative nodes and interpolating for others, along with a mean-field initialization heuristic that accelerates convergence. FastMaxEnt enables practical maximum entropy inference for large networks previously intractable with existing methods.
items.authors: Kojaku, Sadamori*; Li, Xuanchi

items.title: Exploiting Graph Structure for Author Verification
items.abstract: Text-based author verification has various applications, from detecting sock-puppets on platforms like Wikipedia [1] to detecting text generated by large language models (LLMs) [2]. However, the performance of text-based machine learning models trained on source verification tasks may be affected by topic bias in the text, where different authors within the same topic are confused as the same author, and frequency bias (i.e., label imbalance), where there is a skewed distribution in number of authored works [3]. We propose a graph-based method for improving performance of author verification models trained using text embeddings. We construct a graph from authorship verification tests of document pairs, with their verification scores serving as weights on edges between documents. Our method strengthens edges between graph communities and removes edges where there are weak connections between the subgraphs. Afterwards, each isolated subgraph in the resulting graph should correspond to an author identity. Our method exploits graph structure to mitigate effects of topic bias and label imbalance; it boosts AUC scores from 0.9 to 0.96 and needs 8x fewer training examples than current verification models to achieve comparable results. Our work also bridges the gap between author verification and author identification by mapping author identities to subgraph structures. We demonstrate that this can improve mutual-information scores by 36\% over baseline models in one instance. [1] Solorio, Thamar, Ragib Hasan, and Mainul Mizan.” Sockpuppet Detection in Wikipedia: A Corpus of Real-World Deceptive Writing for Linking Identities.” Proceedings of the Ninth International Conference on Language Resources and Evaluation. 2014. [2] Bevendorff, Janek, et al. ”The Two Paradigms of LLM Detection: Authorship Attribution vs Authorship Verification.” Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL. 2025. [3] Nguyen, Trang, et al. ”Improving long-text authorship verification via model selection and data tuning.” Proceedings of the 7th Joint SIGHUM Workshop on Computational Linguistics for Cultural Heritage, Social Sciences, Humanities and Literature. 2023.
items.authors: Smith, Ana*; Zeno, Giselle

items.title: When Fluid Dynamics Meets Epidemics: A Navier–Stokes–Inspired SIR Flow on Networks
items.abstract: Fluid dynamics and epidemic spreading describe seemingly different phenomena, yet both govern how quantities move, accumulate, and dissipate over structured domains. In fluid systems, momentum, pressure, and viscosity determine how mass is transported through space; in epidemics, infection propagates through contact networks under heterogeneous transmission, recovery, and intervention effects. While fluid dynamics explicitly models transport through conservation laws and flow equations, epidemic spreading is often described using compartmental models such as SIR, which emphasize local reaction dynamics at nodes. As a result, transport-related effects in epidemics, such as directional propagation, spatial smoothing, and inertia in spreading dynamics, are typically treated implicitly rather than modeled directly. Motivated by this analogy, we introduce a Navier-Stokes-inspired SIR (NS-SIR) formulation that recasts epidemic spreading as a physical flow evolving on a network. Building on the classical SIR framework, we define the \textit{infection velocity} $u_i(t)$ as the rate of change of infection at node $i$ and model its evolution using graph-based differential operators. As illustrated in \textbf{Fig.~1(Middle)}, the dynamics combine pressure-driven transport, viscous diffusion over network structure, external forcing that captures interventions or behavioral adaptation, and recovery-induced damping. Infection pressure is determined by a graph Poisson equation, which provides a closure condition that enforces conservation of epidemic flow across the network. Together, these components yield a network analogue of the Navier-Stokes equations while preserving epidemiological interpretability. The NS--SIR formulation naturally supports \textbf{data-driven operator discovery}. By decomposing epidemic evolution into explicit graph operators, including gradient, diffusion, pressure-velocity coupling, and nonlinear reaction, the model aligns well with symbolic regression and neuro-symbolic learning approaches. These tools can recover dominant transport pathways, estimate latent advection fields, infer effective viscosity, or identify additional coupling terms from simulated or empirical time series. This perspective enables researchers to assess when classical SIR dynamics provide an adequate approximation and when transport-dominated behavior requires a more expressive physical formulation. Under the assumptions of network homogeneity and flow conservation, the NS-SIR model reduces smoothly to the classical SIR ODEs, demonstrating that the proposed model generalizes standard epidemiological dynamics while capturing spatial, structural, and dynamical heterogeneity.
items.authors: Zhang, Zonghan*; Benbrahim, Marouane; Rashme, Tamanna; Mu, Lin; Chen, Zhiqian

items.title: Approximate Triangular Maximally Filtered Graph
items.abstract: Information Filtering Networks (IFNs), particularly the Triangulated Maximally Filtered Graph (TMFG) [3, 2], offer a solution by generating planar, modular, and sparse graphs (with 3N − 6 edges) that capture topological features such as cliques—often expressions of local shared dynamics [1]. However, the traditional TMFG requires the pre-computation and storage of a dense correlation matrix (N^2 coefficients). This memory and runtime complexity renders the method unscalable for datasets for medium to big data. We present a novel approach that leverages the TMFG graph construction principles while strictly controlling memory and runtime complexity to mitigate combinatorial explosion. We introduce three key innovations by pre-computing a k- nearest neighbors (KNN) graph as our initialization, implementing a memory management strategy to search and estimate missing graph edges only as needed, and finally we restrict further the search space of triangular faces by implementing “forgetting” heuristics. We evaluate our method on synthetic Gaussian mixtures, and our results demonstrate that we are able to preserve the desirable topological features of the original TMFG while achieving drastic reductions in runtime.
items.authors: Yelibi, Lionel*

items.title: Centrality as a form of higher-order cumulative advantage
items.abstract: Competition for attention is a defining feature of many social systems, where early success often amplifies future success through processes collectively described as cumulative advantage (CA) or the Matthew effect. In scientific citation networks, CA is often quantified through direct measures of attention, such as the number of citations a paper receives. However, attention can also spread beyond the node itself, e.g., a paper that is cited by an influential study often gains more visibility, and scientists gain popularity by collaborating with renowned scientists. While such higher-order CAs have been observed, it remains unclear to what extent they are common and contribute to the visibility of works in science. Here, we conduct a systematic study to identify higher-order CAs and their impact on the long-term citation trajectories of scientific publications. We utilize publication data from the American Physical Society (APS) spanning 1883-2019 focusing on papers between 1970- 2002 for the analysis. First-order CA was confirmed by plotting cumulative citations at year t against new citations received in subsequent years. Following this, we first test if higher-order CA exists by replacing cumulative citations with network centrality as a predictor of citation success. We divide the citation network into training and evaluation sets based on focal year t. The training set contains papers and their references published up to and including year t+5, while the evaluation set contains citations accrued afterward. This five-year “training” gap ensures that papers published in year t have sufficient time to form distinct local neighborhoods in the citation network, allowing centrality measures to assign differentiated scores. Centrality values computed on the training network for focal papers are then compared against citations accrued in the evaluation period, and success is measured by whether a paper ranks among the top 10% most cited papers in subsequent five-year windows. We control for first-order effects by comparing papers with similar citation counts at the end of the training period – a condition under which citations alone cannot predict future success better than chance. If network centrality can distinguish which papers go on to receive more citations among such comparable peers, this provides evidence of higher-order CA. We focus on PageRank (PR), which captures the essence of higher-order CA – a paper is important if it is surrounded (cited) by other influential papers – and second-order PageRank (PR2), a biased PR that assigns additional weight to nodes forming triangles. Both PR and PR2 outperform random selection, with precision increasing for higher citation strata (Figure). These results demonstrate that higher-order CA is observable even in off-the-shelf centrality measures not explicitly designed to capture this effect. With this, our focus shifts from detection to understanding: what structural features do these measures encode, and what do they reveal about citation practices in science? Here, interpretability and explainability are central. Ongoing work examines why PR and PR2 perform particularly well by analyzing the local structural patterns and reinforcement mechanisms they capture. We hope to report on these results and more at NetSci 2026.
items.authors: Gangadhar, Akshay*; Fujita, Yoshiaki; Kojaku, Sadamori

items.title: Network Topology Determines When Small AI Models Collectively Outperform a Single Larger Model
items.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) with a greater number of parameters are generally believed to exhibit better performance in task-solving situations compared to smaller models. It is hypothesized that the scaled-up number of parameters of larger LLMs allows stronger memorization and reasoning capabilities, which make emergent abilities more prevail in these models [3]. Inspired by the potential emergent capabilities in smaller models [3] and higher computational and infrastructure demands of larger LLMs [2], this work studies how smaller models can be more effectively leveraged to solve complex problems, with larger LLM performance as a reference point and accounting for cost-effectiveness trade-offs. We consider LLM-driven multi-agent systems (MASs) in task solving centering on network topology. Motivated by results from the complex systems literature [1], we investigate if and what certain topological structures can enable an MAS composed of smaller LLMs to achieve performance comparable to larger models across different tasks. We fix the number of agents (ten) and only vary the collaboration networks where agents are connected and have chances to interact with network neighbors. Initially, each agent works on an assigned task independently. In each subsequent simulation round, agents read their neighbors’ responses and update their own responses. After a preset number of rounds, a final answer is produced using 1) majority voting and 2) a ranker LLM who reviews all agents’ most-updated answers and generates a response as the collective final output. We utilize three benchmarks to measure MAS performances on tasks at difficult, intermediate and easy levels respectively, where difficulty level is determined by the overall and potentially outstanding performances of different LLMs. Six collaboration networks are assessed (Figure 1). Figure 2 presents the MASs’ performance under various collaboration networks on tested subset of tasks. The impact of network structure (bars) is evaluated through comparisons with: 1) the average performance of agents’ initial independent work (olive lines), 2) and the ranker’s response after agents’ collaboration (blue lines), and 3) the larger LLM (red lines). As expected, agent collaboration in a MAS (six bars in each facet) improved performance compared to agents’ initial individual work (olive lines). Facet 1 in Figure 2 shows the existing minimal performance gap between super and small models on easier tasks, as smaller models can sometimes even surpass larger ones on the test subset. The ranker (blue lines) consistently outperforms the larger LLM (red lines) on both easy and hard tasks. However, the scores are achieved on the intermediate tasks shown in Figure 2 when partial credit is not allowed. Otherwise, the same phenomenon also applies, indicating improved (though still imperfect) reasoning. Besides, collaboration impact fueled across networks varies particularly in harder tasks. Notably, random networks (BA, ER, and WS) produce no-less performance gains than the rest on all finished tasks. These preliminary findings suggest that small-model MASs have the potential to match or sometimes even outperform larger models with well-designed topological structures. The robustness of such observed performance gains will be further validated in future experiments across twenty-two network instances and four classes of LLMs from different providers (Alibaba Cloud/Qwen, Meta/Llama, Mistral AI/Mistral and OpenAI/GPT).
items.authors: Xu, Yilun*; Rocher, Luc; Dong, Xiaowen

items.title: From Structure-Coupled to Structure-Decoupled: A Unified ICA Framework for Dynamic Brain State Mapping
items.abstract: Dynamic functional connectivity reflects time-varying neural interactions constrained by white-matter architecture, yet conventional fMRI analyses largely ignore this coupling, limiting interpretability [1]. We propose a Structural–Dynamic Constrained ICA framework to jointly identify structure-coupled and structure-decoupled brain states in schizophrenia. Resting-state fMRI and diffusion MRI from the FBIRN phase III cohort [2] (274 quality-controlled subjects) were preprocessed through the SPM12 and FSL using the pipelines in [3]. Data were parcellated into 53 intrinsic connectivity networks from the NeuroMark_fMRI_1.0 atlas. Dynamic functional network connectivity (dFNC) was computed using a 30-TR sliding window, and structural connectivity (SC) was defined via streamline counts between network pairs. ICA of concatenated SC matrices identified six stable structural bases. A unified multi-objective ICA model [4] jointly maximized functional independence and alignment with these bases to estimate six structure-coupled dynamic states. Structure-decoupled states were obtained by regressing out coupled components using a GLM, followed by ICA of residual functional connectivity. Group effects were assessed using FDR-corrected GLMs. Structure-coupled states aligned significantly with structural bases (r = 0.48–0.61), explaining ~30% of dynamic variance. Structure-decoupled states showed negligible structural correlation but captured substantial residual functional variability. Schizophrenia-related dysconnectivity differed across domains, with distributed alterations in coupled states and more localized default-mode and cognitive-control disruptions in decoupled states. This framework disentangles structure-constrained and intrinsic functional dynamics, improving biological interpretability and sensitivity to schizophrenia-related dysconnectivity.
items.authors: Fouladivanda, Mahshid*; Iraji, Armin; Calhoun, Vince

items.title: Neural algorithmic reasoning for graph coloring via Lovasz-ϑ with contrastive learning
items.abstract: See attachment
items.authors: Le, Thien*; Weber, Melanie

items.title: Diverse Human Dilemmas, Uniform AI Preferences: Mapping Value Trade-Off Networks in Online Advice-Seeking Communities and Large Language Models
items.abstract: People increasingly seek advice online, not only from other users but also from large language model (LLM)–based chatbots [1], which now mediate how individuals navigate personal, relational, and professional dilemmas. Such advice rarely involves identifying a single correct answer; instead, it typically requires navigating trade-offs among competing values. We aim to characterize how LLMs navigate value trade-offs across different advice-seeking contexts. In this study, we examine the value trade-off structure underlying advice-seeking using a curated dataset [2] drawn from four advice-oriented subreddits. Each dilemma is represented in a structured format consisting of a real-life scenario and two alternative courses of action. Building on the bottom-up approach of prior work [3], we inductively construct a hierarchical value framework by aggregating thousands of fine-grained values extracted from individual advice options into progressively higher-level value categories. We find substantial heterogeneity in value trade-off structures across advice-seeking contexts: a women-focused subreddit exhibits the highest network density, indicating more complex value conflicts; women’s, men’s, and friendship-related subreddits show highly correlated patterns; and career advice forms a more distinct structure, with values such as security, respect, and commitment often in tension with autonomy, change, or opportunity. We then evaluate LLM value preferences against these human dilemmas and find that, across models and contexts, LLMs consistently prioritize values related to 'Exploration & Growth over' 'Benevolence & Connection'. This prioritization remains stable across dilemmas drawn from different subreddits, suggesting cross-model and cross-context consistency rather than domain-specific adaptation. Taken together, these findings highlight important societal implications as online communities and LLMs become increasingly embedded in advice-seeking ecosystems. This uniform value orientation highlights a potential risk of value homogenization in AI-mediated advice, raising concerns about how such systems may shape decision-making and normative outcomes at scale. [1] Kumar, H., Chahal, J., Zhao, Y., Zhang, Z., Wei, A., Tay, L., \& Anderson, A. (2025). When AI Gives Advice: Evaluating AI and Human Responses to Online Advice-Seeking for Well-Being. arXiv:2512.08937. [2] Bhatia, S., van Baal, S. T., Wang, F., and Walasek, L. (2025). Computational analysis of 100K choice dilemmas: Decision attributes, trade-off structures, and model-based prediction. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 122(17). [3] Huang, S., Durmus, E., Handa, K., McCain, M., Tamkin, A., Stern, M., Hong, J., \& Ganguli, D. (2025). Values in the wild: Discovering and mapping values in real-world language model interactions. Conference on Language Modeling.
