items.authors: Chen, Zhiyi; Choi, Eun Cheol*; Luo, Yingjia; Wang, Xinyi; Xiao, Yulei; Yang, Aizi; Luceri, Luca

items.title: Time over Time: Fundamental Patterns of Networked Persistence in Cultural Dynamics, Biological Evolution, and Persistent Homology
items.abstract: Time is a seemingly straightforward or at least obvious subject of network science, while at the same time subject to temporal lag in the history of network science, due to the relative simplicity of analyzing cumulative summary statistics, and due to the many possible complications in deciding how we should look at networked temporal phenomena. Even the most simple generative models, such as the ER or BA models do implicitly operationalize a dynamics over time. And many of the most simple iconic examples, such as Zachary’s Karate Club, may typically be analyzed in their cumulative static form, while clearly being the result of an underlying dynamic. At the same time, it took roughly the first half of the last 25 years of network science for the first seminal review paper on temporal networks1 to appear, eventually accumulating nearly 4000 citations so far, clearly indicating a pre-existing gap in research and a wealth of possible conceptual complications. Here, I present a hitherto unseen and surprising alignment of fundamental temporal patterns, that emerge from a perhaps not so straightforward but simple graphical projection of networked persistence, capturing temporal networked phenomena in bipartite situations, where both the link source and the link target partitions carry a temporal property, enabling us to plot so-called persistence plots, loosely speaking depicting time over time. Concrete examples come from a wide variety of domains, both aligning in principle and a selection of fundamental patterns, while also mutually putting in question the respective research domain focus. In the domain of cultural dynamics, we find fundamental complex patterns of networked persistence in publication-topic networks, i.e. publications appearing over time covering topics tied to other times. Another example from the domain of cultural dynamics is the collection lag in art museums, where we find a whole set of complex fundmental patterns, when we plot artist age at artwork production versus museum acquisition.2 In the domain of (biological) evolution, we find similar fundamental patterns in persistence plots, where the possible lifetime of a celular automaton before and after a single point mutation is plotted against each other.3 In the domain of persistent homology, where persistence plots are abundant, we find similar fundamental patterns, when plotting higher-order feature appearance vs disappearance. As I will lay out in my talk, the resonances and contrasts between these examples may take us closer to a better understanding of cultural dynamics, where most reliable data comes in the form of bipartite classifications and measures of similarity, while directed temporal network phenomena, such as inheritance, influence, and causality remain in the realm of imaginative modelling. [1] Holme, Petter, and Jari Saramäki. “Temporal networks.” Physics reports 519, no. 3 (2012): 97-125. [2] Canet Sola, Mar, Antonina Korepanova, Ksenia Mukhina, and Maximilian Schich. “Quantifying Collection Lag in European Modern and Contemporary Art Museums.” In Proceedings of the 16th International Symposium on Visual Information Communication and Interaction, pp. 1-8. 2023. [3] Wolfram, Stephen. “Why Does Biological Evolution Work? A Minimal Model for Biological Evolution and Other Adaptive Processes.” Complex Systems 33, no. 4 (2025). [4] Herbert Edelsbrunner, David Letscher, and Afra Zomorodian. Topological persistence and simplification. Discrete & Computational Geometry, 28(4):511–533, 2002.
items.authors: Schich, Maximilian*

items.title: Shared Leadership in Science
items.abstract: Scientific discovery is a collective endeavor, increasingly driven by teams rather than individual researchers. Yet, we almost exclusively observe the contribution layer of collaboration structures, and due to critical gaps in faculty employment records, we lack a systematic understanding of the role of shared leadership in scientific teams. Here, by linking faculty roster data with bibliometric information and HR records, we distinguish collaborations among faculty members from other teamwork configurations, enabling a large-scale analysis of multi-PI teams in science. Our analysis highlights the increasing prevalence of multi-PI teams across scientific domains, although their prevalence varies substantially across different fields. To quantify the advantages of such a shared leadership structure, we evaluate publication outcomes using metrics that capture a paper’s position and influence within the citation network. In addition to cumulative citation counts, which reflect the accumulation of attention, we assess more profound forms of impact that characterize paper novelty, including how one publication reshapes citation trajectories and combines existing knowledge. Results show that Multi-PI teams are associated with both higher scientific impact and greater novelty, but in systematically different ways across disciplinary contexts. The benefits of shared leadership also depend on team size and organizational arrangement: multi-PI teams within non-lab fields are more likely to produce high-impact work, whereas multi-PI teams within lab fields exhibit stronger associations with novelty. Meanwhile, cross-department teams within the same institution are especially innovative. Together, these patterns suggest that leadership structure is a consequential—and previously overlooked—dimension of scientific team organization, with direct implications for how researchers, universities, and funding agencies structure collaborative work at the frontiers of science.
items.authors: Shao, Erzhuo*; Tripodi, Giorgio; Qian, Yifan; Wang, Dashun

items.title: Destabilizing Community Detection via Node Injection under High Global Agreement
items.abstract: Community detection is a central problem in network science, and robustness is commonly evaluated using global agreement metrics such as normalized mutual information (NMI) and the adjusted Rand index (ARI). While these measures capture overall partition similarity, they can obscure substantial localized changes, particularly when relabeling is concentrated on a small subset of structurally ambiguous nodes near community boundaries. In this work, we investigate the robustness of community detection under network growth driven by node injection. We consider a low-impact growth regime in which newly arriving nodes have small fixed out-degree, no incoming edges, and do not modify existing links, thereby modeling decentralized and non-adversarial network expansion. Even under such mild growth, we find that the community assignments of boundary nodes can change substantially while global agreement metrics remain high. To characterize this effect, we introduce the concept of a manipulability boundary, which captures the trade-off between preserved global agreement and localized node-level instability. Using synthetic directed networks generated by degree-corrected stochastic block models, we evaluate Infomap and Leiden and quantify instability by the fraction of boundary nodes whose community assignments change following injection. Our results demonstrate that small amounts of node injection can induce pronounced boundary relabeling without a corresponding decline in global agreement, and that the magnitude of this effect depends strongly on the community detection algorithm. These findings indicate that robustness assessments based solely on global agreement metrics may systematically overestimate stability in growing networks, underscoring the need for complementary node-level evaluation.
items.authors: Kariyama, So*

items.title: Clustering Ethical Discourse: Semantic Network Analysis of Global South and Global North AI Education Research
items.abstract: Most ethical frameworks for AI in education have been developed in Global North contexts, reproducing well‑documented asymmetries in whose values and priorities inform AI governance , . This study examines how these asymmetries appear in academic discourse by analyzing how ethical dimensions are structurally combined in Global South and Global North AI‑in‑education research. We conducted a Semantic Network Analysis of 54 peer‑reviewed articles (2019–2025) that substantively discuss ethical aspects of AI in education, classified by authors’ institutional region (32 from the Global South and 22 from the Global North) . The analysis builds on the Contextual Model for AI Ethics in Education, whose ten ethical dimensions and operational criteria (Inclusion, Human‑Centricity, Transparency, Evaluation, Compliance, Sustainability, Explainability, Auditability, Plural Epistemology, Collective Participation) are used as network nodes . For each regional subset, we constructed an undirected, weighted co‑occurrence network in which edge weights capture how often pairs of dimensions appear together in the same article, and characterized network structure using clustering coefficient, weighted degree, and betweenness centrality. Inter‑coder reliability for the ten categories reached a Cohen’s Kappa of 0.82. The analysis indicates statistically significant structural differences between regions. Global North networks exhibit higher clustering (0.521 vs. 0.459; p < 0.05), with a dense cluster linking Evaluation, Transparency, Explainability, and Auditability, each pair co‑occurring in 16 articles. Global South networks, in contrast, are organized around Inclusion (weighted degree = 133) and Plural Epistemology (125), whose connection is the strongest edge in the corpus (23 co‑occurrences), with additional hubs involving Evaluation and Transparency . These patterns can be interpreted as reflecting more analytical configurations—where ethical dimensions are treated as discrete, modular components—and more relational configurations—where dimensions are treated as inherently interconnected—resonating with decolonial discussions of AI ethics and with the notion of an “ecology of knowledges” , . Betweenness centrality further reveals different “conceptual bridges”: Human‑Centricity connects otherwise separate clusters in the Global South network, while Sustainability plays this role in the Global North despite its low frequency. Overall, the results show how network topology can make visible epistemological structures embedded in AI‑in‑education discourse and indicate that ethical frameworks developed in one geopolitical context may not transfer straightforwardly to another, which supports the need for regional validation and for governance processes that take Southern epistemologies into account in the design and deployment of AI in education.
items.authors: Gomes, Carlos*

items.title: DQ4FairIM: Fairness-aware Influence Maximization using Deep Reinforcement Learning
items.abstract: The Influence Maximization (IM) problem aims to select a set of seed nodes within a given budget to maximize the spread of influence in a social network. However, real-world social networks have several structural inequalities, such as dominant majority groups and underrepresented minority groups. If these inequalities are not considered while designing IM algorithms, the outcomes might be biased, disproportionately benefiting majority groups while marginalizing minorities. In this work, we address this gap by designing a fairness-aware IM method using Reinforcement Learning (RL) that ensures equitable influence outreach across all communities, regardless of protected attributes. Fairness is incorporated using a maximin fairness objective, which prioritizes improving the outreach of the least-influenced group, pushing the solution toward an equitable influence distribution. We propose a novel fairness-aware deep Reinforcement Learning (RL) method, called DQ4FairIM (Deep $Q$-learning for Fair Influence Maximization), that maximizes the expected number of influenced nodes by learning an RL policy. The learnt policy ensures that minority groupsformulate the IM problem as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) and use deep Q-learning, combined with the Structure2Vec network embedding, earning together with Structure2Vec network embedding to solve the MDP. The main benefit of using RL for this problem is its generalization ability, wherein a policy learnt on a specific problem instance can be generalized to new problem instances without the need to learn the model from scratch. We perform extensive experiments on synthetic benchmark datasets and real-world networks to compare our method with fairness-agnostic and fairness-aware baselines. The results show that our method achieves a higher level of fairness while maintaining a better fairness-performance trade-off than baselines. Additionally, our approach learns effective seeding policies that generalize across problem instances without retraining, such as varying the network size or the number of seed nodes.
items.authors: Saxena, Akrati*; Kumar Yadav, Harshith; Rutten, Bart; Shekhar Jha, Shashi

items.title: Percolation process with cluster-size dependent reactivations
items.abstract: Percolation on networks has traditionally focused on permanent node failure; however, many real-world systems show recovery dynamics where inactive nodes can return to an active state [1], such as epidemic recovery and quantum error correction. To capture these phenomena, we propose a percolation model incorporating a recovery rule based on the size of inactive clusters. The size-dependency reflects the realistic constraint that small-size failures can be easier to restore than large-size ones. In this model, nodes are initially active with probability p, and subsequently, all inactive clusters whose size n is less than or equal to size threshold s, that is n ≤ s, are reactivated. We then measure the system’s robustness by using the size Φ of the giant active component and analyze the global connectivity. In Fig. 1(a,b), we illustrate an example of a reactivation process with the threshold s = 2. Specifically, if the size n of inactive clusters is less than or equal to 2, all nodes within the small clusters are reactivated. Conversely, the clusters with n > s remain inactive because their size exceeds the threshold s. We derive an analytical solution for the model based on a generating function method by mapping the reactivations of small-size components onto a semi-bipartite network. Our theoretical predictions are in excellent agreement with numerical simulations as shown in Fig. 1(c) for Erd˝os-R´enyi (ER) networks with mean degree z = 1.5 and various values of s. We find that the recovery of small clusters significantly promotes the formation of the giant component. Specifically, as the threshold s increases, the percolation threshold pc shifts toward lower values, and the size of the giant component Φ increases for a given p [Fig. 1(c)]. We also explore the interplay between the distribution of finite component sizes [2] and the reactivation process. Our results suggest insights into designing robust real-world systems by quantifying the importance of localized recovery mechanism to prevent global collapse in networked systems.
items.authors: Kang, JaeGyeong*; Min, Byungjoon

items.title: Quantum preferential attachment
items.abstract: The quantum internet is a rapidly developing technological reality [1], yet, it remains unclear what kind of quantum network structures might emerge. Since indirect quantum communication is already feasible and preserves absolute security of the communication channel, a new node joining the quantum network does not need to connect directly to its desired target. Instead, in our proposed quantum preferential attachment (QPA) model [2], it uniformly randomly connects to any node within the proximity of the target, including, but not restricted to, the target itself. This local flexibility is found to qualitatively change the global network behavior. We demonstrate that quantum preferential attachment leads to two distinct classes of complex network architectures, both of which are small-world, but neither of which is scale-free. Our numerical findings are supported by rigorous analytic results, in a framework that incorporates quantum and classical variants of preferential attachment featuring redirection in a unified phase diagram. Besides quantum networks, we expect that our results will have broad implications for classical scenarios where there is flexibility in establishing new connections. References: [1] H. J. Kimble, The quantum internet, Nature 453, 1023 (2008). [2] T. Zhao, B. Maga, P. Dionigi, et al., arXiv:2512.22542 (2025).
items.authors: Maga, Balázs*

items.title: Towards Systemic Risk Management in Large-Scale Networks with Limited Global Information
items.abstract: Numerous systemic failures of various financial, socio-technological, etc., networks comprised of many interdependent strategic components demonstrate difficulty to manage the corresponding systemic risks. This difficulty can be attributed to the inherently collective nature of systemic events requiring availability of global information to the individual components of the large-scale networked system. Based on similarities between systemic events and phase transitions in statistical physics, we suggest feasibility of efficient selfish systemic risk management due to the time scale separation at the onset of systemic events. Theory and practice of phase transitions tell us [3] that at the onset of a systemic event, system behavior is characterized by a small number of “order parameters” describing system on the mesoscopic scale. In a case of continuous systemic events, which are analogous to phase transitions of the 2nd kind, order parameters evolve much slower than the individual system components and thus can serve as global parameters to be used for systemic risk management. Some trusted entity, e.g., government agency, tracks “slow” evolution of a “small number” of the order parameters and communicates the “essential” tracking data to the strategic system components. Then individual components combine these data with the available local information to evaluate and manage their individual systemic risks. Note that while distributed systemic risk mitigation in a network with strategic components can be modelled as a non-cooperative game of the strategic components, the proposed “signaling” transforms the corresponding competitive, i.e., Nash, equilibrium to the correlated equilibrium. Our conjecture is that this transformation allows for a significant reduction in the Price of Anarchy (PoA) which quantifies inefficiency of selfish risk mitigation vs. centralized socially optimal mitigation. We confirm this conjecture on an example of a Susceptible-Infected-Susceptible (SIS) contagion.
items.authors: Marbukh, Vladimir*

items.title: Coupling Social-Context and Contagion Dynamics: A Generic Framework
items.abstract: A growing body of evidence shows that contagion dynamics are shaped not only by contact structure or biological processes, but also by contagious social dynamics that alter behavior, risk perception, and social context. Classic examples include needle-exchange programs that reduced HIV transmission without directly targeting infection, and widespread mask adoption during the COVID-19 pandemic, which lowered effective transmission by modifying susceptibility and exposure rather than curing disease. Across such settings, social context acts as a latent regulatory layer that modulates contagion spread. Motivated by these observations, we propose a generic modeling framework that couples social-context dynamics with contagion processes. The framework consists of two interacting layers defined over the same population: a social-context layer that captures the evolution of individual context through weak-tie interactions and threshold effects, and a contagion layer that models transmission along the contact network. Contagion probabilities are endogenously modulated by social context, creating feedback between internal social states and observable spreading dynamics. The framework generalizes classical contagion and threshold models and provides a unified structure for analyzing intervention-driven and socially regulated spreading phenomena across biological, behavioral, and informational domains.
items.authors: Gupta, Diksha*; Espinoza, Baltazar

items.title: Homophily without locality: Information topology shapes segregation in adaptive networks
items.abstract: Homophily is a pervasive organizing force in networks [1], yet it does not always take the same structural form. In some settings, homophily appears as proximity segregation, with dense local clustering and insulation between groups. Elsewhere, homophily appears as groups differentially occupying central, high-leverage roles within a shared network. Both patterns are widely observed, but it remains unclear why similar homophilous tendencies lead to such different outcomes. Existing network models typically explain homophily through local attachment rules, spatial constraints, or fixed preferences [2]. These approaches naturally generate clustering, but they provide limited insight into hierarchical or positional segregation without introducing additional asymmetries [3]. Coevolutionary network models show how competition for centrality can generate hierarchy, but they fail to explain how homophilous preferences interact with positional games [4]. As a result, the literature lacks a unifying explanation for how the same homophilous rewiring process can produce either local clustering or global hierarchy. We introduce a coevolutionary network model in which agents rewire edges to improve the value of information encountered through network propagation. Following the adaptive rewiring framework of Holme and Ghoshal [4], nodes occupy an undirected graph and update edges through local, degree-preserving swaps. Homophily enters in two ways. First, information transmitted across unlike nodes is down-weighted by a friction parameter (η < 1), inducing a row-stochastic transition kernel that penalizes cross-group paths. Second, agents evaluate information exposure relative to their own group identity, valuing encounters with in-group nodes more than encounters with out-group nodes. Information propagation is modeled as a discounted random walk with factor α. An agent’s utility is the expected discounted value of nodes encountered along walks originating from that agent. Increasing α expands the effective information horizon, shifting rewiring incentives from local neighborhood structure toward global network position. We show that the depth of the information horizon determines the structural resolution of homophilous constraints. When information is local (low α), agents minimize immediate friction through proximity segregation, forming dense, assortative clusters. As the horizon expands (high α), local clustering becomes insufficient to overcome the compounding transmission costs of η over long paths. In this global regime, agents break structural symmetry to form a high-capacity core, yielding positional segregation and hierarchical concentration. These results demonstrate that the form of segregation is not determined by homophily alone, but depends mechanistically on the topology of information that mediates competitive interactions. This provides a unifying explanation for how segregation can arise either as spatial separation or as control of network positions. References [1] McPherson, M., Smith-Lovin, L., and Cook, J. M. “Birds of a Feather: Homophily in Social Networks.” Annual Review of Sociology, 27, 415–444 (2001). [2] Gross, T., and Blasius, B. “Adaptive coevolutionary networks: a review.” Journal of the Royal Society Interface, 5(20), 259–271 (2008). [3] Holme, P., and Newman, M. E. J. “Nonequilibrium phase transition in the coevolution of networks and opinions.” Physical Review E, 74, 056108 (2006). [4] Holme, P., and Ghoshal, G. “Dynamics of Networking Agents Competing for High Centrality and Low Degree.” Physical Review Letters, 96, 098701 (2006).
items.authors: Rosen, Joshua*; Yamakaitis, Mark

items.title: Two synchronization processes in the second-order Kuramoto model with inertia
items.abstract: Synchronization is a fundamental organizing principle across biological, neuronal, and engineered systems---from firefly flashing and cardiac pacemakers to power grid stability and Josephson junction arrays [1,2]. While the first-order Kuramoto model has provided deep insights into spontaneous synchronization, many physical systems involve inertia that fundamentally reshapes collective dynamics. This is particularly relevant for electric power grids, where the swing equation that governs generator dynamics is mathematically equivalent to the second-order Kuramoto model with inertia. A critical yet underexplored question concerns how the shape of the natural frequency distribution $g(\omega)$ influences the synchronization pathways. In first-order systems, Gaussian distributions yield continuous transitions, while uniform distributions produce mixed-order transitions with abrupt jumps [3]. As modern power grids integrate increasingly diverse renewable sources, power injection profiles become more heterogeneous, approaching broad or uniform-like distributions. Understanding how such distributional changes affect synchronization and stability is, therefore, of both theoretical and practical importance. We investigate this question in the second-order Kuramoto model, $m\ddot{\theta}_i + \gamma\dot{\theta}_i = \omega_i + (K/N)\sum_j \sin(\theta_j - \theta_i)$, comparing Gaussian and uniform frequency distributions. Our analysis reveals two fundamentally distinct synchronization pathways. Gaussian distributions exhibit \textit{density-driven} dynamics: a dominant central cluster emerges early and gradually absorbs neighboring oscillators through quasi-static growth, producing a single abrupt transition in the order parameter. In contrast, uniform distributions follow \textit{velocity-driven} dynamics: multiple clusters form simultaneously across the frequency spectrum, with high-velocity clusters acting as organizing centers that partition the system into rotational domains. This mechanistic difference manifests in Devil's Staircase patterns---hierarchical arrangements of synchronized clusters with rational frequency ratios. Although both systems develop such structures, their origins differ: density-driven absorption in Gaussian systems versus velocity-mediated partitioning in uniform systems. Crucially, the velocity-driven mechanism renders uniform systems more fragile: perturbations targeting high-velocity nodes trigger cascading desynchronization, whereas Gaussian systems remain robust under similar disturbances. These findings carry direct implications for decentralized power grids, where frequency stability increasingly depends on high-power nodes with large angular velocities---precisely the nodes whose failure can precipitate system-wide collapse.
items.authors: Kim, Cook Hyun*; Kahng, Byungnam

items.title: Network Data Profile: disease progression network in Korea
items.abstract: Disease networks are powerful tools for identifying disease characteristics and capturing progression trajectories. However, existing disease networks were developed based on network medicine, which can limit their applicability to real-world public health purposes. To address this gap, we developed a comprehensive, unbiased disease progression network and will introduce this dataset at the conference. We used national health insurance claims data of approximately 50 million total Korean population, collected from 2011 to 2022. We used only the primary ICD-10 diagnosis codes and reclassified into 655 disease categories including death. To minimize bias, disease associations were calculated within age and sex strata. Then, Inverse Probability Weighting (IPW) analyses were performed with adjusting confounding and mediating diseases according to the DAG, income, region, BMI, alcohol consumption, smoking, exercise, and disability. Relative risk, attributable risk, and transition probabilities were estimated by IPW. We identified statistically significant disease pairs (RR > 1.5, q-value < 0.00001) and conducted two iterative rounds of IPW analysis to refine the network. The final disease progression network, established after the second round of IPW analysis, consists of 74,745 significant disease pairs. This includes an overall network as well as specific subnetworks stratified by age and sex. At the presentation, we will share how we developed the disease progression network, and node features and edge features. We believe this dataset will serve as a valuable resource for advancing research in network science and network medicine.
items.authors: Yoo, Ki Bong*; Lee, Kyung-Min ; Nam, Ji-Woong ; Liu, Yixuan; Jung, Ye-Won ; Oh, Jeong-Min

items.title: Global landscape of ai policy
items.abstract: The rise of artificial intelligence has drawn sustained attention from stakeholders and policymakers, who face concerns about its potential misuse alongside growing interest in leveraging its capabilities to enhance national competitiveness. Accordingly, a wide range of issues has now come to be addressed under the umbrella of AI policy, spanning regulation, innovation, and economic development. Yet, developing a clear and systematic understanding of the AI policy discourse landscape including how different AI policy discourses evolve over time and how their thematic emphases align with governmental priorities across countries, remains challenging. To address this gap, we map and analyze 111,375 AI policy–related documents from 136 countries published between 2000 and 2025, drawing on summaries collected in the Overton database. Using sentence-level text embeddings and BERTopic, we identify 23 distinct topics in this corpus, which can be grouped into four broader themes through hierarchical clustering of their centroids: sustainability transitions, technology governance, social inequality, and socio-economic growth (Fig. 1A). We then examine the temporal dynamics of these discourses by analyzing the distribution of AI policy documents across different topics over time. Over the past two decades, attention to economic policy and social inequality has increased markedly, encompassing issues such as gender inequality, online harms, and structural inequalities arising from technological transitions in the workforce (Fig.~1B). Next, we analyze cross-national similarities by computing average embedding vectors for each country' s AI policy documents. Countries in similar regions cluster closely together in this semantic space, and this regional structure remains even after removing all country names from the underlying texts, suggesting that it reflects similar policy orientations rather than simple textual markers (Fig. 1C). Finally, we construct a semantic axis contrasting discourses of data and technology sovereignty with those emphasizing openness and globalism. Projecting country-level policy embeddings onto this axis reveals that African countries tend to align more strongly with globalist discourses, whereas European countries are positioned closer to sovereignty-oriented framings (Fig. 1D). By delineating the global landscape of AI policy, this study provides a reference for understanding overall trends in AI policy discourse worldwide, as well as variations in thematic focus across nations.
items.authors: Kim, Munjung; Park, Jaehyuk; yoon, jisung*

items.title: A Multiscale Metric of Effective Distance for Predicting Epidemic Arrival Times
items.abstract: While historical epidemics were driven primarily by local transmission, modern outbreaks unfold through complex human mobility patterns spanning multiple scales, from daily commuting within metropolitan regions to intercontinental air travel. Previous work has defined effective distance metrics on the international aviation network using logarithmic functions of passenger flows along edges, initially based on shortest paths [1] and later extended to incorporate multiple paths and random walks [2]. However, existing implementations neglect the role of short-range mobility that shapes local transmission dynamics, especially in remote areas. We build on this work by developing a multiscale effective distance metric that incorporates short-range commuting fluxes, allowing local mobility patterns to influence network flows alongside long-range air travel. We also replace the assumption of a uniform global mobility rate with heterogeneous node-specific mobility rates derived from combined long- and short-range flows to better capture differences in connectivity across populations. We validate our metric using results from the Global Epidemic and Mobility (GLEAM) metapopulation model, whose mobility layers combine flight data with short-range commuting patterns collected from the Offices of Statistics of countries across five continents [3]. Visualizations reveal a sharper wavefront when nodes are arranged radially according to our multiscale metric (Fig. 1d-f) compared to the random-walk based aviation-only effective distance (Fig. 1a-c), reducing the width of this wavefront (5th to 95th percentiles) by approximately 27%. Our approach shows improved correlation between effective distance and derived arrival times [3], increasing R2 from 0.918 to 0.992 (Fig. 1g-h). These results show that integrating multiple scales enhances the ability of effective distances to reflect realistic spreading patterns. This work can be used to reconstruct outbreak sources and spatial transmission routes from empirical data, and dramatically reduce the computational burden of these inferences compared to simulation-based approaches.
items.authors: Moghaddas, Nima*; Ueda, Minami; Boudreau, Mariah; Chinazzi, Matteo; St-Onge, Guillaume

items.title: A Spectral Framework for Coupled Thermal and Reactive Transport in Pore Network Models
items.abstract: We develop a mathematical framework for \textbf{dynamics on pore-scale networks} that couple fluid flow, species transport, and heat transfer, motivated by fractured aquifer systems [1,3]. The network consists of edges representing pores, along which species and heat undergo advection, diffusion, reaction, and conduction, coupled through nonlinear pressure-driven flow and temperature-dependent wall reactions. These edge dynamics interact through \textbf{vertex-based continuity and Kirchhoff-type flux balance conditions}, yielding a fully coupled multi-physics dynamical system on the network. To enable analytical insight into the resulting \textbf{spreading and interaction of transport processes on the network}, we linearize the governing equations about chemical equilibrium and show that the coupled thermal–species subsystem admits a \textbf{vector-valued generalized eigenvalue problem} arising from linear stability analysis. The associated eigenmodes define a natural spectral basis for representing interacting transport dynamics across the network. Projecting the linearized system onto this basis yields a reduced-order model for modal amplitudes, coupled through pressure, temperature, and concentration variables at network vertices. We validate the spectral reduction against full PDE simulations on pore networks and analyze convergence and mode interactions as functions of key nondimensional parameters. The results demonstrate how complex, nonlinear multi-physics \textbf{dynamics on networks} can be systematically decomposed into interacting transport modes, providing a tractable framework for reduced-order modeling with applications to subsurface energy storage and reactive flow in geological formations.
items.authors: Gu, Binan*; Tilley, Burt

items.title: Effective criminal network disruption via the friendship paradox
items.abstract: Organised criminal groups are increasingly understood to be flexible, decentralised networks, rather than rigid hierarchies [1]. Such groups tend to be resistant to law enforcement attempts to disrupt their activities, and can quickly adapt quickly to such interventions. Moreover, law enforcement strategies to disrupt and dismantle such networks can be extremely resource intensive and differentially effective. In particular, approaches typically require \emph{complete knowledge} of the criminal network structure in order for disruptions to be effective (Fig.~1 (a)-(b)), which is almost never the case for covert networks. Here we introduce \emph{Friendship Paradox Disruption} (FPD), which leverages the well-known friendship paradox [2-3] to disrupt criminal networks (Fig.~1 (c)-(d)). Importantly, rather than requiring complete network knowlege, FPD relies only on \emph{local knowledge} of network structure, for example provided by informants or partial surveillance by law enforcement authorities. In this talk we discuss challenges of collecting data on and studying covert criminal networks, and introduce an approach based on exponential random graph models (ERGMs) [4] to generate synthetic criminal networks from limited data. We review typical disruption strategies and metrics for assessing their effectiveness, as well as presenting a novel disruption metric based on paths between nodes. We compare and contrast the effectiveness of FPD with other disruption approaches (Fig.~1 (e)) on synthetic and real networks [5], and discuss the implications of our findings for law enforcement.
items.authors: Mitchell, Lewis*

items.title: Are scale-free networks also timescale-free?
items.abstract: Our understanding of a physical system's dynamic behavior is often unraveled through its intrinsic timescales, as it responds to external excitation. The classic example is the harmonic oscillator, which is characterized by two timescales: its decay rate (damping) and its oscillation period. But what happens when the system is complex and heterogeneous, such as a scale-free network. Such systems lack a characteristic structural scale, but do they also lack a dynamic timescale? In simple terms, are scale-free networks also timescale-free? To address this, we investigate the network’s response to arbitrary forms of excitation. Our analysis shows that complex systems fall into distinct dynamic classes: Homogeneous dynamics, where, despite the scale-free network structure, the system's actual dynamics exhibit uniform timescales. Timescale-free dynamics, where the systems, indeed, lacks a typical timescale and, instead, features a coexistence of vastly diverse excitation lifetimes. Most excitations are extremely short-lived, but some live on for extended periods, sustained for orders of magnitude above the system's average timescale. These newly discovered timescale-free networks feature special modes, that the network is dynamically wired to preserve for extended periods. These modes, capturing timescale-hubs, decay extremely slowly, and are thus preserved for orders of magnitude longer than all other dynamic states. More broadly, our analysis offers a systematic translation of topology (degree distribution) into dynamics (timescale distribution), that helps us predict the system’s uniquely long-lasting vs. short-lived dynamic states.
items.authors: Barzel, Baruch*; Berger, Guy

items.title: A Diagnostic Framework for Sampling-Induced Distortion in Network Metrics
items.abstract: Empirical network data are almost always sampled, whether due to API limits, partial crawls, or missing data. The chosen sampling method and sampling fraction can significantly distort measured network statistics, so metrics computed on a sampled graph may misrepresent the underlying network. Prior work on graph sampling has mainly focused on designing and comparing algorithms that preserve individual statistics such as degree distributions, clustering, path lengths, or modular structure. Existing studies analyze how node-based, edge-based, and traversal-based samplers affect these properties, but they do not provide a single, interpretable distortion score that is comparable across metrics, sampling schemes, and network types. In this work I propose a simple diagnostic, the normalized distortion (ND), that quantifies how unreliable a given network metric becomes under a chosen sampling scheme and sampling fraction. Given a full “ground-truth” network with a true metric value (for example, the global clustering coefficient or assortativity), I repeatedly sample subgraphs using a fixed sampler and fraction and compute the same metric on each sampled subgraph. From these values I compute the sample mean and sample standard deviation, and then rescale both the bias (the difference between the mean and the true value) and the variability (the standard deviation) by a characteristic scale based on the magnitude of the true value plus a small constant to avoid division by zero. The normalized distortion is defined as the Euclidean combination (square root of the sum of squares) of this relative bias and relative variability, yielding a single dimensionless number that captures how far, on average, the sampled metric deviates from the truth and how unstable it is across samples, all on a comparable relative scale. I demonstrate this framework on four canonical random graph models: Erdős–Rényi (ER), Watts–Strogatz small-world (WS), Barabási–Albert scale-free (BA), and a stochastic block model (SBM). For each model, I consider four samplers (random node, snowball, degree-weighted node, and uniform edges with induction) and sampling fractions between five and thirty percent of the nodes, focusing on two metrics: the global clustering coefficient and the average shortest-path length. The results show that normalized distortion for clustering is often large at low sampling fractions in BA and ER graphs, especially under degree-weighted and uniform-edge sampling, while random node sampling is consistently less distorting; in WS networks, clustering is comparatively robust, with normalized distortion remaining below about 0.4 even at five percent sampling. Average shortest-path length is generally more robust (normalized distortion mostly below about 0.7), but its behavior still depends on sampler and topology: snowball sampling preserves path length particularly well in ER, SBM, and WS, whereas random node sampling can substantially distort distances in BA and WS, and degree-weighted or uniform-edge sampling show marked distortion at intermediate fractions. Overall, the normalized distortion framework turns sampling from a hidden nuisance into a measurable design choice, quantifying how much bias and variability different samplers introduce for each metric and providing a practical, topology-aware calibration tool for choosing metrics, sampling strategies, and sample sizes in empirical network studies.
items.authors: Pandey, Sriniwas*

items.title: Growing a Sympoietic Art Organism: An Experimental Framework for Tracking the Micro-Dynamics of Cultural Evolution
items.abstract: Empirical data recording how cultural artefacts, motifs, and lineages form in real time are of great benefit and interest in the study of cultural evolution. In this work, we introduce an experimental design (nicknamed “Sympoietic Art Organism” in a nod to Haraway) that attempts to richly capture the bottom-up emergence of a shared cultural heritage in a small, controlled setting, as well as the influences at play throughout its evolution. Our approach calls for a small community of artists to engage in active collaborative artmaking over several months to build up a shared creative commons. After an initial “seeding phase” round of art contributions to the commons data repository, participants are asked to contribute at least five new artefacts to the commons every two weeks, over a series of “co-creative cycles”, and with the requirements that each shared piece engage with one or more pre-existing artworks (that is, artworks from earlier cycles) and that we record these influences between works to a shared ledger. We, the ten co-authors of this study, took part in our own experiment over three months, growing a dataset of 411 original artworks, and a network of 725 verified relationships between them. Our experiment yielded a complete history of the creation of cultural artefacts (artworks) by each participant, and of all acknowledged influences from other pieces, allowing us to reconstruct the citation network of contributed artworks. By enforcing synchronous updates within each co-creative cycle such that participants only engaged with artworks from earlier stages, we were able to model the network’s growth as a time series. Our results are well suited for multi-modal analysis. Quantitatively, we observed the structural evolution of the citation network from disconnected nodes into a consolidated core as the system matured. We found the network’s structural dynamics were consistent rich-get-richer feedback, showing that preferential attachment emerged endogenously around specific hub artworks. Qualitatively, the artworks provide a basis for tracing a kind of semantic phylogeny through visual inspection and comparison of contributed artworks, to see how participants adapted, remixed, or subverted specific aesthetic motifs across generations. We propose this experiment as a proof of concept for a scalable ethnographic tool in network science. This approach provides a transparent environment for investigating the mechanisms of cultural inheritance. This work was initiated at the 2025 Complexity Global School, organised by the Santa Fe Institute and TREES group at the Universidad de Los Andes.
items.authors: Dazeley-Gaist, Phileas*; Camacho Cabrera, Gabriel; Thompson, Kyle; Arango Franco, Sara; Bulla Casas, Lina; Abd El Razek, Nadine

items.title: Human vs. Generative AI: A Comparison of Creative Strategies
items.abstract: The rapid advancement of generative AI has renewed optimism that creativity—central to innovation across domains from science to art—can be substantially expanded through human–machine collaboration. While AI systems can recombine vast bodies of existing knowledge in novel ways, prior work suggests that human creativity retains distinctive advantages, particularly in flexibility and contextual adaptation. To enable effective coordination between human and machine creativity, we ask a fundamental question: \textbf{How do machines (Gen-AI) achieve creativity compared to humans, and what processes underlie these differences?} We investigate this question using the Divergent Association Task (DAT) [1], a widely used and replicable measure of creative ideation. Analyzing over 5{,}000 human and machine DAT responses worldwide, we apply text and network analyses to compare creative strategies at both individual and collective levels. First, following theories of cognitive simplicity, we examine step-by-step vocabulary selection, operationalizing word simplicity using five metrics drawn from prior literature: (1) age of acquisition, (2) word frequency, (3) word length, (4) spoken-language tendency, and (5) written-language tendency. We track the first through seventh words produced in each response. Second, we examine categorical jumps between successive words, as larger semantic shifts are thought to signal greater creative potential. To quantify word categories and distances, we use WordNet [2], a tree-structured lexical network (Figure~1A), and compute per-response category counts as well as average, variance, and maximum jump distances. Finally, we examine collective behavior by comparing the total number of categories covered by humans versus machines. Our findings reveal three key insights. First, at the individual level, humans tend to use simpler and more common words than machines, yet collectively generate far greater lexical diversity. Humans use higher-frequency and lower-complexity words (1.62 vs.\ 1.98), but produce substantially more unique words overall (4048 vs.\ 749). Both humans and machines progress from simpler to more complex words over time; however, machines exhibit a steeper increase in complexity in later stages (Figure~1A). Second, at the categorical level (Figure~1B), humans use fewer broad categories per response but explore more fine-grained categories, and collectively cover a much wider semantic space than machines. Finally, while human word-to-word semantic distances are smaller on average (11.19 vs.\ 12.59), they show higher variance (6.22 vs.\ 8.97), consistent with Lévy-flight–like search patterns. Together, these results suggest that the advantage of human creativity lies less in individual output and more in collective variation, highlighting a key complementarity between human and machine creative processes.
items.authors: Yu, Yulin*; Shen , Anthony; Wang, David; Uzzi, Brian

items.title: Emergence of LLM hallucination in science: Evidence from non-existent citations
items.abstract: We focuses on the emergence of LLM hallucination in science.
items.authors: Wang, Yihe; Zhao, Zhenyue; Yin, Yian*

items.title: Genotype networks drive oscillating endemicity and epidemic trajectories in viral evolution
items.abstract: Rapidly evolving viruses use antigenic drift as a key mechanism to evade host immunity and persist in real populations. While traditional models of antigenic drift and epidemic spread rely on low-dimensional antigenic spaces, genomic surveillance data reveal that viral evolution produces complex antigenic genotype networks with hierarchical modular structures. In this study, we present an eco-evolutionary framework in which viral evolution and population immunity dynamics are shaped by the structure of antigenic genotype networks. Using synthetic networks, we demonstrate that network topology alone can drive transitions between stable endemic states and recurrent seasonal epidemics. Furthermore, our results show how the integration of the genotype network of the H3N2 influenza in our model allows for estimating the emergence times of various haplotypes resulting from its evolution. Our findings underscore the critical role of the topology of genotype networks in shaping epidemic behavior and, besides, provide a robust framework for integrating real-world genomic data into predictive epidemic models.
items.authors: Lamata Otín, Santiago*; Rotita Ion, Octavian C. ; Arenas, Alex; Soriano Paños, David; Gómez Gardeñes, Jesús

items.title: Construction of Hidden Metric Space with Community Structure
items.abstract: Recent research has demonstrated that many empirical networks possess a hidden metric space (HMS)—a metric space that underlies and shapes the observable network topology. Distances within the HMS generally reflect the similarity between nodes, making it particularly well-suited for network routing via the greedy routing strategy (GRS). When the HMS of a given network is known, the GRS can establish routes between any pair of nodes without relying on global information, a key advantage over alternative routing methods. Various approaches have been proposed for constructing the HMS, yielding different metric spaces such as planar, hyperbolic, and high-dimensional spaces. However, no principled method exists for selecting the appropriate metric space, and embedding a network into an unsuitable metric space can substantially degrade the GRS routing performance. This work introduces a hierarchical approach for computing the HMS of a given network. Unlike traditional methods that embed networks into a single metric space, our approach relies on the network's community structure to construct a hierarchical HMS, thereby avoiding the performance limitations imposed by a predetermined metric space. Fig. 1(a) illustrates the application of our algorithm to a US population model network, where the result is a two-layer HMS. We demonstrate that this approach can be applied to an arbitrary network regardless of its physical embedding, as shown in Fig. 1(b). Compared to existing embedding algorithms, the hierarchical HMS approach consistently achieves superior routing performance while significantly reducing computational time, particularly for large-scale networks. Moreover, for certain networks, routing performance with the hierarchical HMS surpasses that achieved using real-space coordinates, suggesting that the constructed HMS captures network structure more effectively than physical embedding.
items.authors: Xie, Zhuoxuan*; Motter, Adilson

items.title: Investment Flows in Venture Capital
items.abstract: The distribution of private investment across sectors has attracted sustained scholarly attention, in part because sectoral allocation is a primary determinant of aggregate financial returns and long-run growth. Yet, despite extensive empirical work, there is still no consensus on how such distributions emerge, nor on how profitable investment windows shift over time from one industry to another. A key reason is that industries are typically treated as discrete, well-defined silos, implicitly assuming stable boundaries and limited cross-sectoral spillovers. This representation obscures the underlying technological, functional, and informational continuities that shape capital reallocation and innovation dynamics. To address this limitation, we adopt a bottom-up approach to sector formation. Rather than imposing ex-ante industry classifications, we construct sectors endogenously by clustering firms along three complementary dimensions: similarity in tasks and production activities, following recent advances in task-based representations of firms (Acemoglu, 2024); similarity in products and capabilities, grounded in the economic complexity framework (Tacchella et al., 2012, 2018); and textual similarity in firm descriptions, capturing latent technological and strategic proximity (Gordon & Phillips, 2009). Integrating these dimensions, we embed firms in a high-dimensional network space in which distance reflects economic and technological relatedness rather than administrative classification. Within this network, private investment flows can be interpreted as a diffusion process operating over an irregular, evolving structure. Using three decades of U.S. private investment data, we show that the spatial propagation of investment is not well described by smooth reallocation or sector-by-sector rotation. Instead, it exhibits scaling properties and clustering patterns consistent with fractal geometry, closely resembling growth dynamics in disordered media. In particular, investment expansion follows dynamics analogous to correlated percolation, whereby capital propagates through locally dense clusters of related firms and technologies, occasionally triggering rapid phase transitions when previously disconnected regions of the network become accessible. This perspective reframes sectoral investment dynamics as an emergent phenomenon arising from micro-level firm relatedness, rather than as a sequence of independent sectoral cycles. Profitable investment windows do not simply “move” across predefined industries; they percolate through a continuously reconfiguring economic space shaped by task overlap, product complementarities, and shared narratives. As a result, sectoral boundaries appear porous, endogenous, and time-varying, offering a unifying framework to explain both the persistence of investment clusters and the sudden reorientation of capital toward new technological domains.
items.authors: Zava, Marta*

items.title: Information exchange processes leads to ultra-small world networks
items.abstract: A substantial body of evidence indicates that real-world networks exhibit the small-world property, meaning that the maximal distance between any two nodes grows logarithmically rather than linearly with network size. In 2023, we proposed a game-theoretic approach based on a weighted analogue of betweenness centrality as a utility function, improving upon existing models at that time - most notably the Watts–Strogatz model. Our framework provided an explanation for why, in real social networks, this principle can be stated even more strongly: most social networks are organized such that no individual is more than six connections away from any other, an empirical regularity known as the six degrees of separation. The proposed game-theoretic approach showed that the “six degrees of separation” property naturally arises as an equilibrium outcome in networks where individuals balance their aspiration to improve their centrality,captured by the standard notion of betweenness centrality, against the costs of forming and maintaining connections. However, two important aspects remained unaddressed: first, the motivation for forming directed connections beyond pure intermediation roles (which are clearly relevant in real-world settings), and second, the absence of an explicit payoff formulation. In the present work, we adopt a different perspective. Each agent is now modeled as both a sender and a receiver of information. During information transmission, agents prefer to use shortest paths, as information becomes progressively distorted with each transmission step, akin to the “telephone game.” A node derives utility when a sufficiently large amount of high-quality, minimally distorted information passes through it—both as a destination and as an intermediary. The proposed framework not only reproduces the tendency toward ultra-small-world structures (with only technical modifications relative to our original model), but also naturally accommodates the utility of directed links and allows for agent heterogeneity. Moreover, it provides a promising foundation for analyzing the emerging agentic web: a paradigm of the Internet that, according to current forecasts, may replace the classical web architecture by 2030th.
items.authors: Samoylenko, Ivan*

items.title: The fragile nature of rewarded cooperation in social networks: a mixed game model.
items.abstract: Issues around sustainable development frequently spills down to the tension between self-interest and altruism or in other terms the willingness to take cooperative action at the cost of individual payoffs. This paper investigates some aspects of sustainable cooperation through an extended Prisoner’s Dilemma (PD) game, which is extended along four dimensions. First, the model distinguishes between individualist and conformist attitudes [1]. Indivdualist players follow the standard PD game, while conformists play a coordination game and align their strategy with that of the majority of their social connections. Unlike existing models, this mixed game setup assigns different payoff functions to conformists. Second, adjusted replicator dynamics [2] is applied, where attitudes evolve based on their relative payoffs, recognizing that social attitudes change more slowly than strategy choices. Third, equilibrium outcomes are analyzed within complex social network structures [3]. Fourth, the effect of monetary incentives for cooperation is examined in interaction with attitude dynamics and network structure [4]. Equilibrium results show that cooperation only persists if both cooperation and conformism are in the majority at the game outset, otherwise individualism and defection prevail. If this condition holds, then a stable yet heterogeneous equilibrium can emerge where conformists cooperate and individualists defect. Rewarding promotes a shift toward cooperation, but its effectiveness depends on the composition of the population. If all players are conformists, the reward will be ineffective as the decision rule of conformists only indirectly depend on the reward. The reward encourages individualism, potentially altering the direction of attitude dynamics. While the reward is active, individualism spreads, even if individualists cooperate. In a complete network, this often leads to complete cooperation, but once the reward is withdrawn, the system collapses into full defection. These patterns persist in incomplete networks. The reward boosts cooperation but also promotes individualism, causing cooperation to drop below pre-reward levels once. Network structure plays a crucial role in shaping these dynamics. Sparse social networks help sustain cooperation, but degree asymmetry has an even stronger impact on equilibrium outcomes. Equilibrium cooperation is more likely to persist and the effect of the reward is greater in scale free networks compared to symmetric (Erdős-Rényi type) random or complete networks. Moreover, the dispersion across independent simulation runs is smaller and outcomes are typically closer to full cooperation, reflecting the role of highly connected nodes that bridge local neighborhoods. Scale free network structures also lead to sharper declines in cooperation once the reward is withdrawn. These results are driven by the stronger influence of degree asymmetry on the spread of individualism. [1] Pena, J., Volken, H., Pestelacci, E., Tomassini, M. (2009): Conformity hinders the evolution of cooperation on scale-free networks. Physical Review E 80. DOI:10.1103/PhysRevE.80.016110. [2] Nowak, M.A., May, R.M. (1992): Evolutionary games and spatial chaos. Nature 359, 826–829. DOI:10.1038/359826a0. [3] Szabó, Gy., Fáth, G. (2007): Evolutionary games on graphs. Physics Report 446, 97–216. DOI:10.1016/j.physrep.2007.04.004. [4] Jimenez, R., Lugo, H., Cuesta, J.A., Sanchez, A. (2008): Emergence and resilience of cooperation in the spatial prisoner’s dilemma via a reward mechanism. Journal of Theoretical Biology 250, 475–483. DOI:10.1016/j.jtbi.2007.10.010.
items.authors: Sebestyén, Tamás; Braun, Emese*

items.title: Adaptive Cannistraci Hebb Network Automata Modelling of Complex Networks for Path based Link Prediction
items.abstract: Many complex networks have partially observed or evolving connectivity, making link prediction a fundamental task. Topological link prediction infers missing links using only network topology, with applications in social, biological, and technological systems. T he Cannistraci Hebb (CH) theory provides a topological formulation of Hebbian learning, grounded on two pillars: (1) the minimization of external links within local communities, and (2) the path based definition of local communities that capture homophilic (similarity driven) interactions via paths of length 2 and synergetic (diversity driven) interactions via paths of length 3. Building on this, we introduce the Cannistraci Hebb Adaptive (CHA) network automata, an adaptive learning machine that automatically selects the optimal CH rule and path length to model each network. CHA unifies theoretical interpretability and data driven adaptivity, bridging physics inspired network science and machine intelligence. Across 1,269 networks from 14 domains, CHA consistently surpasses state of the art methods including Structural Perturbation Method (SPM) SPM), Stochastic Block Models SBM )), graph embedding methods, and message passing graph neural networks while revealing the mechanistic principles governing link formation. Our code is available at https://github.com/biomedical cybernetics/Cannistraci_Hebb_network_automata
items.authors: Zhao, Jialin; Muscoloni, Alessandro; Michieli, Umberto; Zhang, Yingtao; Cannistraci, Carlo*

items.title: A graph generation algorithm for network meta-analysis
items.abstract: Network meta-analysis (NMA) is a statistical method to synthesize evidence from multiple clinical trials that compare treatment options for the same condition. The method derives its name from a graphical representation of the data where nodes are treatments, and edges represent comparisons between treatments in trials [1]. Because of the higher-order structures introduced by trials that compare more than two treatments, these networks are more accurately described by bipartite graphs [2]. Here, trials define a second type of node, and edges connect trial nodes to the treatment nodes they compare. NMA is widely used in healthcare decision-making and is an active area of methods development. Model validation, methods comparisons, and investigation of model behaviour require comprehensive simulation studies. However, simulating NMA datasets typically involves manually specifying the network structure, which becomes increasingly difficult for large datasets. Simulated datasets have therefore been limited to small networks (<= 10 treatments) with a limited range of geometries, and trials that involve only two treatments. As a result, simulation studies of NMA often fail to represent the full range of network structures encountered in practice. To overcome these challenges, we develop an algorithm to automatically generate NMA networks with realistic properties. To inform our algorithm, we investigate the typical structural properties of an ensemble of 300 NMA networks drawn from analyses published in 2023. We begin with a bipartite version of the configuration model [3] which samples the degrees of the treatment nodes (the number of trials involving that treatment) and degrees of the trial nodes (the number of treatments compared in that trial) from the observed degree distributions of the ensemble. As shown in Fig. 1, this algorithm generates ensembles of random graphs whose degree distributions closely resemble those of the real world NMAs, both in their bipartite and unipartite representations. To more accurately reproduce the high proportion of star networks observed in the real world ensemble, we adapt the algorithm by sampling the degrees from a mixture model informed by the different distributions associated with star and non-star networks. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first algorithm designed to generate realistic NMA networks. This will facilitate efficient and flexible simulation studies of NMA methods. It also provides a starting point for generating more complex datasets that are becoming increasingly common in this area.
items.authors: Davies, Annabel*

items.title: How Overlapping Input Structures Amplify Systemic Risk in Global Supply Chains?
items.abstract: Disrupting global supply chains (GSCs), recent global events drew attention to their fragility and the systemic risks they pose to the global economy. This paper examines the underlying structural characteristics of GSC networks that exacerbate their vulnerability. Using a general equilibrium model with production networks, similar to [1, 2], we develop an indicator called Structural Vulnerability (SV) based on input-side structural factors, such as supplier concentration and overlapping input linkages. The example in Figure 1 shows that if the input sources of the S1 sector become more concentrated towards itself (Model C) or become correlated with another sector (S3 in Model D), then SV is amplified. We also measure each sector's contribution to vulnerability (denoted by SVC), which shows that S1 has a larger SVC in Model D than in Model B, despite having an identical input structure. Empirical analysis shows that increased economic specialization and interconnectedness have led to a significant rise in systemic risks between 1995 and 2019, especially up until 2012. This is evident in Figure 2, where the SVC of almost all countries and sectors increased between the two dates. We identify that the manufacturing sectors of developing countries were the main contributors to the growing vulnerability. The input structures of developing countries and manufacturing sectors changed in a way that their SVC values got inflated, as shown in Figure 2. The results suggest that the reason behind this increased vulnerability is the increasing homogeneity across sectors’ input sourcing structures, rather than by more concentration within individual sectors. This finding calls for coordination of supply chain and risk management decisions, as micro-level decisions can build up systemic risk by homogenizing input sourcing structures across GSC networks.
items.authors: Gyimesi, András*; Braun, Erik; Longauer, Dóra; Sebestyén, Tamás

items.title: Bridge Criticality in a Spatial Road Network
items.abstract: Transportation network vulnerability has become increasingly important given the critical role infrastructure plays in national mobility and economic resilience and remains an important problem to understand how these systems can withstand and recover from failures and disruptions. While extensive research has examined the robustness of road networks through various frameworks, the critical role bridges play in road networks and the risks posed by bridge infrastructure to transportation network connectivity is understudied. As structural bottlenecks in highway systems, bridge failures can disproportionately impact network connectivity, making their assessment vital for transportation planning and risk management. Here, using National Bridge Inventory data from the Long Term Bridge Performance project of the Federal Highway Administration of the US, we study the National Highway System and develop a percolation analysis framework to study the impact of removal of bridges on the network. Different removal scenarios are compared, and we describe a combined risk score accounting for increases in distance from detours caused by removal of bridges, which can be used to identify critical bridges based on their position in their network as well as features of their infrastructure such as condition and age. Accounting for bridge feature conditions and daily traffic volume highlights the bridges that are critical in disconnecting the network and delays in travel time caused by their disruption can be identified. The figure shows the case for a single county in the state of Iowa, where a small subset of bridges removed lead to non-trivial delays and disconnection of the network. This framework when extended to the entire system can be used to provide actionable insights for infrastructure resilience and planning in the event of disruptions caused by systemic failures or natural disasters.
items.authors: Parameswaran, Aarathi*

items.title: A network of cycles from random minimum cycle bases for material networks
items.abstract: Many material networks are well-described by their cycles. Graphene, carbon nanotubes, and diamond are periodic tilings of hexagonal carbon rings in the plane, on a cylinder, and in 3D space, respectively. The formalism of this is complex. One application we are particularly intereseted in is predicting molecular structures and giant (macroscale) molecules formed in hydrocarbon pyrolysis under extreme temperatures and pressures. We model the ensemble of carbon skeletons as a random graph [1,2]. Our most recent “disjoint loop model” [1] yielded excellent predictions for giant molecules with H/C ratios ≥ 2.5, as in butane (C4H10). In carbon-rich systems the accuracy of our disjoint loop model’s prediction of the size of the largest molecule deteriorates as carbon rings form clusters, thereby violating the “disjoint loop” assumption. This led us to building theory and designing algorithms for extracting cycles to characterize large, complex graphs, as in Fig. 1(a) [6]. We propose a method to sample the minimum cycle bases of a graph uniformly at random. From this, we define a dual graph, a network whose nodes represent cycles in the random minimum cycle basis, and whose edges represent their intersection paths, Fig. 1(d). The set of simple cycles cannot be used because it grows exponentially with graph size. Instead, the conventional approach is to treat the set of cycles as a vector space, where addition is the symmetric difference, and scalars are 0 and 1. Then, cycles can be sampled from a minimum cycle basis (MCB), a basis of the cycle space that minimizes the sum of lengths. Unfortunately, an MCB of a graph is not unique. Because of this, several authors extend MCBs to a unique set of cycles, e.g., Vismara [3] defines the relevant cycles as the union of all the MCBs of a graph. The size of the relevant cycles, however, may still grow exponentially with graph size. Because of the size limitations of the relevant cycles – and other extensions of MCBs – Gleiss et al. [4] and Kolodzik et al. [5] construct equivalence relations for the relevant cycles. These partition the relevant cycles into a smaller number of equivalence classes. We modify this approach and define polyhedron-interchangeability (pi) and short loop-interchangeability (sli) classes. pi and sli classes identify 3D structures and nested rings, respectively. See Fig. 1(b). We sample the MCBs of a graph uniformly at random from the pi and sli classes using a Markov Chain Monte Carlo approach. We post-process this MCB so that pairs of cycles intersect over a single path as in Fig. 1(c). The final MCB permits a notion of dual graph as shown in Fig. 1(d). We sampled MCBs and visualized loop clusters from a large collection of molecular dynamics simulations of hydrocarbon pyrolysis of large systems. We observed a phase transition in the loop cluster size distribution and a counterintuitive statistics of the largest molecule size.
items.authors: Ruth, Perrin*; Cameron, Maria

items.title: Phase Transitions in Heider Balance with Heterogeneous Social Temperatures
items.abstract:
Heider’s balance theory provides a fundamental framework for understanding polarization and alliance formation in social networks through triadic interactions [1]. Most existing stochastic formulations assume a homogeneous social temperature, implicitly treating all interpersonal relations as equally volatile [2]. Empirical social systems, however, are highly heterogeneous: social ties differ widely in stability, susceptibility to change, and exposure to noise [3]. We introduce a generalized Heider balance model in which each link is assigned its own social temperature to capture heterogeneity of fluctuations. By mapping the dynamics onto a higher-order Ising-type system with heterogeneous effective couplings, we derive a distribution-dependent mean-field theory that governs the average opinion $x$:  egin{equation} x=\int_0^{+\infty}	anh\left( eta x^2ight)ho( eta)d eta, \end{equation} where $ eta$ is the heterogeneous inverse temperature and $ho$ is the distribution for $ eta$. The self consistence equation shows that collective behavior depends not only on the average inverse temperature but on the entire distribution of heterogeneity. From the self consistence equation, we establish universal bounds controlled by the third order momentum $\gamma^3$ for the critical transition between polarized and non-polarized states, $C_* \leq \mu_{m C} <  rac{5}{4}\left( rac{4}{3}\gamma^3ight)^{ rac{1}{5}}$, and prove that the homogeneous limit provides a universal lower bound $C_* \approx 1.716$ for the critical average inverse temperature $\mu_{m C}$. Crucially, we theoretically show that light-tailed and heavy-tailed distributions lead to qualitatively distinct phase diagrams as Fig. 1 shows: while increasing heterogeneity such as standard deviation $\sigma$ destabilizes polarization in light-tailed cases, heavy-tailed distributions can sustain polarized states even under arbitrarily large fluctuations.  igskip {\small 
oindent[1] F. Heider, J. Psychol. 	extbf{21}, 107 (1946).\ 
oindent[2] K. Malarz and J. A. Holyst, Phys. Rev. E 	extbf{106}, 064139 (2022).\ 
oindent[3] M. Bagherikalhor, A. Kargaran, A. Shirazi, and G. Jafari, Phys. Rev. E 	extbf{103}, 032305 (2021). }
items.authors: LI, ZHEN*; Izumida, Yuki

items.title: Emergence of Covid-19 Variants via Dominance in a Multi-Variant Epidemic Model
items.abstract: We investigate the emergence and spreading of dominant Covid-19 variants – Alpha, Beta, Delta and Omicron – throughout the Covid-19 pandemic, based on a network multi-mutation epidemic spreading model that we developed. We characterize properties of a pathogen’s mutation that lead it to reach dominance over other mutations that spread simultaneously with it. Based on this model we explain the following pandemic related facts: (i) Three pandemic waves refer to three variants that reached dominance over other coexisting variants during three periods : Alpha wave in 2020, Delta wave in 2021, and the Omicron wave in 2022 - mid-2023. We explain the successive replacements of Alpha by Delta and Delta by Omicron. (ii) The importance and severity ascribed to the Beta variant. In our epidemic spreading model, a pathogen can mutate continuously during the epidemic spreading. We consider pathogens whose mutating parameter is the mortality mean-time and study the evolution of this parameter over the spreading process. Based on this, we compute the probability of an individual (node) carrying a pathogen variant to infect other individuals (nodes), depending on the mutating parameter of the specific variant. We show that less aggressive mutations with a higher mortality meantime have a higher probability of infecting their neighbors and a larger proportion relative to other mutations. Using Epidemiological metrics of the Covid-19 variants, we calculated the parameters of our model for these variants. Based on this, we deduce that the replacements of Alpha by Delta and Delta by Omicron throughout the pandemic, were not due to the emergence of a variant with a much higher infection probability, but rather to the emergence of a variant with a shorter infection mean time and a low mortality probability. We also show that the sufficiently high infection probability of the Beta variant combined with it relatively high mortality probability, makes it a significant variant.
items.authors: Ivry, Aviel; Cohen, Reuven; Patron, Amikam*

items.title: Urban Multilayer Network Framework from the Space-Society Perspective: Modeling, Characterizing and Understanding on the Dynamics of Urban Space
items.abstract: Applying network-based methods to urban issues is increasingly important, yet most studies still separate visible physical networks like road networks from invisible social networks. Research that treats the built environment as a container of human activities and uses complex network analysis methods to reveal characteristics and laws of urban spatial structures remains limited. We propose a modeling strategy that integrates urban physical space with social associations patterns by representing resident activity-related buildings as nodes and social communication as edges (ties). Spatial functional differences among buildings are further used to construct attribute-specific layers, forming a spatial multilayer network of urban area. Preliminarily and empirically, we have finished the analysis on the central urban area of metropolitan Shanghai. We built a single-layer residential space network for 6731 gated communities, using community centroids as nodes and opportunities of inter-community social interaction in public space as edges (ties). On the basis of network measurement, like robustness and degree distribution, the identified “network community” has been proved to be in line with the geographical sense of “regional identity”. Furthermore, the multi-layer network of urban space is an extension of the single attribute network. Because the urban residents’ activities—such as working, trading (including consuming) and leisure—occur in specific buildings, architectural complexes or blocks, the spatial scope of different activities can be defined accurately. Meanwhile, residents move among these functional urban spaces with varying modes, frequencies, and purposes.
