These spatial and behavioral definitions with the connotation of planning theory are very suitable for all the basic conditions of constructing multi-layer network of space. And, residents’ movements define intra-layer ties and cross-layer couplings, satisfying the theoretical conditions for constructing a multilayer spatial network analogous to a diverse ecological system. This spatial multilayer network offers a promising analytical paradigm for capturing urban complexity, encompassing major physical spaces and generalized social linkages. With richer functional details and behavioral data, this integrated spatial–social network approach may yield deeper insights and support predictions of urban evolution. In sum, applying complex network analysis to the spatial–social structure of cities provides new methods and perspectives for urban research and contributes to theoretical development in network science. Keywords: Multilayer Network of Urban Space; Physical Space; Social Interaction; Dynamic Mechanism; Spatial Pattern Research Paradigm
items.authors: Xu, Linxi*; Yang, Fan

items.title: Reconstructing Historical Social Networks as Complex Systems: Analyzing Diaries from the Reform War (1858-1860) in Mexico
items.abstract: Social systems are inherently complex, shaped by dynamic interactions, power structures, and historical contingencies. Understanding how these networks evolve over time is crucial for reconstructing past societies and identifying underlying structural patterns. This study applies network science, text mining, and Natural Language Processing (NLP) [1] to reconstruct historical social networks from personal diaries written during the Reform War (1858-1860) in Mexico, one of the most violent conflicts in mexican history. In previous research [2, 3], we reconstructed networks from Juan de Vildosola’s Diary and developed growth models that capture the social network as seen from the observer’s perspective, as a subgraph of a larger system exhibiting a Barabási-Albert structure, thus revealing key actors and ideological affiliations. Expanding this approach, we analyze four additional diaries from the same period, written by individuals with diverse political perspectives, as well as texts from traditional, non-academic historical sources. Using NLP and named entity recognition (NER), we extract and normalize historical figures, using them as building blocks to construct interaction networks. We hypothesize that these reconstructed networks allow us to examine structural similarities and resilience in wartime societies. This comparative analysis provides new insights into how official historical narratives may oversimplify the complexity of past societies during civil wars. Our findings contribute to modeling social systems as evolving networks, offering an interdisciplinary perspective that addresses key challenges in historical data analysis, such as fragmentation, ambiguity, and bias in primary sources. Furthermore, we demonstrate how computational approaches and exceptional historical documents can serve as valuable methodologies for complex systems research, particularly in the study of civil conflicts and long-term social dynamics. [1] Motilla-Ch ́avez, J.A., Espitia, D., Gal ́an-V ́asquez, E., et al. A Methodological Framework for the Use of AI Tools in Automated Workflows for Generating Validated and Structured Historical Datasets, 17 November 2025, PREPRINT (Version 1) available at Research Square [https://doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.rs-7943432/v1] [2] Motilla-Ch ́avez, J. A., Ugalde, E. & Gal ́an-V ́asquez, E. Social networks from dailies: the observer’s point of view. Appl Netw Sci 9, 67 (2024). https://doi.org/10.1007/s41109-024-00681-9. [3] Motilla-Chavez J, Ugalde E, Gal ́an-V ́asquez E. An ́alisis de un diario de la Guerra de Reforma (1858-1860) en San Luis Potos ́ı, M ́exico, por medio de la teor ́ıa de redes: una propuesta metodolo ́gica para el an ́alisis de textos histo ́ricos. In: Violencia, repre- sentaciones y estrategias. La guerra y sus efectos en M ́exico, Colombia y Guatemala, siglos XVI-XX. COLMEX (2021)
items.authors: Motilla, Jose Antonio*; Espitia , Diego ; Ugalde , Edgardo; Galán-Vásquez, Edgardo

items.title: Multiplicity without Switching in Evolving Correlation Networks: A Coupling–Localization Atlas of Market Organization (US vs EU)
items.abstract: Complex networks are often summarized by a single number, implicitly treating organization as one-dimensional. We report “multiplicity without switching” in financial correlation networks: at comparable overall coupling, the market’s dominant mode supports multiple, distinct organizations, while stress mainly shifts where observations sit in coupling space rather than causing frequent within-band state flips. Using daily sector returns (2006–present) and rolling correlation matrices (90-day windows), we build a coupling–localization atlas and test for bimodality within a fixed-coverage, rank-matched coupling band. Both U.S. and European sector networks show strong bimodality, confirmed by a nonparametric multimodality test with overlap-aware block bootstrap. A declustered high-coupling stress gate reveals occupancy migration in event windows: coupling rises, localization falls, and inflow into the highest-coupling bin increases sharply, while within-band switching remains rare. Results persist under coarser sampling strides and under shrinkage-based correlation estimation.
items.authors: Yu, Hanyu*

items.title: Evaluating the Ability of General-purpose AI Agents to Solve Network Science Problems

items.authors: Li, Xuanchi*; Kojaku, Sadamori; Yang, Kaicheng

items.title: Mixture models uncover polycentric urban structure from mobility networks
items.abstract: The modern city is often characterized by a tension between centralizing forces and decentralizing forces. Human mobility patterns offer a powerful lens to understand a city’s dynamic structure, often revealing complexities hidden in static maps of population or land use. Tracking where and when people travel – their daily moves across the city – yields a map of functional urban connectivity, effectively the "revealed preference" of residents using and experiencing the city. Here we exploit GPS mobility data for urban residents in cities across Germany, France and the United Kingdom to develop a method for understanding how cities balance the forces of centralization and decentralization. In doing so, we find regularities in how people distribute activities in space with implications social mixing, productivity, and sustainability. We analyze GPS traces for 50 million residents across 226 functional urban areas in France, Germany, and the United Kingdom. We use home- and stop-detection to define each trip of length d, the distance between home and stop. Each pooled home hexagon (H3 level 9) is assigned a vector of trip distances. Because these length distributions are comprised of trips generated from different processes, which include leisure activities and commutes, we employ finite mixture models to separate shorter-range routine mobility from longer-range travel within a single, interpretable framework. For each home hexagon we model the empirical distribution of trip lengths with a Gamma mixture representing proximal, medial, and distal mobility scales. Mixture parameters are obtained by maximum-likelihood expectation-maximization and the components are ordered by increasing mean distance. The resulting means represent the typical trip distance within each component, or scale, while the weights quantify the share of all trips undertaken in that scale for the neighborhood in question. To understand how these values vary with respect to urban spatial structure, we estimate an urban nucleus for each city by taking the centroid of the busiest location by GPS pings and computing each neighborhood's distance to that centroid. Fitting mixture models to ~20,000 neighborhoods across cities in Europe, we see a number of important regularities. The distal mean grows with distance to the nucleus: peripheral households pay a systematic distance premium. The proximal distances do not systematically vary by distance to the nucleus, and they are invariant to city size, suggesting that local activity operates at a similar scale no matter than neighborhood; medial distances scale but less so than distal. We show the slope of the best fit line for each city, with brigher colors corresponding to larger cities; we find stratification where larger cities have both lower slopes and lower intercepts: smaller cities are more dependent on their urban centers while larger cities fragment into subcenters. An advantage of this method is that it lets us assign individual trips to components and examine the composition of them. We divide each neighbourhood into socio-economic quantiles to show that 50% and 30% of intergroup interaction across cities comes from distal and medial components, respectively, with implications for social contact given remote work trends; if centralized work declines, intergroup exposure may decrease. Cities face an efficiency–mixing trade‑off, and strengthening medial hubs raises medial weights and flattens medial means, reducing peripheral burdens while preserving access to the core. An ``efficient polycentric'' configuration therefore retains a strong, accessible nucleus while distributing everyday amenities to the medial scale.
items.authors: Renninger, Andrew*

items.title: Emergence and Evolution of Semantic Complexity in Urban Digital Twins
items.abstract: Contemporary urban dynamics are increasingly driven by pervasive digitalization, producing complex interdependencies between online platforms and offline behaviors. To adequately capture and analyze these intertwined processes, such dynamics need to be framed comprehensively through Urban Digital Twins (UDTs), which provide an integrated representation of both digital (e.g.: smart water city traffic solution) and physical urban systems (e.g.: transportation network)[1]. The complexity brought by UDTs raises the need for an systemic approach to trace its emergence and evolution across the evolving spectrum through science of science perspective[2]. While bibliometric indicators and science mapping techniques trace the emergence and evolution of concepts, they often rely on fragmented workflows across multiple tools and custom scripts. This limitation often arises when tracking conceptual development over time. Conceptual evolution is highly sensitive to choices in query formulation, database selection, filtering, and modeling, even minor differences in search strategies, pre-processing, or network construction can yield divergent narratives. Without an integrated workflow, distinguishing robust patterns from analytical artifacts is difficult. This study makes a twofold contribution. First, it develops an integrated pipeline linking literature retrieval (OpenAlex API), pre-processing, network construction and embeddings (Word2Vec), analytical metrics (centrality, edge scaling, community detection, temporal expansion), and interactive visualization (Dash), providing a fully transparent workflow. Second, a front-end interface allows end-to-end investigation of any topic’s conceptual emergence and evolution. These contributions underscore the importance of transparency and reproducibility in bibliometric reviews, illustrated here with literature on ‘Urban Digital Twins’ in titles or abstracts without date restrictions (Figure 1). The network can be specified as a co-occurrence network where for every unordered pair (k1, k2) of distinct keywords that appear together in a publication, a co-occurrence count is increased by one. Jaccard similarity of keyword sets between years is stored as a graph-level attribute and provides a simple indicator of how much the set of active keywords changes from year to year where in year t and t − 1, with keyword sets Kt and Kt−1 and denoted as J(Kt,Kt−1) = |Kt∩Kt−1| |Kt∪Kt−1| . Furthermore, concept-method relations are modeled with a bipartite network from the same dataset. In this representation, one set of nodes corresponds to keywords interpreted as concepts and the other set corresponds to keywords interpreted as methods or technologies. An edge between a concept and a method indicates that they co-occur in at least one publication. To construct this network, the pipeline first classifies keywords into three categories: concept, method, or other. Each unique keyword from the corpus is presented to five open-source language models (Llama 3, Mistral, Gemma 2, Phi-3, and Qwen 2.5). Each model is prompted to return exactly one of the three labels, framed in the context of Urban Digital Twins research to reduce ambiguity. In the end, this study provides a coherent understanding of the conceptual development of Urban Digital Twins and offers a generic, transparent, and reproducible bibliometric method and tool, supplemented by a front-end interactive interface that enables spatiotemporal exploration of the conceptual network.
items.authors: Hilman, Rafiazka*; Koltai, Júlia

items.title: Mapping the Portuguese Business-to-Business Production Network: Structure, Dynamics, and Sectoral Heterogeneity
items.abstract: This paper analyzes Portugal's business-to-business production network using for the first time administrative transaction data from 2016–2024. Drawing on over 45 million firm-to-firm relationships among approximately 770.000 firms, the study documents structural asymmetries and sectoral differences that illuminate the foundations of production networks and their implications for shock propagation and aggregate output volatility in Portugal. The analysis reveals a highly concentrated network: the top 1% of firms by connectivity account for 79% of all firm-to-firm links, while the top 10% capture 96% of observed relationships and 87% of aggregate B2B sales. The network exhibits a pronounced supplier-customer asymmetry that varies systematically by sector, reflecting how production technology and market structure jointly shape network structure. Several network metrics are strongly heavy-tailed (approximately power-law in the upper tail), with important implications for aggregate fluctuations. Shocks to large firms may not average out, potentially generating macroeconomic volatility through granular mechanisms. The distribution of customer relationships shows a fatter tail than that of supplier relationships, a pattern consistent with stronger downstream concentration and potentially more intense propagation of demand-side shocks than supply-side shocks. The analysis also reveals strong regularities: measures of systemic importance scale with firm size, firms face trade-offs between customer counts and sales per customer, and production chain position measures capture distinct and complementary dimensions of value chain structure. Temporal analysis demonstrates network resilience: despite a 12–13% contraction in network activity during the 2020 pandemic, the network recovered rapidly by 2024. These findings advance understanding of production network formation, sectoral vulnerabilities to supply chain disruptions, and the potential for firm-level shocks to generate aggregate volatility through network amplification.
items.authors: Saldias, Martin*

items.title: Subsumption Rules in Higher-Order Contagion
items.abstract: Conventional models of higher-order contagion, such as those defined on simplicial complexes, typically assume that higher-order interactions act as additive reinforcements to pairwise spreading. This synergy is widely recognized as the primary driver of discontinuous phase transitions and multi-stability. In this work, we challenge this additive paradigm by introducing a "subsumption rule": when a higher-order structure (e.g., a $2$-simplex) is activated, its constituent lower-order components (edges) are effectively covered and cease to function as independent transmission channels. By the mean-field approach, we derive that the effective transmission rate in our model follows a binomial inversion. This analytical framework reveals that higher-order structures can counterintuitively act as inhibitors of spreading under certain conditions. By assuming a power-law scaling of the form $\beta_m = \beta_1 \cdot m^\alpha$, we show that our model recovers the classic network spreading behavior if and only of $\alpha=1$. Crucially, we identify a "Structural Braking" regime in the sub-linear case ($\alpha < 1$), where the subsumption of lower-order links outweighs the higher-order promotion, leading to a suppression of the steady-state prevalence. Focusing on $2$-simplices, we systematically map the transition landscape across different scaling regimes: For $\alpha < 1$ (Regime I, suppression): Increasing simplicial density $k_{\Delta}$ leads to a consistent decrease in the steady-state prevalence $\rho^*$, confirming the suppression effect. For $\alpha > 1$ but relatively small (Regime II, weak promotion), the higher-order reinforcement is insufficient to trigger bi-stability. The system remains constrained by the topological saturation limit ($k_{\Delta} \leq k_1/2$), exhibiting a continuous phase transition. At sufficiently large $\alpha$ (Regime III, strong promotion), the model recovers the subcritical transcritical bifurcations and prominent hysteresis loops characteristic of explosive contagion, aligning with classical results but now understood as a competition between subsumption and scaling. Our results provide a unified perspective on how local interaction rules—beyond mere topology—dictate the global phase transitions of complex systems. By bridging the gap between additive synergy and resource-constrained spreading, this work offers a theoretical foundation for more realistic modeling of information and disease diffusion.
items.authors: Yang, Jingbang*

items.title: Resilience of China’s Domestic Inter-Firm Transaction Network via Bow-Tie Architecture and LLM-Optimized OSINT
items.abstract: China’s domestic inter-firm transaction network—often described as the backbone of the world’s factory—has attracted growing strategic attention, yet remains highly opaque. This interest has intensified following the December 2025 revelations surrounding China’s semiconductor self-sufficiency initiatives and the rapid rise of domestic firms such as BYD in the electric vehicle sector. Conventional analyses of China’s domestic supply chain networks, however, are severely constrained by information asymmetry: in government-disclosed corporate filings, approximately 83\% of transaction partners are anonymized [1]. As a result, strategic inter-firm transaction networks are largely invisible in official records, creating a “black box” that prevents systemic resilience assessment. In this study, we focus explicitly on China’s domestic inter-firm transaction network, representing supplier–customer relationships among firms operating within China, rather than international trade flows. To overcome the opacity of official disclosures, we develop a proprietary OSINT pipeline to aggregate fragmented evidence of inter-firm transaction relationships scattered across the web. We target 4,331 listed Chinese firms and 20,000 large Chinese enterprises with operating income exceeding \$130M. Even when focal firms do not disclose their transaction partners, we reconstruct domestic inter-firm links by triangulating partial disclosures from counterpart firms’ investor relations materials and reports from major media outlets. Using the Google Search API, we retrieve the top five sources per firm and process the resulting unstructured text using Qwen 3 with DSPy-optimized prompting [2]. Data reliability is further supported through randomized consistency checks using an LLM-as-a-Judge framework based on Gemini 3 Pro as an auxiliary validation tool. The reconstructed domestic inter-firm transaction network provides an unprecedented view of China’s internal supply chain structure, capturing 26$\times$ more unique firms and 55$\times$ more transaction relationships than official disclosure-based datasets. Crucially, as shown in Figure 1(a), the resulting network restores plausible global topology: whereas official records exhibit an artificial truncation in degree beyond ten partners due to disclosure limits, fragmenting the network and undermining inference, the OSINT-derived network reproduces power-law-like degree patterns commonly observed in real-world inter-firm transaction networks [3]. Bow-tie decomposition reveals a giant strongly connected core (SCC) comprising 68,775 firms (56.2\%), accompanied by an upstream IN component comprising 11.2\% of firms and a downstream OUT component comprising 15.6\%. Simulated targeted attacks further expose structural vulnerabilities concentrated in high-degree hubs, as shown in Figure 1(b). Degree-based hub attacks most effectively degrade global connectivity, yielding a robustness index of $R = 0.0267$, which is slightly lower (i.e., more destructive) than SCC-targeted attacks ($R = 0.0268$), while random failures are substantially less damaging ($R = 0.2496$). These findings indicate that the resilience of China’s domestic supply chain is governed not by the scale of individual firms, but by its structural dependence on a small set of central hub nodes within the domestic inter-firm transaction network.
items.authors: Fukada, Hiroto; Mizuno, Takayuki*

items.title: Assessing the risk in statistically reconstructed firm-level critical raw material supply networks
items.abstract: Critical raw materials (CRMs) are essential for producing goods for everyday life and modern technologies. They are therefore of strategic importance to the EU economy but are also associated with a high risk of supply disruption. For example, over 80% of the global tungsten supply is mined in China and is primarily used to produce extremely hard and durable alloys for high-performance industrial tools, as well as in catalysts, pigments, lighting, and electronic applications. Dependencies on raw materials are often assessed only at the level of mined or minimally processed forms. Vulnerabilities that emerge further downstream are more challenging to identify and require a comprehensive, detailed representation of industrial value chains, which has so far proved difficult to achieve. The assessment is typically performed at the country level, only taking trade into account, not at the firm level, and therefore does not account for domestic production or company ownership. This study introduces (a) a novel approach to map value chain networks for critical raw materials (CRMs), combining AI-based generation, trade data, and statistical validation, and (b) a statistical reconstruction of the underlying firm network. The resulting networks can be used to evaluate and quantify concentration effects and vulnerabilities for individual regions and the global value chains in which they are embedded. To automatically construct a value chain originating from a raw material at the sixth-digit level of the Harmonized System (HS), we use a two-step process. First, we identify a broad set of products related to the extracted or minimally processed material using a previously proposed production network [2]. These product pairs serve as provisional inputs to an LLM, which generates granular production paths from input to output using HS-like product descriptions. The embeddings of these descriptions are then matched to real HS descriptions using cosine similarity. Second, we post-process the preliminary network using four hyperparameters: an embedding threshold, a frequency threshold (how often a link is generated in step 1), an evaluation threshold (LLM-based pairwise validation), and a trade significance threshold (the correlation between input imports and output exports under a permutation test). The hyperparameters are calibrated using a manually researched phosphorus rock network. For the core components, basic phosphorus compounds, phosphates, and phosphatic fertilizers, accounting for about 90% of global phosphate rock use, we recover 100% of actual nodes and links with no false positives. To map the underlying firm network, we identify companies from the ORBIS database involved in the extraction, processing, or manufacturing of the focus critical raw material or products derived from it. This is done by first filtering ORBIS firm descriptions for keywords related to the critical raw material and its broad applications. To assign a firm to specific HS code, we first map the HS-based value chain network to its NACE4 equivalent. To evaluate whether a firm produces a certain HS code, the firm must be assigned to the mapped NACE4 sector or one of the first-order neighbors on the NACE4-based value chain network. This relaxes the strict requirement of an exact NACE4 sector match, as such assignments are often imprecise. We then task an LLM to evaluate whether a specific firm is involved in the production of a given product. If the evaluation is positive, we consider the firm a producer of that product. Thus, for each product in the HS-based value chain network, we obtain a list of producing firms. We then connect the firms using a gravity model of trade, incorporating the underlying input-output structure and trade data [3] as constraints. Figure 1 shows one example of such a network for tungsten, including companies that extract and process tungsten or manufacture intermediate products part of the tungsten value chain. Companies are scored using network centrality, concentration indices, and governance indicators. We discuss how estimates of risk based on firm properties and production structure differ from those based on country-level trade and production data alone. Our findings suggest that analyses based solely on country-level data obscure such relationships between companies, leading to distorted risk estimates. This is particularly the case when companies carry out multiple processing steps within the same country. In summary, we present a method for statistically reconstructing firm-level supply networks based on a mapping of a 6-digit HS code value chain network, providing finer granularity than the country-level. This represents an important step toward understanding global value chains in much greater detail and assessing risk within the supply network, as comprehensive knowledge of these networks can help identify dependencies and evaluate resilience. [1] Fetzer et al. (2024) AI-Generated Production Networks: Measurement and Applications to Global Trade [2] Karbevska, Hildalgo (2025) Mapping global value chains at the product level [3] Gaulier, Zignago (2010) BACI: International Trade Database at the Product-Level. The 1994-2007 Version.
items.authors: Baum, Sophia*; Heß, Maximilian; Klimek, Peter

items.title: Product-level value chains from firm data: mapping trophic levels into economic growth
items.abstract: Global value chains shape everything from the price of coffee to the roll-out of clean technology, yet policy still relies on sector-level input–output maps that gloss over the differences among the single products. Our study reconstructs a detailed product-level value chain by identifying statistically significant input-output relationships between products derived from firm-level trade data. The result is a network of about 5000 products, which provides a tool for analyzing the structure and function of global value chains at an unprecedented level of detail. Leveraging concepts from ecological network theory, we assign a trophic level to each product, providing a topology-based measure of its production stage and allowing us to assess the overall hierarchical structure of the network through the trophic coherence. This enables the computation of the average trophic level of imports and exports for firms and countries, which provides information about industrial specialization patterns and economic growth. We show that the trophic structure of value chains is only detectable at a fine product resolution, and that firms tend to operate with a positive trophic jump - i.e., they export more downstream products than they import. At the country level, we observe a strong negative correlation between export diversification and the average trophic level of imports: more diversified economies import more upstream products, while countries with low diversification are constrained to import downstream goods — a pattern we define trophic barrier. This relationship reflects differences in industrial specialization, with manufacturing-intensive countries like China and Italy importing more basic inputs than service-oriented economies like the US and France. Similarly, we find that trophic jump correlates positively with diversification, indicating that more advanced countries add greater value by transforming basic inputs into complex outputs. Crucially, these trophic metrics are not just descriptive: we demonstrate that both the average trophic level of a country’s imports and its trophic jump are strong predictors of future economic growth. Our detailed, totally data-driven input-output network of products provides a practical reference for trade strategists, development planners, and researchers navigating supply-chain reconfiguration and geopolitical fragmentation.
items.authors: Fessina, Massimiliano; Tacchella, Andrea ; Zaccaria, Andrea*

items.title: Distance Dependent Bayesian Community Detection
items.abstract: Community detection in networks seeks to identify to which cluster the nodes belong. Various traditional methods consider only the connections among the members of the population to make this classification. The rationale behind those methods is that highly connected nodes are more likely to belong to the same cluster than poorly connected ones. Although structure-based community detection algorithms may perform well under some circumstances, these methods ignore information included in many real-world networks, such as the characteristics of their members. For instance, nodal attributes may represent a person's characteristics, such as age, gender, and interests. The node attributes may affect the formation of clusters in a population. This phenomenon is well documented in the social science literature and is known as network homophily. Consequently, models leveraging link density and attribute homogeneity may help improve community detection. The main contribution of this work is to propose a community detection method that guarantees a lower link density across clusters than within clusters for any community pairs and also considers the similarity of the nodes with respect to their attributes. The inference is then performed under a Bayesian nonparametric framework. We assess the performance of the method through an extensive simulation study. Furthermore, we compare our results in real data with state-of-the-art community detection methodology for network data.
items.authors: Beaudry, Isabelle*; Mendoza, Marcelo; Bro, Naim

items.title: From Case Counts to Contact Networks: Inferring Epidemic Coupling in Space and Time
items.authors: Pelcaru, Adrian*

items.title: Beyond Euclidean Distance-Based Clustering: A Network Approach to Unsupervised Classification
items.abstract: Unsupervised classification is a fundamental tool for organizing complex datasets into groups of similar elements, enabling the identification of shared characteristics and underlying processes, while averaging out individual data point variability. Traditional unsupervised learning approaches, such as k-means and hierarchical clustering, typically operate in a vector space and rely on Euclidean distance to compute class centroids and relationships among the data. This setup is often poorly suited to empirical data, where features are correlated, information is redundant, and meaningful relationships may not be adequately captured by Euclidean metrics. Although dimensionality reduction is commonly employed to address feature correlations, Euclidean distances remain susceptible to degradation in moderately to high-dimensional spaces and may fail to encode non-linear or directional dependencies. We introduce a network-based workflow for unsupervised classification that replaces vector-space representations with similarity networks. Pairwise relationships between data points, computed using arbitrary similarity or dependency measures (e.g., cosine similarity or mutual information), are encoded as weighted edges in a fully connected graph. This formulation decouples clustering from Euclidean geometry and enables the use of metrics tailored to the structure of the data and the scientific question of interest. To reveal the underlying informative network topology, we extract a statistically significant network backbone using the disparity filter. Community detection is then applied to the backbone network to identify classes of data points. By simultaneously constructing a network of data points and a network of features, the proposed framework enables the direct interpretability of clusters in terms of their dominant characteristics. We demonstrate the approach on the Geospatial Attributes of Gages for Evaluating Streamflow (GAGES) dataset, comprising 9,067 watersheds in the continental United States described by over 300 environmental variables. The resulting watershed communities show strong spatial coherence and distinct signatures associated with natural and anthropogenic drivers, and correspond to markedly different hydrological behaviors. We show that tuning the workflow parameters controls edge density and community resolution, enabling investigation at different granularities. In addition, network-specific measures, such as degree centrality, can be used to select representative elements within each class, useful to prioritize instruments deployment. Due to its metric flexibility and reliance on network topology, the proposed method is domain-agnostic and applicable to a wide range of high-dimensional datasets.
items.authors: Ciulla, Fabio*

items.title: Disparity-in-Differences: Extracting Hierarchical Backbones of Weighted Directed Networks
items.abstract: Empirical networks often consist of heterogeneous and asymmetrical relations. Existing network backbone extraction methods have successfully identified significant relations based on heterogeneity but are not designed to incorporate asymmetry that may encode hierarchical structures. To fill the gap, motivated by the concept of power-dependence relations [1], this paper proposes a new method named "disparity-in-differences" that measures a statistical significance of asymmetric dependence, which is defined as the difference of normalized edge weights between two nodes. The term "disparity" is adopted from the disparity filter [2], a widely used network backbone extraction method. According to the concept of power-dependence relations [1], the dependence of i on j is equal to the power of j over i. In this framework, power imbalance between i and j can be represented as the difference in dependencies. For two nodes i and j, the normalized weight of an edge i -> j is considered as the extent that i depends on j and vice versa. The difference between the normalized weights of i -> j and j -> i would thus serve as a proxy for a hierarchical relation. To calculate its statistical significance, the proposed method samples values from a null model that assumes edge weights are uniformly distributed over neighbors as used in the disparity filter [2]. A sampled value for a node i (x_{ij}) follows a Beta distribution, Beta(1,k_{i}-1), where k_{i} is the number of out-neighbors of i. If the difference between two associated normalized weights is significantly higher or lower than x_{ij} - x_{ji} values, the proposed method assigns an unweighted edge i -> j or j -> i to indicate j or i is at a higher position in a hierarchy, respectively (Figure 1). For evaluation, the proposed method is applied to a journal citation network, a U.S. airport network, the Enron email network, and a world trade network. Compared to existing network backbone extraction methods, it better captures hierarchical relations that align with journal quality ratings, airport hub categories by size, levels of management, and a core-periphery structure of countries, respectively. Backbones extracted by the disparity-in-differences method will provide insights into not only known hierarchies but also emerging, fading, or missing hierarchical relations. [1] Emerson, R. M. (1962). Power-dependence relations. American Sociological Review, 27(1), 31-41. [2] Serrano, M. Á., Boguná, M., & Vespignani, A. (2009). Extracting the multiscale backbone of complex weighted networks. Proceedings of the national academy of sciences, 106(16), 6483-6488.
items.authors: Kim, Hyunuk*

items.title: Compressibility of Neuronal Activity Across Dynamical Regimes
items.abstract: Complex networks often exhibit rich collective dynamics that depend sensitively on patterns of interactions between nodes. In neuronal systems, these interactions can lead to distinct dynamical regimes—subcritical, critical, and supercritical— each characterized by different patterns of propagation, correlation, and information flow [1,2]. A central hypothesis in this field is that healthy brain activity operates near a critical point [1], enabling efficient communication and flexible computation. This motivates the central question in this work: to what extent is the apparent variability in brain activity driven by a relatively small set of informative correlations, and how does this depend on the underlying neural dynamics? In this work, we study neuronal systems through the lens of information compressibility [3], a measure that quantifies how much uncertainty in collective dynamics can be reduced by a subset of pairwise interactions. This measure is grounded in the minimax entropy principle [4], which seeks the least-biased probabilistic model consistent with a chosen set of constraints. Specifically, neural activity is modeled using a multivariate Gaussian maximum-entropy distribution constrained by selected pairwise covariances. As correlations are added, the model’s entropy decreases, indicating that more structure is being captured. The greedy minimax-entropy algorithm determines, at each step, which correlation, when included, results in the most substantial reduction in global entropy. This process creates an optimal compression curve that measures how effectively network interactions account for collective variability. We replicate the greedy minimax-entropy compression framework introduced in [3] and extend it to synthetic neuronal activity generated using networks of Izhikevich neurons [5]. Our initial results focus on activity simulated on random networks (Erd˝os-R´enyi graphs, p = 0.2) consisting of N neurons with excitatory (80%) and inhibitory (20%) populations [6]. Different dynamical regimes were produced by tuning the effective branching ratio or, equivalently, adjusting the balance of excitation and inhibition. Fig. 1 shows a schematic of the analysis pipeline. Fig. 1A shows an example of simulated neural time series generated in a near-critical regime (branching ratio, σ = 0.9), while Fig. 1B shows its corresponding correlation matrix. Fig. 1C shows the resulting greedy minimax-entropy compression curves across several branching ratios, showing systematic differences in compressibility based on the branching ratio of the dynamics. Fig. 1D summarizes these results by plotting the area above the compression curves in 1C as a function of branching ratio. We find that strongly subcritical dynamics are easy to compress because the activity is highly redundant and quickly dies out. In contrast, supercritical dynamics are harder to compress, as bursting and unstable activity produce noisy correlation patterns. Near-critical regimes fall between these extremes: their activity can be compressed efficiently but not trivially, indicating a balance between structure and variability. Together, these results show that information compressibility provides a clear, system-level indicator of the underlying neural dynamical regime. 1] Beggs & Plenz (2003). Neuronal avalanches in neocortical circuits. Journal of Neuroscience, 23(35), 11167-11177. [2] Shew, Yang, Yu, Roy, & Plenz (2011). Information capacity and transmission are maximized in balanced cortical networks with neuronal avalanches. Journal of Neuroscience, 31(1), 55-63. [3] Weaver, Faskowitz, Betzel, & Lynn (2025). Quantifying the compressibility of the human brain. arXiv:2510.16327. [4] Lynn, Yu, Pang, Palmer, & Bialek (2025). Exact minimax entropy models of large-scale neuronal activity. Physical Review E, 111(5). [5] Izhikevich (2003). Simple model of spiking neurons. IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks, 14(6), 1569-1572. [6] Braitenberg & Sch¨uz (2013). Anatomy of the Cortex: Statistics and Geometry (Vol. 18). Springer Science & Business Media.
items.authors: Chaudhari, Lochan*; Klein, Brennan

items.title: Effects of screen time and activities on mental health in children
items.abstract: Children and adolescents are engaging with digital technologies at increasingly younger ages, and recent years have seen a rapid expansion of interactive tools such as AI chatbots and wellness applications. A growing body of literature links screen time and digital media use to mental health outcomes in youth, including depressive symptoms, sleep disruption, and suicidal ideation, suggesting a complex interplay between individual differences, contextual factors, and environmental characteristics [1,2]. To investigate divergent associations between screen use (e.g., social media, video games, TV, phone), physical activities, and mental health during childhood and early adolescence, we used data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, a large (N ≈ 12, 000) longitudinal (t = 4) dataset. We conducted an exploratory analysis at baseline (year 0), selecting participants with ≥ 80% valid responses across screen time, activity, mental health, and social measures (n = 3, 713). Heterogeneity in screen time and activity profiles was examined by computing subject × subject similarity matrices and applying the Louvain algorithm for modularity maximization [3]. We retained clusters with at least 10% of the subjects, resulting in six clusters covering 91% of the selected participants. Clusters were characterized by screen time and activity patterns, and their associations with mental health outcomes. In Figure 1 we can observe preliminary analysis with different clusters presenting distinct screen usage patterns. For example, in Figure 1B, Cluster 2 expresses higher screen activity reported both by the children and their parents and correspondent higher scores in most problematic mental health outcomes. Further analysis is being developed. Future work will also explore this approach longitudinally, investigating how patterns of screen use and categorized activities relate to mental health trajectories and brain organization, with a focus on developing analytical frameworks that can capture the complex, bidirectional relationships between digital media use and youth mental health.
items.authors: Maurich Novelli, Asia*

items.title: Complexity, Risk and Resilience in Policy Sub-Systems
items.abstract: In this paper we assess systemic resilience [1] by considering risk complexity and risk predictability in a simulation of a policy making sub-system. The aim is to determine whether a range of different risks have a differential impact on the production of consensus or the conditions that lead them to degenerate to polarisation and conflict. We account for different types of power for political agents, such as influence, authority, relational and information advantage. We further acknowledge complexity in that policy domains are interdependent and that external shocks can alter system properties (i.e. affect the terms of reference for the interaction between actors). We also account for policy issue salience among political agents; the different phases of the policy process, which determine the level of their engagement; and the level of agitation contingent to proximity to a decision/vote. We employ data from well documented cases of longitudinal policy making [2] and use t1 as baseline of networks of policy making comparing to t2 with Agent Based Model simulations which provide an opportunity to test for different network and complexity effects. The synthetic data simulate deliberations during a specific stage of the agenda setting and consultation stage in the policy process. The aim is to test for the impact of a range of political risks, including system polarization, sensitivity to conflict during negotiations, system disintegration, and variance from ex ante preferences of policy actors (i.e. risk in the predictability of outcomes ex ante). The model employed is a hybrid network model, combined with an agent-based model. Modeling includes variables for issue salience, clustering level, network properties (reciprocity, transitivity, etc), tie maintenance costs, and agent power among others. Extreme events are also modeled such as external shocks, while agents impact one another via a DeGroot and a bounded-confidence Friedkin-Johnsen influence process. Also modeled is the presence of exceptional agents, such as political entrepreneurs and leaders. Initial results indicate resilient political systems are affected by risks from policy shifts, and shocks from policy debates when they degenerate to echo-chamber exchanges [3]. Resilience also appears to have a negative relationship to entropy risks. Furthermore, the presence of political entrepreneurs significantly affects the probability of achieving policy consensus and reduces policy variance. This study confirms the sensitivity of political outcomes to the interaction between policy system resilience, risk level and risk type.
items.authors: Dimitris CHRISTOPOULOS*

items.title: Comparative study of networks of writers in different historical periods obtained by Wikipedia-mining
items.abstract: From the English version of Wikipedia, we extracted the pages corresponding to persons in the literary world, and we created six networks organized by birth date, from 1700 to 1999. The weighted links among them are not the standard hyperlinks but their more subtle relatedness which is obtained calculating the Google distance[1,2] (GD) between the pages of the most relevant authors in each period (as measured by the in-degree of their Wikipedia pages); the relatedness is the negative exponential of their GD. Many of the characteristics of these networks (their size, average connectivity, size and order of the largest core) follow clear temporal patterns, presenting an exponential growth across time (with an exception for the last historical period which represents a "work in progress"), and that trend is consistent with the trends of Wikipedia as a whole; in spite of that, the strengths of the weighted links remain almost identical across the different historical periods, presenting in all cases a clear bimodal distribution of weak and strong links; the relative weight of weak links increases in time as the relations between writers diversify. We implemented a spectral analysis of the different networks, using the eigenvalue distribution of the normalized Laplacian; while the average eigvenvalue remains constant in time, their distribution narrows; but remarkable, the algebraic connectivity of all the networks remains constant in spite of the size of the networks changing by more than an order of magnitude (from 50 to 1100) and the increasing value of their (Shannon) spectral entropy[3]. Since the networks described above are dense, we used some procedures of sparsification (triangular inequality backbone extraction, standard thresholding and disparity filtering, keeping equal levels of connectivity across networks) in order to further or analysis, and the different discussed lead to similar results. The identity of the writers belonging to the highest cores provides insights on their connections, not gleaned apriori from the structure of Wikipedia itself. In some cases it reveals connections linked to national origin or language, in others to the thematic content of their writing, or some unexpected connections between two different national groups. We studied the process of disintegration of each network upon successive removal of nodes in decreasing order of their degree or closeness or edges in decreasing order of their betweenness, monitoring the evolution of the largest components. The removal of nodes based on closeness uncovered an interesting hierarchical structure, since across all networks and connectivity levels, at certain points the removal of a node results in the splitting of the network in two pieces of similar size, revealing the existence of communities, and sub-communities within them (see Figure 1) which had no links connecting them in the original network (and not visible in the overall structure of the network). Normally these communities split along national or linguistic lines; their study might provide valuable insights to complement what a more "microscopic”, discipline specific, approach can provide. [1] G. Schwartz, Human. Soc. Sci. Comm. 8, 127 (2021) [2]. L. Miccio , C. Gamez-Perez, J.L. Suarez and G. Schwartz, Adv. Comp. Syst. 25, 2240010 [3] M. De Dominico and J. Biamonte., Phys Rev X, 6 ,040062 (2016)
items.authors: Cwilich, Gabriel*; Agapitos, Paschalis; Spivak, Yonatan; Schwartz, Gustavo A.

items.title: All contacts are not equal: Spatiotemporal constraints shape network structure and transmission dynamics
items.abstract: Contact network structure is widely studied because of its influential role in infectious disease transmission. Since the POLYMOD study [1], routine contact surveys have consistently used similar distance (e.g., 2 meters) and duration (e.g., 5 minutes) constraints to define close contacts relevant to respiratory transmission. This standardization facilitates comparison between studies but critically overlooks the unique transmission profiles of respiratory viruses [2]. We hypothesize that pathogens may be limited to different contact network structures based on their spatiotemporal transmission constraints and thus demonstrate distinct transmission dynamics. Using a dataset with fine-scale interaction data, we investigate how changes in contact definition impact contact network structure and respiratory disease dynamics. To characterize how network structure varies with different contact distance and duration thresholds, we use app-based GPS location data from anonymized and opted-in mobile devices aggregated to protect individual privacy. We focus our analysis on locations relevant to public health (e.g., restaurants, grocery stores), transmission constraints that are realistic for respiratory pathogens (e.g., influenza, measles, coronaviruses), and network features known to predict epidemic outcomes (e.g., mean degree, degree heterogeneity, transitivity). After adjusting for network sampling biases [3], we find that contact network structure is sensitive to contact definition (Figure 1). Pathogens capable of transmission over large distances or through brief exposures have access to denser and more heterogeneously connected contact networks, whereas pathogens requiring close proximity or longer interactions are restricted to sparser networks with lower degree heterogeneity. Through network disease modeling, we show that this structural variation leads to distinct transmission dynamics, which can inform the inference of transmission modes for emerging pathogens from epidemiological data. These findings shed light on the evolutionary pressures faced by different respiratory pathogens and help characterize which pathogens are more likely to exhibit superspreading. [1] Mossong J, et al. Social contacts and mixing patterns relevant to the spread of infectious diseases. PLOS Medicine. 2008 Mar;5(3):e74. [2] Leung NHL. Transmissibility and transmission of respiratory viruses. Nat Rev Microbiol. 2021 Aug;19(8):528–45. [3] Kolaczyk ED. Statistical Analysis of Network Data: Methods and Models. New York, NY: Springer; 2009.
items.authors: Taube, Juliana*; Tiu, Andrew; Bansal, Shweta

items.title: Network science techniques applied to soft matter simulations
items.abstract: Soft matter provides a concrete instance of networks leading to complex physical properties. In this poster, we demonstrate the use of network science tools to study transitions in connectivity and mechanics of soft matter systems. These tools provide tangible evidence for the physical mechanisms that underlie material properties in two systems of interest: percolation robustness in ion exchange membranes, and graph coarsening in granular systems. For material systems, the transition between a state of percolation to a state of disjointedness can have important ramifications on their properties. To computationally track a percolation process, we propose the use of k-stub decomposition (KSD) [1], which excludes particles with degree less than k from the network. In physical networks, increasing k has the effect of peeling back layers from the surface; hence, increasing k can remove weakly connected surface dendrites and bottlenecks. For example, in our study [2], anion exchange membranes undergo a sharp increase in ionic conductivity at specific relative humidities, presumably due to percolation of ion channels comprising water molecules. KSD analysis of molecular simulations reveals that this transition takes place when channels are robust at the k = 3 level, revealing that 3 water molecules are needed in the channel at a minimum to enable a vehicular transport of ions. The second system we have studied deals with dense granular suspensions: under the right conditions, these systems undergo shear thickening due to frictional constraints between the particles. As these systems flow, the frictional force chain network (FFCN) evolves as frictional contacts are formed and broken. The properties of FFCN are dependent on the kind of constraints (frictional constants, rolling/sliding frictions, etc.) and conditions (density, shear rate). In this study [3], we used graph coarsening to identify independent force chains, hubs, and terminals. The force chain length distribution and the connectivity of the FFCN provide useful insight into the trends in rheology of these systems. Beyond these two examples, we are currently applying these techniques to other soft matter systems, including complex polymeric mesophases, metamaterials, and nanoparticle self-assembly. [1] Mohseni-Kabir, A., Pant, M., Towsley, D., Guha, S., & Swami, A. (2021). Percolation thresholds for robust network connec- tivity. Journal of Statistical Mechanics: Theory and Experiment, 2021 (1), 013212. [2] Wang, Z., Sun, G., Lewis, N. H., Mandal, M., Sharma, A., Kim, M., ... & de Pablo, J. J. (2025). Water-mediated ion transport in an anion exchange membrane. Nature Communications, 16 (1), 1099. [3] Sharma, S., Sharma, A., & Singh, A. (2025). Frictional contact network in dense suspension flow. arXiv preprint arXiv:2505.22747.
items.authors: Sharma, Abhishek*

items.title: A Metapopulation Network Framework for Evaluating Travel-Based Intervention Strategies for Global Outbreak Control
items.abstract: Early intervention at the source of an outbreak is widely recognized as the most effective way to curb spread in the initial phase. Travel restrictions and border-control measures are a common example: they are frequently among the first internationally deployed actions, despite mixed evidence on their effectiveness. In practice, however, policy responses are often reactive, introduced only after epidemiological thresholds are crossed. Here, we explicitly evaluate timing as the key policy lever by comparing proactive versus reactive travel-based interventions during emerging outbreaks. We model global disease spread on a metapopulation mobility network, in which geographic areas (subpopulations) are connected by empirically travel flows. Within each subpopulation, transmission dynamics follow an SLIR compartmental model, while individuals move between subpopulations along network edges according to mobility data. We evaluate six travel and border-control intervention strategies spanning two policy approaches: reactive measures, in which controls are implemented only after an epidemiological trigger is reached and are applied to travel from the outbreak source, and proactive measures, in which controls are implemented early and reduce mobility more broadly across the network (Fig. 1A & B). Within each category, we consider three interventions: (i) border-control measures only (mobility reduction), (ii) border-control measures combined with targeted mitigation that reduces transmission at the source, and (iii) border-control measures combined with global mitigation that reduces transmission across all locations. Using stochastic numerical simulations on both theoretical random networks and the data-driven Global Epidemic and Mobility (GLEAM) metapopulation model, we systematically vary implementation timing and the intensity of mobility reduction. Results (Fig. 1C) highlight the limitations of reactive, source-focused border measures, which are generally implemented too late to substantially alter international dissemination. In contrast, proactive, early border-control strategies, especially when paired with measures that reduce transmission (global mitigation), provide markedly stronger control of global spread across a wide range of timings and intensities, and can significantly delay or even successfully contain outbreaks. Overall, we offer a general framework to quantify the trade-offs between proactive versus reactive travel restrictions and border control in a globally connected system during the earliest stages of an emerging outbreak. These insights can help inform timely policy design and strengthen preparedness for future emerging infectious disease threats.
items.authors: Bay, Clara*; Davis, Jessica; Chinazzi, Matteo; Vespignani, Alessandro

items.title: Party Time: A Temporal Network Dataset of a Social Celebration and its Bursty Dynamics
items.abstract: Human proximity networks from conferences, classrooms, hospitals, and workplaces have consistently exhibited bursty interaction dynamics [1]. However, many of these contexts involved scheduled interactions of different lengths and participants with different formal roles. Is burstiness an inherent feature of organic social interaction, or limited to settings with heterogeneous types of interactions? Do the temporal statistics of human interaction observed in professional, academic, and public gatherings extend to a private social celebration? We collected high-resolution proximity data from a cocktail hour of a wedding in a Chicago suburb. Ninety-three consenting wedding guests wore Ultra-Wideband (UWB) proximity badges that detected when other badge-wearers were within 1.5m (± 0.1 m, 5s bins), supplemented by coarse location estimates and pre– and post–event surveys on personality and self-reported conversations. Participants self-identified their wedding party affiliation (among friend/family groups), enabling direct social group-level mixing analysis instead of demographic proxies relied upon for previous group-mixing studies (e.g., [2]). We are preparing a de-identified public release of the resulting temporal network, contributing a novel “private party” setting to the public corpus of face-to-face interaction temporal networks. Preliminary comparisons to one-hour windows from SocioPatterns collections [3] (conferences, schools, office, hospital) show similarly robust bursty dynamics in the party context, though with systematic differences. As seen in Figure 1, contact rate (edge weights of the integrated network) distributions agree closely; contact duration distribution remains comparably broad but exhibit an earlier cut-off; the inter-arrival time distribution is steeper yet exhibits a stronger power-law trend than the SocioPatterns contexts. Using the self-identified groups, we are investigating if differences between within-group and cross-group interaction timing contributes to the overall burstiness. Once released, this data collection will enable more empirically grounded modeling of social events, whether for contact tracing simulations or cultural studies, in addition to refining the universality of face-to-face interaction burstiness. [1] Barrat, A., Cattuto, C., “Temporal Networks of Face-to-Face Human Interactions” [2] M. Oliveira, et al., “Group mixing drives inequality in face-to-face gatherings” [3] https://www.sociopatterns.org/
items.authors: Stadlan, Joshua*

items.title: When PageRank Feeds Back: Fixed Points of Personalized Centrality in Complex Networks
items.abstract: PageRank is one of the most influential centrality measures in network science, originally introduced for ranking web pages and later generalized to a wide range of applications in social, biological, economic, and technological networks. While classical PageRank theory focuses on the existence and uniqueness of a stationary distribution for a fixed personalization vector, less attention has been given to PageRank as a functional operator acting on the space of personalization vectors itself. This presentation studies PageRank from this functional perspective. The central aim of the poster is to provide a complete characterization of existence, uniqueness, and structure of fixed points of PageRank functional $PR_\lambda$ in terms of the network’s strongly connected components. Specifically, it is analytically demonstrated that if $\mathcal{G}$ is a strongly connected network, then $PR_\lambda$ admits a unique fixed point, which can be analytically determined. Conversely, when $\mathcal{G}$ is not strongly connected, existence of fixed points is still guaranteed, while uniqueness depends on the structure of the strongly connected components (sinks). Beyond their theoretical significance, these results hold practical implications for dynamic and temporal networks, where the underlying topology evolves over time. In such contexts, the personalization vector itself may vary dynamically, raising the natural question of how to select teleportation distributions that reflect intrinsic structural properties of the network. For example, in web graphs, one could adopt a feedback mechanism in which teleportation probabilities at a given time step are determined by the PageRank distribution from the previous time step. The steady state of such a process, if it exists, corresponds precisely to the fixed point of the operator $PR_\lambda$. Hence, by adopting a functional operator perspective, this work establishes a rigorous analytical framework for PageRank in complex networks, elucidating both the mathematical structure of the operator and its potential for modeling adaptive and temporally evolving systems.
items.authors: Romance, Miguel*

items.title: Bridging contacts as epidemic bottlenecks in a city-scale mobility network
items.abstract: Non-pharmaceutical interventions (NPIs) such as lockdowns and movement restrictions aim to reduce close-range en- counters that can transmit airborne infections. Yet contacts are not equally consequential, their epidemiological impact depends on where they sit in the larger network of human encounters. Here we ask whether a small set of structurally “bridging” contacts can act as transmission bottlenecks and therefore provide high-leverage targets for prevention. We analyze opt-in, anonymized geo-location data from 14 430 Berlin residents with persistent identifiers collected from June to August 2023. From individual trajectories, we reconstruct city-wide mobility and contact networks. A contact is defined by co-location of two individuals within 8 m for at least 2 min, yielding 12 048 observed contacts. Using Louvain community detection on the resulting encounter network, we identify loosely connected mesoscale units and quantify how community structure relates to urban boundaries. Visitation and mixing patterns still align with Berlin’s former east–west division and with current district borders (boundary alignment p << 0.01), indicating that historical and administrative structure continues to shape present-day encounter networks. Classifying edges by community membership shows a near- even split between within-community (6 379) and between-community (5 669) contacts. However, within-community contacts are about 5 x more likely to recur between the same partners, while between-community contacts are more tran- sient. To evaluate intervention leverage, we run SIR-type outbreak simulations and compare edge-removal strategies at a fixed “contact reduction budget.” Removing 30% of edges produces strongly strategy-dependent outcomes: selectively removing between-community edges reduces total infections far more than random deletion of the same size. Relative to the no-removal baseline, the attack rate drops to 0.33 ± 0.05 under targeted inter-community removal versus 0.58 ± 0.08 under random removal, corresponding to 43% fewer infections than random suppression for the same reduction in contacts. These results highlight bridging links between communities as disproportionate drivers of urban transmission and suggest that network-aware policies that selectively limit cross-community mixing could achieve substantial epidemic control with less disruption than uniform restrictions.
items.authors: Zambrano, Marlli*; Thampi, Ashish; Jarynowski, Andrzej ; Rincon, Alejandra; Schulz, Steven ; Belik, Vitaly

items.title: Percolation and Criticality in Hyperuniform Networks
items.abstract: Hyperuniform many-particle systems, which encompass crystals, quasicrystals and certain exotic disordered systems, exhibit an anomalous suppression of density fluctuations on macroscopic length scales relative to those of conventional disordered systems [1]. These systems are well studied in Euclidean space. We propose that networks derived from hyperuniform point configurations show distinctive critical behaviors. In this study, we construct Delaunay triangulation networks of 2D point configurations, in which the points become the vertices and the edges are weighted by Euclidean distance. We investigate the percolation behaviors of networks derived from disordered stealthy hyperuniform systems, which are an important subclass that exhibits a tunable degree of local order via the parameter $\chi$ [2]. We compare them with those constructed from Poisson (uncorrelated) point configurations. We investigate a distance-based bond percolation process in which shorter edges in the triangulation network are assigned higher occupation probabilities according to a tuning parameter $z$. We estimate the percolation thresholds $z_c$ and critical exponents of the networks via finite-size scaling and the Newman–Ziff Monte Carlo algorithm. We find that stealthy hyperuniform networks exhibit lower percolation thresholds than Poisson networks. The higher the value of $\chi$, the lower the percolation threshold, suggesting enhanced connectivity and transport efficiency in networks with hyperuniform spatial embeddings, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:configs}. Moreover, we show that stealthy hyperuniform systems with large $\chi$ belong to the lattice universality class, while the critical exponents of the Poisson and low-$\chi$ systems exhibit consistent shifts. Recent attempts have illustrated the challenges of classifying statistically homogeneous disordered planar networks using classical network centrality measures [3]. Our distance-based percolation method is able to identify and distinguish these networks with suppressed density fluctuations through their emergent clustering properties. [1] S. Torquato and F. H. Stillinger, “Local density fluctuations, hyperuniformity, and order metrics,” Phys. Rev. E, vol. 68, no. 4, p. 041113, 2003. [2] S. Torquato, “Hyperuniform states of matter,” Phys. Rep., vol. 745, pp. 1–95, 2018. [3] E. Newby, W. Shi, Y. Jiao, S. Torquato and R. Albert, "From point patterns to networks: to what extent does the Delaunay triangulation reproduce key spatial and density information?" Journal of Complex Networks, vol. 13, no. 6, 2025.
items.authors: Wang, Yongyi*; Kim, Jaeuk; Jiao, Yang; Stuhl, Izabella; Torquato, Salvatore; Albert, Reka

items.title: Time-varying differential network analysis from tumor-educated platelet transcriptome to capture mechanisms of tumor progression
items.abstract: Differential network analysis has been designed to highlight network changes between conditions: in network biology, this approach can capture tissue-selective processes and identify critical signaling pathways altered during tumor transformation and progression [1]. In oncology, the most appropriate tumor surveillance strategy remains an unsolved issue. Indeed, the typical approach (based on solid biopsies and resource-demanding follow-up programs) has limitations in terms of invasiveness, time resolution and accessibility of molecular data. To date, blood-based liquid biopsies represent a promising source of biomarkers to detect and monitor cancer without invasive procedures. The latest developments in isolating and characterizing various elements in blood, including tumor-educated platelet (TEP) RNA, make it possible to identify cancer-specific genomic and transcriptomic abnormalities in blood. In particular, TEPs are circulating blood cells with a distinct tumor-driven phenotype and act as carriers and protectors of metastases. Some recent studies have shown that the TEPs transcriptome can be used for cancer diagnosis [2]. However, these works are only based on differential expression analysis. The objective of this work is to propose Differential Network Analysis (DNA) to study longitudinal TEPs transcriptomic data. In particular, we applied the Time-varying DNA (tDNA, [3]) to investigate gene network rewiring during glioblastoma (GBM) progression. We downloaded mRNA sequencing data of TEPs from the GEO database under accession number 156902 [4] and we selected GBM samples. Longitudinal data were available for 55 patients, although for a subset of 13 patients a complete set of measurements from time 0 to time 3 was available. Given the K=4 time points, the tDNA approach jointly estimated K-1=3 differential networks ∆, enabling the identification of both consistent and time-specific rewiring patterns driven by key genes. These genes were classified as either common (shared across time points) or time-specific critical nodes. Across the three obtained differential networks, we identified 14 common and 134, 80, and 72 time-specific key genes for ∆_(0-1), ∆_(1-2) and ∆_(2-3), respectively. KEGG enrichment analysis of the identified nodes revealed convergence on immune and inflammatory signaling pathways, including IL-17 signaling (adj-p=0.005), Toll-like receptor signaling (adj-p=0.008), and Herpes simplex virus 1 infection (adj-p=0.01), largely driven by time-specific genes from each differential network. These findings highlight processes associated with tumor progression and immune modulation in glioblastoma [5]. To validate the findings obtained with tDNA, we compared them with DiffCorr approach [6], developed to compare pairs of experimental conditions. This alternative method identified 101, 120, and 102 critical nodes in the three differential networks, selected by intersecting the top 10% of genes ranked by degree, betweenness, and closeness centrality. Seventy-one genes overlapped with those identified by tDNA, including 4 classified as common hubs. However, KEGG enrichment analysis did not highlight significant pathways. In conclusion, these results demonstrate that critical nodes identified in time-varying differential networks obtained from longitudinal TEPs transcriptomic data can detect molecular mechanisms of disease progression with potential clinical impact in tumor monitoring. [1] Yan W et al. Mol Ther Nucleic Acids. 2024. doi: 10.1016/j.omtn.2024.102260. [2] Best MG et al, Cancer Cell. 2015. Doi: 10.1016/j.ccell.2015.09.018. [3] Xu T et al. IEEE/ACM Trans Comput Biol Bioinform. 2021. doi: 10.1109/TCBB.2019.2949039. [4] Sol N et al. Cell Rep Med. 2020. doi: 10.1016/j.xcrm.2020.100101. [5] Parajuli P & Mittal S. J Spine Neurosurg. 2013. doi: 10.4172/2325-9701.S1-004. [6] Fukushima A. Gene. 2013. doi: 10.1016/j.gene.2012.11.028.
items.authors: Vessi, Santiago; Rughetti, Aurelia; Filetti, Marco; Farina, Lorenzo; Petti, Manuela*

items.title: Evidence of Gender-based Network Biases in Respondent-driven Sampling Data
items.abstract: Respondent-driven sampling (RDS) is a network-based method for studying hidden populations, such as people who inject drugs (PWID) (Heckathorn, 1997; Gile et al., 2010). Statistical inference using RDS relies on modeling recruitment as a random walk on the network, assuming participants accurately report their network composition and recruit peers approximately at random (Heckathorn, 1997; Volz and Heckathorn, 2008). In practice, reported networks may not fully reflect the true network structure, and some subgroups may face barriers to participation, potentially biasing statistical inference. Using a uniquely rich RDS dataset (Akiyama et al., 2025) spanning multiple sites and several types of network data, we investigate gender differences in network structure, reporting, and participation, and assess potential biases impacting inference. We analyze RDS data from 3,152 PWID across 17 sites in Kenya, collected as an extension of the TLC-IDU study (NCT01557998), focusing on the reported gender composition of participants’ social networks. Our analysis evaluates whether the observed proportion of women in the sample is consistent with expectations of random recruitment and the observed gender–degree structure of the sample. We further compare gender differences in reported 30-day networks, close contact data, and observed network ties. Population estimates were calculated using standard RDS methods (Volz and Heckathorn, 2008), and network simulation tests were used to assess discrepancies in participation and reporting. Results indicate that participants report more women in their networks than expected, suggesting potential over-reporting or under-participation of women. Across multiple sites, results were more nuanced, but indicate that women report more women in their 30-day networks, close contacts, and observed ties compared to men. These discrepancies highlight challenges in creating valid inferences from reported social network data and provide insight into the complexity of designing targeted interventions for PWID.
items.authors: Costa, Anabel*

items.title: Community-aware hyperbolic embeddings: methodology and application on the FlyWire FAFB dataset
items.abstract: Hyperbolic geometry offers a superior latent space for embedding hierarchical complex networks, yet existing methods frequently overlook the utility of efficient community detection methods. We present CLOVE (Cluster Level Optimized Vertex Embedding), a recently published approach that maps networks to the hyperbolic disk by using community structure to dictate angular sectors —optimized via a Traveling Salesman Problem heuristic. We apply this framework to the massive FlyWire Drosophila FAFB connectome, benchmarking the results against ground-truth physical coordinates. Our analysis demonstrates that incorporating community priors generates resource-efficient embeddings that successfully capture the topological organization of large-scale biological neural networks.
items.authors: Sulyok, Bendegúz*

items.title: The Impact of Network Structure and Prior Assumptions on Network Reconstructability
items.abstract: Many real-world systems from biology to financial markets are governed by complex networks of interactions where the network structures are either directly unobservable or must be inferred from data [1]. Often, researchers perform this network reconstruction task by using time series recordings of node-level dynamics in the system. When reconstructing a network from N node-level time series, there are O(N^2) possible edges to infer. With so many possible edges, any practical network reconstruction method requires regularization to avoid overfitting and yield acceptable results [2]. Regularization, however, can impose structural preferences, producing biases in the network reconstruction. Despite the ubiquity of network reconstruction, these induced structural biases are rarely characterized [3,4]. While there are many techniques for reconstructing networks from time series data, we focus on a nonparametric Bayesian method based on the minimum description length [3]. We use this approach due to its: 1) clear and explicit statistical biases that can be readily interpreted, 2) lack of hyperparameters, the values of which are generally unknown in practical settings, 3) broad applications for a large class of networks, and 4) asymptotic recoverability of the true data-generating model, which is contained within the model class. This last condition ensures that if we record node states for an infinite number of time steps, we are guaranteed to recover the true network exactly, so long as the dynamical process satisfies certain properties [2]. On the contrary, in the case of limited data, our reconstruction will be more heavily influenced by the model’s prior assumptions. In this work, we simulate Ising model dynamics on a range of synthetic networks, and we investigate the following: given a particular choice of statistical bias, how does reconstruction error vary with the class of network in question? While such questions have been considered in the case where network properties are directly conditioned on the prior [3,4], we instead focus on the case when no network statistics are known a priori, which is a realistic scenario in many practical applications with real data. We find that in the low data regime: 1) dense networks are systematically reconstructed as more sparse (Fig. 1a), 2) networks with high clustering are not reconstructed as such (Fig. 1b), and 3) networks with heterogeneous degree are harder to recover (Fig. 1c). This last finding contrasts with recent work [3], highlighting the utility (and perhaps fragility) of strong assumptions in improving reconstruction accuracy. Overall, our results suggest that, in scenarios with limited data, even well-motivated nonparametric priors may not provide sufficiently strong inductive biases to recover the corrected network. [1] Oliver M Cliff et al. Unifying pairwise interactions in complex dynamics. Nature Computational Science (2023). [2] Charles Murphy et al. Duality between predictability and reconstructability in complex systems. Nature Communications (2024). [3] Tiago P Peixoto. Network reconstruction via the minimum description length principle. Physical Review X (2025). [4] Jia-Jie Qin and Gang Yan. High Reconstructability of Degree-Heterogeneous Networks. Physical Review Letters (2025).
items.authors: Glover, Cory*; Weis, Erik; Kumar, Sagar; Ehlert, Joey; Klein, Brennan

items.title: The Hidden Organization of Online Communities: Reddit through the Lens of Stochastic Block Models and Neural Embeddings
