items.abstract: Over the past two decades, online platforms have staged major and challenging events, including the spread of (mis)information, the amplification of unrepresentative voices, toxic public discourse, and coordinated collective action. The exposure and exchange networks they enable provide the structural conditions through which social friction and change emerge. Understanding these networks is therefore crucial for explaining how collective dynamics allow the propagation of news, behavior cascades, norms are enforced, and the coordination of collective action efforts. However, uncovering community structure from observed behavior depends critically on the computational tools employed, which can yield markedly different representations and thus constrain our theories of online collective dynamics. Here, we examine these differences by comparing higher-order communities identified using neural embeddings (NEs) and stochastic block models (SBMs) applied to 13 years of Reddit data from the Pushshift API, comprising approximately 5B comments and 3M users. Our network-based approach reveals a more complex structure of political discussion on Reddit than that identified by previous work using NE representations. Using different SBM configurations, both fixing the number of communities to 30 and inferring it from the data, we find that the traditional “Political” cluster breaks down into 18 and 112 communities, respectively, while absorbing thousands of additional subreddits and thereby losing thematic coherence. Finally, applying a Hierarchical SBM (hSBM) uncovers the intrinsic nestedness of those communities of interest: it iteratively performs community inference and groups the communities present at lower levels into larger blocks as we move up in the hierarchy. This hierarchical approach uncovers self-organization across multiple resolution levels and identifies information corridors through which content moves as users cross-post across communities. Notably, it shows that some non-political subreddits occupy structural positions equivalent to political communities, highlighting organizational dynamics and generative mechanisms that are concealed in neural embedding representations. Overall, we show that network-based methods reveal richer and more nuanced patterns of online political structure than previously identified, underscoring the importance of methodological choices in studies of social media networks.
items.authors: Danus, Lluis*; Gonzalez-Bailon, Sandra

items.title: A continuum limit for dense spatial networks
items.abstract: Many physical systems---such as optical waveguide lattices and dense neuronal or vascular networks---can be modeled by metric graphs, where slender ``wires'' (edges) support wave or diffusion equations subject to Kirchhoff boundary conditions at the nodes. We propose a continuum-limit framework which replaces edgewise differential equations with a coarse-grained partial differential equation (PDE) defined on the continuous space occupied by the network. The derivation naturally introduces an edge-conductivity tensor, an edge-capacity function, and a vertex number density to encode how each microscopic patch of the graph contributes to the macroscopic phenomena. These results have interesting similarities and differences with the Riemannian Laplace-Beltrami operator. We calculate all macroscopic parameters from first principles via a systematic discrete-to-continuous local homogenization, finding an anomalous effective embedding dimension resulting from a homogenized diffusivity. Numerical examples---including periodic lattices, random graphs, and quasiperiodic monotiles---demonstrate that each finite model converges to its corresponding PDE (posed on different manifolds like tori, disks, and spheres) in the limit of increasing vertex density. These high-density networks encode emergent material and functional properties. They reflect the ability of many real-world, space-filling networks to function simultaneously at multiple scales using the continuum as a feature.
items.authors: Holden, Sidney*; Vasil, Geoffrey

items.title: Bridging Simple and Complex Contagions in Belief Dynamics: A Voter Model Extension
items.abstract: The standard voter model of belief dynamics [1] – how beliefs form and evolve – assumes that individuals consistently express their internal beliefs and that these beliefs can change from one period to another. However, social norms and conformity pressures often lead individuals to either withhold beliefs or express beliefs that are contrary to their own [2], while internally held beliefs may exhibit inertia due to psychological mechanisms such as social hysteresis. Our extension of the voter model (Figure 1 (a)) distinguishes between expressed beliefs, following simple contagion dynamics, and internal beliefs, that update through complex contagion mechanisms. Individuals hold binary beliefs on a given topic (a = “Against,” f = “in Favour”) but may express the opposite (af = holding “Against” but expressing “In Favour”). Expressed beliefs update through simple contagion based on the beliefs expressed by neighbors in the previous period, with contagion strength mediated by alignment with one’s internal belief (c when aligned and c′ when misaligned), reflecting the potential burden of expressing beliefs contrary to those internally held. Internal beliefs update based on neighbors’ expressed beliefs but follow a q-voter model, capturing complex contagion dynamics and belief inertia. Mean-field approximations reveal that when individuals face higher contagion pressure to express beliefs contrary to their internal ones (c′ > c), the symmetry typical of voter models breaks down, enabling minority beliefs to persist at higher proportions than their initial frequency in the long term (Figure 1 (b)). These effects are robust across a range of initial conditions. Future work will explore how network structure and belief withholding shape the interplay between simple and complex contagion in real-world contexts.
items.authors: Franco, João*; Robinette, Michael; Lipfert, Jackson; Lovato, Juniper; Hébert-Dufresne, Laurent

items.title: Spatial barriers reshape residents’ emotional experiences in urban China
items.abstract: Emotion is a fundamental human physiological process with well-documented associations across a spectrum of life outcomes—from economic success and career advancement to physical health and longevity. Urban spatial structures profoundly shape residents' emotional experiences in their daily movements. While behavioral barriers—visible obstacles like highways and rivers, as well as invisible ones rooted in socioeconomic segregation and uneven amenity access—have been documented[1], how they relate to emotional experiences remains under-explored. This study investigates whether behavioral barriers shape residents' emotional expressions and how economic conditions, infrastructure availability, and urban density moderate this relationship across cities of different scales and regions. We analyzed 75 million geo-tagged social media posts (Weibo, Chinese version of Twitter) from a cleaned dataset of over 2 million residents' check-ins and textual expressions at POI (Point of Interest) level across 60 Chinese cities. This integrated dataset uniquely enables examination of emotional patterns at both neighborhood and inter-regional scales. Our approach combines: (1) mobility neural embeddings to identify behavioral barriers from observed movement patterns, capturing both visible and invisible constraints that traditional geographic analysis misses[1]; (2) sentiment analysis to quantify emotional divergence; and (3) multivariate regression to examine barrier-emotion relationships across city types. Overall, this study integrates emotional wellbeing, place, and human mobility to reveal how spatial segregation shapes not just physical movement but also residents' psychological experiences[2] and wellbeing[3]. By providing fine-grained spatial evidence, we offer actionable insights for designing healthier urban environments.
items.authors: Zeng, Miao*; Deng, Hong

items.title: Moral Signaling and Network Centrality in Online Opioid Recovery Communities
items.abstract: Online communities can play an important role in supporting recovery from opioid use disorder (OUD), particularly for people who lack access to supportive offline networks. While prior work has examined the content of recovery-related discourse, less is known about how moral language is structured within networks of interaction and how it relates to actor prominence and engagement. We study four large Reddit communities focused on OUD recovery and harm reduction, modeling each community as a directed interaction network in which nodes represent authors and edges represent reply relationships. Using supervised large language models trained to detect ten moral foundations proposed in Moral Foundations Theory (Care, Harm, Fairness, Cheating, Authority, Subversion, Loyalty, Betrayal, Purity, and Degradation), we annotate over half a million posts and comments with moral attributes and enrich network edges with the moral content expressed in interactions. We analyze how moral signaling varies across communities with different recovery philosophies, how moral content relates to engagement, and how it is distributed among central versus peripheral actors. Across all communities, moral language is common within interaction networks. Distinct moral profiles emerge at the community level, reflecting differing norms and treatment orientations. Central actors, those with both high indegree and outdegree, are are more likely to be morally expressive than less connected participants and engage not only in virtue-oriented discourse (e.g., Care), but also in vice-oriented moral language (Degradation, Cheating) often associated with stigma and shame. Rather than suppressing such discourse, central actors may express negative morality as part of community norm negotiation and as a way of engaging with shared experiences of stigmatizing and dehumanizing moral judgments. These findings demonstrate how moral sentiment functions as a relational signal within recovery networks and highlight the value of integrating moral content with network analysis to understand community dynamics. More broadly, the work illustrates how moral signaling and network position jointly shape participation and engagement in online support communities.
items.authors: Glasgow, Kimberly*

items.title: Knowledge Graph-Based Analysis of Curriculum-Industry Alignment: A Case Study
items.abstract: This study proposes a knowledge graph-based framework to analyze curriculum-industry alignment. Using a U.S. university's Computer Science program as a case study, we construct a three-layer heterogeneous knowledge graph linking courses, skills, and jobs. Results show that the curriculum covers programming and database skills well, with strong foundations in algorithms, theory, and AI. However, industrial-level skills such as cloud platforms and DevOps are absent, and emerging roles have lower accessibility than traditional ones. The framework is generalizable to other disciplines and institutions.
items.authors: Yang, Bonan; Banu, Syeda Zunehra; Ercal, Gunes*

items.title: GRNage: An epigenetic clock based on gene regulatory network features
items.abstract: Epigenetic clocks are models that measure biological aging. The divergence of biological age from chronological age is a hallmark of many complex diseases, including cancer. Epigenetic clocks are typically trained to select a set of DNA methylation loci (CpGs) that are predictive of chronological age or other aging phenotypes in a healthy cohort. A limitation of this approach is the lack of interpretability of the selected model components. Sets of CpGs that are predictive of the training outcome may not directly participate in underlying biological mechanisms that are drivers of aging or disease progression. Gene regulatory networks (GRNs) are bipartite graphs connecting transcription factors (TFs) to the genes that they regulate. GRNs encode functional regulatory processes within a network structure. We hypothesized that an aging clock based on GRN network features could provide interpretable functional insight into dysregulated biological aging in cancer. To this end, we introduce GRNage, the first gene regulatory network-based aging clock. GRNage models biological age as a linear combination of GRN properties (TF out-degree; target gene in-degree). The input to GRNage is a GRN estimated with the PANDA algorithm ; the output is an estimated biological age (see Figure). GRNage is trained to select a set of TFs and genes whose degrees are predictive of epigenetic age acceleration as estimated by the Horvath epigenetic clock As gene regulatory processes have been demonstrated to be highly tissue-specific , GRNage is also tissue-specific. Here, we present a skin-based GRNage clock trained in 451 subcutaneous melanoma (SKCM) samples from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA). We tested the relevance of SKCM GRNage in biological context by calculating GRNage of four different tumor metastasis classifications. In the training dataset, the mean GRNage was significantly higher in the regional metastases than the primary tumor (p=0.048 for regional cutaneous/subcutaneous metastases vs. primary, p=0.017 for lymph node metastases vs. primary), while no significant difference was observed between GRNage of the primary tumor and the distant metastasis (p=0.82 for distant vs. primary). In the testing dataset, this pattern was preserved between the primary tumor and regional cutaneous/subcutaneous metastases (p=0.046) GRNage represents a new network-based paradigm in aging research: patterns of epigenetic age can be captured by changes in gene regulatory networks. Importantly, GRNage does not require DNA methylation data, but can be built using only transcriptomic data, which are more widely available. Thus, GRNage opens potential for widespread analysis of epigenetic aging patterns in a range of previously published transcriptomic datasets. Future analyses of the biological context of the features driving GRNage may reveal novel changes in gene regulation associated with accelerated aging in cancer and complex diseases.
items.authors: Shutta, Katherine*; Wu, Helena; DeMeo, Dawn; Quackenbush, John

items.title: A Network of Warring States: Antagonism and the Emergence of Stability in Medieval Japan
items.abstract: Negative relationships significantly influence the structure and dynamics of social networks, even possibly serving a role in stabilizing an otherwise unstable system across time. These connections make a significant contribution to conflict management, facilitate the diffusion of information, and alleviate ideological polarization; however, their role in the stabilization of human social networks remains somewhat ambiguous. We explore this idea by analyzing unique historical data from the Warring States period of medieval Japan, where 2,222 clans engaged in 2,889 battles over 133 years. Our network analysis reveals a temporal shift in the topological location of battles and warring actors. Specifically, we delineate a fragmentation phase (1467–1566), characterized by instability and decentralized conflict, followed by a unification phase (1566–1600), distinguished by centralized conflict. By extending classical balance theory to state-like actors, these findings provide insights into the role of negative ties in long-term social destabilization and stabilization cycles.
items.authors: Ghasemian, Amir*; Shirado, Hirokazu; Christakis, Nicholas

items.title: The Triple Lock: Geometric Obstructions to Partial Information Decomposition
items.abstract: Synergy—information emergent in the whole but invisible to the parts—is a defining signature of complex networks. While Partial Information Decomposition (PID) [1] formalizes this structure via a redundancy lattice, the field remains deadlocked by the lack of a canonical measure. A promising recent inquiry attempts to resolve this ambiguity through Riemannian Information Geometry, defining redundancy as the orthogonal projection of a joint distribution onto a split-independence submanifold [2, 4]. In this work, we prove a No-Go Theorem for Riemannian PID. We demonstrate that the pure Riemannian geometry of the probability simplex is mathematically insufficient to support a consistent decomposition. We identify three irreducible structural obstructions, termed the "Triple Lock": Rank-Width Mismatch: The tangent space spanned by marginal constraints scales linearly with system size n, whereas the PID lattice scales super-exponentially (Dedekind numbers). We deduce that for n ≥ 3, the tangent bundle lacks sufficient rank to linearly encode the full combinatorial complexity of higher-order redundancies, creating an irreducible bottleneck for purely vector-based decompositions. The Work Obstruction (Holonomy): Information measures must be state functions (path-independent). However, the non-vanishing curvature of the Fisher metric implies non-trivial holonomy. We show that integrating local informational updates (gradients of redundancy) is path-dependent; the computed value varies with the trajectory through distribution space, rendering the decomposition ill-defined without an auxiliary flat connection. Möbius Sign Failure: Even effectively freezing the path, geometric projections (m-projections) onto split-independence submanifolds do not guarantee order-preservation on the lattice. This geometric misalignment can structurally force the creation of negative atoms (negative synergy) upon Möbius inversion, putting the Riemannian formulation in direct conflict with the non-negativity axiom of information. These findings compel a fundamental re-evaluation of how we measure informational decompositional structures. For network scientists seeking to disentangle synergy from redundancy. Whether in neural connectomes or social hypergraphs, our results demonstrate that standard metric-based approaches are structurally flawed. We argue that a rigorous calculus of interaction must abandon purely Riemannian methods in favor of the dually flat affine geometry of the exponential family [3], or pivot to purely combinatorial foundations to consistently capture the rich lattice structure of multivariate dependencies. References: [1] P. L. Williams and R. D. Beer, Nonnegative decomposition of multivariate information, arXiv:1004.2515 (2010). [2] X. Niu and C. J. Quinn, A Measure of Synergy, Redundancy, and Unique Information Using Information Geometry, Proc. IEEE ISIT (2019). [3] S. Amari, Information Geometry and Its Applications, Springer (2016). [4] J. W. Kay, A Partial Information Decomposition for Multivariate Gaussian Systems, Entropy 26(7) (2024).
items.authors: Kothari, Vivek*; Lane, Nicholas

items.title: How Network Topology Links to Growth and Stagnation in Online Hate Communities
items.abstract: While online hate speech is frequently analyzed through content lenses, the structural architecture of extremist networks remains under-researched. This study investigates how these network properties link to future engagement patterns across distinct hate ideologies related to the Israel-Palestine conflict. We analyzed a ten year longitudinal dataset of Facebook activity by hate groups. Monthly networks were constructed where nodes represent unique groups and edges represent shared users (those posting in multiple groups). We employed negative binomial regression modeling to determine how group degree (inter-group connectivity), homophily index (ideological similarity), and the proportion of multi-group users predict next month engagement (likes, comments, and shares). Regression results demonstrate that group degree significantly and positively predicts future engagement for Anti-Semitic and Other-Hate groups, but remains non-significant for Islamophobic groups. Homophily negatively predicts engagement in Anti-Semitic groups, suggesting that insularity leads to activity stagnation. This provides a potential explanation for the distinct structural profiles revealed in our baseline analysis: while Islamophobic groups are highly homophilic, Anti-Semitic groups maintain a balanced structure, a configuration that mitigates the engagement penalties associated with high insularity. A high proportion of posts from multi-group users is associated with lower subsequent engagement across all categories, with the pattern most pronounced in Islamophobic silos. These findings suggest hate groups exhibit distinct survival patterns, calling for tailored interventions. Islamophobic groups are self-sustaining yet fragile to internal dilution; disrupting homophilic echoes, such as via nudges toward alternative perspectives, may be effective. Anti-Semitic groups are more networked and less insular, with a risk of backfire effects from hostile interactions; targeting bridging nodes might help contain their spread, though future research is needed to confirm this.
items.authors: Nefriana, Rr.*; Yan, Muheng; Hwa, Rebecca; Lin, Yu-Ru

items.title: Using a Heterogeneous Graph Transformer to repurpose drugs for the treatment of Nipah virus infectious disease
items.abstract: Nipah virus (NiV) is a bat-borne pathogen whose infection in humans causes disease with a very high observed mortality rate, with the NiV-M lineage (identified in Malaysia) having a case fatality of 39% while NiV-B lineage (identified in Bangladesh and India) reporting a much higher mortality range of 60% to 100% in different outbreaks. Since the identification of the first cases of NiV infection in humans in Malaysia in 1998, several outbreaks have occurred in South East Asia. Due to its high mortality rate and lack of effective therapies or vaccines, it is currently listed as a select biological agent by both the US Dept. of Health and Human Services and US Dept. of Agriculture. Therefore, it is crucial to develop treatments for care of infectious cases in order to reduce the health impacts and public health concerns. In this work we leverage a heterogeneous graph transformer model in order to repurpose drugs for the treatment of NiV infectious disease. By using the model in a heterogeneous Knowledge Graph (KG) we hope to harness different varied forms of interactions between different biological entities - such as interactions between drugs and gene/proteins, diseases and gene/proteins, diseases and effect/phenotype (symptoms), drugs and effect/phenotype, etc - in order to predict novel indications between already existing, approved drugs and the disease from NiV infection. This KG based drug repurposing capitalizes on the known heterogeneous interactions between relevant biological entities to overcome the time and costs associated with drug development. Our goal is the attention mechanism will extrapolate from newly-observed genes affected by the NiV infectious disease to perform link-prediction on the KG, by learning from the existing topology of relations in the KG and how these predict existing indications. Previous similar methods for drug repurposing by link-prediction on biological networks mostly relied on simplified homogeneous networks, e.g. similarity-matrix based collapses of heterogeneous data into homogeneous versions, thus missing some information. By utilizing an attention mechanism that is able to process a graph with different types of interactions, and between different types of nodes, our link-prediction model exploits the full breath of available information.
items.authors: Alvarez-Garcia, Luis*; Wuchty, Stefan

items.title: Posterior Inference of Network Growth Mechanism Mixtures
items.abstract: Real-world networks are often shaped by a mixture of multiple growth mechanisms, yet inference is typically performed from a single observed snapshot with no access to growth trajectories. We address this challenge by formulating posterior inference over mixtures of network growth mechanisms directly from a network snapshot, treating the underlying growth process as latent. By marginalizing over possible growth explanations, we infer a posterior distribution over mechanism mixtures rather than selecting a single best-fitting model. We visualize this posterior as an energy landscape over the simplex of mixture weights, revealing how uncertainty and trade-offs between mechanisms depend on the underlying generative process. Using simulations, we show that the inferred posterior geometry captures transitions between mixed and near-pure generative regimes. Even without edge-formation information, the snapshot-based posterior preserves the overall structure of the baseline landscape. These results suggest that posterior geometry provides a way to analyze the joint operations of different mechanisms and shed light on the underlying generative process from limited network observations.
items.authors: Jung, Dawoon*; Ventresca, Mario

items.title: Figures as Interfaces: Toward LLM-Native Artifacts for Scientific Discovery
items.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are transforming scientific workflows, not only through their generative capabilities but also through their emerging ability to use tools, reason about data, and coordinate complex analytical tasks. Yet in most human-AI collaborations, the primary outputs--figures--are still treated as static visual summaries: once rendered, they are handled by both humans and multimodal LLMs as images to be re-interpreted from pixels or captions. The emergent capabilities of LLMs open an opportunity to fundamentally rethink this paradigm. In this paper, we introduce the concept of LLM-native figures: data-driven artifacts that are simultaneously human-legible and machine-addressable. Unlike traditional plots, each artifact embeds complete provenance: the data subset, analytical operations and code, and visualization specification used to generate it. As a result, an LLM can ``see through'' the figure--tracing selections back to their sources, generating code to extend analyses, and orchestrating new visualizations through natural-language instructions or direct manipulation. We implement this concept through a hybrid language–visual interface that integrates LLM agents with a bidirectional mapping between figures and underlying data. Using the science-of-science domain as a testbed, we demonstrate that LLM-native figures can accelerate discovery, improve reproducibility, and make reasoning transparent across agents and users. More broadly, this work establishes a general framework for embedding provenance, interactivity, and explainability into the artifacts of modern research, redefining the figure not as an end product, but as an interface for discovery.
items.authors: Wang, Yifang*; Cao, Nan; Wang, Dashun

items.title: Estimating network clustering with a latent space model from Respondent-Driven Sampling data
items.abstract: The social network structure of hidden and hard-to-reach populations can have important implications for understanding risk and planning interventions in epidemiology and public health. Collecting full or even moderately dense network data from such populations is often infeasible due to the absence of a sampling frame, privacy concerns, and resource constraints. Respondent-Driven Sampling (RDS) is a popular chain-referral sampling method designed for such areas, which relies on a dual-incentive strategy, providing compensation to both participants and recruiters for successful referrals. Despite its broad application, conventional RDS primarily captures partially observed recruitment trees and offers limited insight into the underlying network structures. To address this challenge, we first incorporated a recently developed token-based strategy which supplements limited recruitment coupon distribution with token distribution, creating richer data on social network structure (see Figure 1). Inspired by the work using aggregated relational data (ARD), with information from RDS data and token ties, we propose a latent space model to effectively describe the network structure. The model is mathematically determined through a series of equations derived from the method of moment estimators. The performance of model construction from RDS and token data is verified through simulation. The developed model is also employed to estimate unobserved network structures and obtain network statistics from RDS data. Systematical assessments on the effectiveness of our model are presented, especially in describing network clustering, which has high practical relevance but is impossible to infer from standard RDS data.
items.authors: Jiang, Yun*; Gile, Krista; Nyakowa, Mercy; Fedha, Daniel; Manley, Hannah; Riback, Lindsey; Akiyama, Matthew

items.title: Latent Variable Models for Estimating Feature Interaction Networks
items.abstract: Many types of complex systems, particularly biological systems, involve relationships between entities that are often not directly observed. These relationships can be modeled by networks where nodes denote entities of interest and edges are constructed by indirect means, usually by estimating them from non-relational data. One common type of estimated network is a correlation network, where each node has a vector of measurements or features, and an edge between two nodes is constructed by taking the correlation coefficient between the feature vectors for the two nodes. We consider another type of estimated network, which we call a feature interaction network (FIN). A FIN represents the set of all pairwise interaction coefficients in a generalized linear model (GLM) with pairwise interaction terms between all pairs of the p features. The interaction coefficients can be arranged in a p × p interaction coefficient matrix Θ, where entry θ_jk denotes the coefficient for the interaction term x_j x_k. This matrix can be viewed as a weighted and signed adjacency matrix for the estimated network, where the nodes correspond to the features. Treating feature interactions as a network allows us to estimate different interaction coefficients θ_jk jointly by considering the estimates of the other interaction coefficients θ_j′k′ , which may be related. Conventional regularization methods such as the lasso (ℓ1 penalty) or elastic net (combined ℓ2, ℓ1 penalty), and even methods specifically targeted to interaction terms such as the hierarchical lasso, do not consider the dependencies between edges (interaction terms) because they do not consider the network structure. We propose a structured regularization approach for estimating the interaction coefficients using an approximate low-dimensional structure in addition to conventional regularization techniques such as the elastic net. We call our proposed approach LIT-LVM, denoting linear and interaction terms with a latent variable model and can be used with many types of latent structures including low rank and latent distance models. Our proposed structured regularization for the interaction coefficients offers two main benefits. First, it improves estimation accuracy for the interaction coefficients, leading to better predictive accuracy, especially as p grows relative to the number of samples n, which we demonstrate in simulation experiments and across many real datasets. Second, it provides low-dimensional latent representations of the features that are useful for visualizing and analyzing their relationships, which we demonstrate in an application for modeling donor-recipient compatibility in kidney transplantation.
items.authors: Nemati, Mohammadreza; Huang, Zhipeng; Xu, Kevin*

items.title: From Ultra-Processed Foods to Processing Pathways: A Network-Aware Inference Framework
items.abstract: Ultra-processed foods (UPFs) are consistently associated with an increased risk of metabolic, cardiovascular, hepatic, and inflammatory diseases. However, dominant frameworks for characterizing food processing rely on qualitative and descriptive criteria that collapse highly heterogeneous industrial practices into coarse categorical labels. Foods that differ markedly in ingredients, nutrient profiles, and industrial processing steps and sequences are therefore treated as equivalent, obscuring meaningful variation and potentially masking differential disease risk. Moreover, large-scale product-level analyzes, including our study of more than 70,000 U.S. grocery products, show that food processing information is often incomplete or inconsistently annotated, underscoring the need for systematic methods that can infer fine-grained processing steps. To address these limitations, we constructed a harmonized food-processing database comprising more than 62,000 foods annotated with LanguaL-derived processing descriptors, nutrient profiles, and food-group information aggregated from heterogeneous international sources. Processing annotations are inherently relational and sparse: each food is associated with multiple semantically related descriptors reflecting combinations and sequences of operations, while many plausible food–process associations remain unobserved. This structure naturally induces a bipartite network linking foods and processing descriptors, where observed edges encode partial processing pathways and missing edges represent absent or latent operations. Inferring food processing, therefore, becomes a network learning problem under extreme label sparsity, with the central challenge of distinguishing true absences from missing annotations—a limitation that fundamentally constrains feature-based classifiers lacking access to relational structure. Here, we present a unified inference pipeline that integrates network-aware models with top-k multilabel classification baselines trained on BERT-derived embeddings from curated food metadata. By leveraging network topology, the proposed framework substantially improves recovery of known food–process associations by reducing confusion between absent and unobserved processing operations. The best-performing model achieves Top-5 and Top-10 accuracies of 88.3% and 94.4%, respectively, and a Top-10 recall of 70.9%, despite foods being annotated with only ~5 labels on average out of 269 possible descriptors—corresponding to an 8–15X improvement over random ranking. By reframing food processing as a problem of missing-edge inference in hierarchical bipartite networks, this framework moves beyond coarse UPF categories and enables systematic identification of fine-grained processing steps within and across food groups, informing targeted interventions in food formulation and public health policy. More broadly, it illustrates how network topology can be leveraged to recover latent relational structure from sparse, incomplete annotations, a setting common to many large-scale data integration problems in network science.
items.authors: Ispirova, Gordana*; Aldana, Andres; Menichetti, Giulia

items.title: Omnivory decouples optimal predator fitness from network robustness
items.abstract: The principle of maximum output power is as old as the Lotka-Volterra predator-prey model. This principle posits that under competition, those system configurations are most likely to prevail that can capture and transform usable energy at higher rates. While Lotka–Volterra dynamics and species abundances are now standard tools in ecology, explicitly using energy fluxes as the primary currency in ecological models has received much less attention. Here, we revisit predator–prey systems from a network perspective, using a Lotka–Volterra–type model formulated in terms of energy fluxes between interacting species. We define the output power of a food web as the total energy flux through one or more focal top predators, and study how output power depends on the encounter rates (interaction strengths) along the network’s feeding links. For a broad class of network structures without omnivory, we find that encounter rates that maximize output power also maximize feasibility, understood as the fraction of intrinsic growth-rate vectors that yield positive, coexistence equilibria. In these cases, optimal predator fitness and network robustness converge — changing the interaction strength(s) toward the maximum-power state simultaneously expands the feasibility domain. This qualitative picture is reversed when the network exhibits omnivory, i.e. when at least one predator feeds on both a basal and a non-basal species. Omnivory motifs appear as feed-forward loops in ecological networks [2]. For such motifs, the encounter rates that maximize output power lie near geometric bottlenecks or boundaries of the feasibility domain, so that maximizing predator power systematically reduces feasibility. This result holds even when omnivory is realized through species other than the focal top predator. Our analysis shows that omnivory introduces a structural tension between individual-level optimization (maximum output power of predators) and network-level robustness (feasibility), leading to divergent optimal outcomes at the scales of species and ecological networks.
items.authors: Vikrant, Ankit*

items.title: Networked Dynamics of Toxicity in Reddit Gaming Communities
items.abstract:
Online gaming communities are among the most active and socially complex online spaces, yet they remain persistently affected by toxic behavior such as harassment, aggression, and abusive language, which harms user well-being and discourages participation [1]. While prior work has examined toxicity at the content level, across game genres, or through network dynamics [1,2,3], it remains unclear how community type in terms of game genre and developer scale and user interaction structure jointly shape the prevalence, diffusion, and concentration of toxicity. We present a comparative, network-based analysis of toxicity across Reddit gaming communities using a 2 × 2 framework that crosses game genre (MMORPG vs. Soulslike) with developer scale (AAA/AA vs. indie). We analyze 550,641 posts and comments from nine gaming subreddits, including a general gaming baseline (r/gaming). Each post and comment is scored using two independent automated toxicity detection systems (OpenAI’s omni-moderation model and Detoxify). Using these scores, we construct thread-level reply trees and directed user-reply networks to examine toxicity across multiple levels of social organization. 	extbf{At the community level}, we compare average toxicity prevalence, severity, and category composition across the 2 × 2 design. Soulslike communities and AAA/AA titles exhibit modestly higher average toxicity than MMORPG and indie communities, driven primarily by conflict-oriented language such as insults, obscenity, and violence-related terms [2]. 	extbf{At the thread level}, we observe robust local contagion across all communities: replies to toxic comments are consistently more toxic, and more toxic threads tend to be larger, deeper, and more structurally viral (Figure 1, left). These patterns align with prior work on toxicity contagion and conversational cascades in online discussion networks [3]. We find no systematic differences in these dynamics across genre or developer scale, indicating that toxicity propagation reflects general properties of online conversational structure rather than community-specific cultures or norms. 	extbf{At the user level}, toxicity is both assortative and structurally concentrated (Figure 1, right). Users with similar toxicity levels preferentially interact, and more central users both produce and receive more toxicity, with network centrality differentiating exposure from expression. Taken together, these findings show that toxicity in gaming communities is shaped less by genre or developer scale alone and more by interaction structure and network position. Rather than treating specific communities or genres as inherently toxic, the results highlight the importance of network-aware moderation strategies that focus on early conversational contexts and structurally influential users. More broadly, this study provides a comparative, network-based benchmark for understanding harmful behavior in large-scale online interaction systems.  igskip {\small 
oindent[1] Rahul, Kajla, H., Hooda, J., \& Saini, G. (2020, May). Classification of online toxic comments using machine learning algorithms. In 2020 4th international conference on intelligent computing and control systems (ICICCS) (pp. 1119-1123). IEEE. \ 
oindent[2] Dreier, L., \& Pirker, J. (2023, August). Toxicity in twitch live stream chats: Towards understanding the impact of gender, size of community and game genre. In 2023 IEEE Conference on Games (CoG) (pp. 1-4). IEEE. \ 
oindent[3] Shankaran, V., \& Sharma, R. (2024). Analyzing toxicity in deep conversations: A reddit case study. arXiv preprint arXiv:2404.07879. }
items.authors: Smith, Serafina; David, Glen; Brunn, Lizzy; Nguyen, Vy; Choi, Eun Cheol*; Luceri, Luca

items.title: The Global Network of Resource Flows from Lithosphere to Technosphere
items.abstract: Anthropogenic material fluxes now rival natural geological processes yet are rarely studied within the Earth system framework. Here we present a new approach, global ‘technogeochemical’ flows, and apply it to the roughly 2 Gt/y anthropogenic flows of iron and other critical materials. Using the SESAME-Human Earth Atlas [1], we synthesize disparate data sources to show how critical minerals flow from a few extraction sites, via a few refining plants, to widely distributed in-use stocks. We apply our spatiotemporal approach to other critical materials including copper, lithium, aluminum, lithium, phosphate, and even food production to understand how industrial economic processes are physically embedded in the Earth system. [1] Faisal, A. A., Kaye, M., Ahmed, M., Galbraith, E. D. (2025). The SESAME Human-Earth Atlas. Scientific Data, 12(1), 775.
items.authors: Kaye, Maxwell*; Galbraith, Eric; Al Faisal, Abduallah

items.title: Beyond the CEO Life Cycle: The Influences of Top Management Team and Middle Man- agers from a Faultline Perspective
items.abstract: CEOs’ strategic paradigms are crucial in shaping organizational performance by influencing direction, resource alloca- tion, and competitive positioning. The CEO life cycle framework introduces a longitudinal lens by suggesting that CEOs’ strategic paradigms generally follow an exploration-exploitation-obsolescence pattern, resulting in an inverted U-shaped performance trajectory. This reflects initial performance improvement followed by stagnation and ultimate decline [1]. We extend this framework beyond the sole focus on CEOs’ influence to examine the impacts of faultlines formed between CEOs and top management team (TMT) members, as well as between CEOs and middle managers (MMs), given the critical roles these agents play in strategic formulation and implementation. Drawing on Campbell et al.’s (2023) classification, we conceptualize faultlines based on observable and unobservable attributes shared by CEO and other strategic agents. We propose that while strong faultlines of observable attributes strengthen performance gains during the exploration phase, strong faultlines of unobservable attributes sustain high performance for a longer time during exploitation and attenuate the decline associated with obsolescence. We use a multiplex network approach [2] to model the strength of these faultlines and conduct random coefficient multilevel growth modeling, supplemented by tangent slope analysis, to specify performance trajectory and its changing rates. Hypotheses are tested using longitudinal data gathered from the U.S. higher education context between 2000 to 2022, with university presidents proxying as CEOs, provosts as the TMT members, and deans as MMs. The sample includes 28 presidents, 63 provosts, and 574 deans, yielding 293 year-performance pairs. Performance is assessed across three dimensions: total revenues (financial), operating efficiency (operational), and research salary (innovation). The results provide a more nuanced perspective on the myth of CEO “solo act” performance. Although total revenues show insignificant curvilinear or moderating effects, operating efficiency is significantly enhanced by stronger faultlines of CEO-TMT and CEO-MM unobservable attribute. Specifically, under stronger unobservable attribute faultlines, efficiency increases at a diminishing rate during the initial years of CEO life cycle; conversely, weaker faultlines of unobservable attribute lead performance to enter a plateau stage directly without a salient initial increase. For research salary, this innovation-related dimension increases more rapidly when CEO-MM observable attribute faultline is weaker, suggesting that diversity among MMs may drive early-stage research growth. These findings demonstrate that organizational per- formance is a multidimensional and collective outcome shaped by temporal dynamics, the joint effects of strategic agents at different hierarchical levels, and perceptual changes among those strategic agents.
items.authors: Yang, Biying*; Tsai, Chou-Yu; Eckardt, Rory; Dionne, Shelley; Weng, David

items.title: Tabular Embedding: A Network Representation for High-Performance Computing Applications
items.abstract: Recently, multiple computational hardware platforms, such as the Cerebras Wafer Scale Engine (WSE), have combined a vast number of lower-powered processors to achieve overall high throughput. These platforms offer the potential to dramatically scale up applications that are conducive to being run on them. Here, we propose that bipartite network modeling is such an application and present a technique (tabular embedding) for translating it to computational substrates such as the WSE. We present an application in the context of epidemiology, but see no reason that our technique should not generalize. The primary obstacle to be overcome is enabling efficient communication across the WSE's grid of processors. We achieve an efficient solution to this problem by conceptualizing our epidemiological network as containing two types of nodes: residences and institutions (i.e. school or work). Edges between these nodes indicate the presence of people who live at a given residence and regularly attend a given institution, enabling disease transmission between the two sites. We then develop a tabular layout in which some table cells (which correspond to processors) correspond to houses, some to institutions, and others to edges between houses and institutions (i.e. people). We present an efficient algorithm for modeling disease transmission along networks represented by these layouts.
items.authors: Hasanzadeh Fard, Sanaz*; Dolson, Emily; Andres Moreno, Matthew; Zaman, Luis

items.title: Characterizing academic collaborations by prestige and discipline composition
items.abstract: Social and systemic factors have been shown to structure how scientists collaborate with each other. As is the case with sociodemographic homophily in collaboration [1], many of these factors reflect existing social inequalities that operate in the scientific ecosystem and reinforce existing hierarchies [2]. For instance, controlling for quality, a scientific idea spreads at a higher rate if it originates from a higher prestige institution than from a lower prestige institution [3]. However, academic collaborations are not restricted to the same institutional prestige or discipline, and it remains unclear the degree to which this occurs in the system. In this project, we aim to characterize prestige homophily and cross-disciplinary collaboration rates across five fields to understand how these boundaries structure academic co-authorship. We linked faculty profiles in a census of U.S. faculty from the Academic Analytics Research Center (AARC) to publication data from OpenAlex and applied the SpringRank network embedding algorithm to generate institutional prestige scores based on faculty placement patterns [4]. From these data, we created undirected co-authorship multigraphs in which nodes are faculty from the AARC census, and an edge connects two individuals for each of their co-authored publications. We compared the within-field prestige homophily for the fields of computer science, mathematics, physics, sociology, and philosophy using a measure of network assortativity for continuous node features [5]. We chose these fields because they represent diverse collaboration paradigms and scholastic traditions. We excluded collaborations within the same institution because they likely reflect proximity rather than prestige homophily. The five fields exhibit substantial variation in the prestige homophily of within-field collaborations, ranging from 0.14 in physics to 0.31 in math (Fig. 1). Similarly, rates of interdisciplinary collaboration also vary widely between fields. Almost half of co-authorship pairs involving faculty in philosophy are interdisciplinary. For sociology, this fraction falls to 14% (Fig. 1). Mathematics has both the highest prestige homophily and the second-highest interdisciplinary collaboration rate while philosophy has the highest interdisciplinary collaboration rate but the second-lowest prestige homophily, suggesting a complex relationship between these two lenses on scientific collaboration. Our future work will measure these quantities across a more complete set of fields and examine possible covariates for both prestige homophily and interdisciplinary collaboration. For example, we will test whether prestige homophily is more or less prevalent in interdisciplinary collaborations, clarifying the complex way in which institutional prestige influences who collaborates with whom.
items.authors: Aoki-Sherwood, Benyoshi; Barnes, Kate; Chavez-Ruelas, Carolina*

items.title: Human Mobility Network and COVID-19 Prediction: a Link Prediction approach and a Case Study for New York State

items.authors: He, Jie*

items.title: Early Rules Cast Long Shadows: Dependency Dynamics in Wikipedia’s Regulatory Network
items.abstract: Do rules have their own dynamics? Administrative codes in universities grow slowly as problems get absorbed, shift with leadership, and respond to external shocks [1]. But rules do not exist in isolation. In legal codes, manuals, and online platforms, rules reference each other for justification or exception. These cross-references create dependencies: a rule’s meaning is incomplete without the rules it cites. Prior work showed that Wikipedia’s regulatory pages form exactly such a network, with early rules occupying central positions [2]. Why do some rules become foundational while others remain peripheral? Here we trace the temporal evolution of Wikipedia’s regulatory dependency network to understand how this hierarchy forms and stabilizes. We construct a directed network where nodes are Wikipedia regulatory pages (policies, guidelines, etc.) and edges are cross-references in the text. Figure 1a illustrates this: the Neutral Point of View (NPOV) policy contains links like “please read [[WP:NOT]]” and “content should be [[WP:V]] verifiable.” Each link creates a directed edge. We reconstruct it yearly from 2001 to 2016. Figure 1b shows that the network grows rapidly until 2008, then saturates, consistent with problem absorption [1,2]. The inset shows that in-degree inequality (Gini) rises from 0.59 to 0.81 by 2008, then stabilizes [2]. This reflects cumulative advantage: early rules gain visibility, attract citations, and become difficult to displace. Once formed, the hierarchy persists. Figure 1c traces how this hierarchy forms. In 2001, the emerging core was dominated by NPOV and What Wikipedia Is Not (NOT). By 2003, Verifiability (V) and No Original Research (NOR) emerged, operationalizing neutrality by specifying how to achieve it. By 2008, NPOV was cited by 309 pages, and this core remained stable even as the network grew from 30 to 1,294 pages. Figure 1d reveals the dependency structure. There are asymmetric connections: some rules have far more in-degree than out-degree. This asymmetry indicates hierarchy among rules [3]. Rules with high in-degree but low out-degree are foundational: others depend on them, but they depend on few. NPOV has 452 incoming and 34 outgoing citations (13:1). All four fall below the diagonal, anchoring the network while other rules elaborate on them. Together, foundational rules established in the first two years remain central, making them costly to change. New rules do not displace existing foundations; they attach to them. Wikipedia offers a lens to trace how regulatory hierarchy emerges and persists: what drives this lock-in, is it universal, and what happens when foundations must change?
items.authors: Bhattacharya, Shambhobi*; Youn, Hyejin ; Yoon , Jisung

items.title: Reservoir computers as digital twins for brain dynamics
items.abstract: The brain is a complex network dynamical system, and faithful representations of its dynamics would enable new ways to analyze and predict brain activity using tools from dynamical systems and network science. In this context, a digital twin is a secondary dynamical system coupled to neural measurements that evolves in synchrony with the brain. Such a twin must synchronize to the underlying dynamics, infer unmeasured variables, forecast short-term activity, and capture longer-term patterns of behavior. Together, these capabilities support the study of individual-specific brain dynamics and, in the longer term, the exploration of how targeted interventions might influence neural evolution toward desired regimes. We adopt reservoir computing (RC) as the core modeling framework for these digital twins. RC offers an efficient approach to learning dynamical systems in which a fixed recurrent network evolves in response to input signals, while training is restricted to a linear readout. Because computation arises from the reservoir’s intrinsic dynamics, RC is well suited to modeling continuous-time, nonlinear systems. This architecture enables fast training, low data requirements, and robustness to noise and nonstationarity, while allowing reservoirs to be designed with interpretable network structure such as sparsity, heterogeneity, and symmetry. Here, we report early progress toward establishing the feasibility and interpretability of RC-based digital twins of brain dynamics. Using high-density EEG time-series data, we show that these models can synchronize to observed brain activity, infer latent dynamical variables, and generate stable short-term forecasts. Preliminary analyses further indicate that RCs can reproduce longer-term statistical features of the dynamics, suggesting the ability to capture aspects of the brain’s dynamical “climate.” While the present work focuses on modeling and prediction, it lays groundwork for future control-oriented studies and extensions to additional neural data modalities. From a network science perspective, this framework treats the digital twin itself as a dynamical network coupled to another complex network—the brain. This view enables the application of concepts such as synchronization, observability, and controllability to assess when a learned twin can reliably track and predict brain dynamics. Moreover, the explicit network structure of the reservoir allows systematic investigation of how connectivity, heterogeneity, and symmetry shape dynamical inference and stability, positioning reservoir computing as a natural bridge between data-driven modeling and network science.
items.authors: Chepuri, Ravi*; Hunt, Brian; Pecora, Lou; Girvan, Michelle

items.title: A nonparametric model for multiple categorical node features with arbitrary correlations
items.abstract: Many observable complex networks produce data that is both compositional (properties of nodes) and relational (network edges). However, the development of flexible network models that effectively use both of these data sources simultaneously remains a challenge. The existing standard for nonparametric network inference with metadata involves representing metadata as additional layers of a multilayered stochastic block model (SBM). When the node features are correlated, this approach leads to erroneous structure in the inferred node partition, as well as simultaneous overfitting and underfitting. After addressing the limitations of a "naive" solution to the correlated layer problem based on combining layers, we introduce a microcanonical model that avoids these issues, providing a method that is more suitable for the inferential task at hand while achieving improved data compression. Using synthetic and empirical data, we demonstrate how our model can be used alongside existing community detection methods for joint inference of latent node features, without a priori assumptions about the correlation between these data sources.
items.authors: Weis, Erik*; Mangold, Lena

items.title: Percolation in Growing Stochastic Block Models
items.abstract:
The stochastic block model (SBM) [1,2] is a paradigmatic random graph model used extensively in Network Science for inference and community detection, as it naturally accommodates community structure. Despite its broad applicability, some of its most fundamental properties remain poorly understood: rigorous results for the percolation transition (the emergence of the giant connected component (GCC)) are only known in a limited set of special cases, all involving a fixed number of communities [3,4]. Some important regimes---most notably with a growing number of blocks---have remained largely unexplored. Here, we provide a thorough theoretical analysis of a general class of SBMs, including the special case of the sparse SBM with a growing number of communities. This case is particularly significant since these models are very close to soft random geometric graphs [5], whose percolation properties have so far been analytically intractable [6]. We consider an SBM with $N$ vertices, partitioned into $B_{N}$ blocks of sizes $\{n_{i}\}_{i=1}^{B_{N}}$ such that $\sum_{i=1}^{B_{N}} n_i= N$, where $p_{ij}$ denotes the connection probability between nodes belonging to communities $i$ and $j$. We derive self-consistent equations governing the emergence of the GCC in this model. The critical point is characterized by $\lambda_{\max}\left( oldsymbol{M}ight) = 1$, where $ oldsymbol{M} = \{M_{ij}\}_{i,j=1}^{N}$ with $M_{ij} = n_{j}p_{ij}$ and $\lambda_{\max}(\cdot)$ denotes the absolute value of the largest eigenvalue of the matrix. In the special case where $p_{ij} = q/N$ for $i 
eq j$ and $p$ for $i=j$ (with $p,q$ constants) and all the communities are of the same size $s,$ with the number of communities growing as $B_{N} = \lfloor N/s floor$, the percolation transition occurs at $\lambda_{\max}\left( oldsymbol{M}ight) =  ar{k} = (s-1)p +q =1$, where $ ar{k}$ is the average degree of the model. It is noteworthy, however, that the percolation threshold does not generally occur at average degree $1$, contrary to common belief.  igskip {\small

oindent[2] Nowicki \& Snijders 	extit{Estimation and Prediction for Stochastic Blockstructures} J. Amer. Stat. Ass. 96 1077–1087 (2001)\ % proposes SBM 2 
oindent[3] Allard et al. 	extit{Heterogeneous bond percolation on multitype networks with an application to epidemic dynamics} PRE 79 3 036113 (2009).\
