items.authors: Pieleanu, Luca; Sabhahit, Narayan*; Laber, Moritz; Krioukov, Dmitri

items.title: Predicting Scientific Subfield Adoption in Collaboration Networks: The Role of Exposure Timing and Collaborator Composition
items.abstract: Understanding how collaboration networks are associated with researchers’ entry into new scientific subfields has implications for research policy and scientific innovation. While diffusion theories often assume that greater cumulative exposure to adopting peers increases adoption likelihood [1], empirical evidence remains mixed, particularly when individuals are embedded in environments composed of collaborators at different stages of subfield engagement [2]. We investigate how exposure timing, accumulation, and composition of collaboration networks jointly predict adoption across five scientific fields. Using longitudinal co-authorship networks from SciSciNet [3], we analyze five subfields that experienced substantial growth between 2015-2019: Computer Engineering, Physical Geography, Demographic Economics, Evolutionary Biology, and Combinatorial Chemistry. We define three researcher roles based on adoption: Alices, recent adopters who first published during the growth period; Bobs, at-risk researchers who collaborated with Alices but have not yet adopted; and Charlies, established researchers who entered the subfield before 2015. For each Bob, we track yearly changes in their local collaboration neighborhood and predict whether they publish in the subfield in a given year. For each Bob–year observation, we construct network-derived features capturing (i) the timing of first exposure to Alice, (ii) cumulative and newly accruing exposure to Alice collaborators, and (iii) exposure to Charlie collaborators, reflecting embeddedness in an established collaborator environment. Using gradient-boosted decision tree models with an expanding-window temporal cross-validation approach, we evaluate predictive performance using the AUC-ROC metric and assess interpretability through SHAP analyses and grouped ablations. Across all fields, early exposure to recent adopters (Alices) is positively associated with adoption, while the incremental effect of additional exposure diminishes with prolonged exposure. In contrast, exposure to established collaborators (Charlies) is consistently negatively associated with adoption. Ablation tests support this asymmetry: removing exposure to Charlies produces the largest drop in predictive performance across the fields (mean ΔAUC ≈ −0.08 to −0.10), followed by removal of the timing of first-exposure to Alices (mean ΔAUC ≈ −0.06), while cumulative exposure contributes little once timing and neighborhood composition are accounted for. Together, these findings challenge cumulative diffusion models that focus solely on exposure accumulation. Instead, subfield adoption in scientific collaboration networks depends on when exposure occurs and the structural composition of collaboration networks. By demonstrating the cross-subfield invariance of these temporal and inhibitory network effects, our work highlights the need for diffusion models that incorporate network roles, timing, and competing social signals in the study of scientific adoption. [1] Centola, D. The Spread of Behavior in an Online Social Network Experiment. Science 329, 1194–1197 (2010). [2] Goel, S., Watts, D. J. \& Goldstein, D. G. The structure of online diffusion networks. in Proceedings of the 13th ACM Conference on Electronic Commerce 623–638 (ACM, Valencia Spain, 2012). [3] Lin, Z., Yin, Y., Liu, L. \& Wang, D. SciSciNet: A large-scale open data lake for the science of science research. Sci Data 10, 1–22 (2023).
items.authors: Banerjee, Nandini; Gomez-Zara, Diego*

items.title: Coordination through Reply Chains in Information Operation Campaigns
items.abstract: Social media platforms are exploited in a well calibrated, orchestrated manner by malicious coordinated groups [1], referred to as Information Operations (IOs) or Coordinated Campaigns (CC). The objectives are to garner support or to influence people through misleading information. IOs pose serious threats to organic dissemination of information. Particularly during election times, IOs can potentially disrupt free and fair democratic processes. In this work we focus on how malicious actors try to assert influence artificially through replies to the original posts. We present a network-based framework that investigates reply posts involved in IOs. Specifically, we study coordinated replies on social media platforms using IO datasets which comprise 26 IO campaigns consisting of IO posts from different state actors (eg. Russia, China, etc.) identified by platform through their transparency website and control posts, curated by Seckin et al. [2]) . We restrict ourselves to reply posts and construct a reply graph for each campaign. Each reply post is categorized as belonging to IO campaign or control. Due to space limitations, we focus on two contrasting campaigns [2] from China (large reply network) and Armenia (small reply network). Further, we consider only those replies whose corresponding original posts are present in the dataset. Reply networks are trees rooted in the original posts, where the rest of the nodes are replies to the original posts or to other replies present either in star or chain formation as depicted in Fig~\ref{fig:chain_patterns}. Table~\ref{tbl:stats} shows reply graph statistics with respect to chains, the average delay (in minutes) between original post and direct reply as well as the last reply, and distinct user accounts engaged in these interactions. In the IOs from China, 33\% of chains (3,750 out of 11,204) have IO replies, as compared to Armenia with only 5\% (16 out of 307). The Chinese campaign has 39 mixed reply chains containing both IO and control replies while no such mixed chains were found for Armenia. IO reply chains are shorter, both on average and in terms of maximum chain length, than control reply chains in both campaigns. In addition, IO reply chains have much higher out-degree and lower in-degree when compared with control reply chains. Temporally, we measure average delay (in terms of minutes) of direct reply and last reply to the original post. IO replies in the Chinese campaign are exceedingly marked with large delays when compared to control replies, a trend which is reversed in Armenian campaign. Lastly, the Chinese campaigns employ more distinct user accounts than the Armenian campaigns. To conclude, this study investigates signatures in foreign information campaigns on social media that can be shown using reply chains. Such signatures could be used for detecting campaigns in real time. \label{tbl:stats} \begin{tabular}{cccccc} \hline Metric & \multicolumn{3}{c}{China} & \multicolumn{2}{c}{Armenia} \\ \hline Nodes & \multicolumn{3}{c}{26,285} & \multicolumn{2}{c}{739} \\ Edges & \multicolumn{3}{c}{17,137} & \multicolumn{2}{c}{463} \\ \hline & IO & Ctrl & Mix & IO & Ctrl \\ \hline Number of Chains & 3750 & 7415 & 39 & 16 & 291\\ Avg Chain Length & 2.42 & 2.68 & 4.08 & 2 & 2.66 \\ Min Chain Length & 2 & 2 & 3 & 2 & 2 \\ Max Chain Length & 17 & 99 & 14 & 2 & 21 \\ Max Out-Degree & 151 & 84 & 2 & 14 & 2 \\ Avg Out-Degree & 3.61 & 2.34 & 0.84 & 6.19 & 0.68 \\ Avg In-Degree & 0.59 & 0.63 & 0.75 & 0.50 & 0.62 \\ \hline Avg Delay Direct Reply & 2094 & 25 & - & 25 & 50 \\ Avg Delay Last Reply & 2151 & 33 & 229 & 25 & 61 \\ \hline Max Distinct Accounts & 6 & 3 & 2 & 2 & 2 \\ Avg Distinct Accounts & 1.46 & 1.1 & 2 & 1.06 & 1.04 \\ \hline \end{tabular} \end{wraptable}
items.authors: Narain, Stuti*; Narain, Shradha; Kaushal, Rishabh; Iamnitchi, Adriana

items.title: From Topological to Functional Resilience in Interdependent Networks
items.abstract: For decades, network science has treated structural percolation as the foundational heuristic for resilience [1,2,3]. While mathematically elegant, this approach is insufficient for critical infrastructure because it conflates the means of connectivity (physical topology) with the ends (service delivery). Classical percolation implicitly assumes an isomorphism between topological and functional layers. While valid for homogeneous random networks, this equivalence breaks down in spatially embedded systems characterized by functional heterogeneity, such as communication backbones, power grids, transport, water and gas, and brain networks [4]. Consequently, standard structural metrics can become poor proxies for service viability in heterogeneous spatial infrastructures, indicating collapse while functional sub-graphs persist [5,6]. We model such systems as physical–functional interdependent networks, where functional placement is a latent, operator-configurable control parameter. Functions (e.g., routing, storage, peering) are assigned weights based on criticality and deployed under centralized (hub-concentrated) and distributed (regionally replicated) policies onto the physical topology. We simulate disruption cycles on three national optical backbones [7] using biased site percolation followed by hub-prioritized restoration. To quantify resilience, we introduce Service Continuity as the fraction of nodes retaining access to a function and Service Coverage as the fraction of geographic regions with active service presence. Are structural percolation metrics reliable indicators of service resilience? We identify a systematic premature-collapse bias in topological metrics. At the point where Service Continuity degrades to 0.8, algebraic connectivity exhibits 9-17 times lower values and the largest connected component 4-7 times lower values across the three networks, prematurely signaling system failure while services persist (Figure 1). Can function placement increase resilience without rewiring? On the same physical topologies, distributed placement yields 13–30% higher resilience for both service metrics. This is quantified by the area under the resilience curve as nodes are sequentially removed. As a result, we show resilience can be engineered through intelligent functional reconfiguration without structural modification to the underlying topology. Is there a universally optimal placement across attack and recovery? We observe a resilience-recovery asymmetry wherein distributed placement exhibits superior resistance during degradation, while centralized placement facilitates steeper restoration. This trade-off implies that no single configuration optimizes performance across both phases, with distributed architectures acting as shields during attack and centralized architectures acting as springs during recovery. This suggests that optimal architecture is phase-dependent, requiring adaptive reconfigurations to maximize resilience. [1] C. M. Schneider et al., “Mitigation of malicious attacks on networks,” PNAS, (2011). [2] G. Dong et al., “Robustness of network of networks under targeted attack,” Physical Review E, (2013). [3] R. Cohen et al., “Resilience of the Internet to random breakdowns,” Physical Review Letters, (2000). [4] S. Boccaletti et al., “The structure and dynamics of multilayer networks,” Physics Reports, (2014). [5] J. Sterbenz et al., “Resilience and survivability in communication networks,” Computer Networks, (2010). [6] Y. Lou et al., “Structural robustness of complex networks: a survey of a posteriori measures,” IEEE CSM, (2023). [7] R. Matzner, A. Ahuja, et al., “TopologyBench: systematic graph-based benchmarking for optical networks,” JOCN, (2024).
items.authors: Ahuja, Akanksha *; Herzberg, Sam N.; Rafel, Albert; Lord, Andrew; Zapata, Alejandra B.; Savory, Seb J.

items.title: Social Interaction, Campus Engagement, and Academic Outcomes: A Data-Driven Study
items.abstract: This study investigates the role of students’ on-campus presence and social networks in academic performance, using real-time Wi-Fi connectivity data and student career records from the University of Parma, Italy. We observe higher academic performance among students who spend more time on campus, particularly those who attend more lessons. In addition, students within tightly connected groups—identified through co-presence across multiple buildings and synchronous movements—tend to exhibit similar academic outcomes, as well as similar degree programmes and attempted exams, all of which are consistent with peer effect. Interestingly, not all groups deliver academic benefits, and small and highly interactive networks display significant variability in achievements. Among them, focused study groups—students attempting the same exams—demonstrate a clear advantage in results, while less focused groups tend to achieve worse results than the average student. These two categories of groups are mainly characterized by spending most of their campus time attending classes, in the case of focused groups, and by devoting a large share of their campus time to activities outside class hours, in the case of less focused groups. Nevertheless, class attendance generally encourages extra time on campus. This analysis provides novel insights into how creating space and opportunities on campus for students to develop social interactions influence academic performances. It also offers valuable guidance for institutions seeking to improve student retention and achievements through data-driven strategies.
items.authors: Caldarelli, Guido*

items.title: Dynamic Bayesian Networks for Modelling Progression of Hepatosplenic Multimorbidity Related to Schistosomiasis
items.abstract: Multimorbidity, which is the existence of two or more chronic conditions within an individual, is poorly understood in low-middle income countries. Schistosomiasis—caused by a parasitic flatworm—is a major cause of liver and spleen multimorbidity. No timelines and risk factors for the progression of schistosomiasis-related hepatosplenic multimorbidity have been previously studied due to the lack of methods to handle complex condition interactions and insufficient data. Here we studied 35 liver and spleen conditions related to severe illness of portal hypertension as dynamic graphical processes using Dynamic Bayesian Networks (DBNs). Point-of-care B-mode ultrasound was used to examine 4330 individuals (aged five and older) from 52 rural villages across Uganda within the SchistoTrack Cohort [1] over the period 2022-2025. 54% of participants were found to be multimorbid. We used DBNs, built on top of Bayesian networks, to introduce temporal modelling by learning two time-slice directed acyclic graphs (DAGs), and with stationarity assumptions, generalized to represent the transitions to over > 2 year. A DBN with intra- and inter-time edges was learned on our participants to reveal clinically accurate disease progression pathways, while through simulations, we obtained forecasts of the evolution of condition risks over time. Covariates as previously deemed important for cross-sectional analyses of hepatosplenic multimorbidity were incorporated to assess key risk factors in hepatosplenic disease progression, to answer further questions on the long-term efficacy of the treatment. As an example, Fig. 1 shows the dynamics between conditions related to periportal fibrosis (diverse types of liver fibrosis concentrated around the main portal vein related to schistosomiasis), where transitions inferred by the DBN indicated that severe periportal fibrosis typically takes two years to progress from when mild liver fibrosis is first observed. During the two year progression period, individuals would also have higher probabilities of developing hypersplenism (enlarged spleen) and a thickened gall bladder wall. Having both would lead to the highest probability of subsequently developing gastro-oesophageal varices, a life-threatening complication of severe portal hypertension. Hypersplenism was additionally associated with increased risk of splenic varices, splenorenal shunts, and cirrhosis. In contrast, praziquantel treatment showed no measurable influence on the temporal progression of hepatosplenic conditions, suggesting the only available treatment for schistosome infection does not reliably affect the downstream evolution of hepatosplenic pathology. More broadly, this work demonstrates the usefulness of DBNs as a temporal tool for understanding complex disease progression and supporting clinical decision-making. [1] Schistotrack: a prospective multimorbidity cohort. https://www.bdi.ox.ac.uk/research/schistotrack.
items.authors: Zhi, Yin-Cong*; Anguajibi, Victor; Oryema, John; Nabatte, Betty; Opio, Christopher; Kabatereine, Narcis; Chami, Goylette

items.title: Uncovering Unique, Redundant, and Synergistic Granger-Causal Effects in Networks of Random Processes via Partial Information Decomposition
items.abstract: Understanding how complex, system-wide dynamics emerge from the interactions among the units of a distributed network is still a challenging research field in Network Science. Partial Information Decomposition (PID) represents a well established framework decomposing the mutual information (MI) that a target random variable shares with a set of source variables to highlight how it is distributed among the sources, i.e., the unique information exclusively available from each source, the redundant information that can be obtained from at least two different sources, and the synergistic information that is revealed only when multiple sources are considered simultaneously [1]. Since it is formulated for random variables, PID cannot directly quantify causal information among random processes, despite causality being essential for identifying driver–response relationships within complex networks. To overcome this limitation, we exploit the PID framework to decompose Granger causality (GC) from N source random processes {S1, ...SN } to a target process T into unique, redundant and synergistic information flows. (FS→T ) [2]. In this case the PID decomposes the overall information transferred to a target as: FS→T = F R S→T + F S S→T + N i=1 F U Si →T , where F R S→T and F S S→T are the redundant and the synergistic GC from all drivers to the target, and F U Si →T is the unique GC from the ith driver to the target. Exploiting the formalism of state-space models [2] we provide a joint time and frequency domain formulation which highlights distinct high-order behaviors of Granger causal interactions. In a simulated network of vector autoregressive random processes (Fig. 1), we show that varying the coupling parameter c strongly affects redundant and synergistic causal contributions, localized at 35 Hz and 10 Hz and associated with chain-mediated and common-child structures, respectively (Fig. 1 (c,b)). When c = 0, the dynamics are dominated by a single direct interaction, with only the unique contribution FS1 →T present at 10 Hz, reflecting a causal interaction from S1 toward T (Fig. 1(a,d)). Conversely, for c = 1 the unique contribution arises exclusively from the direct causal effect S2 → T , while redundancy emerges at 35 Hz due to information flowing along the chain S3 → S2 → T (Fig. 1(a,d)). Pure synergistic causal interactions are observed only for intermediate values of c, where the causal influences of S1 and S2 on the target are simultaneously present at 10 Hz.
items.authors: Antonacci, Yuri*; Spaccaforno, Salvatore

items.title: Recreational boating networks before, during, and after the COVID-19 pandemic: iterating upon Minnesota aquatic invasive species management tools
items.abstract: In Minnesota, USA, there are over 10,000 lakes and more than 800,000 registered boats, creating a highly dynamic and complex network of boat activity. Given that approximately 6.5% of Minnesota lakes are infested with aquatic invasive species known to be spread by biofouled boats and boating equipment, preventing further spread through this pathway to the thousands of nearby uninfested lakes presents both a management challenge and priority. A thorough understanding of boat movements and associated risk factors has led to the development of innovative network-based tools to understand introduction risk, develop optimal inspection plans, and identify collaboration networks. However, current tools risk becoming out of date as boater movement patterns are dynamic and existing models may no longer represent this dynamic pathway. Here, we advance our understanding of boater connectivity networks by building upon past work and evaluating the potential impact of the COVID-19 pandemic. To predict boater-mediated lake connectivity between 9,233 lakes across three time-periods we developed networks using data from the Minnesota Department of Natural Resources’ watercraft inspection program from 2018 to 2023 and XGBoost machine learning algorithm. We integrated adjustments to account for observation bias in the input data. Analyses suggest stable networks and an increase in connectivity during the peak-pandemic and decrease in the two years following. This approach combines high-performing predictive methods and existing management efforts to expand data-driven resources and leverage collaboration among managers, scientists, and stakeholders to support decision-making and strategic innovation across regions with patchy data availability.
items.authors: Tilsen, Molly*; Kinsley, Amy

items.title: Dynamical analysis of a parameter-aware reservoir computer
items.abstract: Despite the widespread success of reservoir computing (RC) in time-series prediction and forecasting, a mechanistic understanding of how RC learns and reproduces dynamical transitions remains largely unexplored. In particular, existing studies have focused primarily on predictive performance, with almost no systematic investigation of dynamical transitions; especially discontinuous transitions such as crises. We first addressed this gap for continuous transitions by showing that when the reservoir is trained to predict systems undergoing Hopf bifurcations, it itself undergoes a Neimark–Sacker bifurcation, with its critical point lying in close proximity to that of the original system. This demonstrated that RC internalizes phase-space structure rather than acting as a black-box predictor [1]. Building on this foundation, we provided the first mechanistic explanation of how RC reproduces discontinuous transitions. By analyzing the internal dynamics of trained reservoirs, we showed that RC faithfully reproduces boundary and attractor- merging crises, undergoing the same crisis mechanisms and exhibiting identical scaling exponents as the target systems, despite its high dimensionality and distinct dynamics [2]. Together, these results establish a unified dynamical-systems framework for understanding how reservoir computing learns both continuous and discontinuous critical transitions.
items.authors: Dishant Sisodia*; Sarika Jalan

items.title: Coupled dynamics on hypergraphs: Impact of random architecture on unstable fixed points
items.abstract: Building upon previous finite-size analyses of globally coupled oscillator systems, we investigate the dynamics of a finite population of Kuramoto oscillators in which pairwise interactions are defined on an Erd˝ os–R´ enyi (ER) random graph, while higher-order interactions are globally (all-to-all) coupled. The higher-order coupling contributes exclusively to the backward transition, thereby generating a bistable (hysteretic) regime. The global nature of the higher-order interaction facilitates analytical treatment and reduces numerical complexity. We demonstrate, through a combination of numerical simulations and analytical arguments, that finite-size fluctu- ations induce a transition to the synchronized state in ER networks. In contrast, analysis in the thermodynamic limit predicts the absence of a forward synchronization transition, reflected in the divergence of the critical coupling as the system size tends to infinity. To elucidate this behavior, we develop a numerical procedure to determine unstable fixed points for network-based interactions. The structure of these unstable solutions explains the indeterminate nature of the forward critical coupling in ER networks and supports the absence (presence) of a forward transition in the infinite-size (finite-size) regime. We further characterize finite-size effects by systematically varying the average degree, connection probability of the ER network, and the higher-order coupling strength. The resulting distributions of the critical coupling exhibit qualitatively distinct features compared to the globally coupled case, consistent with the behavior of the corresponding unstable fixed points.
